UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA

Acts of the General Assembly
of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky
Passed at the Regular Session of the General
Assembly, which was begun in the city of
Frankfort, Kentucky, on Tuesday, January the
second, 1962, and ended Friday, March the
sixteenth, 1962.

DUNNE PRESS
PRINTERS TO THE COMMONWEALTH OF KENTUCKY
LOUISVILLE, KENTUCKY

ii

Digitized by Google
Original from
UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA

FOREWORD
The 1954 General Assembly imposed upon the Legislative
Research Commission the duty of furnishing the printer correct
copy of bills and resolutions for inclusion in the Kentucky Acts.
Section 57.160 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes stipulates that
bound copies of Kentucky Acts be delivered and distributed with
in sixty days after the end of a regular session.
This time limit is based on sound reasons. The majority of
legislation enacted by any session of the General Assembly be
comes effective as law ninety days after adjournment. Responsi
ble public officers and other interested citizens need to have a
copy of the Kentucky Acts prior to this effective date. Until the
publication of the Kentucky Acts in 1956 the most prompt de
livery had been some 120 days following the close of the session.
According to available records, this edition of the Kentucky Acts
is the first for a regular 60-day legislative session which has been
forwarded to the members of the General Assembly within the
60 days provided for in Section 57.160 of the Kentucky Revised
Statutes.
This edition of the Kentucky Acts involves one major change
from previous publications. Both the 1962 Senate Rules and
House Rules provided that bills amending existing sections of
the Kentucky Revised Statutes would carry the new matter in
italics and the language being deleted in brackets. This pro
cedure enabled members of the General Assembly and other
interested persons to determine the changes being made in exist
ing law without constant referral to the Kentucky Revised
Statutes. The same format has been followed in this volume,
and we hope it will be a convenience for all users.
In the preparation of this volume of the 1962 Kentucky Acts
the Secretary of State has furnished access to copy of filed en
rolled bills and resolutions. The staff of the Legislative Research
Commission compared the prepared page proofs with the enrolled
bills. The staff is especially grateful to Secretary of State Henry
H. Carter and Mrs. Lucille Barclay of his staff for their coopera
tion in making the files of the enrolled bills available despite the

fact that it must often have interfered with their office routine.
In order to expedite furnishing copy to the printer, bills and
resolutions are inserted by chapters according to the order in
which they received gubernatorial action and were filed in the
office of the Secretary of State. Mr. Courtenay J. Walker, Ad
ministrative Assistant to the Director, has had primary responsi
bility for the preparation of this volume.
WILSON W. WYATT
Chairman
CHARLES WHEELER
Director
Frankfort, Kentucky
May 15, 1962
iv

ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
OF THE
COMMONWEALTH OF KENTUCKY
PASSED AT THE REGULAR SESSION OF THE GENERAL ASSEM
BLY, WHICH WAS BEGUN IN THE CITY OF FRANKFORT, KEN
TUCKY, ON TUESDAY, JANUARY THE SECOND, 1962, AND ENDED
FRIDAY, MARCH THE SIXTEENTH, 1962.
CHAPTER 1
(H. R. 9)
A JOINT RESOLUTION making an appropriation to the Legislative
Research Commission for special expenses incurred in connection
with sessions of the General Assembly and declaring an emergency.
WHEREAS, additional personnel are necessary to expedite properly the
business of the Senate and the House of Representatives; and
WHEREAS, the present appropriation of the Legislative Research Com
mission is insufficient to provide adequate clerical services to the
General Assembly;
NOW, THEREFORE,
Be it resolved by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
That there be appropriated out of the General Expenditure
Fund to the Legislative Research Commission the sum of one
thousand seven hundred and fifty dollars per calendar day dur
ing all regular and special sessions of the General Assembly.
This appropriation shall be available for expenditure during the
1961-62 fiscal year.
WHEREAS, the General Assembly is now in session and it
is necessary that these funds be available immediately, an emer
gency is declared to exist and this resolution shall become effec
tive upon its passage and approval by the Governor, and shall
be retroactive to include payment of compensation to such per
sonnel from and including January 2, 1962.
Approved January 18, 1962

2 Acts of the General Assembly
CHAPTER 2
(H. R. 10)
A JOINT RESOLUTION establishing a joint committee on State Govern
ment Operations and making an appropriation.
WHEREAS, the duties and proper functions of the General Assembly
include the duty to study and review the operations, past and current,
of all phases of state government; and
WHEREAS, the interests of the public and of constitutional government
require that the public be fully informed as to the operations of
state government; and
WHEREAS, the Governor in his message before the General Assembly
of Kentucky requested the appointment of a joint committee on
state government operations;
NOW, THEREFORE,
Be it resolved by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. The Committee on Committees of the House
of Representatives and of the Senate, respectively, shall upon the
passage and approval of this Resolution forthwith appoint a joint
committee on state government operations to be composed of
five members of the House of Representatives to be appointed
by the Committee on Committees of the House of Representatives
and four members of the Senate to be appointed by the Com
mittee on Committees of the Senate. The Committee on Com
mittees of the House of Representatives shall appoint as members
of the Joint Committee four members of the majority party and
one member of the minority party. The Committee on Com
mittees of the Senate shall appoint as members of the joint com
mittee three members of the majority party and one member of
the minority party. The Committee appointed hereunder shall
constitute a Committee of the General Assembly. The Commit
tee when appointed shall elect its chairman from among its
members.
Section 2. The Committee on State Government Operations
shall investigate fully any phase or phases of state government
that may appear to the Committee to require the attention of
the General Assembly. The Committee shall determine the scope
of and define the area or areas that it will study.
Section 3. The Committee appointed hereunder shall con
stitute a Committee of the General Assembly with all the powers

Chapter 3 3
granted by Section 39 of the Constitution and by Sections 6.072
and 6.080 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes. The Committee is
hereby authorized to employ an attorney to assist in the conduct
of any investigation and such secretarial help as it deems
necessary.
Section 4. The Committee shall make its final report be
fore date set for sine die adjournment and shall deliver copies
of its report to the Legislative Research Commission for publica
tion. The Committee may announce its findings through such
other media of communication as it may determine.
Section 5. There is hereby appropriated out of the General
Fund the sum of ten thousand dollars ($10,000) to carry out
the purposes of this resolution. Any unexpended portion of this
appropriation shall lapse to the General Fund as of the sine die
adjournment of this session of the General Assembly.
Approved January 23, 1962
CHAPTER 3

Kentucky and its various officers, departments, boards, institutions,

C. School Foundation Program
1962-63 $108,729,457
1963-64 $112,327,879
The State Board of Education shall not approve any working
budget or salary schedule for local boards of education for the
school years 1962-63 and 1963-64 in which the total amount bud
geted for teachers' salaries is not equal to the amount budgeted for
teachers' salaries in 1961-62 plus the additional amount for teach
ers' salaries and guarantee received by the district under this
appropriation. The amount which any district receives under
any guarantee provision shall be presumed to be for teachers'
salaries and shall be placed in the teachers' salaries budget and
allotted to the various teachers' salary ranks in a proportionate
manner in keeping with KRS 157.390. No working budget or
salary schedule shall be approved in which the average salary paid
any rank of teacher is not at least equivalent to the allotment
for that rank and in which any teacher is paid less than ninety
per cent of the Foundation Program allotment for that rank,
except where there is a decline in local revenue of that district
after the district has levied the maximum ad valorem tax rate
permitted by law. Any such reduction permitted shall be only
for the amount of the decline in local revenue.
In the event any local board of education claims there is a
decline in local tax resources in that district, the State Board of
Education shall certify such claim to the Department of Revenue
whose duty it shall be to determine whether or not there is a
decline in local tax resources of such district and if so, the amount
of such decline in local tax resources. The Department of Revenue
shall make its certification to the State Board of Education and
said Board shall accept said certification as final.
The Superintendent of Public Instruction shall not allot to
any school district more basic classroom units than the number of
teachers employed by that school district for basic classroom
units. Entitlement for basic classroom units obtained in schools
or buildings of one classification shall not be allocated to schools
or buildings of another classification, for which purposes the
classifications shall be as follows :
( 1 ) One-teacher schools ;

Chapter 3 11
(2) Schools, other than one-teacher schools, with less
than one hundred pupils in average daily attendance; and,
( 3 ) Schools, other than one-teacher schools, with more
than one hundred pupils in average daily attendance.
The Superintendent of Public Instruction shall allot class
room units for superintendents, principals, their certified assistants
and special instructional personnel as provided in KRS 157.360,
subsection 6, only for personnel who are qualified for their re
spective positions under provisions of law and regulations of the
State Board of Education and who maintain programs approved
under regulations of the State Board of Education.
The number of units alloted by the Superintendent of Public
Instruction for vocational education and the education of excep
tional children shall not exceed the following:

In the event that the number of units outlined above is not
utilized for the particular classes specified, the unused units may
be allotted to Exceptional Children and Mental Retardation.
The Superintendent of Public Instruction shall not allot a full
classroom unit for any non-isolated one-teacher school with fewer
than 27 pupils in average daily attendance and no full class
room units shall be allotted for any one-teacher school which has
fewer than 12 pupils in average daily attendance.
Facilities that are used to house school children shall be ap
proved by the Superintendent of Public Instruction under KRS
162.060 and 162.160. No alterations or additions of old buildings
shall be made without prior approval of the Superintendent of
Public Instruction. No space shall be rented or purchased and
converted to classroom use without prior approval of the Super
intendent of Public Instruction. The State Board of Education

12 Acts of the General Assembly
may adopt regulations for the approval on an annual basis of
temporary facilities for the housing of school children but these
regulations shall not permit the division of any present room by
partitions of nonstructural material. No classroom units shall be
allotted for a second teacher in the same classroom. No facilities
shall be approved for classroom use which are detrimental to the
health of or dangerous to the safety of school children.
If a school district operates any school or grade of a school
for a term of less than nine scholastic months or for a school day
of less than the minimum required by KRS 158.060, the average
daily attendance of such school shall be reduced proportionately
by the Superintendent of Public Instruction in determining the
allotment of classroom units provided for by regulations of the
State Board of Education.
If at the time of the tentative allotment of school funds under
KRS 157.310 through 157.440, it shall be determined that in
sufficient revenue is available to finance in full the Foundation
Program, the Superintendent of Public Instruction shall not allot
any classroom units under the special growth or loss provisions
of KRS 157.360, subsection 3.
Notwithstanding the provisions of KRS 157.400, any un
expended balance at the end of the fiscal years 1961-62, 1962-63,
and 1963-64 shall lapse to the General Fund.
The Superintendent of Public Instruction is authorized to
allocate an amount not to exceed $25,000 in each fiscal year of
the biennium for the cost of planning and developing a program
for educational television facilities and curricular standards within
the Commonwealth.

PART TWO
STATE ROAD FUND
There is established for the purpose of paying all costs of
operation and maintenance of the Department of Highways and

30 Acts of the General Assembly
for carrying on its activities and functions, a State Road Fund
which shall consist of all the revenues realized from fees, taxes
and license fees levied for state road purposes now authorized
by law, and any additional or other revenues, grants or funds,
including any amount which may be received from Federal aid,
bond funds, or any other source pursuant to the provisions of
KRS 177.030, made available for the construction or reconstruc
tion of public roads or for any other of the activities admin
istered by the Department of Highways. Such receipts and
revenues, together with any balance in the existing State Road
Fund as of June 30, 1962 and June 30, 1963, shall constitute
the State Road fund for the fiscal years 1962-63 and 1963-64.
There is appropriated from the State Road Fund the following
amounts for the following departments, agencies, and subdi
visions of the State Government, for the following purposes:
I. HIGHWAY DEPARTMENT GENERALLY
1. DEPARTMENT OF HIGHWAYS
A. Department of Highways. For use by the Department
in its activities, functions, and duties performed by it as pre
scribed by law, all monies received and placed to the credit of
the State Road Fund, not otherwise appropriated in this or
any other Act; provided, however, that an annual budget for
each fiscal year shall be submitted by the Department of High
ways as required by Part Two, Section III of this Act.
2. RURAL HIGHWAYS
A. Division of Rural Highways. For improvement, con
struction, reconstruction and maintenance of county roads and
bridges, which have not been accepted by the Department of
Highways for maintenance, the sum of $5,000,000 for each of
the fiscal years 1962-63 and 1963-64. This appropriation is to be
expended in accordance with the provisions of KRS 179.410 to
179.430. Not more than six per cent (6%) of this appropriation
shall be used for administration.
B. Secondary and Rural Roads. For the construction, re
construction, and maintenance of secondary and rural roads, two-
sevenths of all revenue received under the provisions of KRS

Chapter 3 31
138.220, 138.565, and 177.320. This appropriation is in addition
to the amount appropriated under Section I, subsection 2, Para
graph A, of Part Two of the Appropriation Act.

III. ADMINISTRATION AND EFFECT
The Department of Highways shall submit an annual budget
for each fiscal year to the Department of Finance for approval
by the Commissioner of Finance immediately before the fiscal
year begins. The budget shall be based upon the most current
state road fund revenue estimates and contractual and non
contractual federal aid in which the Department will be eligible
to receive during that fiscal year.
The appropriations made by this Act are subject to the
provisions of Chapters 12, 42. and 45 of the Kentucky Revised
Statutes except where additional requirements are imposed by
this Act. Nothing in this Act shall be construed to repeal any
appropriation for the fiscal year to the State Road Fund ending
June 30, 1962.
PART THREE
REVOLVING, TRUST AND AGENCY FUNDS
There is appropriated to the various boards, commissions,
institutions, agencies and/or subdivisions of the State Government
only as are listed herein for the fiscal year ending June 30, 1963,
and for the fiscal year ending June 30, 1964, all of the fees
( which include fees for board and room, athletics, student activi
ties) and rentals, admittances, sales, bond proceeds, licenses
collected by law, contributions, gifts, subventions, federal grants
and other federal receipts and other miscellaneous receipts pro
duced or received by any of the following agencies of the State
Government, except as otherwise specifically provided, for the
use and benefit of each of the below-named agencies of the State
Government for the purposes for which each fund is authorized.
Receipts which are received by each of the respective agencies
of the State Government listed herein shall be placed to the
credit of a Revolving or Trust and Agency Fund out of which
shall be established separate Revolving or Trust and Agency
Fund Accounts for the use and benefit of these agencies of the
State Government for purposes for which each fund is authorized.
In the event receipts received and credited to any Revolving
or Trust and Agency Fund Account during fiscal year 1962-63
or fiscal year 1963-64 and any balance forwarded to the credit

34 Acts of the General Assembly
of any such account from the previous fiscal year exceed the
appropriation made by specific sum to said account in this Part
of this Appropriation Act, the excess is appropriated to the credit
of said account for the fiscal year in which the excess occurs;
provided, however, that said excess shall become available for
expenditure for the purpose of the account during said year only
with the authorization of the Commissioner of Finance and
approval of the Governor.
Except as herein otherwise provided, the appropriate agency
may requisition for expenditure from the Revolving or Trust and
Agency Fund Account, through the Department of Finance, dur
ing the fiscal year ending June 30, 1963, and during the fiscal
year ending June 30, 1964, all receipts placed to the credit of the
Revolving or Trust and Agency Fund for such year, out of which
has been established a Revolving or Trust and Agency Fund
Account for each separate agency during such fiscal year, plus
any balance which said agency may have had forwarded from
the preceding year on or before September 30, of the then
current fiscal year, by the Department of Finance. On or before
the beginning of each fiscal year, each department shall submit
to the Department of Finance schedules showing estimated
receipts and estimated expenditures by quarterly period.

Chapter 3 45
PART FOUR
GENERAL PROVISION
1. Nothing in this Act shall be construed to repeal any ap
propriation for the fiscal year ending June 30, 1962, and nothing
in this Act is to be construed to amend or alter the provisions
of Chapter 42 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes pertaining to the
duties and powers of the Commissioner of Finance. All ques
tions that arise in interpreting any appropriation in this Act as
to the purpose for which the same may be expended shall be
decided by the Commissioner of Finance in accordance with the
detailed estimates embodied in the budget report, and support
ing work papers. as considered by the General Assembly, and
the decision of the Commissioner of Finance shall be final and
conclusive.
2. WHEREAS, the finances of the Commonwealth can not
be ascertained at this time, and since the actual needs of each
officer, department, board, commission, institution, or subdivision
of the State Government can only be anticipated and not finally
determined bv the General Assembly, and since the total amount
of revenue accruing to the General Expenditure Fund and to the
General Road Fund from which these appropriations are made
can only be anticipated through existing laws and those that
may hereafter be enacted, it is the purpose and intention of this
Act that the expenditures for each fiscal year under Part 1 of
this Act shall not exceed the total financial resources anticipated
to accrue to the General Expenditure Fund and that the expendi
tures for each fiscal year under Part 2 of this Act shall not exceed
the total financial resources anticipated to accrue to the General
Road Fund for the same fiscal year respectively- As the condi
tions can not be fully determined until after the adjournment of
the 1962 General Assembly, and as the needs of each officer,
department, board, agency, commission, institution, or subdivision
of the State Government and the total financial resources accruing
to the General Expenditure Fund and General Road Fund can
not be fully determined at this time, it is the intention and the
purpose of this General Assemblv that the Commissioner of
Finance, with the advice of the Commissioner of Revenue and
the approval of the Governor of the Commonwealth, is author
ized, empowered and directed to ascertain and determine the

46 Acts of the General Assembly
needs of each officer, department, board, commission, institution
or subdivision of the State Government, and to ascertain the total
financial resources anticipated to accrue to the General Expendi
ture Fund and General Road Fund of the Commonwealth, and
upon the ascertainment and determination of said facts, he, the
Commissioner of Finance, in the manner set forth and as provided
by general law, is authorized, empowered and directed by the
General Assembly to prevent an overdraft or deficit in any fiscal
year for which appropriations are herein made, by equitably
reducing, according to need, without discrimination, the appro
priations herein made in Part 1 and Part 2 to any officer, depart
ment, board, commission, institution, or subdivision of the State
Government; provided that the power vested shall not permit
any reduction of the appropriation of any officer, department,
board, commission, institution, or subdivision of the State
Government that will actuallv impair the necessary constitutional
functions of any agency whose operations and functions are
determined to be necessary constitutional functions of govern
ment.
3. Revenues collected in 1962-63 in excess of the revenue
estimate of $232,000,000, but not to exceed $1,000,000 shall be
appropriated to the General Contingency Fund for expenditure
in accordance with Part I, Section 3., C. of this Act.
4. If the Commissioner of Finance determines at any time
during the 1962-64 biennium that funds appropriated for Vet
erans Bonus Debt Service are in excess of amounts required to
meet principal and interest payments during the biennium, the
Commissioner of Finance shall cause the appropriations to be
reduced by the amounts not required for principal and interest.
The appropriation for capital construction shall be increased in
an amount equal to the reduction in the appropriation for Vet
erans Bonus Debt Service.
5. Anything in any statutes of the Commonwealth to the
contrary notwithstanding, Eastern Kentucky State College, West
ern Kentucky State College, Murray State College, Morehead
State College, Kentucky State College and the University of
Kentucky are hereby authorized and empowered, with the ap
proval of the Commissioner of Finance, to utilize funds available

Chapter 4 47
from any appropriated source, for the purpose of cooperating
to the fullest extent possible with federal agencies in such pro
grams as may be proposed by the Federal Government involv
ing the use of federal funds for educational purposes, programs,
and buildings in conjunction with the colleges and the University.
6. In enacting this Appropriation Act, it is the deliberate
intention of the General Assembly to enact each section, and each
subsection thereof, as a separate and/or specific appropriation
and law, and if any section or any subsection thereof shall be
held invalid, or unconstitutional, the decision of the courts shall
not effect or impair any of the remaining sections, subsections,
or provisions contained herein.
7. All continuing appropriations in existence at the time
this Act takes effect are discontinued and repealed by this Act,
except as provided by Chapters 12, 42, 45, 56, 157, 177, and 341
of the Kentucky Revised Statutes. All laws and parts of laws
in conflict with any of the provisions of the above enactment, to
the extent of such conflict, are repealed.
Approved January 31, 1962
CHAPTER 4
(H. B. 7)
AN ACT changing the classification of the City of Hindman, in Knott
County.
WHEREAS, satisfactory information has been presented to the General
Assembly that the population of the City of Hindman, in Knott
County, is such as to justify its being classified as a city of the fifth
class.
NOW, THEREFORE,
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. The City of Hindman, in Knott County, is trans
ferred from the sixth to the fifth class of cities.

Section 2. Whereas funds are now on deposit sufficient to
provide for water and sewage systems, an emergency is declared
to exist and this Act shall become effective upon its passage and
approval by the Governor.

CHAPTER 5
(H. B. 72)
AN ACT relating to rules of the Court of Appeals.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 30.170 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is amended
to read as follows:
( 1 ) The Court of Appeals shall adopt and promulgate rules:
(a) Defining the practice of law;
(b) Prescribing a code of ethics governing the pro
fessional conduct of attorneys and the practice of law;
(c) Establishing practice and procedure for disciplin
ing, suspending and disbarring attorneys;
( d ) Organizing and governing a Bar Association of the
attorneys of the state to act as an administrative agency of the
Court of Appeals for the purpose of enforcing such rules as are
adopted and promulgated by the court under this section; pro
viding for the government of the State Bar as a part of the
judicial department of state government, and for such divisions
thereof as the Court of Appeals determines; requiring all persons
practicing law in this state to be members in good standing of
the State Bar; and fixing the form of its organization and opera
tion;
(e) Fixing fees upon recommendation of the board
of Bar Commissioners [not to exceed ten dollars.] to be paid for
the purpose of administering this section and the rules adopted
and promulgated under it, and for the collection and disbursement
of such fees.

Chapter 6 49
( 2 ) The rules of court adopted and promulgated under this
section shall supercede all laws or parts of law in conflict there
with, to the extent of the conflict.
(3) No rule adopted and promulgated under this section
shall prevent a person not holding himself out as a practicing
attorney for writing a deed, mortgage or will, or prevent a person
from drawing any instrument to which he is a party.
Approved February 7, 1962
CHAPTER 6
(S. B. 45)
AN ACT relating to narcotic drugs.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 218.010 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
The following words and phrases, as used in this chapter,
shall have the following meanings, unless the context otherwise
requires:
(1) "Person" includes any corporation, association, copart
nership, or one or more individuals.
(2) "Physician" means a person authorized by law to
practice medicine in this state and any other person authorized
by law to treat sick and injured human beings in this state and
to use narcotic drugs in connection with such treatment.
(3) "Dentist" means a person authorized by law to prac
tice dentistry in this state.
(4) "Veterinarian" means a person authorized by law to
practice veterinary medicine in this state.

50 Acts of the General Assembly
(5) "Manufacturer" means a person who by compounding,
mixing, cultivating, growing, or other process, produces or pre
pares narcotic drugs, but does not include an apothecary who
compounds narcotic drugs to be sold or dispensed on prescrip
tions.
(6) "Wholesaler" means a person who supplies narcotic
drugs that he himself has not produced nor prepared, on official
written orders, but not on prescriptions.
(7) "Apothecary" means a licensed pharmacist as defined
by the laws of this state and, where the context so requires, the
owner of a store or other place of business where narcotic drugs
are compounded or dispensed by a licensed pharmacist; but noth
ing in this chapter shall be construed as conferring on a person
who is not registered nor licensed as a pharmacist any authority,
right, or privilege, that is not granted to him by the pharmacy
laws of this state.
(8) "Hospital" means an institution for the care and treat
ment of the sick and injured, approved by the State Board of
Health as proper to be entrusted with the custody of narcotic
drugs and the professional use of narcotic drugs under the direc
tion of a physician, dentist, or veterinarian.
( 9 ) "Laboratory" means a laboratory approved by the State
Board of Health as proper to be entrusted with the custody of
narcotic drugs and the use of narcotic drugs for scientific and
medical purposes and for purposes of instruction.
(10) "Sale" includes barter, exchange, or gift, or offer
therefor and each such transaction made by any person, whether
as principal, proprietor, agent, servant, or employe.
(11) "Coca leaves" includes cocaine and any compound,
manufacture, salt, derivative, mixture, or preparation of coca
leaves, except derivatives of coca leaves which do not contain
cocaine, ecgonine, or substances from which cocaine or ecgonine,
may be synthesized or made.
( 12 ) "Opium" includes morphine, codeine, and heroin, and
any compound, manufacture, salt, derivative, mixture, or prepara
tion of opium, but does not include apomorphine or any of its
salts.

Chapter 6 51
(13) "Cannabis" includes all parts of the plant Cannabis
sativa L., whether growing or not; the seeds thereof; the resin
extracted from any part of such plant; and every compound,
manufacture, salt, derivative, mixture, or preparation of such
plant, its seeds, or resin; but shall not include the mature stalks
of such plant, fiber produced from such stalks, oil or cake made
from the seeds of such plant, any other compound, manufacture,
salt, derivative, mixture, or preparation of such mature stalks
(except the resin extracted therefrom), fiber oil, or cake, or the
sterilized seed of such plant which is incapable of germination.
(14) "Narcotic Drugs" includes coca leaves, opium, isoni-
pecaine (the substance identified chemically as l-methvl-4-
phemylpiperidine-4-carboxylic acid ethvl ester, or any salt thereof
by whatever trade name identified), cannabis, every other sub
stance neither chemically nor physicially distinguishable from
them, and any drug having an addiction-forming or addiction-
sustaining liability similar to morphine or cocaine which is desig
nated [by proclamation of the President of the United States
and] by regulation of the State Board of Health as a narcotic
drug.
( 15 ) "Federal Narcotic Laws" means the laws of the United
States relating to opium, coca leaves, and other narcotic drugs.
( 16 ) "Official written order" means an order written on a
form provided for that purpose by the United States Commis
sioner of Narcotics, under any laws of the United States making
provision therefor, if such order forms are authorized and re
quired by Federal law, and if no such order form is provided,
then on an official form provided for that purpose by the State
Board of Health.
(17) "Dispense" includes distribute, leave with, give away,
dispose of, or deliver.
(18) "Registry number" means the number assigned to
each person registered under the Federal Narcotic Laws.
Section 2. Section 218.080 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:

macist or practitioner.

3. Section 218.210 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
isamended to read as follows:
(
1
) Any person who sells, supplies, prescribes, administers,
dispenses or furnishes, or causes to be sold, supplied, prescribed,
administered, dispensed or furnished, any narcotic drug in viola
tion of chapter 218
ofthe Kentucky Revised Statutes [this chap
ter] to
aperson under twenty-one years of age, shall be guilty

Chapter 6 53
of a felony and shall be fined not more than twenty thousand dol
lars and confined in the penitentiary for not less than twenty
years, and may be so confined for a longer period or for life.
(2) (a) Any person in possession of any narcotic drug in
violation of any provision of chapter 218 of the Kentucky Revised
Statutes [this chapter, except KRS 218.250], shall be guilty of
a felony and shall, for the first offense be punished by confine
ment in the penitentiary for not less than two years nor more
than ten years and by a fine of not more than twenty thousand
dollars, and for each subsequent offense be punished by confine
ment in the penitentiary for not less than five years nor more
than twenty years and by a fine of not more than twenty thousand
dollars.
( b ) Any person who violates any provision of chapter 218
of the Kentucky Revised Statutes [this chapter], for which a
specific penalty is not otherwise provided [except KRS 218.250] ,
shall be guilty of a felony and shall, for the first offense, be
punished by confinement in the penitentiary for not less than
five years nor more than twenty years and by a fine of not more
than twenty thousand dollars, and, for each subsequent offense
be punished by confinement in the penitentiary for not less than
ten years nor more than forty years, and by a fine of not more
than twenty thousand dollars.
(3) No court or judge thereof shall postpone or suspend
judgment or sentence or probate a defendant when the defendant
has pleaded guilty to or been convicted of selling, supplying, pre
scribing, administering, dispensing or furnishing, or causing to be
sold, supplied, prescribed, administered, dispensed or furnished,
any narcotic drug in violation of chapter 218 of the Kentucky
Revised Statutes [this chapter] to a person under twenty-one
years of age, or when the defendant has previously pleaded
guilty to or been convicted of a violation of any of the other
provisions of chapter 218 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes [this
chapter] , except KRS 218.250, or has previously pleaded guilty
to or been convicted of a violation of any of the narcotic laws
of the United States of America or any state or territory thereof.
(4) Whenever a person charged with a violation of sub
section (1) of KRS 218.250 waives a jury, the court or judge

54 Acts of the General Assembly
thereof may, upon the defendant pleading or being found guilty,
sentence the defendant to imprisonment for twelve months with
out the intervention of a jury. Notwithstanding the provisions
of subsection (2) of KRS 218.250, no court or judge thereof shall
postpone or suspend judgment or sentence or probate a defendant
when the defendant has pleaded guilty to or been convicted of
a violation of KRS 218.250, unless the defendant consents to
enter, and forthwith enters an institution approved by the court
for the treatment of narcotic addicts and remains therein until
discharged by said institution; failure of the defendant to do so
shall automatically revoke his probation and require the prompt
entry of judgment and service of sentence in the county jail.
(5) Any person who violates subsection (2) of KRS 218.160
shall be punished by a fine of not less than one hundred dollars
nor more than five hundred dollars, and for each subsequent
offense shall be punished by imprisonment in the county jail
for ninety days and by a fine of not less than five hundred dollars.
Approved February 9, 1962
CHAPTER 7
(S. B. 46)
AN ACT relating to city-county boards of health in counties containing
cities of the second class.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 212.640 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is amended
to read as follows:
In any county containing a city of the second class the fiscal
court of the county and the legislative body of the citv of the
second class may, by joint action, establish a city-county health
department. The department when established shall be governed
by a city-county board of health composed of six [five] members,
one of whom shall be either the mayor or city manager of the city,

Chapter 9 55
whichever is appointed by the city legislative body, one of whom
shall be the county judge, one of whom shall be a dentist, and
three of whom shall be physicians appointed in like manner as
dental and physician members are appointed on county boards
of health and to hold office as provided in KRS 212.020.
Approved February 9, 1962
CHAPTER 8
(S. B. 49)
AN ACT relating to barbiturate drugs.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 217.471 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is amended
to read as follows :
Duly licensed pharmacists may sell, dispense and furnish any
barbiturate only upon the prescription of a practitioner and then
only if the container of such barbiturate bears a label showing
the name and address of the pharmacy or pharmacist, the name
of the person for whom the barbiturate was prescribed, the name
and address of the prescribing physician, the date of filling the
prescription, and if for an animal, the specie and owner of same.
All such prescriptions shall be numbered and filed and retained
for at least two years. No such prescription shall be refilled except
upon authorization of the prescribing practitioner. A record shall
be kept of all such refills.
Approved February 9, 1962
CHAPTER 9
(H. B. 65)
AN ACT relating to the Judicial Branch of State Government, with par
ticular relation to Circuit Judges and Judges and Commissioners of
the Court of Appeals, and the Director of the Administrative Office
of the Courts; making provisions for service, compensation and
expenses of circuit judges as Special Commissioners of the Court of
Appeals; and making provisions with respect to retirement systems
for Circuit Judges and Judges and Commissioners of the Court of
Appeals and the Director of the Administrative Office of the Courts.

56 Acts of the General Assembly
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. A new section of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is created to read as follows :
Each regular circuit judge shall be, ex officio, a special com
missioner of the Court of Appeals, and as such shall perform such
duties and render such services, at such times and places, co
extensive with the Commonwealth, as may be required by the
Court of Appeals. The duties and services may include, but are
not limited to, intermediate review of appealed cases. Each such
special commissioner shall be paid out of the State Treasury a
salary at the Tate of $2,400 per annum, which shall be in addition
to his salary as circuit judge. Expenses necessary to the per
formance of assigned duties of special commissioners shall be
paid out of the State Treasury upon approval by the Court of
Appeals. Should any such special commissioner fail or neglect,
without cause, to perform any duty assigned to him, the Court
of Appeals shall forthwith remove him from the office of special
commissioner by entering an order on its record, copies of which
shall be transmitted to the person removed and to the Department
of Finance.
Section 2. A new section of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is created to read as follows:
The judicial retirement system established by KRS 21.350
to 21.580 shall extend to and embrace circuit judges and the
Director of the Administrative Office of the Courts, upon their
election to participate therein in accordance with the provisions
of Section 3 of this Act. The word "service" as used in KRS 21.370
to 21.480 means service as a judge or commissioner of the Court of
Appeals, or as a regular circuit judge, or as Director of the Admin
istrative Office of the Courts, including service in one and then
another of those capacities.
Section 3. A new section of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is created to read as follows:
Each circuit judge in office on July 1, 1962, and the Director
of the Administrative Office of the Courts in office on that date,

Chapteb 9 57
may within 30 days after that date, and any circuit judge or
Director of the Administrative Office of the Courts thereafter
taking office may within 30 days after taking office, elect to
make monthly contributions to the judicial retirement system
established under KRS 21.350 to 21.480 in an amount equal to
three percent of his monthly official salary, including salary as
special commissioner of the Court of Appeals. The election shall
be effective to establish membership in the system as of July 1,
1962, or as of the date the judge or director thereafter took office,
as the case may be. The election shall be addressed and filed,
and have effect, the same as an election made by a judge or
commissioner of the Court of Appeals under KRS 21.360.
Section 4. Section 21.370 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
Except as provided in subsection (2) of KRS 21.410 and in
subsection (2) of KRS 21.420 no benefits shall be payable under
KRS 21.350 to 21.480 [21.510,] to any member, [judge or com
missioner,] or to his widow, [or children,] unless he has com
pleted at least eight years or service, [as a judge or commissioner
of the Court of Appeals or in one and then the other of those capa
cities.] including service before becoming a member. No widow
of a retired member shall be entitled to any benefits unless she
was the wife of the member at the time he retired.
Section 5. Section 21.380 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows :
The normal retirement date or age of a member shall be his
sixty -fifth [seventieth] birthday; [except that in the case of a
member who was over sixty years of age when he took office as a
judge or commissioner the normal retirement date or age shall be
the tenth anniversary of his becoming a member.] however, KRS
21.350 to 21.480 [21.510,] does not require that a member retire
at the normal retirement date.
Section 6. Section 21.400 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
( 1 ) A member who retires on or after his normal retirement
date shall receive a service retirement allowance, payable monthly
during his lifetime, in an amount per month equal to five percent

of his final compensation multiplied by the number of years of
his service, [as a judge or commissioner of the Court of Appeals ( ]
including service before becoming a member of the system [ ) ] ,
but in no event to exceed one hundred percent of final compen

monthly compensation of the member for the sixty months of
service [as a judge or commissioner] immediately preceding his
retirement, [.] including, in the case of a circuit judge, com
pensation received as special commissioner of the Court of
Appeals.
(2) A member who retires before his normal retirement
date shall be vested with the right to receive, when he reaches his
normal retirement age, a service retirement allowance computed
and payable in accordance with the provisions of subsection ( 1 )
of this section, on the basis of the number of years of his actual

(3) In lieu of the right provided for in subsection (2) of

monthly service retirement allowance equal in amount to the
monthly allowance that would have become payable under sub
section ( 2 ) of this section when he reached his normal retirement
age, reduced actuarially in accordance with his actual age at the

the age of sixty-five at the time of retirement shall have the option
of commencing to receive at once a monthly service retirement
allowance equal to the monthly allowance that would have be
come payable under subsection (2) of this section when he
reached his normal retirement age.]
Section 7. Section 21.430 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes

June 16, 1960, and until their successors are appointed and have

original appointing authority, for the unexpired term.

9. Sections 23.300 to 23.380, inclusive, of the Ken
tucky Revised Statutes are repealed, except that Sections 23.330
and 23.340 shall continue in force with respect to special circuit

1,

Section 10.
Anew section of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
iscreated to read as follows:
All of the assets of the special circuit judge fund established
under KRS 23.350 to 23.370 hereby are transferred to the Judicial
Retirement Board established under KRS 21.430 and shall be
available to that board for the purposes of KRS 21.350 to 21.480;
provided, however, that so long as any of the special circuit judges
appointed under KRS 23.300 to 23.380 prior to July
1,1962, con

the assets so transferred and the income therefrom shall be held
separate and apart for the purpose of paying compensation to
such special circuit judges or benefits to such circuit judges;
provided, further, that the assets of the Judicial Retirement Board
as of June 30, 1962, and the income therefrom, shall also be held
separate and apart for the purpose of paying benefits to the
judges and regular commissioners of the Court of Appeals or their

Section 11. A new section of the Kentucky Revised StatutesJ
is created to read as follows:
The existing membership of the Director of the Administra
tive Office of the Courts in the Kentucky Employes Retirement

shall have membership in the Kentucky Employes Retirement
Svstem.
Section 12. A new section of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is created to read as follows:
The sums appropriated by the General Appropriation Act of
the 1962 General Assembly to the Circuit Judges' Retirement
System for the fiscal years 1962-63 and 1963-64 hereby are trans
ferred to the Judicial Retirement Board for the purposes of KRS
21.350 to 21.480. There hereby is appropriated to the administra
tive office of the Court of Appeals, out of the General Expenditure
Fund in the State Treasury, the sum of $148,800 per year for

CHAPTER 10
(H. B. 91)
AN ACT relating to state taxes on legal actions, proceedings and appeals.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 142.011 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is amended
to read as follows:
(1) A tax of five [three] dollars hereby is levied on each
original action in the circuit court, commencing with or without
original process; on each original action in other courts when the
amount in controversy, exclusive of interest and costs, exceeds
fifty dollars; on each appeal to the circuit court or quarterly court;
on each traverse of forcible entry or detainer; and on each pro
ceeding to revive a judgment. A tax of twenty-five [five] dollars
hereby is levied on each appeal to the Court of Appeals.
(2) The revenue derived from the taxes imposed by this
section shall be paid into the State Treasury and credited to the
General Expenditure Fund, for general state purposes.
Approved February 10, 1962
CHAPTER 11
(H. B. 108)
AN ACT relating to county law libraries.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 172.200 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is amended
to read as follows:
( 1 ) Upon the adoption of this optional plan, the circuit
judge shall appoint one member of the county's bar, and the
members of the county's bar shall, by majority vote, elect an
other of their number, which two attorneys shall, with the
county attorney of the county, constitute and be designated as
"Trustees, County Law Library."

62 Acts of the General Assembly
(2) The trustees shall serve for a term of two years or
until their successors are elected or qualified.
(3) The trustees shall be in charge of the county law
library, and they shall make purchases of the various state and
federal case reports, textbooks, legal encyclopedia, and all other
books usually incident to or customarily found in law libraries,
or necessary to the protection of the rights of litigants, and they
shall cause same to be properly arranged in the county law
library, directing the ex officio librarian in the exercise of his
duties.
(4) The trustees shall exercise their absolute discretion in
the purchase of books, pamphlets, periodicals, and other ma
terials, and in the appointment and compensation of personnel
to assist the ex officio librarian in the handling of materials and
in the maintenance of the library, but the trustees [they] shall
not contract for any such purchases and appointments so as to
create an indebtedness greater than the anticipated revenues for
the following eighteen months, the anticipated revenue being
based upon the preceding eighteen months' revenue, and any
indebtedness of the county law library fund shall not be con
sidered in any way an indebtedness of the county, but shall be
an indebtedness of the county law library fund only, and all
creditors must look only to the county law library fund for satis
faction of their indebtedness.
(5) The trustees shall designate one of their number as
treasurer and he shall be accountable for the receipt, deposit,
and disbursement of all sums received for the operation of the
county law library. He shall be bonded by a corporate bond, the
cost of which shall be paid out of the receipts of the library
fund. He shall deposit all sums received by him as treasurer in
a regular banking depository, and he shall pay for all purchases
made by the trustees by check or draft, keeping a true and ac
curate account of all sums received and expended by him. He
shall annually file a written report with the circuit judge of
the county showing all sums received by him, together with the
court from which they were received, and an itemized state
ment of all expenditures made by him. The treasurer shall turn

Chapter 12 63
all funds over to his successor, together with a full inventory
of the county law library, and together with a full and complete
itemized statement of all outstanding accounts.
Approved February 13, 1962
CHAPTER 12
(H. B. 125)
AN ACT relating to old-age, survivors and disability insurance coverage
of public employes.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 61.410 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
(1) It is hereby declared to be the policy of the General
Assembly to extend the Federal Old-Age, Survivors and Disability
Insurance coverage to all public employes regardless of whether
such employes are occupying positions which are covered by a re
tirement system; provided, however, that no employe occupying a
position to which KRS 161.220 to 161.710 are applicable shall be
held to fall within the class of persons sought to be affected by
this statement of policy except for employes of the state colleges
and public junior colleges.
[ ( 1 ) In order to extend to certain employes of the Common
wealth and its political subdivisions, and interstate instrumentali
ties, not now members of public retirement systems, and to the
dependents and survivors of such employes, the basic protection
accorded to others by the Old-Age and Survivors Insurance
System embodied in the Social Security Act, it is hereby declared
to be the policy of the General Assembly to provide such protec
tion to such employes, and their dependents and survivors, on as
broad a basis as is permitted under the Social Security Act.]
(2) It is also the policy of the General Assembly that the
protection afforded public employes by membership in a retire
ment system or by the right to receive periodic benefits under

64 Acts of the General Assembly
such a retirement system will not be impaired as a result of any
agreement made between the Commonwealth and the Secretary
pursuant to KRS 61.410 to 61.500.
[(2) Subject to the provisions of KRS 61.430 (6) and the

coverage under a public retirement system, it is declared to be
the policy of the General Assembly to extend the aforesaid pro
tection to the employes of the state colleges enumerated in KRS
164.290, and their dependents and survivors.]
(3) The General Assembly hereby ratifies the extension of
Federal Old-Age, Survivors and Disability Insurance coverage to
public employes in positions covered by a retirement system prior
to the effective date of this Act provided that the procedures
specified by Ky. Acts, 1956, Chapter 32, Section 3 (6) were sub
stantially followed.

Section 4. A new section of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is created to read as follows:
( 1 ) In order for any service performed by an employe
in a position covered by a retirement system to be included in
an agreement as permitted by KRS 61.430, a majority of the
members of such retirement system must vote in the affirmative
on the question of whether the service covered by the retirement
svstem should be included in the agreement. The procedure for
obtaining the vote of the members of the retirement systems shall
be as follows:

ance with the requirements of Section 218 (d) (3) of the Social
Security Act;
(c) The votes shall be cast in secret on written ballots;
(d) An opportunity to vote shall be given only to those
employes who occupied positions covered by such retirement
system, and were members of such system, on the date upon
which the notice of referendum was given and on the date upon
which the referendum was held; and

Social Security Act have been fulfilled, the Governor or any
agency or individual designated by him shall so certify to the

a re

subdivisions, there are deemed to exist separate retirement sys

CHAPTER 13
(H. B. 131)
AN ACT relating to the Department of Education; recognizing that the
State Board of Education is an educational agency and instrumen
tality of the Commonwealth; amending KRS 156.010 and 156.070 to
confer upon said Board certain powers concerning Area Vocational
Schools and concerning television in aid of education and other proper
functions; and declaring an emergency.
Section 1. KRS 156.010 is amended to read:
( 1 ) The Department of Education shall consist of the
State Board of Education and the Superintendent of Public
Instruction, who shall be the head of the department.
(2) The Department of Education shall exercise all the
administrative functions of the state in relation to the manage
ment and control of the public common schools, of vocational
education and rehabilitation of West Kentucky Vocational School,
the Kentucky School for the Blind, and the Kentucky School for
the Deaf, and may exercise certain powers and functions re
lating to Area Vocation Schools, and relating to television in aid
of education and other proper public functions, as provided in
KRS 156.070, as amended.
(3) The Kentucky School for the Deaf Advisory Board
and the State Textbook Commission are included in the Depart
ment of Education and constitute divisions thereof, but each
shall continue to exercise all the functions conferred upon it by
law.
Section 2. A new Section of KRS is hereby enacted, the
same to be designated KRS 156.035, and to read:
The Kentucky State Board of Education is recognized
to be a public body corporate and politic, and an agency and
instrumentality of the Commonwealth in the performance
of essential governmental functions.
Section 3. KRS 156.070 concerning the general powers
and duties of the State Board of Education is hereby amended
to read:

76 Acts of the General Assembly
( 1 ) The State Board of Education shall have the manage
ment and control of the common schools, public vocational
education and vocational rehabilitation, West Kentucky Voca
tional School, and the Kentucky School for the Blind.
(2) It is hereby authorized to purchase, lease, rent, or
erect one or more buildings in the following cities or counties:
Ashland, Harlan County, Hazard, Jefferson County, Madisonville,
Somerset, and Warren County, in order to establish and provide
for the operation of schools for the purpose of making available
facilities for vocational training, according to curricula established
and approved by the Board, and to be operated in accordance
with the State Plan for Vocational Education and policies of
the Board. The management and operation of these schools,
including all duties, powers, rights, purposes, responsibilities,
properties and funds, are vested in the Board. In addition to
such appropriations of State funds as may be made to the Board
for such schools, the Board may receive therefor grants, contribu
tions, matching funds, gifts, devises and bequests from any
source, whether federal or state, public or private.
(3) (a) It is hereby authorized to lease from the State
Property and Buildings Commission, or others, whether public
or private, any lands, buildings, structures, installations and facili
ties suitable for use in establishing and furthering television as
an aid or supplement to classroom instruction, throughout the
Commonwealth, and for incidental use in any other proper public
functions. Any such lease may be for any initial term commenc
ing with the date of such lease and ending with the next ensuing
June 30 which is the close of the then current fiscal biennium of
the Commonwealth with exclusive options in favor of the Board
to renew the same for successive ensuing bienniums (July 1 in
each even year to June 30 in the next ensuing even year); and
the rentals may be fixed at such sums in each biennium, if re
newed, as will be sufficient to enable the State Property and
Buildings Commission to pay therefrom the maturing principal
of and interest on (and provide reserves for) any revenue bonds
which said State Property and Buildings Commission may deter
mine to be necessary and sufficient, in agreement with the Board,
to provide the cost of acquiring such television facilities, with
appurtenances and such costs as may be incident to the issuance
of such bonds.

Chapter 13 77
( b ) That each such option of the State Board of Education
to renew the lease for succeeding biennial term may be exer
cised at any time after the adjournment of the session of the
General Assembly at which appropriations shall have been made
for the operation of the state government for such succeeding
biennial term, by notifying the State Property and Buildings
Commission in writing, signed by the Superintendent of Public
Instruction, and delivered to the Commissioner of Finance as
a member of said Commission; provided, however, that such
option shall be deemed automatically exercised, and the lease
automatically renewed for the succeeding biennium, effective on
the first day thereof, unless a written notice of the Board's elec
tion not to renew shall have been delivered in the office of the
Commissioner of Finance before the close of business on the
last working day in April immediately preceding the beginning
of such succeeding biennium.
(c) The Board shall not itself operate such leased tele
vision facilities, or undertake the preparation of the educational
presentations or films to be transmitted thereby, but may enter
into one or more contracts to provide therefor, with any public
agency and instrumentality of the Commonwealth having, or
able to provide, a staff with proper technical qualifications, upon
which agency and instrumentality the Board,, through the Su
perintendent of Public Instruction and the Department of Edu
cation, ^ represented in such manner as to coordinate matters
of curriculum with the curricula prescribed for the public schools
of the Commonwealth. Any such contract for the operation of the
leased television facilities may permit limited and special uses
of the television facilities for other programs in the public in
terest, subject to such reasonable terms and conditions as the
Board and the operating agency and instrumentality may agree
upon; but any such contract shall affirmatively forbid the use
of the television facilities, at any time or in any manner, in the
dissemination of political propaganda or in furtherance of the
interest of any political party or candidate for public office, or
for commercial advertising. No lease between the Board and
the State Property and Buildings Commission shall bind the
Board to pay rentals for more than one fiscal biennium at a
time, subject to the aforesaid renewal options. The Board may
receive and may apply to rental payments under any such lease

78 Acts of the General Assembly
and to the cost of providing for the operation of such television
facilities not only appropriations which may be made to it from
State funds, from time to time, but also contributions, gifts,
matching funds, devises and bequests from any source, whether
federal or state, and whether public or private, so long as the
same are not conditioned upon any improper use of the television
facilities in a manner inconsistent with the provisions hereof.
[2] (4) The State Board of Education may, on the recom
mendation and with the advise of the Superintendent of Public
Instruction, prescribe, print, publish and distribute at public
expense such rules, regulations, courses of study, curriculums,
bulletins, programs, outlines, reports and placards as it deems
necessary for the efficient management, control and operation of
the schools under its jurisdiction. All rules and regulations pub
lished or distributed by the board shall be enclosed in a booklet
or binder on which the words "Informational Copy" shall be
clearly stamped or printed.
[4] (5) In its capacity as a board of control for the West
Kentucky School, the board may receive by any legal mode of
conveyance, and hold and enjoy, property of any description.
Section 4. Whereas unusual conditions of unemployment
in certain sections of the State necessitate the training and re
training of youth in those sections in order to make a livelihood
available for such young people coming into the labor market,
and this Act will promote such training and retraining, an emer
gency is declared to exist, and this Act will become effective
upon its passage by the General Assembly and approval by
the Governor.
Approved February 13, 1962

CHAPTER 15
(H. B. 132)
AN ACT relating to the State Property and Buildings Commission, amend
ing KRS 56.440, KRS 56.450, KRS 56.520, and KRS 56.550, and de
claring an emergency.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. KRS 56.440 is hereby amended to read: As used
in KRS Chapter 56, unless the context otherwise requires:
(1) "Commission" means the State Property and Buildings
Commission.
(2) "Real estate" includes lands together with improvements
thereon and appurtenances thereto.
(3) "Building" includes any structure or improvement upon
real estate of a permanent nature; and additionally includes any
sites, structures, equipment, machinery or devices for the purpose
of establishing, developing or furthering television in aid of educa
tion or in aid of any other proper public functions, whether or
not the same would otherwise be legally defined as buildings.
(4) "Building project" includes the construction, reconstruc
tion and structural maintenance of buildings, the installation of
utility services, including roads and sewers, and the purchase and
installation of equipment, facilities and furnishings of a perman
ent nature for buildings, and for television purposes as referred
to in sub-section (3) hereof.
(5) "State agency" means any state officer, department,
hoard, commission, institution, division or independent agency as
defined in Chapter 12 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes.
yas

80 Acts of the General Assembly
(6) "Department" means the Department of Finance.
Section 2. KRS 56.450 is hereby amended to read:
(1) There is recognized, as an independent agency of the
state within the meaning of Chapter 12 of the Kentucky Revised
Statutes, a State Property and Buildings Commission composed of
the Governor, who shall be chairman thereof, the Lieutenant
Governor, the Attorney General, the Commissioner of Finance
and the Commissioner of Revenue, or their alternates as herein
after authorized and permitted.
(2) No member of the commission shall receive any salary,
fee or other remuneration for his services as a member of the
commission but each member shall be entitled to be reimbursed
for his ordinary traveling expenses, including meals and lodging,
incurred in the performance of his duties.
(3) The commission constitutes a public body corporate
with perpetual succession and power in its name to contract and
be contracted with, sue and be sued, adopt bylaws, have and use
a corporate seal, and exercise all of the powers granted to private
corporations generally in the Kentucky Revised Statutes, Chapter
271, except as the same may be inconsistent with KRS 56.440 to
56.550.
(4) Subject to the provisions of Section 4 of this Act, but
notwithstanding any other provision of the Kentucky Revised
Statutes to the contrary, all revenue bonds issued by state agencies
except those bonds issued by the State Colleges, the University,
or The Turnpike Authority of Kentucky, shall be issued under
the provisions of Chapter 56 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
provided further that as an additional and alternative method
for the issuance of revenue bonds under the provisions of this
Chapter 56 of Kentucky Revised Statutes, upon application of
any State agency and approval by the Commission, the Commis
sion acting for and on behalf of said state agency may issue
revenue bonds in its own name in accordance with the terms and
provisions of Chapter 58 of Kentucky Revised Statutes secured
by and payable solely from all or any part of the revenues of said
state agency as may be specified and provided in such approved
application, and any covenants and undertakings of said state

Chapter 15 81
agency in such approved application with regard to the production
of such revenues and the use, application or disposition thereof
may be enforced by the holders of any such revenue bonds or by
any Trustee for such bondholders.
(5) (a) Each member of the Commission is hereby author
ized and empowered to designate by an instrument in writing
over his signature and filed with the Secretary as a public record
of the Commission, an Alternate with full authority to attend in
the absence of the appointing Member for any reason, any prop
erly convened meeting of the Commission and to participate in
the consideration of, and voting upon, business and transactions
of the Commission. Any such designation of an Alternate may, in
the discretion of the appointing Member, be limited upon the face
of the appointing instrument, to be effective only for a designated
meeting or only for specified business; or the same may be shown
on the face of the appointing instrument to be on a continuing
basis, whenever the appointing Member is unable to attend, but
always subject to revocation by the appointing Member in an
instrument of like formality, similarly filed with the Secretary
as a public record of the Commission. Any party transacting
business with the Commission, or materially affected thereby,
shall be entitled to accept and rely upon a joint certificate of the
Secretary of the Commission and any Member of the Commission
concerning the designation of any Alternate, the time of desig
nation, the scope thereof, and if of a continuing nature whether
the same has been revoked, and when; and such joint certificate
shall be made and delivered, to any such party within a reasonable
time after written request is made therefor with acceptable identi
fication of the business or transaction referred, and of the re
questing party's interest therein. Each Alternate shall be a person
on the staff of the appointing Member, or in the employ of his
agency or department of the Government of the Commonwealth,
as the case may be.
(b) Any three members of the Commission shall constitute
a quorum and shall by majority vote be authorized to transact any
and all business of the Commission. One alternate may be sub
stituted for a member in order to establish a quorum provided that
no business is transacted beyond the scope of authority of such
alternate.

82 Acts of the General Assembly
Section 3. KRS 56.520 is hereby amended to read:
(1) The commission is authorized and empowered to issue
and sell revenue or other authorized bonds, in carrying out the
provisions of this chapter, in such denominations and in such
amount or amounts, as may be deemed to be for the best interest
of the Commonwealth, for any of the following purposes:
(a) To acquire real estate;
(b) To pay all or any part of the expense or cost of or
incidental to a building project;
(c) To defray the cost of plans, specifications, blue prints,
architectural fees and other expenses authorized to be incurred
under the terms of KRS Chapter 56.
( 2 ) Any revenue bonds so issued shall bear interest at a rate
not exceeding five percent per annum, payable semi-annually,
and shall become due and payable not more than forty years from
the date of issuance. The payment of the bonds, together with
the interest thereon, may be secured by a pledge and a first
lien on all of the receipts and revenue derived, or to be derived,
from the rental or operation of the property involved. Neither
the payment of any bond, nor the interest thereon, issued under
the authority of KRS Chapter 56, shall constitute an indebtedness
of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, nor shall any bond or interest
thereon be payable out of any fund except such funds as may be
derived from rentals or other revenues derived from the operation
of the properties or from such revenues as may have been, or may
be, available for such purpose, or purposes, by law.
(3 ) All bonds which may be issued by the Commission under
the provisions of KRS Chapter 56, shall be sold as provided in
KRS 42.070 [after having been duly advertised in a newspaper of
general circulation in the Commonwealth during three consecu
tive weeks and in such other advertising as the department may
prescribe if it finds additional advertising is necessary] and after
newspaper advertising conforming to the requirements of KRS
Chapter 424.
(4) All competitive bids for the sale of such revenue bonds
shall be opened and read publicly by the Commissioner of Fi
nance or his representative at a designated place, day and hour,
all of which shall be announced in the advertising made relative
thereto.

Chapter 15 83
(5) In the event the commission issues and sells bonds for
a building project as authorized by KRS Chapter 56, insurance,
including fire and windstorm, casualty, catastrophe, use and oc
cupancy and such other insurance as the commission may deem
advisable, shall be carried in connection with the building
project, and it may so obligate and bind itself in a trust indenture
securing the payment of such bonds. Any such insurance shall
be paid for out of funds available for the project.
(6) All money from the sale of bonds shall be deposited in
the State Treasury and shall be credited to a special account to
be designated as directed by the department, and no part thereof

poses authorized by KRS Chapter 56, together with the cost

penses directly related thereto. The commission may provide in
