the State Local Finance Officer shall, within thirty days there
after, render to the judge of each county having deposits in said
fund a statement thereof for each bond issue of that county.
On or about the first day of May of each year, the State Local
Finance Officer shall deliver to the county judge, as chairman
of the county budget commission, an estimate of the principal
and interest requirements of outstanding bonds issued by said
county or of the proportionate annual amount which should be
deposited in a sinking fund.
(6) Disbursements from the County Sinking Fund shall,
when authorized by the State Local Finance Officer, be made
in the same manner as other claims on the Commonwealth are
paid. Disbursements may be made only for (a) the payment of
principal or interest, or both, of the bonds for which the deposit
was made, and (b) the investment of such funds as is hereinafter
authorized.
(7) All coupons and bonds for the payment of which
deposits are made in the County Sinking Fund shall be paid
either directly by the State Local Finance Officer or by the
bank designated as paying agent. Such bank may be paid a
reasonable fee for such services by the Department of Finance
[Revenue] out of its appropriation. All paid bonds and coupons
shall be surrendered to the State Local Finance Officer and can
celed, and shall be delivered to the judge of each county along
with the annual statement provided for in this section.

106 Acts of the Genebal Assembly
Section 5. Section 66.390 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
Ownership of obligations of Kentucky counties shall dis
qualify the Commissioner of Finance [Revenue] or any of his
appointees from serving in any capacity incident to the adminis
tration of KRS 66.280 to 66.380, or KRS 68.220, 68,250, 68,270,
68.280, 68.310, 68.320 or 68.370.
Section 6. Section 66.990 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
(1) Any officer who willfully fails to perform his duty
under KRS 66.010 to 66.040 shall be deemed guilty of misfeasance
in office, and shall be fined not more than one hundred dollars.
(2) The Commissioner of Finance [Revenue] or any of his
appointees who performs any act in violation of the provisions of
KRS 66.390 shall be removed from office or employment and
made ineligible for reappointment in the same or any similar
capacity, and upon conviction shall be fined not more than five
hundred dollars and imprisoned for not more than ninety days,
or both so fined and imprisoned.
(3) Any county or state officer who knowingly violates any
of the provisions of KRS 66.280 to 66.390 shall, in addition to
the specific liabilities imposed for violating any of the provisions
of those sections, be guilty of a misdemeanor and, upon con
viction thereof, shall have his office declared vacant, and may
also be fined not more than five hundred dollars or imprisoned
for not more than ninety days, or both so fined and imprisoned.
Section 7. Section 68.020 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
(1) The county treasurer shall receive and receipt for all
money due the county from its collecting officer or from any
other person whose duty it is to pay money into the county
treasury, and shall hold such money subject to the order of the
fiscal court. He shall not disburse any money received by him
for any purpose other than that for which it was collected and
paid over to him, and when he pays out money he shall take a
receipt therefor.

Chapter 25 107
(2) He may, and when directed by the fiscal court shall,
institute actions in the name of the county against all delinquent
sheriffs or collectors of the county, and against any one having
money belonging to the county who fails or refuses to pay it
over on demand when due. He shall keep a record of all actions
he is directed to institute on behalf of the county, showing their
condition and the money collected thereunder.
(3) He shall keep an accurate detailed account of all
money received and disbursed by him for the county, and shall
keep books of accounts of the financial transactions of the county
in the manner required by any uniform system of accounting
prescribed by the Department of Finance [Revenue].
(4) He shall keep his office at the county seat, in a room
or rooms provided by the fiscal court. He shall keep his office
open for the collection of money at all reasonable times, except
on Sundays and legal holidays.
( 5 ) The county treasurer shall, when required by the fiscal
court, settle his accounts as county treasurer, and within thirty
days after the close of each fiscal year, he shall make a full and
complete settlement for the preceding fiscal year with the fiscal
court or with a person or persons whom the fiscal court, by order
of record, appoints to make settlement with him. In case of a
vacancy, the county judge shall call a special term which shall
proceed in the manner it deems proper to settle the accounts of
the county treasurer.
Section 8. Section 68.220 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
The fiscal affairs of each county, except those pertaining to
education, shall be administered by the fiscal court under a
uniform budget system. The county budget shall provide for
all the funds to be expended by the county from current revenue
for each fiscal year. The Department of Finance [Revenue] shall
classify the counties for budget purposes upon the basis of their
populations and expenditures, or upon some other proper basis,
and may classify them in the same manner for the purpose of
prescribing accounts.

108 Acts of the General Assembly
Section 9. Section 68.340 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
( 1 ) The clerk of the fiscal court shall keep an appropriation
ledger, containing separate accounts for each budget fund. Each
account shall show in detail the amount of the original appro
priation, all transfers to or from the budget fund, all expenditures
duly approved by the fiscal court and properly chargeable to the
fund, and the unencumbered balance remaining in the fund.
(2) The county treasurer shall keep a budget ledger, in
which he shall, immediately after the adoption of the county
budget by the fiscal court, credit each budget fund provided for
in the budget, and he shall account for each budget fund
separately.
(3) The appropriation ledger, the books of the county
treasurer and all fiscal court orders relating thereto or relating
to claims of any nature shall be open for inspection at all reason
able times to any taxpayer, and the county treasurer's books shall
at all times be open to inspection by the members of the fiscal
court.
(4) All books, blanks and forms required to be used by
county officials in the administration of the fiscal affairs of the
county under the budget laws shall be provided and paid for by
the county and approved by the Department of Finance
[Revenue].
Section 10. Section 68.350 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
The Department of Finance [Revenue] shall examine the
budget records and make such investigations as may be neces
sary to determine the condition of the financial affairs of each
county. If the department finds that any county officer or mem
ber of a county budget commission is willfully violating or neg
lecting the duties imposed by KRS 68.210 to 68.370, it may
institute legal proceedings in the Franklin circuit court to compel
compliance with the law.
Approved February 27, 1962

Chapter 26 109
CHAPTER 26
(H. B. 78)
AN ACT relating to soil conservation districts.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 262.240 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is amended
to read as follows:
( 1 ) A supervisor's term begins on January 1 following his
election. The three supervisors elected with the highest number
of votes in the general election of 1960 shall serve for four years;
the other two supervisors elected in 1960 shall serve for two years.
In the event only five nominating petitions for supervisors are
filed, the commission shall declare the nominees elected without
an election, and shall name three of the nominees to serve terms
of four years, and two to serve terms of two years. In 1962 two
supervisors shall be elected for four years and thereafter super
visors shall be elected for four years as their terms expire.
Nominating petitions for supervisors shall be filed with the com
mission not less than forty-five days prior to the day of the
election. No such nominating petition shall be accepted by the
commission unless it is signed by twenty-five or more owners of
land lying within the boundaries of the district. Landowners
may sign more than one nominating petition to nominate more
than one candidate for supervisor. In the event nominating peti
tions for only the number of supervisors to be elected are filed,
the commission shall declare the nominees elected without hold
ing an election. The election shall be conducted by the commis
sion as provided for under KRS 262.220.
(2) A supervisor shall hold office until his successor has
been elected and has qualified. Vacancies shall be filled for the
unexpired term by appointment by the commission.
(3) A supervisor may be reimbursed for expenses neces
sarily incurred in the discharge of his duties and may be paid a
per diem for attending meetings or otherwise discharging the
obligations of his office.
(4) A supervisor shall be a resident of the county, or dis
trict in which he serves as a supervisor [for which he is elected] ,

110 Acts of the General Assembly
and upon moving from the county, or district [in which the dis
trict is located] , the supervisor shall be ineligible to serve as a
supervisor and his office shall be vacant.
(5) Any supervisor may be removed by the commission
upon notice and hearing for neglect of duty or malfeasance in
office. A supervisor shall not be removed except pursuant to
this subsection.
Approved February 27, 1962
CHAPTER 27
(H. B. 79)
AN ACT relating to watershed conservancy districts.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 262.740 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
( 1 ) Within thirty days after a watershed conservancy dis
trict is created, nominating petitions may be filed with the board
of supervisors [shall cause an election to be held therein,] for
the election of a board of directors consisting of five members,
who shall hold office for a term of four years. Such board of
directors shall, under the supervision of the board of super
visors, be the governing body of the watershed conservancy dis
trict. The board of supervisors shall give due notice of the elec
tion of directors and shall, as nearly as practicable, conduct the
election in the manner prescribed by K.RS 262.220.
(2) If the territory embraced within a watershed con
servancy district lies within more than one soil conservation
district, each of said additional districts with minority of the
land involved in the watershed shall be entitled to elect three
additional directors.
(3) The board of directors shall annually elect from its
membership a chairman, secretary, and treasurer. The treasurer
shall execute an official bond for the faithful performance of

Chapteb 28 111
the duties of his office to be approved by the board of directors.
Such bond shall be executed with at least three solvent personal
sureties whose solvency must exceed the amount of the bond,
or by a surety company authorized to do business in this state,
and shall be in an amount determined by the board of directors.
If the treasurer is required to execute a surety company bond,
the premium on the bond shall be paid by the board of directors.
(4) Each person desiring to be a director of a watershed
conservancy district shall file a nominating petition with the
board of supervisors, signed by twenty-five or more landowners
within the watershed conservancy district of the county involved,
or, if less than fifty landowners are involved a majority of such
landowners. Nominating petitions for the election of any di
rector following the first election of members of the board after
creation of the district shall be filed with the board of super
visors not less than forty-five days prior to the day of the regular
election. If the candidates nominated do not exceed the positions
available they shall be declared elected. No person shall be
eligible to be a director of a watershed conservancy district who
is not a landowner in the district in which he seeks election.
Approved February 27, 1962
CHAPTER 28
(H. B. 81)
AN ACT relating to revenue and taxation.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 133.070 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
Property in territory annexed by another civil division shall
not be subject to ad valorem taxation in the annexing civil division
until the assessment date next following the date of annexation.

112 Acts of the General Assembly
[ ( 1 ) All assessments of property made in territory that is
later annexed to another civil division shall be certified by the
county tax commissioner making the assessment to the annexing
civil division, and submitted by it to its supervisors to be equal
ized. When equalized, the regular annual tax of the annexing
division shall be levied by the proper authority, placed upon its
tax bills and collected as its other taxes. The taxes, if allowed
to become delinquent, shall be subject to the same interest and
penalty charged and enforced in the manner provided by the law
governing the annexing division.]
[(2) If the supervisors are about to adjourn or have ad
journed when the assessment is certified, their session may be
extended for a reasonable time or thev shall be reconvened to
pass upon such certified assessment. Should the assessment and
equalization be completed after the regular levy of taxes for that
year has been made, a levy may thereafter be made, as soon as
practicable, for that year's tax.]
Approved February 27, 1962
CHAPTER 29
(H. B. 82)
AN ACT relating to revenue and taxation.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 132.220 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
(1) Deposits belonging to a resident of Kentucky in any
national bank not of this state or in any bank, trust company,
or combined bank and trust company not organized under the
laws of this state and unmanufactured tobacco insofar as it is
subject to taxation by KRS 132.190 and 132.200 shall be listed,
assessed and valued as of September 1 of each year. [Motor
vehicles shall not be assessed as of January 1, 1961, but shall
be listed, assessed, and valued as of March 1, 1961, and as of

Chapter 29 113
March 1 of each year thereafter.] Money in hand shall be listed,
assessed, and valued as of September 1 of each year. Shares of
stock, notes, bonds, accounts, and other credits, whether se
cured by mortgage, pledge, or otherwise, or unsecured, and all
interest in such property, unless otherwise provided by law
shall be listed, assessed, and valued as of the beginning of busi
ness on January 1 of each year. All other taxable property and
all interest in other taxable property, unless otherwise specific
ally provided by law, shall be listed, assessed, and valued as of
January 1, 1962, and January 1 of each year thereafter. [January
1 of each year.] It shall be the duty of all persons owning or
having any interest in any property taxable in this state to list
or have listed such property with the county tax commissioner
of the county where it is located, between January 1 and March 1
in each year, except as otherwise provided by law. All persons
in whose name property is properly assessed shall remain bound
for the tax, notwithstanding they may have sold or parted with it.
(2) Any taxpayer who has a reasonable excuse for not
listing his property before the county tax commissioner or his
deputy may appear before any person authorized to administer
an oath and make the necessary affidavit required by the sched
ule as to the value of his property and may then return the
schedule to the county tax commissioner. This shall be a com
pliance with the law in his particular case.
(3) Real property or any interest therein shall be listed
in the taxing district where it is located, by the owner of the
first freehold estate therein, and it shall be assessed without ref
erence to conflicting title. If the owner fails to list the property,
the county tax commissioner shall nevertheless assess it. The
county tax commissioner may swear witnesses in order to ascer
tain the person in whose name to make the list.
(4) Real property shall be assessed in the name of the
owner, if ascertainable by the tax commissioner, otherwise in the
name of the occupant, if ascertainable, and otherwise to "Un
known owner." The undivided real estate of any deceased person
may be assessed to the heirs or devisees of such person without
designating them by name.
(5) Beginning in the year 1951, all real property exempt
from taxation by Section 170 of the Constitution shall be listed

114 Acts of the General Assembly
with the county tax commissioner in the same manner and at
the same time as taxable real property. The county tax commis
sioner shall maintain an inventory record of such tax exempt
property, but such property shall not be placed on the tax rolls.
Section 2. Section 132.285 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
(1) Any city may by ordinance elect to use the annual
county assessment for property situated within such city as a
basis of ad valorem tax levies ordered or approved by the legis
lative body of the city. Any city making such election shall
notify the Department of Revenue and county tax commissioner
prior to the next succeeding assessment to be used for city levies.
In such event the assessment as finally determined for county
tax purposes shall serve as a basis of all city levies for the
fiscal year commencing after the county assessment date. Each
city which elects to use the county assessment shall annually
appropriate and pay each fiscal year to the office of the county
tax commissioner for deputy and other authorized personnel
allowance, supplies, maps and equipment, and other authorized
expenses of the office, one and one-half cent for each one
hundred dollars of the assessment applicable to the city tax
levies, or thirty thousand dollars, whichever, is smaller. This
allowance shall be based on the assessment as of the previous
January 1. Each county tax commissioner shall at the end of
each month file a claim with the city for the salaries of his
deputies, other personnel, and other expenses of his office in
curred during the month which are reimbursable from city funds;
and the city shall order payment in an amount not to exceed
the appropriation authorized by this section. Notwithstanding
any statutory provisions to the contrary, the assessment dates for
such city shall conform to the corresponding dates for the county,
and such city may by ordinance establish additional financial
and tax procedures that will enable it effectively to adopt the
county assessment. The legislative body of any city adopting
the county assessment may fix the time for levying the city
tax rate, the fiscal year, due and delinquency dates for taxes
and any other dates that will enable it effectively to adopt the
county assessment, notwithstanding any statutory provisions to
the contrary. Any such city may, by ordinance, abolish any office

Chapter 29 115
connected with city assessment and equalization; except that
in the case of a city assessor who is elected by the qualified
voters of the city, the office may not be abolished before the
end of the term of such assessor. Any city which elects to use
the county assessment shall have access to the assessment records
as soon as completed and may obtain a copy of that portion
of the records which represents the assessment of property with
in such city. Once any city elects to use the county assessment,
such action cannot be revoked without notice to the Department
of Revenue and the county tax commissioner six months prior
to the next date as of which property is assessed for state and
county taxes.
(2) In the event any omitted property is assessed by the
county tax commissioner as provided by KRS 132.310 such assess
ment shall be considered as part of the assessment adopted by
the city according to subsection (1).
[(3) A city may adopt due and delinquency dates for real
property, personal property, and motor vehicles or any combi
nation thereof identical to the respective dates for state and
county taxes.]
Section 3. Section 132.485 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
( 1 ) The county clerk shall furnish weekly to the county
tax commisioner a copy of the registration certificate for each
motor vehicle and housetrailer registered. The address including
the county in which the owner of the motor vehicle or house-
trailer resides shall be shown on the copy of the registration
certificate furnished. The county tax commissioner shall forward
to the appropriate county tax commissioner all registration cer
tificates showing a county of residence other than his own.
All copies of transfers of registered motor vehicles and house-
trailers dated on or before May 1 in the current year shall be
forwarded by the county clerk to the county tax commissioner.
The county tax commissioner is authorized to list the motor
vehicle or housetrailer listed on the registration certificate on
the property tax rolls without notifiying the taxpayer.
(2) The registration of a motor vehicle or housetrailer

116 Acts of the General Assembly
with the county clerk in order to operate it or permit it to be
operated upon the highways shall be deemed consent by the
registrant thereof for the motor vehicle or housetrailer to be
assessed by the county tax commissioner at a valuation deter
mined from a standard manual prescribed by the Department of
Revenue for valuing motor vehicles or housetrailers for assess
ment unless the registrant appears in person before the tax com
missioner to assess the vehicle.
(3) The registration of a motor vehicle on or before the
date that the registration of such vehicle is required is prima
facie evidence of ownership on January 1.
[(3) This section does not apply to motor vehicles and
housetrailers owned and operated by agencies of the state and
federal governments.]
( 4 ) This section does not apply to motor vehicles and house-
trailers owned and operated by public service companies, com
mon carriers or agencies of the state and federal governments.
[(4) The registration of a motor vehicle on or before the
date that the registration of such vehicle is required is prima
facie evidence of ownership on March 1.]
Section 4. Section 133.220 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
( 1 ) The Department of Revenue annually shall furnish to
each county clerk tax bill forms designed for adequate account
ing control sufficient to cover the taxable property on the rolls.
These tax bill forms shall be furnished not later than ten days
after the Kentucky Tax Commission has rendered its certifica
tion. The county annually shall furnish the county clerk with
sufficient tax bill forms for his use in the preparation of tax
bills for all taxpayers who are liable for a poll tax and who own
no property assessed for taxation.
(2) After receiving the forms the county clerk shall make
out for the use of the sheriff or collector a correct tax bill for
each taxpayer in the county whose property has been assessed
and whose valuation is included in the certification provided in
KRS 133.180. If the bills are bound, the cost of binding shall

Chapter 29 117
be paid out of the county levy. Each tax bill [other than a bill
for tax on a motor vehicle,] shall show the rate of tax upon
each one hundred dollars worth of property for state, county, and
school purposes; the name of the taxpayer and his mailing ad
dress; the number of acres of farm land and its value; the num
ber of lots and their value; the amount and value of notes and
money; the value of mixed personal property; the county poll;
the school poll; the school tax; and the total amount of taxes
due the state, county, school fund, and other levies. The county
clerk shall attest each bill by signing it officially. Provision shall
be made for the sheriff to have a stub, duplicate, or other proper
evidence of receipt of payment of each tax bill.
(3) Tax bills prepared in accordance with the certification
of the Kentucky Tax Commission shall be delivered to the sher
iff or collector by the county clerk before September 15 of
each year. The clerk shall take a receipt showing the number of
tax bills and the total amount of tax due each taxing district as
shown upon the tax bills. The receipt shall be signed and ac
knowledged by the sheriff or collector before the county clerk,
filed in the county court, and recorded in the order book of the
county court in the manner required by law for recording the
official bond of the sheriff.
(4) Upon delivery to him of the tax bills, the sheriff or
collector shall mail a notice to each taxpayer, showing the total
amount of taxes due the state, county, school fund, and other
levies, the date on which the taxes are due, and any discount
to which the taxpayer may be entitled upon payment of the taxes
prior to a designated date.
Section 5. Section 134.020 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
(1) All state, county, and district taxes, except as other
wise provided by law, shall be due and payable on September
15 following the assessment; except that all taxes in any year on
unmanufactured tobacco, money in hand, or money on deposit
outside this state, shall be due and payable on the second suc
ceeding September 15 following the assessment, unless otherwise
provided by law.

118 Acts of the General Assembly
(2) Any taxpayer who pays his state, county, or district
taxes by November 1 after they become due in any year shall
be entitled to two percent discount thereon, and the sheriff
shall allow the discount and give a receipt in full to the taxpayer.
The sheriff may, at any time after the taxes mentioned in this
section become due, receive less than the face amount of the
tax bill as a credit on the amount due, including the amount
of any penalties then due; and every such payment shall be
credited upon the tax bill or upon sheets annexed thereto for
that purpose, and acknowledged in writing or by a rubber stamp,
indicating the amount so paid to the sheriff.
(3) All state, county, and district taxes, except as other
wise provided by law, shall become delinquent on January 1
following their due date. [except that all state, county, and dis
trict taxes on motor vehicles shall become delinquent on De
cember 1 following their due date in the year 1961 and on De
cember 1 following their due date in each year thereafter.]
(4) Any taxes which are not paid by the date when they
become delinquent shall be subject to a penalty of six percent on
the taxes due and unpaid; except that taxes which became de
linquent on January 1 shall be subject to a penalty of only two
percent on the taxes due and unpaid, if paid on or before the
last day of January. The sheriff shall collect the penalty and
account for it as he is required to collect and account for taxes.
Section 6. Section 136.120 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
(1) Every railway company, sleeping car company, chair
car company, dining car company, gas company, water company,
ferry company, bridge company, street railway company, inter-
urban electric railroad company, express company, electric light
company, electric power company, telephone company, tele
graph company, bus line company, regular route common carrier
trucking company, air transport company, pipeline company,
common carrier water transportation company, and every other
like company or business performing any public service shall
annually pay a tax on its operating property to the state and to
the extent such property is liable to taxation shall pay a local

Chapter 29 119
tax thereon to the county, incorporated city, and taxing district
in which its operating property is located.
(2) The property of such taxpayers shall be classified as
operating property, nonoperating tangible property, and non-
operating intangible property. Nonoperating intangible property
within the taxing jurisdiction of the Commonwealth shall be
taxable for state purposes only at the same rate as the intangible
property of other taxpayers not performing public services, and
operating property and nonoperating tangible property shall be
subject to state and local taxes at the same rate as the tangible
property of other taxpayers not performing public services.
(3) The Department of Revenue shall have sole power
to value and assess all of the property of every corporation, com
pany, association, partnership, or person performing any public
service, including those enumerated above and all others to whom
this section may apply, whether or not such operating property,
nonoperating tangible property, or nonoperating intangible prop
erty has heretofore been assessed by said department, and shall,
except as provided in subsection (4) of this section, allocate
such assessment as provided by KRS 136.170, and shall certify
operating property liable to local taxation and nonoperating tan
gible property to the counties, cities, and taxing districts as pro
vided in KRS 136.180. All of the property assessed by the
department pursuant to this section shall be assessed as of Decem
ber 31 each year for the following year's taxes, and the hen
therefor shall attach as of such assessment date. Provided,
however, that in the case of a taxpayer whose business is pre
dominantly nonpublic service and the public service business in
which he is engaged is merely incidental to his principal business,
the department shall in the exercise of its judgment and discretion
determine, from evidence which it may have or obtain, what
portion of the operating property is devoted to the public service
business subject to assessment by the department under this
section and shall require the remainder of the property not so
engaged to be assessed by the local taxing authorities.
(4) (a) The Department of Revenue shall likewise have
sole power to value and assess all the property within the taxing
jurisdiction of this Commonwealth of every refrigerator car com
pany, tank car company, coal car company, every like car com

120 Acts of the General Assembly
pany, and every irregular route common carrier truck company
and may, in determining the proportion of the value of such
property within the taxing jurisdiction of Kentucky, take into
consideration the car or truck mileage made by the cars or trucks
of such companies. The department shall not certify the assess
ment of the rolling stock of such companies to any county, city,
or taxing district, but shall apportion and certify the operating
property, other than rolling stock, to the counties, cities, and
taxing districts where such property is located. The rolling stock
of such companies shall be taxable for state purposes only at the
rate of two dollars and fifty cents upon each one hundred dollars
of value, and shall not be subject to taxation by a county, city,
or other taxing district.
(b) The property of taxpayers within the meaning of
paragraph (a) of this subsection shall likewise be classified as
operating property, nonoperating tangible property, and non-
operating intangible property. The nonoperating intangible
property of the companies within the meaning of this subsection
which are within the taxing jurisdiction of the Commonwealth
shall be taxable for state purposes at the same rate as the intan
gible property of nonpublic service taxpayers.
[(5) (a) Notwithstanding the provisions of subsections
(3) and (4) of this section, every corporation, company, asso
ciation, partnership, or person performing any public service who
is required to be assessed under KRS 136.115 to 136.200 shall
annually list for assessment with the county tax commissioner and
other local assessors in the county in which the vehicle is regis
tered in the same manner as all other persons all of its motor
vehicles registered in Kentucky. Any motor vehicle required to
be registered by the Department of Motor Transportation shall
be listed for assessment with the county tax commissioner and
other local assessors in the county in which it is principally
operated.
(b) Every corporation, company, association, partnership,
or person performing any public service, required by subsection
(5) (a) to list its motor vehicles with the county tax commis
sioner and other local assessors, shall pay the tax to the county
sheriff and other local collectors in the same manner required
for the payment of other ad valorem taxes.]

Chapter 29 121
Section 7. Section 136.160 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
(1) The Department of Revenue shall determine the fair
cash value of the operating property of a domestic public service
corporation as a unit. The fair cash value of the operating
property shall be equalized.
(2) The Department of Revenue shall determine the fair
cash value of the operating property of a foreign public service
corporation or a domestic public service corporation with property
or routes in Kentucky and outside Kentucky as a unit according
to subsection ( 1 ) . The fair cash value of the operating property
everywhere valued as a unit shall be apportioned to Kentucky
based on the average of the property factor and the business
factor. The fair cash value of the operating property in Kentucky
shall be equalized.
(a) The property factor shall fairly reflect the amount of
operating property operated, owned, or leased in Kentucky
compared to the total amount of operating property operated,
owned, or leased everywhere. An allocable portion of the rolling
stock, aircraft, and watercraft of a common carrier shall be
included in the operating property, operated, owned, or leased
in Kentucky. This factor may be a single factor or an average
of several factors.
(b) The business factor shall fairly reflect the utilization
of the operating property operated, owned, or leased in Kentucky
compared to the utilization of the operating property operated,
owned, or leased everywhere. This factor may be a single factor
or an average of several factors.
[(3) The Department of Revenue shall deduct the motor
vehicle assessment made with the county tax commissioner after
the valuation of the operating property in Kentucky is made and
equalized according to subsection (1) or (2) of this section.]
( 3 ) The nonoperating tangible and nonoperating intangible
property of public service corporations whose operating property
is valued according to either subsection ( 1 ) or (2) shall be valued
by the Department of Revenue in the same manner and according
to the same standards as if this property were valued by the

122 Acts of the General Assembly
county tax commissioner in the county where the property has a
taxable situs.
Section 8. Section 132.710, 134.415, 186.035 and 281.634
of the Kentucky Revised Statutes are repealed.
Section 9. Any and all amounts derived from the levy and
collection of the taxes levied by this Act shall be credited to the
general fund.
Section 10. WHEREAS, the 1960 General Assembly en
acted a comprehensive law covering the assessment, collection,
and delinquency dates for motor vehicles subject to ad valorem
property taxation; and, WHEREAS, such law was declared un
constitutional by the courts; and, WHEREAS, it is necessary to
eliminate confusion and doubt as to the assessment, collection,
and delinquency dates on motor vehicles, an emergency is de
clared to exist, and this Act shall become effective upon its
passage and approval by the Governor.
Approved February 27, 1962
CHAPTER 30
(H. B. 85)
AN ACT relating to tobacco warehouses.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 248.430 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is amended
to read as follows:
(1) Any person who receives or undertakes to receive or
take care of leaf tobacco for sale at public auction, whether with
or without compensation, shall post or cause to be posted, a
notice, in a conspicuous place upon his premises, stating the
number of pounds in the aggregate, actually sold, and the average
price per pound received on account of each day's sale. He shall

Chapter 31 123
post the notice not later than 9 a.m. on the day following the sale.
He shall also, not less than thirty minutes prior to such a sale,
post at a point in the office of his warehouse, convenient and
accessible for public inspection, a typewritten or printed list
showing the true name and post-office address of the owner and
producer and the number of pounds of tobacco of each person
whose tobacco will that day be offered for sale in the warehouse.
No tobacco shall be delivered to or received by any warehouse
for sale at public auction, unless the true names and post-office
addresses of the producer and owner of the tobacco are furnished
the warehouse by the person delivering the tobacco. He shall
also post on the outside of the scale house and in the farmer's wait
ing room, if such places are in the warehouse, a schedule setting
forth the rates of all commissions, fees, discounts, and deductions.
(2) No person shall, in the notice required by subsection
( 1 ) , falsify the actual number of pounds sold or the average price
of the tobacco sold, or falsely list the name, post-office address
or number of pounds of tobacco of any producer or owner whose
tobacco will that day be offered for sale, or furnish a false name
or address of the owner or producer to any warehouse.
Approved February 27, 1962
CHAPTER 31
(H. B. 102)
AN ACT relating to the County Employes Retirement System.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 78.510 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
As used in KRS 78.510 to 78.880 unless the context other
wise requires:
(1) "System" means the County Employes' Retirement
System;

124 Acts of the General Assembly
(2) "Board" means the Board of Trustees of the system;
(3) "County" means any county participating in the sys
tem in accordance with appropriate executive order, as provided
in KRS 78.530, or any joint city-county agency acting as an in
dependent entity or other independent municipal corporation
except cities, by order appropriate to its governmental structure
and provided the board is willing to accept such agency or cor
poration;
(4) "Examiner" means the medical examiner or examiners
as provided in KRS 78.510 to 78.880;
(5) "Employe" means every regular full-time appointed or
elective officer or employe of a participating county. The term
shall not include persons engaged as independent contractors,
and seasonal, emergency, temporary and part-time workers [per
son regularly employed in rendering service to and for a county
and whose compensation is payable in whole or in part by such
county, and shall include all full time regular employes of the
county]. In case of any doubt, the board shall determine if a
person is an employe within the meaning of KRS 78.510 to
78.880 [. The judge of the county, county court clerk, county
attorney, sheriff, jailer, coroner, surveyor, county commissioners,
justice of the peace and constable, and all compensated em
ployes assisting these officials in the performance of their official
duties shall be considered county employes within the meaning
of KRS 78.510 to 78.880] ;
(6) "Employer" means a county, the electorate of a county,
or any authority of such county having the power to appoint
or elect an employe to office or employment in such county;
(7) "Member" means any employe who is included in the
membership of the system;
(8) "Service" means the total of current service and prior
service as defined herein;
(9) "Current service" means the number of years and com
pleted months, expressed as a fraction of a year, of employment
as an employe, on and after July 1, 1958, for which creditable
compensation is paid, [by the employer during which employ
ment the employe is a member of the system, except as other

Chapter 31 125
wise provided in KRS 78.730;] An employe shall be credited with
one month of current service only in those months he receives
compensation for at least one hundred hours of work;
(10) "Prior service" means the number of years and com
pleted months, expressed as a fraction of a year, of employment
as an employe, prior to July 1, 1958, for which creditable com
pensation was paid [by the employer]. An employe shall be
credited with one month of prior service only in those months
he received compensation for at least one hundred hours of work;
(11) "Beneficiary" means the estate of a member or a
[any] person entitled to receive benefits under KRS 78.510 to
78.880;
(12) "Accumulated contributions" at any time means the
sum of all amounts deducted from the compensation of a member
and credited to his individual account in the members' contribu
tion account, together with interest credited on such amounts,
and any other amounts the member shall have contributed there
to, including interest credited thereon;
(13) "Creditable compensation" means all salary, wages
and fees paid to the employe as a result of services performed for
the employer [the compensation to an employe working the full
working time for his position]. In cases where compensation
includes maintenance and other perquisites, the board shall fix
the value of that part of the compensation not paid in money;
( 14 ) "Final compensation" of a member means the credit
able compensation of [to] the member during the five fiscal
[calendar] years he was paid at the highest average monthly rate
of any of the last ten years of creditable compensation preceding
the member's retirement date divided by the number of months
of service during that five year period multiplied by twelve. Said
five years may be fractional and need not be consecutive [, and
include only years during which creditable compensation is
received by the member. In no case will final compensation be
less than that determined on the assumption that the member
retired on his normal retirement date. All benefits shall be paid
in accordance herewith];
(15) "Retirement allowance" means the retirement pay
ments to which a member is entitled;

126 Acts of the General Assembly
(16) "Actuarial equivalent" means a benefit of equal value
when computed upon the basis of such actuarial tables as are
from time to time adopted by the board;
(17) "Normal retirement date" means the sixty-fifth birth
day of a member [or the date on which the member completes
ten years of service, whichever occurs later, except that in no case
will a member's normal retirement date be later than his seventy-
fifth birthday. The normal retirement date for any member who
has passed his seventy-fifth birthday on the date he becomes a
member shall be the date he becomes a member, except as other
wise provided in KRS 78.670. Notwithstanding anything to the
contrary contained in this subsection, the normal retirement date
of a member shall not be earlier than July 1, 1960] ;
(18) "Fiscal year" of the system means the twelve months
from July 1 through the following June 30.
Section 2. Section 78.530 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
(1) Each county will participate in the system by appropri
ate order authorizing such participation which has been entered
and duly recorded in the records of the county fiscal court. The
authority to issue and properly record such order of participation
being hereby granted, permits such county to participate in the
system. The effective date of such participation shall be July 1
of any year following June 19, 1958.
(2) Once a county participates it shall thereafter continue
to participate.
(3) In counties having a population of seventy-five thou
sand or more the county fiscal court, upon request of either the
clerk of the county court or the sheriff of the county, shall enter
an appropriate order authorizing the participation in the system,
said order to be duly recorded in the records of the fiscal court.
Provided, however, anything in KRS 78.510 to 78.880 to the con
trary notwithstanding, the fiscal court shall not be liable for
"normal contribution" or "past [prior] service liability contribu
tion" with reference to the county court clerk and his employes
and deputies and the sheriff and his employes and deputies, and
further that the "normal contribution" and "past [prior] service

Chapter 31
liability contribution" shall be considered office expenditures and
shall be paid by the county court clerk and by the sheriff respec
tively.
Section 3. A new section of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is created to read as follows:
( 1 ) Any member of the system on July 1, 1962, and whose
employment has previously been terminated without prejudice
and who has been refunded his accumulated contributions, there
by losing service credit, may regain such credit by paying to the
system the amount refunded with interest at a rate determined by
the board provided such payment is made prior to January 1, 1963.
(2) Any person who becomes an employe of a participating
county after July 1, 1962, and whose employment has been ter
minated without prejudice prior to July 1, 1962, and who has
been refunded his accumulated contributions, thereby losing
service credit, may regain such credit by paying to the system
the amount refunded with interest at a rate determined by the
board provided such payment is made within six months of re
employment.
(3) Effective July 1, 1962, and thereafter any employe
of a participating county who did not elect membership as pro
vided in KRS 78.540(2) may obtain credit for prior service and
for current service by paying to the system the contribution he
would have paid had he elected membership with interest at the
rate determined by the board and an additional sum equal to
fifty percent of this amount. The payment of the fifty percent
sum shall be considered as accumulated contributions of the in
dividual member.
(4) Service credit obtained under this section shall be dis
allowed if a member does not obtain twelve months additional
current service after the date such rights under this section have
been exercised.

CHAPTER 33
(H. B. 104)
AN ACT relating to elections.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 118.450 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
(1) The printing and delivery of ballots and cards of in
struction to voters shall, in city elections, be paid for by the city.
In all other elections, the printing of the ballots and cards of
instruction for the voters in each county, and the delivery of
them to the several voting precincts, shall be paid for by the
county.
(2) The cost of all elections held in any county shall be
allowed by the fiscal court and paid by the county treasurer
except as otherwise provided by law.

144 Acts of the General Assembly
(3) When the cost of any election has been allowed by
the fiscal court and paid by the county treasurer, as provided
in this section or in any other section requiring the county to
pay the cost of an election, the county treasurer shall certify
the amount of such expenses, indicating each item of expense
separately, together with a statement of the number of precincts
in the county, the date and kind of election to the Department
of Finance. Upon receipt of such certification and upon being
satisfied as to the correctness thereof, the Department of Finance
shall issue its warrant upon the state treasurer in favor of the
county treasurer for one half of the amount of such expenses
but not exceeding in any event an amount equal to twenty dol
lars for each precinct in the county. All sums paid under this
section shall be in addition to any sum paid by the state for
ballot paper in elections.
(4) Each county shall acquire voting machines meeting
the specifications of KRS 125.040 for each precinct in which
such machines are not already in use, no later than the primary
election in 1963; provided, however, that (a) counties contain
ing a population of 21,000 or more shall acquire voting machines
for each precinct therein prior to the primary election in 1961;
(b) counties containing a population of over 13,000 and less
than 21,000 shall acquire voting machines for each precinct
therein prior to the primary election in 1962; and (c) counties
containing a population of 13,000 or less shall acquire voting
machines for each precinct therein prior to the primary election
in 1963; provided, however, that nothing contained herein shall
be construed to prevent a county from acquiring voting machines
prior to the general election in November, 1960.
(5) In all counties where voting machines are used, the
cost of elections shall be certified by the county treasurer to the
Department of Finance in the same manner as set out in sub
section (3) of this section, except that for each precinct where
voting machines are used and so certified, the sum of fifty dollars
shall be allowed to the county.
(6) Any county [which fails] failing to acquire voting
machines in some manner prior to the time herein required shall
[no longer be entitled to receive] thereafter be prohibited from
receiving any of the payments provided for in subsections (3)

and (5) of this section until such machines have been acquired.
(7) [The proposed budget of any county shall be disap
proved by the State Local Finance Officer if such budget fails
to provide for the payment of rentals in connection with any out
standing voting machine revenue bonds issued for the benefit
of the county by the State Property and Buildings Commission
as authorized by statute,] Payments to any county under the pro
visions of subsections (3) and (5) [subsection (6)] of this sec
tion shall be terminated if and whenever it shall fail to renew a

tion of voting machines by such commission for the use of such

Approved February 27, 1962
CHAPTER 34
(H. B. 124)
AN ACT relating to the amount of public assistance, proration in case of
deficiency and the consideration of resources.
Be it enacted by the General Assembltj of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 205.210 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is amended
to read as follows:
(1) The amount of public assistance to be granted shall
be determined with due regard to the needs and resources of
the individual and family as prescribed by regulation, provided,
that if available funds during a budgetary period are insufficient
to meet the full needs of the recipients in all or any of the cate
gories of public assistance, the funds available shall be reasonably
prorated to recipients in such categories according to pending
and anticipated applications during such budgetary period.

146 Acts of the General Assembly
(2) Notwithstanding any other provisions of law to the
contrary, resources in any given case shall be deemed to include
among other things:
(a) The income and any property belonging to any ap
plicant or recipient; except the exemptions of the kind and amount
of income prescribed by regulations within the scope of the Social
Security Act, its amendments and federal regulations [that the
first fifty dollars of earned income per month of an applicant
or recipient of assistance to needy blind shall be disregarded
in determining the needs and resources of such applicant or re
cipient] ;
(b) The income and any property of the spouse living
with any needy aged, [or] needy blind, or needy permanently
and totally disabled person [applicant] ;
(c) Such income and resources as may be available to
applicants or recipients from persons legally liable for their
support.
(3) The value of any property voluntarily transferred by
any applicant or recipient or the spouse living with any applicant
or recipient for the purpose of establishing eligibility for public
assistance shall be deemed a resource of such applicant or re
cipient under this section.
Approved February 27, 1962
CHAPTER 35
(H. B. 130)

Unless otherwise expressly provided by law, no bond, obli
gation or security shall be required of the Commonwealth or

monwealth in order to prosecute any remedy in the courts of
