Chapter 36 147
CHAPTER 36
(H. B. 134)
AN ACT providing for the suitability of a home for children in the Aid
to Dependent Children Program.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. A new section of Chapter 205 of the Kentucky
Revised Statutes is created to read as follows:
The basic objective of the aid to dependent children pro
gram is to maintain needy children in their own homes and to
assist parents to provide care for their children essential to
healthy growth and development. Home environments that are
contrary to the best interest of children are incompatible with
this objective. Therefore, the purpose of this Act is to establish
a plan for improving the living conditions of a child receiving
aid to dependent children; and, in order to carry out the pur
pose, the state and local governmental authorities shall combine
their efforts and resources in providing a home conducive to the
well being of such a child. This Act shall be known as the "Suit
able Home Law of 1962."
Section 2. A new section of Chapter 205 of the Kentucky
Revised Statutes is created to read as follows:
The department shall evaluate the suitability of the home
in which the dependent child lives at the time of the initial de
termination and thereafter when needed. In evaluating the home,
consideration should be given but not limited to the following:
(1) Evaluating the suitability of a home in terms of the
past and present moral environment, physical care, and super
vision provided in the home, the social, educational, and religious
opportunities provided for the child, and the use of income or
public assistance grant toward the needs of the child;
(2) Making a decision relative to whether the desirable
factors outweigh the undesirable factors, and the apparent ca
pacity and desire of the person with whom the needy child lives
to improve the home.

148 Acts of the General Assembly
Section 3. A new section of Chapter 205 of the Kentucky
Revised Statutes is created to read as follows:
If the home is unsuitable according to the evaluation made
under Section 2 of this Act but there is reason to believe that
elimination of the undesirable conditions can be effected, the
department will take the following action:
(1) Give intensive case work service to improve the un
desirable conditions in the home.
(2) When intensive efforts over a reasonable period have
failed to improve the home conditions, the department shall de
termine if any other relative or person specified by the Social
Security Act is maintaining a suitable home and is willing to
take custody and care for the child in their home. Upon an af
firmative finding, the department shall, if the parents or rela
tive with whom the child is living consent, assist the parent or
relative in effecting the placement.
(3) If there are no suitable relatives or other specified
persons available for the care of the child in their home, or if
the relative with whom the child lives refuses to agree to either
improving the home condition or to permit the voluntary re
moval of the child from the home, the department shall file a
petition for court action with the appropriate county court. The
department shall give in writing the reason for the petition and
request a determination from the court within a thirty day period.
Section 4. A new section of Chapter 205 of the Kentucky
Revised Statutes is created to read as follows:
When a petition has been filed in the court with evidence
of an unsuitable home, the judge shall hold a hearing and shall
by order:
(1) Request additional evidence from the department or
any other agency interested in the welfare of children and on the
basis of such evidence remove the child from the home or dis
miss the petition; or
(2) Commit the child to the Department of Child Welfare
as provided in KRS 200.110; or
(3) Appoint a Special Committee, nothwithstanding KRS

Chapter 37 149
387.281, to supervise the expenditure of public assistance funds
if this is a particular family problem in the unsuitable home sit
uation.
Section 5. A new section of Chapter 205 of the Kentucky
Revised Statutes is created to read as follows:
Assistance within the eligibility requirements of the aid to
dependent children program shall not be denied to a needy child
on the basis that the home conditions are unsuitable, while the
child continues to reside in the home. Assistance will be con
tinued during such time as efforts are being made either to im
prove the home conditions or to make arrangements for the child
elsewhere.
Approved February 27, 1962
CHAPTER 37
(H. B. 135)
AN ACT relating to the recovery and reporting of Public Assistance
monies paid in error or obtained through fraud.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. A new section of Chapter 205 of the Kentucky
Revised Statutes is created to read as follows:
If the commissioner finds that public assistance payments
have been made in error to any person, or if any person knowingly
makes a false statement or misrepresentation of a material fact
or knowingly fails to disclose a material fact to the Division of
Public Assistance for the purpose of obtaining or increasing any
initial or continuing public assistance payments, the commissioner
may take the following actions:
(1) Withhold future public assistance payments until all
overpayments are repaid or until assistance is reinstated through
the appeal process set forth in KRS 205.230.
( 2 ) Institute civil action in the circuit court to recover pay
ments made in error or made as the result of fraud.
(3) Report all facts in the case to the county attorney for
prosecution under KRS 205.990 if fraud is indicated.
Approved February 27, 1962

150 Acts of the General Assembly
CHAPTER 38
(H. B. 136)
AN ACT relating to the eligibility for public assistance.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 205.200 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is amended
to read as follows:
(1) A needy aged person, a needy blind person, a needy
child, a needy permanently and totally disabled person, or a
person [relative] with whom a needy child lives shall be eligible
to receive a public assistance grant only if he has made a proper
application or an application has been made on his behalf in the
manner and form prescribed by regulation. No individual shall
be eligible to receive public assistance under more than one cate
gory of public assistance for the same period of time.
(2) The commissioner shall, by regulations, prescribe the
conditions of eligibility for public assistance in conformity with
the Social Security Act, its amendments and federal regulations
in regard to an individual who is:
(a) an inmate of a public institution;
(b) a patient in an institution for tuberculosis or mental
diseases;
(c) a patient who has been diagnosed as having tubercu
losis or psychosis and is a patient in a medical institution.
(3) The commissioner may, by regulation, prescribe as a
condition of eligibility that a needy child regularly attend school,
and may further, by regulation, prescribe the degree of relation
ship of the person or persons in whose home such needy child
must reside.
(4) No child born out of wedlock [after June 15, 1950]
shall be eligible under KRS 205.160 to 205.250 and KRS 205.260
to 205.320 as a needy child until after a bastardy proceeding in
volving the child has been initiated. The requirement of this sub
section may be waived as prescribed by regulation if a bastardy
proceeding cannot be had or would be futile, or would not be to
the best interest of such child, or its mother or contrary to the
public interest.

Chapter 39 151
(5) Public assistance shall not be payable to or in behalf
of any individual who has taken any legal action in his own behalf
or in the behalf of others with the intent and purpose of establish
ing eligibility for such assistance.
(6) The department shall promptly notify the appropriate
law enforcement officials of the furnishing of aid to dependent
children in respect to a child who has been deserted or abandoned
by a parent.
(7) A needy aged person, a needy blind person, a needy
child or a needy permanently and totally disabled person shall not
be eligible to receive a public assistance grant unless he is a
resident of this state at the time of receiving aid, and has been
a resident of this state for such period of time prior thereto as
is prescribed by regulation.
Approved February 27, 1962
CHAPTER 39
(H. B. 137)
AN ACT relating to the administration of Public Assistance.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 205.010 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is amended
to read as follows:
As used in KRS 205.160 to 205.320, unless the context re
quires otherwise:
(1) "Department" means the Department of Economic
Security;
(2) "Commissioner" means the Commissioner of Economic
Security or his authorized representatives;
(3) "Division" means the Division of Public Assistance in
the Department of Economic Security;
(4) "Public Assistance" means money grants, assistance in
kind or services to or for the benefit of needy aged, needy blind,

152 Acts of the General Assembly
needy permanently and totally disabled persons, needy children
or persons [relative] with whom a needy child lives or a family
containing a combination of these categories;
(5) "Needy child" means a child under the age of eighteen
who has been deprived of parental support by reasons prescribed
by regulations within the scope of the Social Security Act, its
amendments and federal regulations, [reason of the death, ab
sence or incapacity of a parent, and who is unable to provide for
himself] and who does not otherwise have provided for him a
subsistence compatible with decency and health;
(6) "Parent," in addition to natural or adopted parents, shall
include step-parent and persons unrelated by marriage but main
taining an established family relationship;
(7) "Needy aged" means a person who has attained the age
of sixty-five and who is unable to provide for himself and who
does not have otherwise provided for him a subsistence compatible
with decency and health;
(8) "Needy blind" means a person who has no vision or
whose vision is so defective as to prevent the performance of
ordinary activities for which eyesight is essential and who is un
able to provide for himself and who does not have otherwise
provided for him a subsistence compatible with decency and
health;
(9) "Person [Relative] with whom a needy child lives"
means the individual, prescribed by regulation, with whom such
child is living in a place of residence maintained by such indi
vidual by himself or together with one or more other persons;
(10) "Needy permanently and totally disabled" means a
person eighteen years of age or older and who has a permanent
physical or mental impairment, disease or loss that substantially
precludes him from engaging in useful occupations within his
competence and who is unable to provide for himself and who
does not have otherwise provided for him a subsistence compati
ble with decency and health;
(11) "Private institution" means any establishment or place
other than a public institution operated or maintained by any
individual, association, corporation or other organization which

Chapter 40 153
provides a group living arrangement for four or more individuals,
who are cared for and maintained in residence for compensation
or otherwise;
(12) "Public institution" means any establishment or place
which is the responsibility of and administered by the state or
any political subdivision thereof providing a group living arrange
ment in which one or more individuals are cared for and main
tained in residence;
(13) "Public medical institution" means any public insti
tution the primary purpose of which is to furnish hospital care
and medical treatment.
Approved February 27, 1962
CHAPTER 40
(H. B. 141)
AN ACT relating to city-county planning commissions.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 100.330 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is amended
to read as follows:
(1) Each commission shall consist of five "city members"
and five "county members."
(2) The city members shall be chosen in the following man
ner: The city manager and the city engineer shall be ex officio
members of the commission. If there is no city manager, the
mayor shall be an ex officio member. If there is no city engineer,
the mayor shall appoint some other city administrative official
to serve as an ex officio member. The mayor shall appoint the
remaining three city members who shall be residents of the city
and shall hold no other city or county office. All appointments
by the mayor shall be subject to approval by the city legislative
body.
(3) The county members shall be chosen in the following
manner: the county judge shall designate one member of the

154 Acts of the General Assembly
county legislative body and the county engineer to serve as ex
officio members of the commission. If there is no county engineer,
the county judge shall appoint some other county administrative
official to serve as an ex officio member. The remaining three
county members shall be chosen by the county judge and shall be
residents of the county outside the limits of the city and shall hold
[holding] no other city or county office. All appointments by
the county judge shall be subject to approval by the fiscal court.
(4) The citizen members initially appointed by the mayor
shall hold office according to designation in their appointment,
one of them for two years, and the others for terms of four years
each, and upon the expiration of their respective terms, the suc
cessor of each shall hold his office for a term of four vears.
Vacancies in the offices of members appointed by the mayor
shall be filled by successors appointed by the mayor for the un
expired term.
(5) The county citizen members initially chosen by the
county judge shall hold office one of them for two years, the
others for terms of four years each. Upon expiration of their
respective terms, the successor of each shall hold his office for
a term of four years. Vacancies in the office of the appointive
county members shall be filled by a successor appointed by the
county judge with the approval of the fiscal court for the remain
der of the term of office in which the vacancy has occurred.
(6) Where any ex officio member in the employment of the
city or county vacates his official position he shall ipso facto vacate
his office as a member of the commission. The terms of the ex
officio members shall correspond to their respective official ten
ures, except that the terms of the city and county appointive
administrative officials shall terminate respectively, with the terms
of the mayor and county judge selecting them. Any incumbent
otherwise qualified under this section shall hold office until his
successor is chosen and qualified; provided, however, that ap
pointed members may be removed by the appointing authority
on written charges after a public hearing.
Approved February 27, 1962

Chapter 4T lSf>
CHAPTER 41
(H. B. 146)
AN ACT relating to homes for the aged.
WHEREAS, there is a growing need for additional housing and care
facilities for elderly persons; and
WHEREAS, many facilities presently in operation are inadequate; and
WHEREAS, there are resources available at federal, state and local levels
of government which are not being completely utilized toward the
alleviation of the problem; and
WHEREAS, the state government should promote interest in the pro
vision of housing for the elderly and should provide leadership, in
formation as to available resources and technical assistance to public
or private groups or individuals engaged in the development of such
housing and care facilities; and
WHEREAS, the state government should provide a means of financing
or partial financing of needed facilities in those instances in which
no other financial assistance is available.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. As used in this Act:
(1) "Nursing home" and "personal care home" have the
meaning that they are given in KRS 216.610;
(2) "Fund" means the Nursing Home and Personal Care
Home Loan Fund;
(3) "Board" means the Nursing Home and Personal Care
Home Loan Fund Board.
Section 2. The Department of Economic Security shall be
responsible for promotion of interest in the development of
additional facilities for the housing and care of the elderly, and
for providing consultative and technical assistance to public and
private groups engaged in the development of such facilities. The
department's functions shall include but not be limited to:
(a) Promotion of local and community interest in the prob
lem of housing and care for the elderly.
( b ) Assisting local housing commissions in the development
of low rent housing projects for the elderly.

156 Acts of the General Assembly
(c) Provision of information as to need for facilities in
particular areas or locations.
(d) Provision of advice and assistance in the planning of
facilities as to area to be served, size, type, staffing,
operation and maintenance.
( e ) Provision of information as to the availability of federal
financial assistance and the procedures which should
be followed in applying for such assistance.
(f ) Provision of information as to the availability of state
financial assistance and the procedures which should
be followed in applying for such assistance.
(g) Provision of information as to the availability of private
financial assistance.
(h) Provision of information as to licensing requirements
of the state or its political subdivisions.
Section 3. (1) The Nursing Home and Personal Care
Home Loan Fund is established. Loans may be made from the
fund for constructing, equipping, remodeling or re-equipping
nursing homes or personal care homes.
(2) The Nursing Home and Personal Care Home Loan
Fund Board is established. The board is composed of the Com
missioners of Health, Economic Security, Finance and Mental
Health. The Commissioner of Economic Security shall be the
Chairman.
Section 4. The board shall promulgate regulations govern
ing the granting of loans under this Act. The regulations shall
provide:
(1) The rate of interest to be charged.
(2) Whether security is to be required, and if so, the
amount and kind of security.
( 3 ) Standards for determining the feasibility and soundness
of projects.
(4) The form and content of applications.
(5) Percentage of total project cost to be loaned in each
category authorized.
(6) Other matters that the board determines necessary to
carry out the purposes of this Act of the purpose
expressed in KRS 216.620.

Chapter 42 157
Section 5. The Commissioner of Finance with the approval
of the Governor shall have authority to transfer to the fund an
amount not to exceed $150,000 in the 1962-1964 biennium from
funds appropriated to the Department of Economic Security for
Nursing Home Care and Homes for the Aged.
Approved February 27, 1962
CHAPTER 42
(H. B. 158)
AN ACT relating to urban renewal and redevelopment.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 99.350 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is amended
to read as follows:
( 1 ) If the council of any community by resolution finds and
declares (a) that there exist in the community slum areas or
blighted areas, (b) that there is need in the community for the
exercise of powers, functions, and duties conferred by KRS
99.330 to 99.510, and (c) that the exercise of such powers, func
tions and duties by an agency created and established pursuant
to this section would be more efficient and in the public interest
than the exercise of such powers, functions and duties by the
community or the housing commission of the community pur
suant to KRS 99.470, an agency to be known as the urban reneioal
and community [slum clearance and] development agency of
the city or county, as the case may be, shall thereupon exist for
such community with the powers, duties and functions provided
for in KRS 99.330 to 99.510.
(2) Upon adoption of the resolution, the mayor shall be
promptly notified and he shall appoint, with the approval of a
majority of the council, five resident electors of the community
as members of the agency.
(3) Three of the members who are first appointed shall be
designated to serve for terms of one, two and three years re-
years re--e

158 Acts of the General AssemblV
spectively, and the remaining two oi such members shall be
designated to serve for terms of four years each, from the date of
their appointment. Any appointments heretofore made for a term
of five years are hereby confirmed and declared to be appoint
ments for a term of four years from the date of such appointment.
Thereafter members shall be appointed as aforesaid for a term
of office of four years, except that all vacancies occurring during
a term shall be filled for the unexpired term. A member shall
hold office until his successor has been appointed and qualified.
(4) The agency shall elect a chairman from among its
members. The term of office as chairman of the agency, unless
otherwise prescribed by the council shall be for the calendar
year, or for that portion thereof remaining after each such
chairman is designated or elected.
(5) The powers of each agency shall be vested in the
members thereof then in office. Members of an agency or their
staff shall receive their actual and necessary expenses, including
traveling expenses, provided, however, that such other compen
sation, if prescribed, shall be paid exclusively from community
funds.
(6) For inefficiency or neglect of duty or misconduct in
office, a member of an agency may be removed by the council
but the member may be removed only after he shall have been
given a copy of the charges at least ten days prior to a public
hearing thereon and has had an opportunity to be heard in person
or by counsel.
(7) No officer or employe of the community, or of the
agency, who in the course of his duties is required to participate
in the formulation of plans or policies for the redevelopment of
a development area, or to approve such plans or policies, shall
acquire any interest in any property included within a develop
ment area within the community. If any such officer or employe
owns or has financial interest, direct or indirect, in any property
included within such development area he shall immediately
disclose, in writing, such interest to the agency and to the council
and such disclosure shall be entered in the minutes of the agency
and of the council. Failure to so disclose such interest shall
constitute misconduct in office. No payment shall be made to

Chapter 43 159
any member or officer of an agency for any property or interest
therein acquired by the agency from such member or officer
unless the amount of such payment is fixed by court order in
eminent domain proceedings, or unless such payment is unani
mously approved by the council.
(8) When an agency is created for any community, the
council may at that time, and from time to time thereafter,
appropriate such amounts of money to the agency as it deems
necessary for the administrative purposes of the agency. The
administrative purposes of the agency may include staff and
consultants for research, studies and surveys, designation of de
velopment areas, preparation of development plans, estimates
of the cost of acquisition, clearance and conditioning of land
for redevelopment, estimates of the fair use value of the land and
any other administrative expenses considered necessary by the
council.
( 9 ) Each such agency shall file with the council a detailed
report of all its transactions, including a statement of all revenues
and expenditures, at such intervals as the council may prescribe.
(10) At any time after two years after the appointment
of the first five members of the agency, if such agency shall
not theretofore have redeveloped or acquired land for, or com
menced the redevelopment of, a project, or entered into any
contracts for redevelopment, the council may by resolution
declare that the offices of the members of the agency shall be
vacated and thereupon the capacity of the agency to transact
business or exercise any power shall be suspended and shall
remain suspended until the appointment of new members to
the office of the agency as hereinbefore set forth in this section.
Approved February 27, 1962
CHAPTER 43
(H. B. 196)
AN ACT relating to bills passed by the General Assembly.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:

160 Acts of the General Assembly
In any bill passed by the General Assembly that amends
one or more statute sections, it is the intent of the General
Assembly to delete by amendment from such statute sections all
words, figures and punctuation enclosed in brackets (i.e. [ ] ) in
the text of the statute sections as set out in the bill.
Approved February 27, 1962
CHAPTER 44
(S. B. 18)
AN ACT relating to Commonwealth's attorneys' stenographers.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 69.100 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is amended
to read as follows:
In counties having a population of 200,000 or over the
Commonwealth's attorney shall appoint four [three] stenogra
phers for terms of four years subject to removal at any time
by the Commonwealth's attorney. Three [Two] of the stenog
raphers shall do stenographic work and typewriting for the
Commonwealth's attorney and perform other duties assigned
to them by the Commonwealth's attorney. They shall each re
ceive an annual salary of not less than $2700. One stenographer
shall perform the duties of record clerk and shall receive an
annual salary of not less than $3600. The salaries of all stenog
raphers shall be payable out of the county levy in equal monthly
installments and such salaries may be increased by the fiscal
court in accordance with the provisions of KRS 64.520. The
stenographers shall have the same power of administering an
oath as a notary public.
Approved March 3, 1962

Chapter 46 161
CHAPTER 45
(S. B. 88)
AN ACT relating to the State Fire and Tornado Insurance Fund.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 56.180 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is amended
to read as follows:
( 1 ) If at the end of any fiscal year the moneys and secur
ities to the credit of the State Fire and Tornado Insurance Fund
exceed two million dollars, any such excess shall be available to
the Department of Finance for the purpose of improving condi
tions relating to life and fire safety in state properties. Such im
provements may include but not be limited to the construction
and equipping of a fire house in the Capital City. Allocations
by the Department of Finance shall be made on the basis of
recommendations made pursuant to KRS 56.170 by the Commis
sioner of Insurance.
(2) If conditions with respect to life and fire safety in state
properties are such as not to require the expenditure of all excess
funds for such improvements, then the Commissioner of Insur
ance and the Commissioner of Finance shall determine the amount
of such excess not so required and may authorize its transfer to
the capital construction fund.
Approved March 3, 1962
CHAPTER 46
(S. B. 91)
AN ACT relating to the form of certain public securities, amending
Section 61.390 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes.
WHEREAS, insurance companies and other large purchasers of municipal
bonds sometimes prefer to designate the particular form or denomina
tion of the securities which they purchase, and will offer advantageous
prices for issues which permit some exercise of choice in such mat
ters, and

162 Acts of the General Assembly
WHEREAS, the issuer of such securities may also find a certain freedom
of choice as to the form of the instrument to be convenient in other
respects,
NOW, THEREFORE,
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 61.390 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
(1) Any bonds heretofore or hereafter authorized to be
issued by the slate, any county, municipality, taxing or school dis
trict, including any corporation acting as an agency or instru
mentality of such unit, whether created by statute, or organized
under the provisions of the non-profit corporation laws, under
the provisions of any law heretofore or hereafter enacted and
any interest coupons attached thereto may, if so authorized
by the governing body of such unit, bear or be executed
with the facsimile signature of any official authorized by
such law to sign or to execute such bonds or coupons; pro
vided, however, that each such bond shall be manually signed
by at least one official of such unit. In case any such law shall
provide for the sealing of such bonds with the official or corporate
seal of such unit or of its governing body or any official thereof, a
facsimile of such seal may be imprinted on the bonds if so
authorized by the governing body of such unit, and it shall not
be necessary in such case to impress such seal physically upon
such bonds.
(2) In case any such law shall provide that bonds issued
thereunder shall be negotiable, such bonds shall be offered for
sale as negotiable bonds, payable to bearer, with or without
attached coupons for the payment of interest and with or without
privilege of registration as to principal only, or registration as
to both principal and interest, as the governing body of the issuing
unit may determine; but such governing body may nevertheless
additionally provide, in its discretion, that any purchaser of all
or any of such bonds shall have a right and privilege to designate,
subject to such conditions and restrictions as the governing body
may prescribe, that such bonds, or any of them, be issued in the
first instance in fully registered non-negotiable form, or in one
or more denominations of such purchaser's own choosing, or both,

Chapter 47 163
whether in negotiable or non-negotiable form, with or without
attached coupons, with or without registration provision, and with
or without rights of conversion or reconversion from one form and
denomination to another, at the option of the holder or holders
from time to time.
(3) In case any officer whose signature or a facsimile of
whose signature shall appear on any bonds or coupons shall cease
to be such officer before the delivery of such bonds, such signature
or such facsimile shall nevertheless be valid and sufficient for all
purposes the same as if he had remained in office until such de
livery, and any bond may bear the facsimile signature of, or may
be signed by, such persons as at the actual time of the execution
of such bond shall be the proper officers to sign such bond al
though at the date of such bond such persons may not have been
such officers.
( 4 ) The words "public securities" as used herein shall mean
bonds, notes or other obligations for the payment of money
issued by this state, by its political subdivisions, or by any de
partment, agency or other instrumentality of this state or of any
of its political subdivisions.
(5) This section shall not repeal any other law authorizing
the execution of public securities with facsimile signatures or seals.
Approved March 3, 1962
CHAPTER 47
(S. B. 120)
AN ACT relating to the National Guard.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 38.030 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
The Governor may direct the commanding officer of the
troops ordered into active state service for the purpose of resist

164 Acts of the General Assembly
ing invasion, suppressing rebellions, insurrections, riots or threats
thereof; suppressing any active, unlawful or threatened violence
to persons or property in this state; authorizing the arrest of
all persons engaged in aiding or abetting therein; investigating
any acts of treason, sabotage or attempted sabotage and to cause
the arrest of such persons engaged therein or aiding and abetting;
and/or protecting life and property in the event of the emergency
or disaster. The Governor may by his order prescribe the duty to
be performed by the troops thus called into active field service.
The Governor may direct the commanding officer of such military
forces thus ordered into service to report to any of the following
named civil officers of the district, county, city or town; circuit
judge, sheriff, county judge or mayor; and such civil officers
may direct the specific object to be accomplished by such military
force, but the tactical direction and disposition of the troops and
the particular means to be employed to accomplish the object
shall be left solely to the officers of the National Guard. Troops
shall not be relieved from active field service except by order
of the Governor, [to report to any circuit judge, county judge
or mayor. The judge or mayor may direct the specific object
to be accomplished, but the tactical direction and disposition
of the troops and methods used shall be left solely to the discretion
of the troop commander.]
Section 2. Section 38.040 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
(1) All commissioned officers and warrant officers of the
Kentucky National Guard shall be appointed by the Governor,
upon the recommendation of The Adjutant General and the com
manding officers of the organizations to which such officers are
to be assigned for duty, and shall be appointed according to
grade in the branch or corps of the service in which they are
appointed. No officer or warrant officer shall be appointed until
he shall have successfully passed such tests as to his physical,
moral and professional fitness as are or shall be prescribed by
Federal law for Federal recognition as an officer.
( 2 ) All appointments as staff officers shall be of individuals
with previous military experience. [Appointment shall be held
until the appointee reaches sixty years of age or is relieved by
the Governor.]

Chapter 47 165
(3) The Governor may issue such regulations governing
the appointment of commissioned officers and warrant officers
in the Kentucky National Guard as may be necessary in order to
conform to the requirements made by Congress for participation
in Federal appropriations for the Kentucky National Guard.
(4) No officer or warrant officer except The Adjutant
General shall retain the grade to which he may have been
appointed or promoted unless, within a reasonable length of time,
he obtains Federal recognition for such grade.
Section 3. Section 38.070 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
Every officer shall [, within ten days after his commission
is tendered to him, or within ten days after he has been notified
personally or by mail that it is held in readiness for him by a
superior officer.] take and subscribe to [the following] an oath
of office as prescribed by the Department of Defense. ["I,
do solemnly swear that I will support
and defend the Constitution of the United States and the Con
stitution of Kentucky against all enemies, foreign and domestic;
that I will bear true faith and allegiance to the same; that I will
obey the orders of the President of the United States and of the
Governor of the Commonwealth of Kentucky; that I make this
obligation freely without any mental reservation or purpose of
evasion, and that I will well and faithfully discharge the duties
of the office of in the National
Guard of the United States and of the Commonwealth of Ken
tucky upon which I am about to enter, so help me God."]
The oath shall be taken and subscribed before an officer [of
the National Guard of the grade of general or field officer, sum
mary court, or any civil officer] authorized to administer oaths.
In case of neglect or refusal to take and subscribe to the oath,
[within the time mentioned,] the commission shall be canceled
by the Governor and a new appointment shall be made to fill
the vacancy.
Section 4. Section 38.090 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
( 1 ) All able-bodied citizens of Kentucky, of good character,

166 Acts of the General Assembly
who can read and write the English language, who are between
the ages of seventeen and thirty-five years with no prior service;
between thirty-six and under thirty-eight with one year of service;
thirty-eight and under forty-one with two years of service and
forty-one and under fifty-seven with two years plus the number
of years applicant is over age forty, and who have passed the re
quired physical examination and who are not ineligible for en
listment or re-enlistment in the Armed Forces of the United
States, subject to current United States Armed Forces regulations
covering enlistments and re-enlistments, may be enlisted or re-
enlisted in the Kentucky National Guard for a term of [not less
than] one or more [than three] years.
( 2 ) No person dishonorably discharged or discharged with
out honor from the Armed Forces of the United States shall be
eligible for enlistment in the Kentucky National Guard.
(3) Every person who enlists or re-enlists shall sign and
make oath to an enlistment paper before an officer authorized to
accept enlistments.
( 4 ) Enlisted persons may be transferred to or from organi
zations, as provided by regulations. [approved by the Governor.]
(5) By order of the Governor in case of emergency, enlist
ments in the Kentucky National Guard may be extended for
one year.
Section 5. Section 38.110 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
An officer of the Kentucky National Guard shall hold his
commission so long as he is entitled to Federal recognition in
that grade [receive Federal pay and allowances] unless separated
[retired] from the service prior to that time by reason of resig
nation, disability or for cause to be determined by a court-martial
or efficiency board legally convened for that purpose.
Section 6. Section 38.130 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
The Governor may order any commissioned officer before
an efficiency board consisting of three officers senior in rank to
the officer whose fitness for service is under examination. The

Chapter 47 167
board shall have the powers of courts of inquiry and courts-
martial to subpoena witnesses and produce testimony and shall
inquire into the moral character, capacity and general fitness
[for the service] of the commissioned officer. [, and record and
return the testimony taken and a record of the proceedings.] The
board shall make a record of all proceedings. If the findings of
the board are unfavorable to the officer and are approved by the
Governor, he shall be discharged from the service. No officer
shall participate in the examination or decision of the board in
any case in which the decision would affect his own grade or
promotion. Failure to appear when ordered before a board
constituted under this section shall be sufficient grounds for
a finding of the board that the officer ordered to appear be
discharged.
Section 7. Section 38.140 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
(1) An enlisted man shall not be discharged before the
expiration of his term of service except by order of the Governor
or by sentence of court-martial approved by the Governor. A
soldier upon his discharge from service, shall be given a certifi
cate of discharge or report of separation from service. [signed by
his commanding officer] .
( 2 ) The types of discharges used shall be the same as those
authorized [promulgated] by the [Chief of the] National Guard
Bureau.
Section 8. Section 38.200 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
Except when in the service of the United States, officers
and enlisted men, when employed in active state service, be
ginning with the day they assemble at their armories or other
designated place until the day they return thereto and have been
properly relieved, inclusive, fractional parts of a day counting as
a full day, may [shall] receive pay at the following daily rates:

170 Acts of the General Assembly
Section 9. Section 38.210 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
For duty at all encampments or other exercises ordered by
the Governor, except when authorized and paid by the Federal
Government, each commissioned officer and enlisted man may
[shall] receive the pay and allowances provided in KRS 38.200,
[Members of rifle teams or other teams of officers and men com
peting in any competition for which they volunteer shall not
receive pay, but may receive actual and necessary expenses,]
including transportation and subsistence.
Section 10. Section 38.220 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
For service and attendance upon boards appointed by the
Governor, [general] courts-martial and courts of inquiry as a
member, judge advocate, recorder or witness, or upon inspection
or other duty ordered by the Governor, officers and enlisted
men, when not engaged on duty for which pay is provided, may
[shall] receive the same pay and allowances as is provided for
active state service.
Section 11. Section 38.240 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
(1) Any member of the National Guard who is incapaci
tated from pursuing his usual business or occupation by reason
of any injury received or disease contracted when on duty as as
sembled therefor under lawful authority, which injury or disease
directly results from such service, shall be entitled to receive the
pay provided by KRS 38.200 and actual necessary expenses for
care, hospitalization and medical attendance during the period
of such incapacity. Where a claim is made under this section,
the Adjutant General shall, from time to time, designate medical
officers to examine the claimant, and may direct the claimant
to be removed to and treated in a designated hospital. If the
claimant refuses to permit the examination, or to go to the hos
pital or to follow the advice given or treatment prescribed for him
therein, he shall forfeit his right to any claim, pay or allowance
under this section.

Chapter 47 171
(2) If the death of any member of the National Guard
results from an injury received or disease contracted when on
duty or assembled therefor under lawful authority, the state shall
pay his funeral expense, but not to exceed five hundred [two
hundred fifty] dollars.
( 3 ) The body of an officer or enlisted man who dies while
in active state service, shall, under the direction of his command
ing officer and at the expense of the state, be promptly prepared
for burial, enclosed in a suitable casket, and returned to his late
residence under a proper escort; but the cost to the state of the
casket and preparing the body for burial shall not exceed the sum
of five hundred [two hundred and fifty] dollars.
(4) If any member of the National Guard, when on duty
or assembled therefor under lawful authority, does any act in
the discharge of his duty that causes him to be proceeded against
by civil court action, the Attorney-General shall represent him
in the civil proceedings. If, under the same conditions, a member
of the National Guard is proceeded against in a criminal action,
The Adjutant General may, with the approval of the Governor,
appoint legal counsel to represent him in the criminal proceedings.
The compensation paid the legal counsel shall be fixed by the
Adjutant General, with the written approval of the Governor.
( 5 ) All disbursements under this section shall, upon proper
certification be paid from the appropriation for the Department
of Military Affairs.
Section 12. Section 38.250 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
(1) Every unit of the Kentucky National Guard shall as
semble for drill and instructions not less than forty-eight times
each year, and shall participate in encampments, maneuvers or
other exercises at least fifteen days in each year unless excused
therefrom by the Governor. The number present in order to
obtain credit for the drill, period of time and type of instruction
shall be such as is prescribed by the Governor.
(2) All employees of the Commonwealth of Kentucky
who are members of the Kentucky National Guard and/or the

172 Acts of the General Assembly
Reserve Forces of the United States shall be entitled to fifteen
days annual military leave with pay.
Section 13. Section 38.290 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
(1) Premises occupied by the Kentucky National Guard
for military purposes shall be under the control of the Senior
National Guard [commanding] officer using them.
(2) All military property furnished for the use of the sev
eral organizations of the Kentucky National Guard shall be kept
in the armory or storerooms of the command, except when in
use for authorized military purposes.
Section 14. Section 38.990 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
( 1 ) Any person who makes a false oath as to any statement
contained in the enlistment papers mentioned in subsection (3)
of KRS 38.090 shall be fined not less than ten nor more than fifty
dollars.
(2) Any civil officer who fails to execute process and
sentences when required to do so under KRS 38.400 shall be fined
not more than fifty dollars for each offense.
(3) Any master, keeper, or other person in charge of a
tollgate, toll bridge or ferry who willfully hinders or delays any
member of the Kentucky National Guard or Kentucky Active
Militia going to or returning from any parade, encampment, drill,
or meeting which he is required by proper authority to attend,
or who refuses free passage to any member, conveyance or other
property in violation of KRS 38.430 shall be fined not more than
fifty dollars for each offense.
(4) Any person who violates any of the provisions of KRS
38.440 shall be fined not less than ten nor more than one hundred
dollars, or imprisoned in the county jail for not more than six
months, or both.
(5) Any person who violates any of the provisions of KRS
38.450 shall be fined not less than ten nor more than one hundred
dollars.

Chapter 48 173
(6) Any person who violates any of the provisions of sub
section (1) of KRS 38.460 or who aids in enforcing any provision
prohibited by subsection (2) of KRS 38.460 shall be fined not
less than one hundred or more than five hundred dollars or
imprisoned in the county jail not less than six months, or both.
(7) Any person who trespasses upon property occupied by
the Kentucky National Guard or Kentucky Active Militia con
trary to the orders of the officer in charge of such property shall
be fined not less than one hundred nor more than five hundred
dollars or imprisoned in the county jail for six months, or both.
(8) Any witness who refuses to appear in answer to a
subpoena, to answer questions before a military court or board
is guilty of contempt and may be fined not more than fifty dollars
bv such court or board.
Section 15. Sections 38.230 and 38.340 of the Kentucky
Revised Statutes are repealed.
Approved March 3, 1962
CHAPTER 48
(S. B. 121)
AN ACT relating to the Active Militia.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. A new section is added to Chapter 37 of the
Kentucky Revised Statutes to read as follows:
(1) Officers and enlisted men of the Kentucky Active
Militia shall be subject to the Code of Military Justice as set
forth in Chapter 35 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes.
Section 2. A new section of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is created to read as follows:
The Department of Military Affairs shall have the power
to accept the provisions of KRS 342 for the benefit of members

174 Acts of the General Assembly
of the Kentucky Active Militia while such members are on active
state service.
Section 3. Section 37.170 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
( 1 ) The Governor is hereby authorized to enlist, organize,
maintain, equip, discipline and pay when called into active field
service a volunteer defense force [unit] other than the National
Guard, to be known as the Kentucky Active Militia, which shall
consist of able bodied males who are residents of the State of
Kentucky and who are between the ages of eighteen and sixty
four. [ ( At no time shall the number of enlisted men and officers
in the Kentucky Active Militia exceed two thousand. ) ]
( 2 ) Whenever the President of the United States shall call
any part of the National Guard of this state into active Federal
service, the Governor is hereby authorized to organize the
Kentucky Active Militia under such regulations as the Secretary
of Defense [War] may prescribe for discipline and training.
(3) The Governor shall have the power to alter, divide,
annex, disband or reorganize any organization of the Kentucky
Active Militia whenever in his judgment the efficiency of the
state forces will thereby be increased, and he shall have power
to change the organization so as to conform to the regulations
now or hereafter prescribed by the laws of the United States for
the organization of the National Guard or Militia.
Section 4. Section 37.200 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
(1) The Governor of Kentucky is hereby authorized to
appoint and commission the necessary officers for the Kentucky
Active Militia. The Adjutant General of the state with the
approval of the Governor shall prescribe rules and regulations
governing the enlistment, organization, administration, pay,
equipment, discipline and discharge of the personnel of the Ken
tucky Active Militia; obtain by requisition or purchase from the
Secretary of Defense [War] such necessary arms and equipment
as may be secured from the [War] Department of Defense, or
procure from some other source the necessary arms and equip
ment to maintain and equip the Kentucky Active Militia; and

Chapter 48 175
provide the necessary space for the housing of property and
for the purpose of drills and instruction.
(2) All officers shall be appointed and commissioned by
the Governor. All men shall be enlisted by the unit commanders
and may [shall] be discharged upon order of the Governor
for the same reasons as men are discharged from the National
Guard, when such discharge is requested in writing by the unit
commanders.
Section 5. Section 37.230 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
(1) Commanding and staff officers of organizations [regi
ments] and units [battalions] are hereby authorized to make
such visits of instruction and inspection, when authorized by the
Adjutant General to do so, as in his discretion may be necessary.
When making said visits they may be compensated as provided
in KRS 38.200 and KRS 36.140. [shall serve without compensa
tion but shall be allowed reasonable expenses.]
Section 6. Section 37.290 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
( 1 ) If the death of any member of the Active Militia results
from an injury received when on duty or assembled therefor
under competent authority or lawful order the Commonwealth
of Kentucky shall pay his funeral expenses but not to exceed
$500.00 [$250.00].
Section 7. Section 37.300 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
The body of an officer or enlisted man, who dies while in
active field service, or on active duty while his command is
absent from its home station, shall, under the direction of his
commanding officer, be promptly prepared for burial, enclosed
in a suitable casket, and returned, at the expense of the state,
to his late residence under a proper escort; provided however, that
the cost of the casket and preparing the body for burial shall
not exceed the sum of $500.00 [$250.00] as allowed by KRS
37.290. All disbursements under this section and under KRS
37.280 and 37.290 shall be a proper charge against the state and

176 Acts of the General Assembly
shall be paid from the Treasury on properly certified and
approved vouchers.
Section 8. Section 37.190 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is hereby repealed.
Approved March 3, 1962
CHAPTER 49
(S. B. 132)
AN ACT relating to prisons and prisoners.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. (1) Any prisoner who incites or partici
pates in a prison riot, or similar disturbance, or willfully dam
ages or destroys state property, shall, upon conviction, be con
fined in the penitentiary for not less than one nor more than
five years, such confinement to be served consecutively to any
other sentence then being served.
(2) Any prisoner who holds any person as a hostage for
any reason, shall, upon conviction, be confined for not less
than one nor more than five years, such confinement to be served
consecutively to any other sentence then being served.
(3) If a person other than the prisoner suffers bodily
injury while a prisoner is engaged in the activities described
in subsections (1) or (2), the prisoner shall, upon conviction,
be confined for not less than two nor more than twenty-one
years, to be served consecutively to any other sentence then
being served.
Section 2. Section 432.390 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
(1) Any person convicted of a crime who, while serving
a sentence of imprisonment in the penitentiary, escapes from
the penitentiary or wherever he may be confined, or attempts

Chapteb 50 177
to escape by concealing himself within the confines of the in
stitution or by attempting to scale the enclosure surrounding
the institution, or flees from whatever bounds he may be as
signed, whether under guard or as a trusty, shall be further con
fined in the penitentiary for not less than three nor more than
six years, to commence after he has served out the sentence for
which he was originally confined.
(2) Any convict confined in a locked cell, dormitory, hos
pital or other lockup in the penitentiary who escapes therefrom
to another part of the penitentiary, or aids another convict in
doing so, shall be confined in the penitentiary for not less than
one nor more than six years, to commence after he has served
out the sentence for which he was originally confined [to be
added to the term of his former conviction] .
(3) For any offense mentioned in Subsection (1) or Sub
section (2) of this section, the circuit court of the county in
which the convict was confined, or of the county from which
he was sent out under assignment to work, or for any other reason,
shall have jurisdiction.
Approved March 3, 1962
CHAPTER 50
(S. B. 133)
AN ACT relating to the licensing of Insurance Agents.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 304.521 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
( 1 ) Each applicant for license as agent, solicitor, or adjuster
shall, prior to the issuance of any such license, personally take
and pass to the satisfaction of the commissioner a written [an]
examination given by the commissioner as a test of his qualifica
tions and competence; but this requirement shall not apply to

178 Acts of the General Assembly
(a) Applications as to limited licenses, as travel insurance
agents only, under KRS 304.529.
(b ) Applicants who at any time within the five-year period
next preceding [prior to the] date of application have been li
censed in this state under a license similar to the license applied
for and who are deemed by the commissioner to be fully qualified
and competent.
(c) Applicants for license as non-resident agent who have
fulfilled qualification requirements in their state of residence
and who are deemed by the commissioner to be fully qualified!
and competent.
(d) Applicants for an agent's or solicitor's license covering
the same kinds of insurance as an agent's or solicitor's license
then held bv them.
[(2) Applicants for the renewal of licenses in force on
September 1, 1950, or issued thereafter, and adjusters in business
as such on September 1, 1950, shall not be required to take an
examination except as provided in subsection (3) of this section.]
(2) [3] The commissioner may at any time require any
licensed agent, solicitor, or adjuster to take and successfully pass
an examination testing his competence and qualifications as a
condition to the continuance or renewal of his license, if the
licensee has been guilty of violation of this code, or has so con
ducted his affairs under his license as to cause the commissioner
reasonably to desire further evidence of his qualifications. In any
such event the commissioner shall give not less than thirty days
notice of the requirement before the examination, and the li
censee's license shall in any event remain in effect until the result
of the examination has been determined by the commissioner.
(3) [4] The commissioner shall give, conduct and grade
all such examinations in a fair and impartial manner, and with
out unfair discrimination as between individuals examined.
(4) [5] The commissioner may appoint an advisory board
to make recommendations to him with respect to the scope, type,
and conduct of written examinations under KRS 304.511 to
304.560 and the times and places in the state where they shall be
held. The advisory board, if appointed, shall consist of qualified

Chapter 50 179
persons selected from among insurance company officers and
employes, general agents and managers; and licensed insurance
agents and adjusters on such basis as to provide fair representa
tion as to the various tvpes of insurers. The members of the
advisory board shall serve without pay but, upon the authorization
of the commissioner, shall be reimbursed for their reasonable
expenses in attending meetings of the advisory board.
Section 2. Section 304.567 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
( 1 ) Each applicant for a license to act as a life insurance
agent within the state shall submit to a personal written examina
tion to determine his competence with respect to life insurance
and annuity contracts and his familiarity with the pertinent pro
visions of the laws of this state, and shall pass the same to the
satisfaction of the commissioner; except that no such written
examination shall be required of:
( a) Applicants who at any time within the five-year period
next preceding [prior to the] date of application have been li
censed in this state under a license similar to the license applied
for and who are deemed by the commissioner to be fully qualified
and competent.
(b) An applicant for a renewal license [Applicants who at
any time prior to the date of application have been licensed in
this state under a license similar to the license applied for] unless
the commissioner determines that such examination is necessary
to establish the competency of such individual, and in which case
[however, in the event the applicant is currently licensed,] the
commissioner shall give not less than thirty days notice of the
requirement before the examination and the licensee's license
shall in any event remain in effect until the result of the exami
nation has been determined by the commissioner.
(c) [b] In the discretion of the commissioner, an applicant
whose license to do business or act as a life insurance agent in
this state was suspended less than one year prior to the date of
application.
(2) The commissioner shall establish rules and regulations
with respect to the scope, type and conduct of such written exam
inations and the times and places within this state where they

180 Acts of the General Assembly
shall be held. Applicants shall be permitted to take such ex
aminations on any business day at the principal office of the
commissioner.
(3) No person who shall have taken and failed to pass two
examinations given pursuant to this section shall be entitled to
take any further examination until after the expiration of six
months from the date of the last examination in which he failed.
If such person shall thereafter fail to pass two more such examina
tions he shall not be eligible to take any further examination
until after the expiration of one year from the date of his last
unsuccessful examination. An examination fee shall be paid for
each and every examination.
(4) The commissioner may appoint an advisory board to
make recommendations to him with respect to the scope, type
and conduct of written examinations and the times and places
in the state where they shall be held. This advisory board, if
appointed, shall consist of individuals experienced in the life
insurance business, and may include life insurance company

life insurance agents. The members of the advisory board shall
serve without pay but, upon the authorization of the commissioner,
shall be reimbursed for their reasonable expenses in attending
meetings of the advisory board.
Approved March 3, 1962
CHAPTER 51
(S. B. 134)
AN ACT relating to military leave for public employees.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. A new section of Chapter 61 of Kentucky Re
vised Statutes is enacted to read:
All officers and employees of this state, or of any depart
ment or agency thereof who are members of the National Guard

Chapter 52 181
or of any reserve component of the armed forces of the United
States, or of the reserve corps of the United States Public Health
Service, shall be entitled to leave of absence from their respective
duties, without loss of time, pay, regular leave, impairment of
efficiency rating, or of any other rights or benefits, to which they
are entitled while in the performance of duty or training in the
service of this state or of the United States under competent
orders, for a period in any calendar year not to exceed that
specified in this act. Such officers or employees while on such
leave shall be paid their salaries or compensations for a period
or periods not exceeding fifteen calendar days or ten working
days if the employee's position is based upon a five day work
week; twelve days if the employee's position is based upon a six
day work week; fifteen days if the employee's position is based
upon a seven day work week, in any one calendar year.
Section 2. All officers and employees of counties, munici
palities, school districts, or other political subdivisions of the state
who are members of the National Guard or of any reserve com
ponent of the armed forces of the United States, including the
United States Public Health Service, may be granted annual
military leave by their respective employers as provided in Sec
tion 1 of this act.
Approved March 3, 1962
CHAPTER 52
(S. B. 137)
AN ACT relating to Mortgage Guaranty Insurance.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. "Mortgage guaranty insurance" means insurance
against financial loss by reason of non-payment of principal,
interest and other sums agreed to be paid under the terms of any
note or bond or other evidence of indebtedness secured by a
mortgage, deed of trust, or other instrument constituting a lien
or charge on real estate.

182 Acts of the General Assembly
Section 2. A. An insurer, in order to qualify for writing
mortgage guaranty insurance, must haye the same capital and
surplus as that required of a multiple line company by KRS
304.073.
B. An insurer transacting any class of insurance other than
mortgage guaranty insurance is not eligible for the issuance of
a certificate of authority to transact mortgage guaranty insurance
in this commonwealth, nor the renewal thereof.
C. A foreign or alien insurer writing mortgage guaranty
insurance shall not be eligible for the issuance of a certificate
of authority in Kentucky unless it has demonstrated a satis
factory operating experience in its state of domicile.
Section 3. The unearned premium reserve shall be com
puted in accordance with KRS 304.369, except that all premiums
on risks written for one year or less must be reserved on a monthly
pro rata basis, and the reserve for those policies covering a risk
period of more than five years shall be computed in accordance
with formulae filed by the insurer and approved by the Commis
sioner of Insurance.
Section 4. For the protection of the people of this Com
monwealth and for the purpose of protecting against the effect
of adverse economic cycles, the company shall establish a con
tingency reserve which shall be maintained for 180 months. To
provide for this, the company shall, annually, contribute 50% of
the earned premiums to this reserve. The earned premiums so
reserved may be released, annually, after the specified time of
180 months has elapsed. However, subject to the approval of
the Commissioner, this reserve may be available only for loss
payments, when the loss ratio (incurred losses to premiums
earned) exceeds 20%. This amount so used shall reduce the
next subsequent annual release to surplus from the established
contingency reserve.
Section 5. A mortgage guaranty insurer shall not at any
time have outstanding a total liability, net of reinsurance, in
excess of twenty-five times its capital, unassigned funds and
contingency reserve. It shall not insure loans secured by prop
erties in a single housing tract or a contiguous tract ( not separated

Chapter 53 183
by more than one-half mile) in excess of 10% of its capital,
unassigned funds and contingency reserve. Coverage may be
provided only if the improvement on such real estate is a resi
dential building or buildings designed for occupancy by not
more than four families.
Section 6. The case basis method shall be used to determine
the loss reserves, which shall include a reserve for claims
reported and unpaid and a reserve for claims incurred but not
reported.
Section 7. Mortgage guaranty insurance shall be subject
to the provisions of KRS 304.625 to 304.644 for the purposes of
rate making.
Section 8. All policy forms and endorsements shall be filed
with and be subject to the approval of the Commissioner of
Insurance. With respect to owner-occupied single family dwell
ings, the mortgage insurance policy shall provide that the bor
rower shall not be liable to the insurance company for any
deficiency arising from a foreclosure sale.
Section 9. No bank, savings and loan association, insur
ance company or other lending institution, any of whose author
ized real estate securities are insured by mortgage guaranty
insurance companies may state in any brochure, pamphlet, report
or any form of advertising that the real estate loans of the bank,
savings and loan association, insurance company or other lending
institution are "insured loans" unless the brochure, pamphlet,
report or advertising also clearly states that the loans are insured
by private insurers and the names of the private insurers are
given and shall not make any such statement at all unless such
insurance is by an insurer authorized to write this coverage in
this Commonwealth.
Approved March 3, 1962

CHAPTER 56
(H. B. 76)
AN ACT relating to revenue and taxation.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 131.120 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
( 1 ) If an aggrieved taxpayer has petitioned for review, as
provided in KRS 131.110, he may, within thirty days [fifteen
days] after the mailing of notice of the final decision of the
Kentucky Tax Commission, appeal from the decision on any
question of law, including adequacy of evidence, to the Franklin
circuit court. Two copies of the taxpayer's petition of appeal
shall be filed with the Department of Revenue on the day it is
filed with the clerk of the Franklin circuit court. The aggrieved
taxpayer shall file as a part of his petition to the Franklin circuit
court the record before the department, including all the plead
ings, orders and such parts of the evidence as he believes neces
sary to develop the points involved. The Commonwealth may
certify other portions of the evidence. To facilitate adjudication
of the taxpayer's claims, the department shall furnish him, on
payment of a reasonable fee to be fixed by the department, a
certified copy of such parts of the record as he may require.
The case shall then be heard by the court on the record, except
that either party may introduce evidence of fraud.
(2) Except as otherwise expressly provided, the provisions
of the Rules of Civil Procedure [Civil Code] shall govern pro
cedure involving appeals from any order, ruling or finding of the
Kentucky Tax Commission.
Section 2. Section 131.130 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
Without limitation of other duties assigned to it by law, the
following powers and duties are vested in the Department of
Revenue:
(1) The department mav make rules and regulations, and
direct proceedings and actions, for the administration and en
forcement of all tax laws of this state.

Chapter 56 187
(2) The department, by representatives appointed by it in
writing, may take testimony or depositions, and may examine
the records, documents, files and equipment of any taxpayer or
of any person whose records, documents or equipment will furnish
knowledge concerning the tax liability of any taxpayer, when it
deems this reasonably necessary for purposes incident to the
performance of its functions. The department may enforce this
right by application to the circuit court in the county wherein
the person is domiciled or has his principal office, or by applica
tion to the Franklin circuit court, which courts may compel
compliance with the orders of the department.
( 3 ) The department shall prescribe the style, and determine
and enforce the use or manner of keeping, of all assessment and
tax forms and records employed by state and county officials, and
mav prescribe forms necessary for administration of any revenue
law.
(4) The department shall advise on all questions respecting
the construction of state revenue laws and the application thereof
to various classes of taxpayers and property.
(5) The department may require the Commonwealth's at
torneys and county attorneys to prosecute actions and proceedings
and perform other services incident to the enforcement of laws
assigned to the department for administration.
(6) The department may conduct research in the fields of
taxation, finance and local government administration, and publish
its findings, as the commissioner may deem wise.
(7) The department may make rules and regulations neces
sary to establish a system of taxpayer identifying numbers for
the purpose of securing proper identification of taxpayers sub
ject to any tax laws or other revenue measure of this state, and
may require such taxpayer to place on any return, report, state
ment or other document required to be filed, any number assigned
pursuant to such rules and regulations.
Approved March 3, 1962

188 Acts of the General Assembly
CHAPTER 57
(H. B. 86)
AN ACT relating to boards of education.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 160.280 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
Members of boards of education shall receive no salaries,
but members of county boards of education may receive a per
diem of ten dollars and their actual expenses for each regular
or special meeting attended, and may be reimbursed for other
actual and necessary expenditures incurred in the district in the
performance of their duties authorized by the board. In no case
shall the expenses incurred within the district or per diem of
any member exceed two hundred dollars per annum. Members
of boards of education may be reimbursed for actual and neces
sary expenditures incurred, outside the district in the perform
ance of their duties authorized by the board. All claims shall be
made out according to law and filed with the secretary of the
board and shall be approved and paid as other claims against
the board.
Approved March 3, 1962

Section 2. A new section of Kentucky Revised Statutes is
enacted to read as follows:
( 1 ) Any member of the system on July 1, 1962, and whose
employment has previously been terminated without prejudice
and who has been refunded his accumulated contributions, there
by losing service credit, may regain such credit by paying to the
system the amount refunded with interest at a rate determined by
the board provided such payment is made prior to January 1,
1963.
( 2 ) Any person who becomes an employe of a participating
department after July 1, 1962, and whose employment has been
terminated without prejudice prior to July 1, 1962, and who has

Chapter 58 193
been refunded his accumulated contributions, thereby losing
senice credit, may regain such credit by paying to the system the
amount refunded with interest at a rate determined by the board
provided such payment is made within six months of re-employ
ment.
(3) Effective July 1, 1962, and thereafter anv employe of a
participating department who did not elect membership as pro
vided in KRS 61.525(2) may obtain credit for prior service and
for current service by paying to the system the contribution he
would have paid had he elected membership with interest at
the rate determined by the board and an additional sum equal
to fifty percent of this amount. The payment of the fifty percent
sum shall be considered as accumulated contributions of the in
dividual member.
(4) Effective July 1, 1962, and thereafter, an employe of a
participating department, which did not participate in the sys
tem effective July 1, 1956, may obtain credit for current service
between July 1, 1956, and the effective date of participation of
bis department by paying to the system the contribution he would
have paid had his department participated on July 1, 1956, with
interest at the rate determined by the board and an additional
sum equal to fifty percent of this amount. The payment of the
fifty percent sum shall be considered as accumulated contribu
tions of the individual member.
(5) Service credit obtained under this section shall be dis
allowed if a member does not obtain twelve months additional
current service after the date such rights under this section have
been exercised.

61.635 so that the retirement allowance (not one-half thereof)
shall be continued during the life time of the beneficiary. Nomi
nation of beneficiary may be changed by the member at any

member in such manner and on such forms as shall be required

( 1 ) The system shall be administered by a Board of Trus

(3) The members selected by the membership of each of the

ance with regulations established by the Board of Trustees. Ex
cept in the case of vacancies already existing on the effective

due to expire. Special elections shall be held to fill vacancies in

Board member to be elected, in any regular or special election,

clared elected. The Executive Secretary shall conduct the elec
tions under the terms of the regulations established, and three dis
creet persons shall be appointed by him to count the ballots and
tabulate the votes. Costs of an election shall be payable from the
funds of the system for which the member is elected.

20S Acts of the General Assembly
(4) Any vacancy which may occur, shall be filled in the
same manner provided for the selection of the particular member
for a full term. Vacancies shall be filled for the unexpired term
only.
(5) Membership on the Board of Trustees shall not be in
compatible with any other office unless a Constitutional incom
patibility exists, and no member sJwll be subject to removal from

[On the first day of the month following March 25, 1960,

ministered by a Board of Trustees consisting of the Commis
sioners of Finance, Economic Security and Personnel, and the

pointed by the Governor, at least one of whom shall have been

members shall be four years, and they may be removed only

Book; provided, however, that the terms of the members initially

System prior to its reorganization under this section, and one

of the County Employes Retirement System prior to its re

(6) [(2)] Board members who do not otherwise receive a

(D [(3)] The board shall meet at least once in each

Chapter 58 209

(8) [(4)] The Board shall:

210 Acts of the General Assembly
Section 20. Section 61.650 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
(1) The board shall be the trustee of the several funds
created by KRS 61.510 to 61.700 and shall have full power to
invest and re-invest such funds, subject to the limitations that
no investments shall be made except upon the exercise of bona
fide discretion, in securities which, at the time of making the
investment, are, by law, permitted for the investment of funds
by fiduciaries in this state. Subject to such limitations, the board
shall have full power to hold, purchase, sell, assign, transfer or
dispose of, any of the securities or investments in which any of
the funds created herein have been invested, as well as of the
proceeds of such investments and any monies belonging to such
funds.
(2) It is declared to be the policy of the Commonwealth
to promote the mutual investment of the funds of the [three]
retirement system [to be] administered by the [under this] Board
of Trustees established under KRS 61.645, and such other public
retirement systems as may wish to cooperate, and also, where
practicable, to encourage the retention of capital funds within
this state and region. In furtherance of this policy the Board of
Trustees is empowered to incorporate or otherwise establish, either
with or without the cooperation of other public retirement sys
tems, such an organization, or organizations (corporate, trust, or
otherwise) as may be desirable to secure the mutual investment
of the funds of the [three] systems under its control, and such
other public systems as may wish to join, and to promote the
policies herein described. Such corporation or other organization
shall be deemed a governmental agency, organized for a govern
mental purpose and not for private profit, and its articles of in
corporation, trust indenture or other controlling document shall
provide that it shall function under the control of its board of
directors or trustees for the purposes and under the limitations
established by this statute and by the aforesaid articles of incor
poration, trust indenture, or other controlling document. Such
board of directors or trustees shall be elected not more frequently
than once yearly by the votes of the chief administrative officers
of the retirement systems which are members of the organization,
casting their ballots in accordance with the directives of their
respective boards of trustees, and each system shall have a vote

Chapter 58 211
or votes in proportion to its investment. Members of the boards
of trustees of the investing systems shall be eligible to serve on

ganization formed hereunder, and at least one-half of those so

represent.
Section 21. Section 61.660 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
The State Treasurer shall be the custodian of the fund
created by KRS 61.510 to 61.700 and he shall be responsible
on an official surety bond for all cash and securities in his cus
tody. All registered securities in the fund created by KRS 61.510
to 61.700 shall be registered in the name of "Kentucky Em
ployes Retirement System of the Commonwealth of Kentucky"
and every change in registration, by reason of sale or assignment

nating such person and bearing on its face the specimen signa

of the board.
Section 22. Section 61.670 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
As soon as practicable after its organization, the board shall
