CHAPTER 85
(S. B. 168)
AN ACT relating to formal training programs for certain state govern
ment personnel.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. The Commissioner of Economic Security in
coordination with the Department of Personnel is authorized to
establish formal training programs within the Department of
Economic Security or within any of the bureaus, divisions or
sections of the department for the training of necessary personnel
for the administration of the programs of the department. When
courses of study, applicable to the program processes of the de
partment, are not available through departmental instruction,
arrangements may be made for the training of employes in any
public or private school or institution having available facilities
for that purpose and such training shall be deemed to be a part
of the departmental training program. Training of employes in
public or private schools or institutions for this purpose shall be
deemed a part of research assignments to be completed during
the period of study, such assignments to relate directly to the

328 Acts of the General Assembly
work assignment of the employe. After consulting with the
Department of Personnel, position classifications in the research
series shall be established for employes on such work study
assignments, and funds of the department may be used to pay
salaries commensurate with the appropriate classification while
the employe is receiving such training. Any employe who is
paid a salary while receiving such training shall be required to
enter into a contract, prior to receiving the training, that he will
complete a specified work assignment, and that unless he con
tinues in the employ of the department for at least a period
equivalent to the training period, immediately following the
completion of such training, the state will hold a claim against
him for the amount of salary paid during the training period, and
he will repay to the department the sum paid to him by the
department during the period of his training.
Section 2. The Commissioner of Child Welfare in coordi
nation with the Department of Personnel is authorized to establish
formal training programs within the Department of Child Welfare
or within any of the divisions or sections of the department for
the training of necessary personnel for the administration of the
programs of the department. When courses of study, applicable
to the program processes of the department, are not available
through departmental instruction, arrangements may be made
for the training of employes in any public or private school or
institution having available facilities for that purpose and such
training shall be deemed to be a part of the departmental train
ing program. Training of employes in public or private schools
or institutions for this purpose shall be deemed a part of research
assignments to be completed during the period of study, such
assignments to relate directlv to the work assignment of the
employe. After consulting with the Department of Personnel,
position classifications in the research series shall be established
for employes on such work study assignments, and funds of the
department may be used to pay salaries commensurate with the
appropriate classification while the employe is receiving such
training. Any employe who is paid a salary while receiving such
training shall be required to enter into a contract, prior to
receiving the training, that he will complete a specified work
assignment, and that unless he continues in the employ of the

Chapter 86 329
department for at least a period equivalent to the training
period, immediately following the completion of such training,
the state will hold a claim against him for the amount of salary
paid during the training period, and he will repay to the depart
ment the sum paid to him by the department during the period
of his training.
Approved March 9, 1962
CHAPTER 86
(S. B. 169)
AN ACT relating to voting machines.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 125.020 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
The fiscal court of any county may purchase or lease, from
available funds or from the proceeds of bonds which may be
issued for that purpose, voting machines, including extra or
reserve machines, for use in regular, special and primary elec
tions in such precincts of the county as are designated by the
fiscal court by an order entered of record. The fiscal court may,
prior to any election, authorize the use of additional voting ma
chines in any particular precinct. Such machines may be pur
chased or leased at any time, but no order directing the use of
voting machines in any precinct shall be made by the fiscal
court less than sixty days prior to the election at which the
use is to be made, nor unless the machines are available for use
and have been placed in the custody of the county clerk at the
time the order is made. The precincts in which voting machines
are to be used may be changed from time to time by order of
the fiscal court, subject to the same time limit set forth in the
preceding sentence.
Approved March 9, 1962

330 Acts of the General Assembly
CHAPTER 87
(S. B. 170)
AN ACT providing for the education of children who are deaf-blind,
authorizing expenditures of money and providing for the implemen
tation of a program for such education.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. As used in this Act, unless the context otherwise
requires:
(1) "Deaf-blind children" includes any child whose com
bination of handicaps of deafness and blindness prevents him
from profiting satisfactorily from educational programs provided
for the blind child or the deaf child.
Section 2. The State Department of Education is hereby
authorized to expend available funds for the purpose of sending
children who are deaf-blind to any facility, school, or institution,
within or without the Commonwealth, which provides a qualified
program of education for such children. Such funds may be
spent for evaluation and diagnosis, room, board, tuition, trans
portation, and other items which are necessarily relevant to the
education of such children.
Section 3. The Division of Special Education of the State
Department of Education is hereby authorized to determine if
deaf-blind children should be educated in a facility, school, or
institution, within or without the Commonwealth. The Division
of Special Education of the State Department of Education,
subject to the approval of the State Board of Education, shall
cooperate with existing agencies such as the Kentucky School
for the Deaf and the Kentucky School for the Blind, in the
coordination of their educational activities for such children.
Section 4. The State Department of Education is hereby
authorized to adopt and promulgate such rules and regulations
as it deems necessary and proper for carrying out the purposes
of this Act.
Section 5. This Act shall become effective on July 1, 1962.
Approved March 9, 1962

Chapter 88 331
CHAPTER 88
(S. B. 173)
AN ACT relating to elections.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 89.460 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
(1) At all elections held under the city manager form of
government the ballots shall be on a different sheet from all other
ballots to be used in the election and shall be in the form therein
prescribed, except the words "City Ballot" shall be at the head
thereof. Any public questions submitted by the commission to
the electorate shall be printed on the city ballot. The names of
all candidates for each office shall be printed on the ballot in a
single column. The order in which the names of the candidates
for each office are to be printed on the ballot shall be determined
by lot. [The names of the candidates for each office shall be
printed on the first fifty ballots in alphabetical order, and on each
of the succeeding fifty ballots the names shall be printed in the
same order except that the first name on the preceding fifty
ballots shall be shifted to the last place on the next succeeding
fifty ballots, and the last name so shifted from the first place on
the preceding fifty ballots shall be succeeded by the second name.
A like change or rotation shall be made throughout in the printed
order of names for every fifty ballots] .
(2) The sheriff of the county shall provide for each per-
cinct in the city a separate box for the reception of ballots used
in the election of the municipal officers. The election officers
in each precinct shall issue the ballots provided for by this section
in the same manner as other ballots are issued at the election, and
observe as to these ballots such regulations for the issuing and
deposit of ballots as may be prescribed for elections generally.
( 3 ) At the opening of the polls each judge shall appoint an
inspector and challenger, whose duty it shall be to act as such
in the precinct in which he is appointed, and in so acting the
inspector and challenger shall be governed by the provisions of
the general election law with reference to inspectors and chal
lengers.

332 Acts of the General Assembly
(4) No election officer or other person within the election
booth shall tell or indicate to a voter, by word of mouth or other
wise, the political affiliation of any candidate.
(5) The provisions of the general election laws as to the
duties of the county clerk and other public officers in the matter
of printing and distributing ballots, issuing them to the voters,
receiving and depositing them in the ballot boxes, and counting
and preserving them, and in all other particulars except as other
wise provided in this section, shall be applicable in all respects.
Section 2. Section 118.175 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
When any city so provides by ordinance, the ballots used
at any election in which city officers are to be elected shall con
tain, in a single vertical column, the names of candidates for such
city officers grouped according to the offices they seek, and such
candidates shall be immediately arranged under and beneath
the title of office such candidates seek. The order in which the
names of the candidates for each office are to be printed on the
ballot shall be determined by lot. [The names of the candidates
for such office in each group shall be listed in rotating order,
each candidate to have his name first in order on an equal number
of ballots, as determined by the clerk of the county court from
the ratio of the number of candidates to the total number of bal
lots to be printed.] Each group of candidates for each separate
office for which such candidates are to be elected shall be sep
arated from the preceding or succeeding groups by a horizontal
line transversing the entire ballot and spaced so as to avoid
confusion on the part of the voter.
Section 3. Section 160.230 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
The ballot shall be in the form prescribed for ballots by
the general election law, except that no party emblem or dis
tinguishing mark shall be placed upon the ballot, save the words
"School Ballot" at the head thereof. The order in which the
names of the candidates are to be printed on the ballot shall be
determined by lot. [The name of all candidates for member
ship on the board of education shall be printed on the first fifty

Chapter 89 333
ballots in a single column in the order the petitions are filed. On
each of the succeeding fifty ballots the names shall be printed in
the same order, except that the last name on the preceding fifty
ballots shall be shifted to the first place, and so on thereafter
throughout, a like change being made in the printed order of
names for every fifty ballots. The ballots shall be so bound that
in the book of ballots for each voting precinct each candidate's
name will appear first on approximately the same number of
ballots as that of every other candidate.] As many additional
lines shall be left blank as there are members to be elected from
the district or division as the case may be.
Approved March 9, 1962
CHAPTER 89
(H. B. 117)
AN ACT relating to the Department of Public Safety creating a Board
of Boiler Rules.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. This Act shall be known and may be cited as
the Boiler Safety Act, and, except as otherwise provided herein,
shall apply to all boilers.
As used in this Act, ( 1 ) "Boiler" or "boilers" shall mean and
include steam or vapor boilers, hot water heating boilers and hot
water supply boilers.
(2) "Commissioner" means the Commissioner of Public
Safety.
( 3 ) "Department" means the Department of Public Safety.
(4) "ASME" means American Society of Mechanical Engi
neers.
Section 2. ( 1 ) In the Department of Public Safety there
shall be a Board of Boiler Rules, which shall hereafter be referred

334 Acts of the General Assembly
to as the Board, consisting of five members including the Com
missioner who shall serve as chairman. The other members
shall be appointed to the Board by the Governor; one for a term
of one year, one for a term of two years, one for a term of three
years, and one for a term of four years, or until their successors
are appointed and qualified. At the expiration of their
respective terms of office, they, or their successors identifi
able with the same interest respectively as hereinafter provided,
shall be appointed for terms of four years each. The Governor
may at any time remove any member of the Board. Upon the death
or incapacity of any member the Governor shall fill the vacancy for
the remainder of the vacated term with a representative of the
same interests with which his predecessor was identified. Of these
four appointed members, one shall be a practical steam operating
engineer of high pressure boilers, or any other representative of
owners and users of high pressure boilers within the State, one
shall be a representative of the boiler manufacturers within the
state, one shall be a representative of a boiler insurance company
licensed to do business within the state, and one shall be a repre
sentative of the boilermakers within the state. The Board shall
meet at least four times each year at the Capitol or other place
designated by the Board. No approval, decision or ruling of the
Board shall be effective unless supported by the vote of at least
three members thereof.
(2) The members of the Board shall serve without salary
and shall receive their actual necessary expenses, incurred while
in the performance of their duties as members of the Board, to
be paid in the same manner as in the case of other state officers.
Section 3. After reasonable notice and opportunity to be
heard, the Commissioner of Public Safety, upon advisement by the
Board, shall, by general order, rule or regulation, fix reasonable
standards for the safe construction, installation, inspection
and repair of boilers in this state. Orders, rules and regulations
prescribed by the Board shall be enforced by the Department of
Public Safety.
Section 4. No boiler which does not conform to the rules
and regulations formulated by the Commissioner governing new
construction and installation shall be installed and operated in this

Chapter 89 335
state after twelve months from the date upon which the first
rules and regulations under this Act pertaining to new construc
tion and installation shall have become effective.
Section 5. ( 1 ) The maximum allowable working pressure
of a boiler carrying the ASME Code symbol shall be determined
by the applicable sections of the code under which it was con
structed and stamped.
(2) The maximum allowable working pressure of a boiler
which does not carry the ASME Code symbol shall be computed
in accordance with the ASME "Suggested Rules Governing Ex
isting Installations."
( 3 ) This Act shall not be construed as in any way prevent
ing the use or sale of a boiler referred to in this section provided
it has been made to conform to the rules and regulations of the
Commissioner governing existing installations, and provided,
further, it has not been found upon inspection to be in an
unsafe condition.
Section 6. (1) This Act shall not apply to boilers under
Federal control.
(2) This Act shall not apply to the following boilers:
(a) Boilers located on farms and used solely for agri
cultural purposes;
( b ) Boilers located at any oil refineries;
(c) Steam or vapor boilers used for heating purposes
carrying a pressure of not more than 15 pounds per square
inch gauge, and which are located in private residences or in
apartment houses of less than six families;
(d) Hot water heating boilers carrying a pressure of
not more than 30 pounds per square inch gauge, or hot water
supply boilers which are located in private residences or in
apartment houses of less than six families;
( e ) Any unfired pressure vessels used as containers for
liquefied petroleum gases and subject to the jurisdiction of
the Department of Public Safety under Kentucky Revised
Statutes, Chapter 234.

336 Acts of the General Assembly
Section 7. The Department shall employ Boiler Inspectors
who shall have had at the time of appointment not less than five
years practical experience in the construction, maintenance, re
pair or operation of high pressure boilers and unfired pressure
vessels as a mechanical engineer, practical steam operating engi
neer, boilermaker, or boiler inspector, and who shall have passed
the examination provided for in Section 9 of this Act.
Section 8. (1) In addition to the Boiler Inspectors
authorized by Section 7 of this Act, the Department shall
upon the request of any company authorized to insure
against loss from explosion of boilers in this state, issue to any
boiler inspectors of said company commissions as Special Boiler
Inspectors, provided that each such Special Boiler Inspector be
fore receiving such commission, shall satisfactorily pass the ex
amination provided for in Section 9 of this Act, or, in lieu of
such examination, shall hold a Commission of Competency as
an inspector of boilers for a state that has a standard of exami
nation substantially equal to that of this Commonwealth or a
Commission as an inspector of boilers issued by the National
Board of Boiler and Pressure Vessel Inspectors.
(2) Such Special Boiler Inspectors shall receive no salary
from, nor shall any of their expenses be paid by, the state and
the continuance of a Special Boiler Inspector's Commission shall
be conditioned upon his continuing in the employ of a boiler
insurance company duly authorized as aforesaid and upon his
maintenance of the standards imposed by this Act.
(3) Such Special Boiler Inspectors shall inspect all boilers
insured by their respective companies, and, when so inspected,
the owners and users of such insured boilers shall be exempt
from the payment to the State of the inspection fees as provided
for in Sections 12 and 14 of this Act.
(4) Each company employing such Special Boiler Inspec
tors shall within thirty days following each internal boiler in
spection made by such inspectors, file a report of such inspection
with the Department upon appropriate forms prescribed by the
Department. Reports of external inspections shall not be required
except when such inspections disclose that the boiler is in a
dangerous condition.

Chapter 89 337
(5) Inspectors, whether employees of the Department or
Special Inspectors, shall have free access, during reasonable hours,
to any premises in the state where a boiler is being constructed
or is being installed, for the purpose of ascertaining whether
such boiler is constructed and installed in accordance with the
law, and any orders, rules or regulations in existence at that time.
Section 9. Examination for Boiler Inspectors or Special
Boiler Inspectors shall be in writing and shall be given by the
Department of Personnel. The record of an applicant's exami
nation shall be accessible to said applicant and his employer.
Section 10. (1) Any Boiler Inspector's appointment or
commission may be suspended by the Department, after due
investigation, for the incompetence or untrustworthiness of the
holder thereof for willful falsification of any matter or statement
contained in his application or in a report of any inspection made
by him. Written notice of any such suspension shall be given
by the Department, to the inspector, and in the case of Special
Boiler Inspector, his employer.
(2) A person whose appointment or commission has been
suspended shall be entitled to apply, after ninety days from the
date of such suspension for reinstatement of such appointment
or commission.
Section 11. (1) On and after July 1, 1962, each boiler
used or proposed to be used within this state, except boilers ex
empt under Section 6 of this Act, shall be thoroughly inspected
as to their construction, installation and condition as follows:
(a) Power boilers, meaning boilers operating at steam or
vapor pressures in excess of 15 pounds per square inch gauge,
shall be inspected annually both internally and externally while
not under pressure and shall also be inspected annually ex
ternally while under pressure if possible;
(b ) Low pressure steam or vapor heating boilers, hot water
heating boilers and hot water supply boilers shall be inspected
both internally and externally biennially where construction will
permit;

338 Acts of the General Assembly
(c) A grace period of two months beyond the periods
specified in subdivisions (a) and (b) of this section may elapse
between inspections;
(d) The Department may at its discretion permit longer
periods between inspections.
(2) The inspections herein required shall be made by a
Boiler Inspector or by a Special Boiler Inspector provided for in
this Act.
( 3 ) If at any time a hydrostatic test shall be deemed neces
sary, same shall be made, at the discretion of the inspector, by
the owner or user thereof.
(4) All boilers to be installed in this state after the twelve
months period from the date upon which the rules and regula
tions of the Board shall become effective, shall be inspected
during construction as required by the applicable rules and reg
ulations of the Department by a Boiler Inspector authorized to
inspect boilers in this state, or, if constructed outside of the
state, by an inspector holding a Certificate of Competency as an
inspector of boilers for a state that has a standard of examination
substantially equal to that of this state as provided in Section 9.
Section 12. (1)
If,upon inspection,
a boiler
isfound to
comply with the rules and regulations of the Board, the owner
or user thereof shall pay to the Department the sum
of two dollars ($2.00) and the Boiler Inspector, or his duly au
thorized representative, shall issue to such owner or user an
Inspection Certificate bearing the date of inspection and specify
ing the maximum pressure under which the boiler may be
operated. Such Inspection Certificate shall be valid for not more
than fourteen months from its date in the case of power boilers,
and twenty-six months in the case of low pressure steam or vapor
heating boilers, hot water heating boilers or hot water supply
boilers. Certificates shall be posted under glass in the room
containing the boiler inspected or, in the case of
aportable boiler,
shall be kept in
ametal container, either fastened to the boiler
or kept in
atool box accompanying the boiler.
(2) No Inspection Certificate issued for an insured boiler
inspected
by
aSpecial Boiler Inspector shall be valid after the

Chapter 89 339
boiler for which it was issued shall cease to be insured by a
company duly authorized by this state to carry such insurance.
(3) The Commissioner or his authorized representative
may at any time suspend an Inspection Certificate when, in his
opinion, the boiler for which it was issued, cannot be operated
without menace to the public safetv, or when the boiler is found
not to comply with the rules and regulations herein provided.
A Special Boiler Inspector shall have corresponding powers with
respect to Inspection Certificates for boilers insured by the com
pany employing him. Such suspension of an Inspection Certifi
cate shall continue in effect until such boiler shall have been
made to conform to the rules and regulations of the Board, and
until said Inspection Certificate shall have been reinstated.
Section 13. After twelve months following the date on
which this Act becomes effective, it shall be unlawful for any
person, firm, partnership or corporation to operate in this state
a boiler under pressure without a valid Inspection Certificate.
The operation of a boiler without such Inspection Certificate,
or at a pressure exceeding that specified in such Inspection Cer
tificate, shall constitute a misdemeanor on the part of the owner,
user or operator thereof and shall be punishable by a fine not
exceeding five hundred dollars, or imprisonment not to exceed
three months, or both, at the discretion of the court. Each day
of such unlawful operation shall be deemed a separate offense.
Section 14. (1) The owner or user of a boiler required
by this Act to be inspected shall pay to the Department, upon
completion of inspection, fees in accordance with the following
schedule:
(a) Power Boilers:
Internal Inspections
Boilers of less than 50 sq. ft. of heating surface $ 5.00
Boilers over 50 sq. ft. of heating surface and
less than 4,000 sq. ft. of heating surface 12.00
Boilers over 4,000 sq. ft. of heating surface and
less than 10,000 sq. ft. of heating surface 15.00
Boilers over 10,000 sq. ft. of heating surface 20.00

External Inspections
Boilers of less than 50 sq. ft. of heating surface 3.00
Boilers over 50 sq. ft. of heating surface 5.00
Not more than the equivalent of the internal and
external inspection fees shall be charged or collected for
any and all inspections as above of anv boiler in any
one year.
(b) Low Pressure Boilers:
Inspection of heating boilers without a manhole _$ 5.00
Inspection of heating boilers with a manhole 10.00
Inspection of hot water supply boilers 3.00
Not more than one fee shall be charged or col
lected for any and all inspections as above of any low
pressure boiler in any two year period.
(c) Hydrostatic Tests:
When it is necessary to make a special trip to wit
ness the application of a hydrostatic test, an additional
fee based on the scale of fees applicable to an internal
inspection of the boiler shall be charged.
(d) All other inspections, including shop inspections and
inspection of second-hand or used boilers made by the
Boiler Inspector shall be charged for at the rate set by
regulation rule or order promulgated by the Commis
sioner upon advice of the Board.
(2) All fees received bv the Department shall be held in
a revolving fund from which the expenses of administering this
Act may be paid.
Section 15. The Boiler Inspectors shall furnish a bond in
the sum of five thousand dollars ($5,000.00) each, conditioned
upon the faithful performance of their duties and upon a true
account of moneys handled bv them respectively and the pay
ment thereof to the proper recipient. The cost of said bonds
shall be paid by the Department.

Chapter 90 341
Section 16. Any person aggrieved by an order or act of
a Boiler Inspector, under this Act, may, within fifteen days of
notice thereof, appeal from such order or act to the Commis
sioner who shall, within thirty days thereafter, hold a hearing,
after having given at least ten days written notice to all interested
parties. The Commissioner shall, within thirty days after such
hearing, issue an appropriate order either approving or disap
proving said order or act. A copy of such order by the Board
shall be given to all interested parties.
Within thirty days after any order or act of the Board, any
person aggrieved thereby may file a petition in the Franklin
Circuit Court for a review thereof. The court shall summarily
hear the petition and may make any appropriate order or decree.
Approved March 9, 1962
CHAPTER 90
(H. B. 152)
AN ACT relating to offenses against persons.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. Any person who causes the death of a human
being by an act creating such extreme risk of death or great
bodily injury as to manifest a wanton indifference to the value of
human life according to the standard of conduct of a reasonable
man under the circumstances shall be guilty of involuntary man
slaughter in the first degree and shall be confined in the peniten
tiary for not less than one nor more than fifteen vears.
Section 2. Any person who causes the death of a human
being by reckless conduct according to the standard of conduct
of a reasonable man under the circumstances shall be guilty of
involuntary manslaughter in the second degree and shall be
imprisoned in the county jail for a term not exceeding twelve
months or fined a sum not exceeding five thousand dollars or both.
Approved March 9, 1962

342 Acts of the General Assembly
CHAPTER 91
(H. B. 185)
AN ACT relating to motor vehicles.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 189.070 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows: (1) No person shall operate
any motor vehicle upon a highway outside of a business or resi
dence district at any time from a half-hour before sunset to a
half-hour before sunrise unless that vehicle carries the number
and type of flares, electric lanterns, or other signals which the
Department of Motor Transportation shall by regulation require.
(2) Whenever any motor truck and its lighting equipment
are disabled during a period when lighted lamps must be illumi
nated on vehicles and the truck cannot immediately be removed
from the main traveled portion of a highway outside of a business
or residence district, the person in charge of the vehicle shall
cause to be placed upon the highway such flares, lanterns and
other signals as the Department of Motor Transportation shall by
regulation require, and such flares, lanterns or other signals shall
be placed at such distances and in such positions as required by
such regulations.
Section 2. Section 189.221 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
No person shall operate on any highway, except such high
ways as may be designated by the Commissioner of Highways
under the provisions of KRS 189.222, any of the following trucks,
trailers or vehicles:
(1) Any motor truck, [or] semitrailer, [truck] trailer or ve
hicle which exceeds 11& feet in height or 96 inches in width,
including any part of the body or load;
(2) Any motor truck, except a semitrailer truck, which ex
ceeds 26M feet in length, including any part of the body or load;
(3) Any semitrailer truck which exceeds 30 feet in length,
including any part of the body or load;

Chapter 91 343
(4) A [any] truck, [or] semitrailer truck or truck and trailer
unit which exceeds 18,000 pounds gross weight, including the
load; [or 600 pounds per inch of the combined width of the tires
upon which such vehicle may be propelled]
(5) Any truck, semitrailer truck or tractor-trailer unit which
exceeds a gross weight equal to the sum of 600 pounds per inch
of the combined width of the tires upon which such vehicle may
be propelled, but in no event more than 18,000 pounds.
Section 3. Section 189.730 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
( 1 ) The commissioner, uniformed officers and troopers of
the Department of Public Safety and Department of Motor
Transportation and such other officers and employees of the
department as the commissioner may designate, may, at any
time, upon reasonable cause to believe that a motor vehicle
is unsafe or not equipped as required by law, or that its equip
ment is not in proper adjustment or repair, require the driver
of such motor vehicle to stop and submit such vehicle to an
inspection and such test with reference thereto as may be
appropriate.
(2) In the event such vehicle is found to be in unsafe con
dition or any required part or equipment is not present or is not
in proper repair and adjustment, the officer shall give a written
notice to the driver and shall send one copy to the Department
of Public Safety. Said notice shall require that such motor vehicle
be placed in safe condition and its equipment in proper repair
and adjustment within a period of forty-eight hours or else remove
said motor vehicle from the public highways and not operate the
same on the highways until said motor vehicle is repaired and
placed in a safe condition.
(3) In the event the use of such vehicle in its present con
dition would, in the reasonable judgment of the arresting officer,
endanger the life of any member of the public, the officer shall
require the vehicle to be parked, at the owners expense, and not
operated until it is placed in a safe condition.
Approved March 9, 1962

344 Acts of the General Assembly
CHAPTER 92
(H. B. 224)
AN ACT relating to revenue and taxation.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 138.130 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
As used in this Act, unless the context requires otherwise:
(1) "Department" means the Department of Revenue.
(2) "Manufacturer" means any person who manufactures
or produces cigarettes within or without this state.
[(2) "Manufacturer" means any person engaged in the
manufacture or production of cigarettes.]
( 3 ) "Retailer" means any person who sells to a consumer or
to any person for any purpose other than resale.
(4) "Sale at Retail" shall mean a sale to any person for any
other purpose other than resale.
(5) "Cigarettes" shall mean and include any roll for smok
ing made wholly or in part of tobacco, or any substitute for to
bacco, irrespective of size or shape and whether or not such to
bacco is flavored, adulterated or mixed with any other ingredient,
the wrapper or cover of which is made of paper or any other sub
stance or material, excepting tobacco.
(6) "Sale" or "sell" shall mean any transfer for a considera
tion, exchange, barter, gift, offer for sale, advertising for sale,
soliciting an order for cigarettes, and distribution in any manner
or by any means whatsoever.
[(5) "Sale" includes, in addition to its ordinary meaning,
the possession of cigarettes for consumption, irrespective of how
acquired. Possession of cigarettes by any person other than a
manufacturer, wholesaler, or retailer, as defined in this section,
shall be prima facie evidence of possession for consumption.]
(7) "Tax Evidence" shall mean and include any stamps,
metered impressions, or other indicia prescribed by the Depart

Chapteb 92 345
ment by regulation as a means of denoting the payment of tax.
(8) "Person" shall mean and include any individual, firm,
co-partnership, pint venture, association, municipal or private cor
poration whether organized for profit or not, Commonwealth of
Kentucky or any of its political subdivisions, estate, trust, or any
other group or combination acting as a unit, and the plural as
well as the singular.
( 9 ) "Resident Wholesaler" shall mean any person who pur
chases at least seventy-five percent of all cigarettes purchased by
him directly from the cigarette manufacturer on which the cig
arette tax provided for herein is unpaid, and who maintains an
established place of business in this state where he attaches cig
arette tax evidence, or receives untaxed cigarettes.
(10) "Nonresident Wholesaler" shall mean any person who
purchases cigarettes directly from the manufacturer and main
tains a permanent location or locations outside this state where
Kentucky cigarette tax evidence is attached or from where Ken
tucky cigarette tax is reported and paid.
[(4) "Wholesaler" means any person who sells cigarettes
at wholesale or distributes cigarettes to be sold at retail, and shall
include any manufacturer, distributor, jobber, broker, agent, or
other person, whether or not enumerated in this section, who so
sells or so distributes cigarettes.]
(11) "Sub-Jobber" shall mean any person who purchases
cigarettes from a wholesaler licensed under this Act on which the
Kentucky cigarette tax has been paid and makes them available
to retailers for resale.
( 12 ) "Vending Machine Operator" shall mean any person
ivho operates one or more cigarette vending machines.
( 13 ) "Transporter" shall mean any person transporting cig
arettes obtained from any source to any destination tvithin this
state, other than cigarettes transported by resident wholesalers,
nonresident wholesalers, sub-jobbers, vending machine operators
and unclassified acquirers licensed under the provisions of this
Act provided that it shall not include any person transporting
no more than 1,000 cigarettes at one time.

346 Acts of the General Assembly
(14) "Unclassified Acquirer" shall mean any person in this
state who acquires cigarettes from any source on which the Ken
tucky cigarette tax has not been paid, and who is not a person
otherwise licensed under the provisions of this Act.
Section 2. Section 138.140 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
(1) A tax shall be paid on the sale of cigarettes [made of
tobacco, or any substitute for tobacco,] within the state at [the]
a proportionate rate of two and one-half cents on each twenty
cigarettes [or fraction thereof] . This tax shall be paid only once,
regardless of the number of times the cigarettes may be sold in
this state.
(2) The burden of paying such tax shall be passed, through
subsequent sales of the cigarettes taxed, to the ultimate purchasers
and consumers of such sigarettes, and such tax so passed shall be
deemed a direct tax paid by the said ultimate purchasers and
consumers.
[ ( 2 ) This tax shall be paid by affixing stamps in the man
ner and at the time set forth in this section and in KRS 138.150
to 138.200. The stamps shall be affixed to each individual pack
age of cigarettes by the wholesaler within seventy-two hours after
the cigarettes are received by him, or if received by a retailer they
shall be affixed within twenty-four hours after the cigarettes are
received by him. All cigarettes must be tax paid before being
sold or otherwise distributed in this state.]
[(3) The manner and methods of affixing stamps shall be
prescribed by the Department of Revenue in such a way as to
enforce the provisions of KRS 138.130 to 138.200.]
Section 3. Sections 138.145, 138.150, 138.160, 138.170,
138.180, 138.190, 138.200, 137.040, 137.050, 137.070, 137.080,
137.090, 137.100 and 137.110 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
are repealed in their entirety.
Section 4. A new section of Kentucky Revised Statutes is
created to read as follows:
(1) No person other than a manufacturer shall acquire
cigarettes in this state on which the Kentucky cigarette tax has

Chapter 92 Ml
not been paid, nor act as a resident wholesaler, nonresident whole
saler, vending machine operator, sub-jobber, transporter, unclassi
fied acquirer of such cigarettes without first obtaining a license
from the Department as hereinafter set out.
(2) Each resident wholesaler shall secure a separate license
for each place of business at which cigarette tax evidence is af
fixed or at which cigarettes on which the Kentucky cigarette tax
has not been paid are received. Each nonresident wholesaler
shall secure a separate license for each place of business at which
evidence of Kentucky cigarette tax is affixed or from where Ken
tucky cigarette tax is reported and paid. Such a license or licenses
shall be secured on or before July 1 of each year, and each licensee
shall pat/ the sum of twenty-five dollars for each such year or
portion thereof for which such license is secured.
( 3 ) Each sub-jobber shall secure a separate license for each
place of business from which Kentucky tax-paid cigarettes are
made available to retailers, whether such place of business is lo
cated within or without this state. Such license or licenses shall
be secured on or before July 1 of each year, and each licensee
shall pay the sum of twenty-five dollars for each such year or
portion thereof for which such license is secured.
(4) Each vending machine operator shall secure a license
for the privilege of dispensing Kentucky tax-paid cigarettes by
vending machines. Such license shall be secured on or before
July 1 of each year, and each licensee shall pay the sum of five
dollars for each year or portion thereof for which such license is
secured. No vending machine shall be operated within this Com
monwealth without having prominently affixed thereto the name
of its operator, together with the license number assigned to such
operator by the Department. The Department shall prescribe by
regulation the manner in which the information shall be affixed
to the vending machine.
( 5 ) Each transporter shall secure a license for the privilege
of transporting cigarettes within this state. Such license shall be
secured on or before July 1 of each year, and each licensee shall
pay the sum of five dollars for each such year or portion thereof
for which such license is secured. No transporter shall transport
any cigarettes without having in actual possession an invoice or

348 Acts of the General Assembly
bill of lading therefor, showing the name and address of the con
signor and consignee, the date acquired by the transporter, the
name and address of the transporter, the quantity of cigarettes
being transported, together with the license number assigned to
such transporter by the Department.
(6) Each unclassified acquirer shall secure a license for
the privilege of acquiring cigarettes on which the Kentucky cig
arette tax has not been paid. Such license shall be secured on or
before July 1 of each year, and each licensee shall pay the sum of
five dollars for each such year or portion thereof for which such
license is secured.
(7) Nothing in this Act shall be construed to prevent the
Department from requiring a person to purchase more than one
license if the nature of such person's business is so diversified
as to justify such requirement.
(8) The Department may by regualtion require am/ person
licensed under the provisions of this Act to supply such infor
mation concerning his business, sales, or any privilege exercised,
as is deemed reasonably necessary for the regulation of such li
censees, and to protect the revenues of the state. Failure on the
part of such licensee to comply with the provisions of this Act,
or any regulations promulgated hereunder, or to permit an in
spection of premises, machines or vehicles by an authorized agent
of the Department at any reasonable time shall be grounds for
the revocation of any license issued by the Department, after due
notice and a hearing by the Department. The Commissioner of
Revenue may assign a time and place for such hearing and may
appoint a conferee who shall conduct a hearing, receive evidence
and hear arguments. Such conferee shall thereupon file a report
with the Commissioner together with a recommendation as to
the revocation of such license. In the event the Commissioner
directs a revocation of such license upon such report, an appeal
may be had in accordance with Kentucky Revised Statutes, Chap
ter 131, Section 110 and Kentucky Revised Statutes, Chapter 131,
Section 120. Any person whose license has been revoked for the
wilful violation of any provision of this Act shall not be entitled to
any license provided for in this Act, or have any interest in any
such license, either disclosed or undisclosed, either as an indi

Chapter 92 349
vidual, partnership, corporation or otherwise, for a period of one
year after such revocation.
(9) No license issued pursuant to the provisions of this
Act shall be transferable or negotiable.
( 10 ) Every manufacturer located or doing business in this
state shall keep written records of all shipments of cigarettes to
persons within this state, and shall submit reports of such ship
ments as the Department may require by regulation.
(11) No person licensed under this Act except nonresident
wholesalers shall either sell to or purchase from any other such
licensee untaxpaid cigarettes.
Section 5. A new section of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is
created to read as follows:
( 1 ) The cigarette tax imposed by this Act shall be due when
any licensed wholesaler or unclassified acquirer takes possession
within this state of untaxpaid cigarettes.
(2) The tax shall be paid by affixing evidence of tax pay
ment in the manner and at the time set forth in this section. The
evidence of tax payment shall be affixed to each individual pack
age of cigarettes by a resident wholesaler within seventy-two
hours after the cigarettes are received by him. The evidence of
tax payment shall be affixed to each individual package of cigar
ettes by a nonresident wholesaler prior to the introduction or
importation of the cigarettes into the territorial limits of this state.
The evidence of tax payment shall be affixed by an unclassified
acquirer within twenty-four hours after the cigarettes are re
ceived by him.
(3) The Department shall by regulation prescribe the form
of cigarette tax evidence, the method and manner of the sale and
distribution of such cigarette tax evidence, and the method and
manner that such evidence shall be affixed to the cigarettes.
(4) Units of cigarette tax evidence shall be sold at their
face value, but the Department shall allow as compensation to
any licensed wholesaler an amount of tax evidence equal to
eighteen cents face value for each three dollars of tax evidence
purchased at face value. The Department shall have the power

350 Acts of the General Assembly
to withhold compensation from any licensed wholesaler for failure
to abide by any provisions of this Act or any regulations promul
gated thereunder. Any refund or credit for unused cigarette tax
evidence shall be reduced by the amount allowed as compensation
at the time of purchase.
(5) No tax evidence may be affixed, or used in any way,
by any person other than the person purchasing such evidence
from the Department. Such tax evidence may not be transferred
or negotiated, and may not, by any scheme or device, be given,
bartered, sold, traded, or loaned to any other person. Unaffixed
tax evidence may be returned to the Department for credit or
refund for any reason satisfactory to the Department.
(6) In the event any retailer shall receive into his possession
cigarettes to which evidence of Kentucky tax paijment is not
properly affixed, he shall within twenty-four hours notify the De
partment of such fact. Such notice shall be in writing, and shall
give the name of the person from whom such cigarettes were re
ceived, and the quantity of such cigarettes, and such written notice
may be given to any field agent of the Department. The written
notice may also be directed to the Commissioner of Revenue,
Frankfort, Kentucky. If such notice is given by means of the
United States mail, it shall be sent by certified mail. Any such
cigarettes shall be retained by such retailer, and not sold, for a
period of fifteen days after giving the notice provided herein. The
retailer may, at his option, pay the tax due on any such cigarettes
according to rules and regulations to be prescribed by the De
partment, and proceed to sell the same after such payment.
(7) Cigarettes stamped with the cigarette tax evidence of
another state shall at no time be commingled with cigarettes on
which the Kentucky cigarette tax evidence has been affixed, but
any licensed wholesaler, licensed sub-jobber or licensed vending
machine operator may hold cigarettes stamped with the tax evi
dence of another state for any period of time, subsection two of
this section notwithstanding.
Section 6. A new section of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is created to read as follows:
In lieu of the affixing of cigarette tax evidence to individual
packages of cigarettes as the means of denoting payment of the

Chapter 92 351
cigarette tax imposed by this Act, the Department may prescribe,
by rules and regulations sufficient to protect the revenue of this
state, a method of reporting, payment and collection of such tax,
without the affixing of tax evidence to individual packages of
cigarettes. In the event such a system is adopted no compensation
for reporting for the purpose of such tax in excess of two percent
of the lax due shall be allowed to any person.
Section 7. A new section of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is created to read as follows:
(1) It is declared to be the legislative intent of this Act
that any untaxpaid cigarettes held, owned, possessed, or in con
trol of any person other than as provided in this Act are con
traband and subject to seizure and forfeiture as hereinafter set
out.
( 2 ) Whenever any peace officer of this state, or any repre
sentative of the Department, finds any untaxpaid cigarettes with
in the borders of this state in the possession of any person other
than a licensee authorized to possess untaxpaid cigarettes by the
provisions of this Act, such cigarettes shall be immediately seized
and stored in a depository to be selected by such officer or agent.
At the time of such seizure, the officer or agent shall deliver to
the person in whose custody such cigarettes are found a receipt
for such cigarettes. The receipt shall state on its face that any
inquiry concerning any such goods seized shall be directed to
the Commissioner of Revenue, Frankfort, Kentucky. Immediately
upon such seizure, the officer or agent shall notify the Commis
sioner of Revenue of the nature and quantity of the goods seized.
Any such seized goods shall be held for a period of twenty days
and if after such period no person has claimed such cigarettes as
his property, the Commissioner shall cause the same to be exposed
to public sale to any person authorized to purchase untaxpaid
cigarettes. Such sale shall be on notice to be published in a
newspaper as defined in Ketnucky Revised Statutes, Chapter 424,
and at a time not less than seven nor more than fourteen days
after the date of such publication. All proceeds, less the cost of
sale, from any such sale shall be paid into the Kentucky State
Treasury for general fund purposes.
( 3 ) It is declared to be the legislative intent that any vend

352 Acts of the General Assembly
ing machine used for dispensing cigarettes on which Kentucky
cigarette tax has not been paid is contraband and subject to seiz
ure and forfeiture. In the event any peace officer or agent of the
Department finds any vending machine within the borders of
this state dispensing untaxpaid cigarettes, he shall immediately
seize any such vending machine and store the same in a safe
place to be selected by him. He shall thereafter proceed as pro
vided in subsection two of this section and the Commissioner of
Revenue shall cause any such vending machine to be sold, and
the proceeds applied, as set out in subsection two of this section.
(4) No cigarettes, on which the tax imposed by this Act
has not been paid, shall be transported within this state by any
person other than a manufacturer or a person licensed under the
provisions of this Act. It is declared to be the legislative intent
that any motor vehicle used to transport any such cigarettes by
other persons is contraband and subject to seizure and forfeiture.
In the event any peace officer or agent of the Department finds
any such motor vehicle, he shall immediately seize such motor
vehicle and store it in a safe place to be specified by him. He shall
thereafter proceed as provided in subsection two of this section
and the Commissioner of Revenue shall cause any such motor
vehicle to be sold, and the proceeds applied, as set out in sub
section two of this section.
(5) The owner or any person having an interest in any
goods, machines or vehicles seized as provided under subsections
one, two, three and four of this section may apply to the Com
missioner of Revenue for remission of the forfeiture for good cause
shown. If it is shown to the satisfaction of the Commissioner of
Revenue that the owner was without fault in the possession, dis
pensing or transportation of such untaxpaid cigarettes, he shall
remit the forfeiture. In the event he determines that such posses
sion, dispensing or transportation of untaxpaid cigarettes was wil
ful or intentional he may nevertheless remit the forfeiture on con
dition that the owner pay a penalty to be prescribed by him of
not more than fifty percent of the value of the thing forfeited.
All taxes due on untaxpaid cigarettes shall be paid in addition to
such penalty, if any.
( 6 ) An appeal shall be taken from any order entered by the
Commissioner of Revenue in any forfeiture provided for by this

Chapter 92 353
section, which appeal shall be prosecuted as provided by Ken
tucky Revised Statutes, Chapter 131, Section 110, and Kentucky
Revised Statutes, Chapter 131, Section 120.
(7) Any licensee under this Act who violates any provision
of this Act, or any regulation promulgated hereunder, shall be
come indebted to the Commonwealth in the sum of five hundred
dollars for each violation. Such civil penalty may be collected
by action in the Franklin Circuit Court.
(8) Any manufacturer who fails to keep written records,
and submit reports thereof to the department, as required by the
provisions of subsection ten of section four of this Act, shall be
come indebted to the Commonwealth in the sum of one thousand
dollars for each violation. Such penalty may be enforced by
action of the Franklin Circuit Court.
(9) Any manufacturer doing business within this state
without having complied with the provisions of Kentucky Re
vised Statutes, Chapter 271, Section 385 as to designation of
process agent shall, by so doing of business, be deemed to have
made the Secretary of State its agent for the service of process in
any civil action instituted in the Franklin Circuit Court for the
recovery of the penalty provided herein. In any such action, the
complaint shall set forth the post office address of the home office
of the manufacturer.
( 10) Any nonresident person licensed under the provisions
of this Act shall, at the time of application for such license, desig
nate some resident of this state as a process agent for the purpose
of service of civil process in any civil action originating in any
court of this Commonwealth, and service upon such person so
designated shall be sufficient to bring such nonresident person
before any court of this Commonwealth for all purposes.
(11) If any person becomes delinquent in the payment of
any tax due under the provisions of this Act, he shall, in addition
to any such tax due, pay a penalty equivalent to twenty percent
of the tax due.
(12) If any person shall act in the capacity of a licensee
under the provisions of this Act without having secured a license
as provided herein, he shall, in addition to the payment of the fee
for such a license, pay a penalty equivalent to twenty percent of
such fee.

354 Acts of the General Assembly
Section 8. No person shall sell or offer to sell to any other

CHAFrER 93
(H. B. 228)
AN ACT relating to revenue and taxation.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 140.160 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
( 1 ) The Department of Revenue shall have full supervision
of the collection of all taxes due under the provisions of this
chapter, including the power to institute suit in this and other
states. It may employ attorneys and other persons necessary
to carry out the full intent and purpose of this chapter. The
department shall furnish, upon application, blank forms covering
such information as may be necessary to determine the amount
of tax due the state on the transfer of all property subject to tax.
(2) The department may cause personal representatives or
beneficiaries to file all statements required by this chapter with
the clerks of the proper courts and with the department, and
may require them to furnish any additional information deemed
necessary to support the computation of [determine] the amount
of tax that should be paid by the estate. The personal repre
sentative shall in every case, when he files his inventory in the
county court, file a duplicate thereof with the department.

360 Acts of the General Assembly
The personal representative shall compute the taxes imposed by
this chapter on the tax return provided by the department. Such
tax return shall be filed with the department within 18 months
after the death of the decedent or at the time payment of the
tax is made pursuant to KRS 140.210. [The department may
examine all statements filed by personal representatives and
beneficiaries.]
(3) Except as herein provided, no action to enforce the
collection of the tax imposed by this chapter shall be commenced
more than ten years after the cause of action first accrued. In
case the settlement of an estate is delayed because of litigation
or other unavoidable cause, as described in subsection (1) of
KRS 140.210, such delay shall suspend the limitation, prescribed
by this subsection, until the cause of delay is removed. In the
case of a fraudulent return or any other fraudulent representation
affecting the amount of or the liability for the tax imposed by
this chapter notwithstanding any provision of limitation provided
elsewhere, the tax due by reason thereof may at any time be
assessed and collected by the methods set out in this chapter,
including action in a court of competent jurisdiction.
Section 2. Section 140.170 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
(1) The county court, upon the request of the personal
representative or any interested party, shall appoint some com
petent person as appraiser of the estate. The appraiser shall give
notice to all persons having an interest in the estate and to such
other persons as the court may by order direct, and shall then
appraise the property belonging to the estate. His report shall
be filed with the county court and a copy thereof with the
Department of Revenue. He shall be paid for his services out
of the funds of the estate, on the certification of the county court,
the amount to be fixed bv that court. The total compensation
of the appraiser shall not exceed one-tenth of one percent of the
total appraised value of the estate for inheritance tax purposes,
but there shall be a minimum allowance of five dollars, together
with the appraiser's actual and necessary traveling expenses.
(2) After investigation, the department may change [shall
fix] the value of the estate for inheritance taxes and advise the

Chapter 93 361
representatives of the estate of this changed valuation after the
receipt of a completed tax return and full payment as shown by
the tax return.
(3) No appraiser shall accept any fee or reward from a
personal representative, trustee, legatee, next of kin or heir of
the decedent, or from any other person liable to pay the tax or
any portion thereof.
(4) No person shall willfully and knowingly subscribe to
or make any false statement of fact, or knowingly subscribe to or
exhibit any false paper or false report with intent to deceive any
appraiser.
(5) The department shall keep a record of all returns,
reports, and schedules attached thereto required by this chapter
for 12 years, [made by appraisers, the cash value of annuities,
life estates and terms of years, and the amount of all taxes as
sessed by it. The department may record all other information
which it deems desirable or proper.]
Section 3. Section 140.190 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
(1) All taxes imposed by this chapter shall be computed
and paid [assessed by the Department of Revenue] on the [full
and] fair cash value of the property transferred at the rates
provided. All personal representatives, trustees and beneficiaries
shall be personally liable for the taxes until they are paid, but
onlv to the extent that same come into their hands, and in no
case shall the personal representative or trustee be liable for a
greater sum than passes through his administration. [Notice of
the amount of the taxes shall be mailed to the personal repre
sentative, trustee or other person liable therefor by the depart
ment, and, upon request, to any other person by whom the taxes
are payable, but failure to receive this notice shall not excuse
nonpayment or invalidate the taxes.]
(2) The heir, devisee or other donee shall be personally
liable for the tax on real property, as well as the personal repre
sentative or trustee, and if the personal representative or trustee
pays the tax he may, unless the tax is made an expense of
administration bv the will or other instrument, recover the tax
from the heir, devisee or other donee of the real property.

362 Acts of the General Assembly
( 3 ) The taxes shall be and remain a lien upon the property
transferred and upon all property acquired by the personal repre
sentative or trustee, until the taxes are paid or a bond given
as provided in KRS 140.210. The lien may be enforced as other
liens on similar property are enforced. The lien shall not affect
any personal property after it has passed to a bona fide purchaser
for value, but the owner of securities may not have them trans
ferred to him by the issuing corporation until the permit required
by this chapter has been filed. The lien upon any real property
may be discharged by the payment of all taxes due and to become
due.
( 4 ) When a conveyance made by a decedent in his lifetime
is subject to tax, and the property thus conveyed is personal
property without the state or is removed from the state before
the tax is paid, the tax shall become a lien upon all property of
the decedent; the personal representative shall collect the taxes
due on account of the conveyance, and may be authorized to sell
any property subject to the hen for the payment thereof, as in
other cases.
Section 4. A new section of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is created to read as follows:
The department may make such audits, appraisals, and
examinations of records according to KRS 131.130 to properly
supervise the collection of all taxes due under the provisions of
Chapter 140 of Kentucky Revised Statutes. A completed tax
return with full payment attached shall be final one year after
receipt by the department unless an audit has been initiated with
due notice to the personal representative, except:
(a) If any assets of the estate were not reported on the tax
return filed with the department, or
(b) If any information was not revealed to the department
which would affect the amount of tax due.
Section 5. WHEREAS, it is necessary that the effective
date correspond to the beginning of a fiscal year for administra
tive feasibility and efficiency, this Act shall become effective
July 1, 1962.
Approved March 9, 1962

Chapter 94 363
CHAPTER 94
(H. B. 229)
AN ACT relating to revenue and taxation.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 136.070 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
( 1 ) With respect to taxable years beginning after Decem
ber 31, 1961, every corporation owning property or doing busi
ness in this state, except banks and trust companies, foreign in
surance companies, domestic life insurance companies, foreign
and domestic building and loan associations, and corporations
that, under existing laws, are liable to pay a franchise tax, shall
pay to the state an annual license tax based upon the value of
its capital stock, as provided by Section 4 hereof [in this section] .
(2) Every corporation not exempted by subsection (1) of
this section shall pay an annual license tax of seventy cents on
each one thousand dollars of that part of the value of its capital
stock represented by property owned and business transacted
in this state, which shall be ascertained by finding the proportion
that the property owned and business transacted in this state
bears to the aggregate amount of property owned and business
transacted in and out of this state, which factors shall be deter
mined as hereinafter set forth, and such tax shall be computed
by the taxpayer. [The corporation may pay at this rate upon
the entire value of its capital stock, and in that event it shall not
be required to report as provided in subsection (2) of KRS
136.090. Its failure so to report shall be conclusive evidence
that the corporation elects to pay upon the entire value of its
capital stock, and it shall do so, and the Department of Revenue
shall fix its taxable value.]
(3) No corporation required to pay an annual license tax
under this section shall pay a less sum therefor than ten dollars.
Section 2. Section 136.090 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
(1) [In order to ascertain the amount of taxes due ar

364 Acts of the General Assembly
payable by] Corporations liable to taxation under KRS 136.070
[, every such corporation] shall file with the Department of
Revenue each year, on forms prepared by the department, a
written report, verified by the affidavit of the president or secre
tary of the corporation. This report shall give the name of the
corporation; the name of the state or government under the laws
of which it is incorporated; the date of incorporation; the place
of its principal office in and out of this state; the name and
address of its president and secretary; the name and address of
its authorized agent or attorney upon whom process may be
executed in this state; the name and address of the officer or
agent in charge of its business in this state; and the nature and
kind of business in which it is engaged.
(2) The report shall also give the total value of all of the
property owned and used by the corporation; the value [and
location] of the property owned and used by it in this state;
the aggregate amount of business transacted by it during the pre
ceding calendar year or fiscal year; the amount of such business
transacted in this state; and such other facts [bearing on proper
taxation] as the department requires.
Section 3. Section 136.100 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
(1) If the corporation operates on a calendar year basis,
the reports required under KRS 136.090 shall be filed on or
before April 15 in each year. If the corporation operates on a
fiscal year basis, the reports shall be filed on or before the
fifteenth day of the fourth month following the close of each fiscal
year. The reports shall cover operations for the preceding calen
dar or fiscal year, as the case may be. Domestic corporations
hereafter incorporated, and foreign corporations hereafter be
coming the owners of property or transacting business in this
state, shall make their reports to the Department of Revenue on
or before the first filing date succeeding their incorporation or
succeeding their becoming the owners of property or transacting
business in this state, and shall in all respects be subject to the
provisions of KRS 136.070 to 136.110 the same as corporations
already in existence.
(2) A corporation may change its reporting period from a

Chapter 94 365
calendar year to a fiscal year, or from a fiscal year to a calendar
year, by securing written permission from the department. If a
corporation so changes its basis of reporting, the first report filed
on the new filing date shall cover operations for the period be
tween the close of the old accounting period and the close of the
new accounting period. The assessment of value shall be com
puted in the same manner as on any other return, but the tax due
shall be computed on that proportionate part of the assessment
that the period between the close of the old accounting period
and the new accounting period bears to the entire twelve-month
period.
(3) In any case where two or more corporations merge,
consolidate, or otherwise combine into a single corporation after
the close of the taxable year, but before the beginning of the
succeeding taxable year, all factors used to determine the cor
poration license tax assessment shall be computed on the basis
of the consolidated accounting records of such corporations.
Section 4. Section 136.110 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is repealed in its entirety.
[(1) The Department of Revenue, from the reports and
from any additional information it may secure, shall ascertain
and fix the fair value of that part of the capital stock of the
corporations upon which the license tax imposed by KRS 136.070
shall be based. The department in arriving at the value of the
capital stock of such corporations may consider the market value,
book value, earnings or any other recognized criteria, or com
bination of criteria, of value. It may in any case require any ad
ditional information it deems necessary to enable it to perform its
duties. The department shall notify each corporation of the
amount so assessed, by mail.
(2) The corporation shall have thirty days from the date
of notice of assessment in which to pay the tax to the department,
by check or money order payable to the State Treasurer, or to
protest the assessment to the department. After the protest has
been acted upon bv the department, the corporation protesting
shall have fifteen davs in which to pav the tax found to be due
or to appeal as provided in KRS 131.110 and 131.120. If any
corporation fails to file a report required by KRS 136.090, th^

366 Acts of the General Assembly
department may make a jeopardy assessment as provided in
KRS 131.150.]
'
Section 5. A new section of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is hereby created to read as follows:
(1) If the capital stock of a corporation is listed with
recognized exchanges and sold in such quantities, and under such
conditions, as to permit determination of a monthly value, the
monthly high and low sales prices of the stock during the taxable
year are to be averaged to obtain a yearly average which shall be
applied to the total number of shares outstanding. The result
ing figure will constitute the value of the capital stock of such
corporation.
( 2 ) 7/ the market value of the capital stock cannot be deter
mined pursuant to subsection (1), the annual income over a period
of five years, if possible, or if not, the years available, shall be
weighted and averaged, giving greater weight to the more recent
years. The resulting average income shall be capitalized at ten
per cent and the result averaged with the net worth of the com
pany. Net worth shall include long-term indebtedness, advances
by affiliated companies, inter-company accounts, capital stock,
surplus, or any other items representing additional capital used
and employed in the business. The figure obtained therefrom
will constitute the value of the capital stock of such corporation.
Provided, however, that the value as determined herein shall not
be less than the net worth of the company reduced by a percent
age determined as follows:
( a ) when the book value of fixed assets is less than ten per
cent of total assets, the percentage reduction shall be five per
cent;
( b ) when the book value of fixed assets is at least ten per
cent but less than thirty per cent of total assets the percentage
reduction shall be ten per cent;
( c ) when the book value of fixed assets is at least thirty per
cent but less than fifty per cent of total assets, the percentage
reduction shall be fifteen per cent;
(d) when the book value of fixed assets is fifty per cent or
more of total assets, the percentage reduction shall be twenty
per cent.

(3) In the event that the method for determining the value

reaches an unconstitutional result, or the department determines
that said method, as applied in a particular case, results in a value

the department shall prescribe a method to obtain a more accurate
valuation.

Section 7. A new section of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is created to read as follows:
(1) As soon as practicable after each return is received,
the department shall examine and audit it. If the amount of tax
computed by the department is greater than the amount returned
by the taxpayer, the excess shall be assessed by the department
within four years from the date the return was filed, except as
provided in subsection (2) of this section, and except that in the
case of a failure to file a return, or of a fraudulent return, the
excess may be assessed at any time. A notice of such assessment
shall be mailed to the taxpayer. The time herein provided may
be extended by agreement between the taxpayer and the de
partment.
(2) For the purpose of subsection (1) of this section, a
return filed before the last day prescribed by law for the filing
thereof shall be considered as filed on such last day.

under the provisions of KRS 136.070 shall be appropriated for
General Fund purposes.
