Section 9. Section 136.990 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
(1) Any county clerk who fails to comply with the pro
visions of subsection (2) of KRS 136.020 shall be fined not less
than ten dollars nor more than twenty-five dollars.
( 2 ) Any corporation that fails to pay its taxes, penalty and
interest as provided in subsection (2) of KRS 136.050, after be
coming delinquent, shall be fined fifty dollars for each day the
same remains unpaid, to be recovered by indictment or civil
action, of which the Franklin circuit court shall have jurisdiction.
(3) Any public service corporation, or officer thereof, that
willfully fails or refuses to make reports as required by KRS
136.130 and 136.140 shall be fined one thousand dollars, and
fifty dollars for each day the reports are not made after March
31 of each year.
(4) Any superintendent of schools or county clerk who
fails to report as required by KRS 136.190, or who makes a false
report, shall be fined not less than fifty dollars nor more than
one hundred dollars for each offense.
(5) Any railroad corporation that fails to furnish reports
as required by KRS 136.220 shall be fined not less than five
hundred dollars nor more than ten thousand dollars.
(6) Any officer of a railroad corporation who knowingly
makes false statements relative to the reports required by KRS
136.220 shall be confined in the penitentiary for not less than
one nor more than five years.
(7) Any person who violates any of the provisions of sub
section (2) of KRS 136.250 shall be fined not more than one
thousand dollars. If the offender is an individual, he may in
addition be confined in the county jail for not more than six
months, or both so fined and imprisoned. If the offender is a
corporation, the principal officer or the officer responsible for
the violation, or both such officers, may be punished by such im
prisonment. The fine shall be in addition to the penalties pro
vided by subsection (3) of KRS 136.250.
(8) Any company or association that fails or refuses to

370 Acts of the General Assembly
return the statement or pay the taxes required by KRS 136.330 or
136.340 shall be fined one thousand dollars for each offense.
(9) Any insurance company that fails or refuses for thirty
days to return the statement required by KRS 136.330 or 136.340,
under the oath of some principal officer or general agent or
manager of this state, and to pay the tax required by KRS 136.330
or 136.340, shall forfeit one hundred dollars for each offense.
The Director of the Division of Insurance shall revoke the author
ity of the company or its agents to do business in this state, and
shall publish the revocation in some newspaper of this state.
(10) (Subsection (10) repealed, 1958, c. 126, § 12; ef
fective June 19, 1958)
(11) Any person who violates subsection (3) of KRS
136.390 shall be fined not less than one hundred dollars nor more
than five hundred dollars for each offense.
(12) Where no other penalty is mentioned for failing to
do an act required, or for doing an act forbidden, by this chapter,
the penalty shall be not less than ten dollars nor more than five
hundred dollars.
(13) The Franklin circuit court shall have jurisdiction of
all prosecutions under subsection (8), (9), (10) and (11) of this
section.
( 14 ) If any taxpayer fails to make or file a return as required
by Section 2 of this act on or before the due date of the return,
then, unless it is shown to the satisfaction of the department that
the failure is due to reasonable cause, five per cent of the tax
found to be due by the department shall be added to the tax for
each thirty days or fraction thereof elapsing between the due
date of the return and the date on which it was filed, but the total
penalty shall not exceed twenty-five percent of the tax provided,
however, that in no case shall a penalty be less than ten dollars.
( 15 ) If the tax imposed by Section 1 of this act, whether
assessed by the department, or the taxpayer, or any installment
or portion of the tax is not paid on or before the date prescribed
for its payment, it shall be collected as a part of the tax, interest
upon the nonpaid amount at the rate of six per cent per annum
from the date prescribed for its payment until payment is actually

Chapter 95 371
made to the department. If the assessment made by the de
partment is not paid within the time prescribed, or protested in
accordance with KRS 131.110, the taxpayer shall pay as a penalty,
in addition to the amount of tax and other applicable penalties,
one per cent of the tax for each month that the tax remains
nonpaid.
Approved March 9, 1962
CHAPTER 95
(H. B. 236)
AN ACT relating to the immunization of children against diphtheria,
tetanus, poliomyelitis and pertussis.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. As used in this Act the term "child" means a
person under 18 years of age.
Section 2. Except as otherwise provided in Section 4 of
this Act, all parents, guardians, and other persons having care,
custody or control of any child shall have the child immunized
against diphtheria, tetanus, poliomyelitis and pertussis within
twelve months after birth. Such persons shall also have booster
immunizations administered to the child in accordance with the
regulations of the State Board of Health.
Section 3. Except as otherwise provided in Section 4 of
this Act, no child shall be eligible to enroll as a student in any
public or private elementary or secondary school without first
presenting a certificate from a duly licensed medical or osteo
pathic physician stating that the child has been immunized
against diphtheria, tetanus and poliomyelitis in accordance with
the provisions of this Act and the regulations of the State Board
of Health. The governing body of private and public schools
shall enforce the provisions of this section.
Section 4. Nothing contained in this Act shall be construed

372 Acts of the General Assembly
to require the immunization of any child at a time when, in the
written opinion of his attending physician, such immunization
would be injurious to the child's health. Nor shall this Act be
construed to require the immunization of any child whose parents
are members of a nationally recognized and established church
or religious denomination, the teachings of which are opposed
to medical immunization against disease, and who object in
writing to the immunization of such child on that ground. Pro
vided, however, that in the event of an epidemic in a given area,
the State Board of Health may, by emergency regulation, require
the immunization of all persons within the area of epidemic,
against the disease responsible for such epidemic.
Section 5. Any person who violates any provision of this
Act shall be fined not less than ten dollars nor more than one
hundred dollars, or imprisoned for not less than ten days nor
more than sixty days, or both.
Approved March 9, 1962

Section 3. A new section of the Kentucky Revised Statutes

tered under KRS 186.050 (3), may issue notice to such owner or

(4) The question for determination at any hearing held at

CHAPTER 97
(H. R. 43)
A CONCURRENT RESOLUTION requesting the motorcar industry to
incorporate in all American motorcars those devices and structures
that are known to be capable of reducing the number of highway
deaths and injuries.
WHEREAS, many principles to be applied for the reduction of crash
injuries in motorcars have been known for over thirty years, have
been in use in aviation practice for fifteen years, and were explicitly
presented in 1948 in the Journal of the American Medical Association
by Woodward; and
WHEREAS, the principles are a part of basic engineering knowledge,
and have been drawn to the attention of executive personnel in the
motorcar industry by medical and other groups many times; and
WHEREAS, the motorcar industry gave only token recognition to these
principles and practices in its products for the 1956 model year, and,
despite promises of definite progress in succeeding years, has pro
duced no significant improvements in the years since 1956,
NOW, THEREFORE,
Be it resolved by the House of Representatives of the Common
wealth of Kentucky; the Senate concurring therein:
That the Commonwealth of Kentucky requests the motorcar
industry to incorporate in all American motorcars those devices
and structures which they know are capable of reducing the
number of deaths and injuries now occurring on our streets and

Chapter 98 379
highways; and also that the Commonwealth of Kentucky expresses
the sincere hope that the motorcar industry can and will incorpo
rate the necessary changes to make the individual cars less lethal,
avoiding the need for the instigation of Federal controls such as
have been imposed upon other businesses which did not police
themselves.
Approved March 9, 1962

CHAPTER 100
(S. B. 326)
AN ACT relating to atomic energy and projects related thereto; making
an appropriation; and declaring an emergency— The Conley-Swope
Act.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:

Chapter 100 381
Section 1. (a) The statement of the public policy of the
Commonwealth relative to participation in the development and
utilization of atomic energy for peaceful purposes to the maxi
mum extent consistent with the common defense and security
and with the health and safety of the public, as enacted in
Chapter 146, 1958 Acts of the General Assembly, and later
amendatory Acts, is hereby re-affirmed; and this Act is declared
to be supplemental thereto rather than in derogation thereof.
(b) In recognition of changed and changing conditions in
the expansion, growth and development of utilization of atomic
energy and by-products thereof, for peaceful purposes in medicine,
generation of power, commerce, industry, and other fields of
increasing number, it is declared that such public policy of the
Commonwealth now requires implementation in such manner
as will enable the Commonwealth to discharge new responsibili
ties in licensing and safeguarding the storage, use, processing and
reprocessing of radioactive materials, and in providing for per
manent burial or other disposal of radioactive materials, and to
develop new opportunities in the promotion and advancement of
uses of atomic energy by encouraging and assisting the establish
ment or location of industrial plants in the Commonwealth (sub
ject to proper protection of the public health and safety) thus
promoting the general welfare by providing new opportunities
for gainful employment.
Section 2. It is the purpose of this Act to implement the
public policy of the Commonwealth as set forth in Section 1
hereof, by creating The Kentucky Atomic Energy Authority, a
corporate agency and instrumentality of the Commonwealth, to
act in the establishment of certain facilities which must neces
sarily be owned and controlled by a sovereign state, and to pro
vide sites suitable for the establishment and development of
facilities by governmental bodies and private enterprise, to finance
or provide financial assistance in connection therewith, to deter
mine the location and use thereof, and to assist in the coordination
of activities and functions of departments, commissions and other
agencies and instrumentalities of the Commonwealth having law
ful control or responsibilities in various aspects of the whole sub
ject of atomic energy and the uses thereof.

382 Acts of the General Assembly
Section 3. As hereinafter used, unless the context clearly
indicates otherwise:
( 1 ) "atomic energy" means all forms of energy released in
the course of nuclear fission, nuclear fusion, or other nuclear
transformation;
(2) "Atomic Energy Commission" or "Commission" means
the Atomic Energy Commission of the United States or any suc
cessor thereto in the exercise of the authority and functions
thereof;
(3) "Authority" means "The Kentucky Atomic Energy
Authority" created and established by this Act;
(4) "Executive Director" means the person holding the
position of that title by appointment of the Authority from time
to time;
(5) "revenue bonds" means the revenue bonds which the
Authority is herein authorized to issue in furtherance of the law
ful and proper functions and powers of the Authority, and which
do not constitute an indebtedness of the Commonwealth within
the meaning of debt-limiting provisions of the present Constitu
tion of the Commonwealth, but are payable as to principal and
interest solely from one or more special funds, and from revenues
to be derived by the Authority and pledged to be paid into anv
such special fund from the activities and operations which the
Authority is empowered to acquire;
(6) "governmental agency" means the Commonwealth, the
United States or any State thereof, any foreign government (un
less such foreign government be one with which nuclear trans
actions are forbidden by law or regulation of the Atomic Energy
Commission) or any division, department, or licensed agency or
instrumentality of any of them, including agencies and instrumen
talities in corporate form, whether created by legislative enact
ment or organized as non-profit corporations under general cor
poration laws for the purpose of serving as agencies or instru
mentalities of either of them;
(7) "private enterprise" means any person who is not an
officer, agent or employee of the United States or of the govern
ment of any State or Territory of the United States or of any

Chapter 100 383
foreign government; and any firm, co-partnership, association,
foundation or corporation which is not a governmental agency,
whether or not operated for monetary profit;
(8) "project", without other descriptive designation, means
any undertaking of the Authority in furtherance of its lawful
objectives and purposes, whether alone or by agreement with any
governmental agency or with private enterprise, such as, but not
limited to nuclear power stations, nuclear testing reactors, plants
for chemical or metallurgical processing of source and special
nuclear materials, plants for chemical processing of irradiated
nuclear material, and plants for the separation of isotopes of
uranium and plutonium, and includes supporting and related
projects.
Section 4. (1) The Governor, the Lieutenant Governor,
the Commissioner of Economic Development, the Commissioner
of Health, the Attorney General and their respective successors
in office, and five citizens appointed by the Governor, shall be
a body corporate and politic constituting a public corporation and
governmental agency and instrumentality of the Commonwealth
by the name of "The Kentucky Atomic Energy Authority," with
perpetual succession and with power in that name to contract
and be contracted with, sue and be sued, have and use a corporate
seal, adopt by-laws for the governing of its affairs and amend
them at will, and to exercise, in addition to the powers and func
tions specifically stated in this Act, all of the usual powers of
private corporations to the extent that the same are not incon
sistent with specifically enumerated powers.
(2) The five citizen members shall be appointed for terms
of four years, their initial appointments, however, being two for
terms of one year, and one each for terms of two, three and
four years from the effective date of this Act. At least one of
the members so appointed shall be a nominee of Kentucky
Spindletop Research Center, Incorporated who shall be chosen
by the Governor from a list of three names to be submitted to
him by that organization. All appointments to fill vacancies
shall be for the unexpired term only. No member appointed
under this subsection shall be removed from office except for
good cause shown and entered on the Executive Journal.

384 Acts of the General Assembly
(3) The Governor shall be the Chairman of the Authority
and the Lieutenant Governor shall be Vice-Chairman, by virtue
of their respective offices. The members shall elect from among
their number a secretary and a treasurer; or may determine by
resolution that the Executive Director, hereinafter referred to,
shall hold either or both of such offices. The duties of the officers
shall be as prescribed in the by-laws. The members of the Author
ity shall name an Executive Committee of three members to which
power to act for the Authority may be delegated subject to such
limitations as may be established in the By-Laws.
(4) The members of the Authority shall receive no com
pensation for their services in that capacity, but shall be entitled
to reimbursement for all reasonable expenses necessarily incurred
in connection with performance of their duties and functions as
such members.
( 5 ) Six members of the Authority shall constitute a quorum
for the transaction of business, and in the absence of a quorum,
one or more members may adjourn from time to time until a
quorum is convened.
(6) The Authority shall meet not less frequently than
quarterly, and otherwise as often as may be necessary to the
proper and timely performance of the functions and powers
herein conferred upon it. In its by-laws, or by resolution spread
at large upon its official minutes, the Authority may prescribe
periodic meetings at stated times and places; and meetings held
upon such stated occasions (including any adjournments thereof
to reconvene at another time and place definitely set forth in the
Minutes ) shall constitute regular meetings at which any business
may be transacted without notice to the members. Special meet
ings may be called by the Chairman or by any three members, and
notice of the time, place, and business to be transacted thereat
shall be given to the members at such time and in such manner
as may be prescribed in the by-laws. Members may waive notice
of any meeting orally or in writing, before, at, or after the meeting;
and the presence of a member at any meeting shall constitute a
waiver of notice on his part unless he makes specific objection on
the ground of want of proper notice.
(7) The Authority may appoint one or more advisory com
mittees consisting of not more than seven members each to

Chapter 100 385
consider and advise the Authority upon all matters submitted to
them by the Authority. Members of advisory committees shall
serve without salary for such terms not to exceed four years, as
the Authority may determine, and shall be entitled to reimburse
ment for their actual and necessary expenses incurred in the per
formance of their official duties.
Section 5. The chief executive officer of the Authority
shall be its Executive Director, who shall be a person determined
in the discretion of the Authority to possess adequate qualifica
tions in technical, legal or administrative aspects of atomic energy
who shall be employed by resolution of the Authority, perform
such services and exercise such powers as may be prescribed by
the Authority, and shall serve at the pleasure of the Authority.
His compensation shall be fixed by the Authority, and in addition
thereto he shall be allowed travel, subsistence and other proper
expenses incurred in the performance of his duties as may be
provided from time to time for other employes of the Common
wealth. He may by resolution be elected or appointed to serve
as the secretary or treasurer of the Authority, or both.
Section 6. The Attorney General shall be the legal officer
of the Authority, but shall designate one or more qualified As
sistant Attorneys General to serve as advisors to the Authority, the
Executive Director, and other staff members as occasions may
arise.
Section 7. The Commissioner of Health shall be the health
and safety officer of the Authority, but shall designate one or
more qualified staff-members of the Department of Health to
advise the Authority, the Executive Director, and staff-members
of the Authority in such matters as occasions may arise.
Section 8. The Authority shall establish its principal office
at Frankfort, and may establish branch or field offices wherever
it may determine that expeditious performance of its functions
may from time to time require.
Section 9. The Authority shall have the following general
powers, in addition to specific powers enumerated in this Act:
( 1 ) To acquire by purchase, exercise of the right of eminent

386 Acts of the General Assembly
domain, grant, gift, devise or otherwise, the fee simple title to
or any acceptable lesser interest in any lands, and by lease or
other contract the right to use and occupy any lands, selected
in the discretion of the Authority as constituting necessary, de
sirable or acceptable sites for projects of the Authority, including
any and all lands adjacent to a project site as in the discretion of
the Authority may be necessary or suitable for satellite projects
or restricted areas; but in all instances lands which are to be
designated as radioactive waste material sites shall be acquired
in fee simple absolute and dedicated in perpetuity to such purpose;
(2) Except in the cases of radioactive waste material sites,
to convey to private enterprise, or to lease to private enterprise
for such term as in the discretion of the Authority may be in the
public interest, any lands so acquired, either for a fair and reason
able consideration or solely or partly as an inducement to the
establishment or location in the Commonwealth of any atomic
facility, project, satellite project, or nuclear storage area; but sub
ject to such restraints as may be deemed proper to bring about a
reversion of title or termination of any lease in the event the gran
tee or lessee, as the case may be, shall cease to use the premises
or facilities in the conduct of business or activities consistent with
the purposes of this Act;
(3) To construct, reconstruct, maintain, repair, operate,
and regulate projects at such locations within the Commonwealth
as may be determined by the Authority;
(4) To issue revenue bonds of the Authority payable solely
from the revenues, rentals, rates, charges and other funds,
pledged for their payment, for the purpose of paying all or any
part of the cost of any one or more projects, and to refund any
of its bonds;
(5) To fix by contract, or to establish and revise from time
to time and charge and collect revenues, rentals, rates and charges
for use of the services and facilities of projects;
(6) To combine for financing purposes any two or more
projects;
(7) To establish and enforce rules and regulations for the
use of any project;

Chapter 100 387
(8) Without reference to Chapter 56 of the Kentucky Re
vised Statutes, to acquire and hold in the name of the Authority
real and personal property in the exercise of its powers and the
performance of its functions and duties under this Act, and to
dispose of the same with due regard for public health and safety,
except in the case of radioactive waste material sites, which may
be disposed of only to the Commonwealth itself, or to the United
States;
(9) To designate the locations and establish, limit and
control such points of ingress to and egress from each project
as the Authority may determine to be necessary or desirable to
insure the proper operation and maintenance of such project, and
to prohibit entrance to such project from any point or points not
so designated;
(10) To make and enter into such contracts and agreements
with governmental agencies and private enterprise as may be
necessary or incidental to the performance of its duties and the
execution of its powers under this Act;
(11) To employ scientists, consulting engineers, health
officers, attorneys, accountants, construction and financial ex
perts, superintendents, managers, and such other employes and
agents as may be necessary in the judgment of the Authority, and
to fix their compensation;
( 12 ) To receive and accept from any governmental agency,
or from private enterprise, grants for or in aid of any project, in
money, property, labor or other things of value, to be held, used
and applied only for the purposes for which such grants and
contributions may be made;
(13) To expend any funds provided under the authority
of this Act to publicize the facilities and services of a project or
projects to governmental agencies and private enterprise; and
(14) To do all acts and things necessary or convenient to
carry out the powers expressly granted in this Act.
Section 10. (1) The Authority is hereby authorized to
provide, at one time or from time to time, for the issuance of its
revenue bonds for the purpose of paying all or any part of the cost
of any one or more projects undertaken pursuant to this Act. The

388 Acts of the General Assembly
principal of and the interest on such bonds shall in each instance
be payable solely from a special fund provided for such payment,
and from identified revenues pledged to be set aside and deposited
in such special fund. The bonds of any issue may be in one or
more series and any one or more such series may enjoy equal
or subordinate status with respect to the pledge of funds from
which they are payable, shall be dated, shall bear interest at
such rate or rates not exceeding six per cent per annum, shall
mature at such time or times not exceeding the fortieth (40th)
anniversary of their respective dates, all as may be provided by
the Authority, and may be made redeemable before maturity, at
the option of the Authority, at such price or prices and under
such terms and conditions as may be fixed by the Authority prior
to the issuance of the bonds. The Authority shall determine the
form of the bonds, including any interest coupons to be at
tached thereto, and shall fix the denomination or denominations
of the bonds and the place or places for payment of principal
and interest, which may be at any bank or trust company within
or without the Commonwealth. The bonds shall be signed by
the facsimile signature of the Chairman of the Authority, and the
seal of the Authority or a facsimile thereof shall be affixed thereto
and attested by the manual signature of the secretary of the
Authority, and any coupons attached thereto shall bear the fac
simile signature of the Chairman of the Authority. In case any
officer whose signature or a facsimile of whose signature shall
appear on any bonds or coupons shall cease to be such officer
before the delivery of such bonds, such signature or such fac
simile shall nevertheless be valid and sufficient for all purposes
the same as if he had remained in office until such delivery.
All bonds issued under the provisions of this Act shall have and
are hereby declared to have all the qualities and incidents of
negotiable instruments under the Uniform Commercial Code of
the Commonwealth. The bonds may be issued in coupon or in
registered form, or both, as the Authority may determine, and
provision may be made for the registration of any coupon bonds
as to principal alone and also as to both principal and interest,
and for the reconversion into coupon bonds of any bonds regis
tered as to both principal and interest. The Authority may sell
such bonds in such manner, either at public or private sale, and
for such price as it may determine will best effect the purposes

Chapter 100 389
of this Act, but no such sale shall be made at a price so low as to
require the payment of interest on the money received therefor
at more than six per cent per annum computed with relation to
the absolute maturity of the bonds in accordance with standard
tables of bond values, excluding, however, from such computa
tions the amount of any premium to be paid on redemption of
any bonds prior to maturity.
( 2 ) The proceeds of the bonds of each issue shall be used
solely for the payment of the cost of the project or projects for
which such bonds shall have been issued, and shall be disbursed
in such manner and under such restrictions, if any, as the Authority
may provide in the proceedings authorizing the issuance of such
bonds or in the trust indenture securing the same. If the pro
ceeds of the bonds of any issue, by error of estimates or other
wise, shall be less than such cost, additional bonds mav in like
manner be issued to provide the amount of such deficit, and,
unless otherwise provided in the proceedings authorizing the is
suance of such bonds or in the trust indenture securing the same,
shall be deemed to be of the same issue and shall be entitled to
payment from the same fund without preference or priority of
the bonds first issued. If the proceeds of the bonds of any
issue shall exceed such cost, the surplus shall be deposited to the
credit of the sinking fund or funds for such bonds or anv account
or accounts therein as the Authority shall have provided in the
proceedings or trust indenture authorizing and securing such
bonds.
( 3 ) Prior to the preparation of definitive bonds, the Author
ity may, under like restrictions, issue interim receipts or temporary
bonds, with or without coupons, exchangeable for definitive bonds
when such bonds shall have been executed and are available for
delivery. The Authority may also provide for the replacement
of any bonds which shall become mutilated or shall be destroved
or lost. Bonds may be issued under the provisions of this Act
without obtaining the consent of any department, division, com
mission, board, bureau, or agency of the Commonwealth, and
without any other proceedings or the happening of anv other con
ditions or things, except as specifically required by this Act.
Section 11. Revenue bonds issued by the Authority under
the provisions of this Act shall not be deemed to constitute a

390 Acts of the General Assembly
debt of the Commonwealth or of any political subdivision thereof,
or a pledge of the faith and credit of the Commonwealth or of
any such political subdivision, but such bonds shall be payable
solely from the funds provided therefor under the provisions of
this Act. All such revenue bonds shall contain on the face thereof
a statement to the effect that neither the Commonwealth nor the
Authority shall be obligated to pay the same or the interest thereon
except from revenues of the project for which they are issued,
and that neither the faith and credit nor the taxing power of
the Commonwealth or of any political subdivision thereof is
pledged to the payment of the principal of or the interest on such
bonds.
Section 12. In the discretion of the Authority, any bonds
issued under the provisions of this Act may be secured by a trust
indenture by and between the Authority and a corporate trustee,
which may be any trust company or bank having the powers of
a trust company within or without the Commonwealth. Such
trust indenture or the proceedings providing for the issuance of
such bonds may pledge or assign the revenues, rentals, rates and
charges to be received, but shall not convey or mortgage any
project or anv part thereof. Such trust indenture or proceedings
may contain such provisions for protecting and enforcing the
rights and remedies of the bondholders as may be reasonable and
proper and not in violation of law, including covenants setting
forth the duties of the Authority in relation to the acquisition of
property and the construction, improvement, maintenance, repair,
operation and insurance of the project or projects in connection
with which such bonds shall have been authorized, the rentals,
rates, charges and other revenues to be established and collected,
and the custody, safeguarding and application of all moneys.
It shall be lawful for any bank or trust company incorporated
under the laws of the Commonwealth which may act as deposit
ory of the proceeds of bonds or of revenues to furnish such in
demnifying bonds or to pledge such securities as may be required
by the Authority. Any such trust indenture may set forth the
rights and remedies of the bondholders and of the trustee, and
may restrict the individual right of action by bondholders. In
addition to the foregoing, any such trust indenture or proceed
ings may contain such other provisions as the Authority may deem
reasonable and proper for the security of the bondholders. All

Chapter 100 391
expenses incurred in carrying out the provisions of such trust in
denture or proceedings may be treated as a part of the cost of the
operation of the project or projects.
Section 13. All moneys received pursuant to the provisions
of this Act, whether as proceeds from the sale of bonds or as
revenues, shall be deemed to be trust funds to be held and applied
solely as provided in this Act. The proceedings or the trust
indenture shall provide that any officer with whom, or any bank
or trust company with which, such moneys shall be deposited
shall act as trustee of such moneys and shall hold and apply the
same for the purposes hereof, subject to such regulations as this
Act and such proceedings or trust indenture may provide.
Section 14. Any holder of bonds issued under the provisions
of this Act or any of the coupons appertaining thereto, and the
trustee under any trust indenture, except to the extent the rights
herein given may be restricted by such trust indenture or pro
ceedings, may, either at law or in equity, by suit, action, man
damus or other proceedings, protect and enforce any and all
rights under the laws of the Commonwealth or granted under
this Act or under such trust indenture or the proceedings author
izing the issuance of such bonds, and may enforce and compel
the performance of all duties required by this Act or by such
trust indenture or proceedings to be performed by the Authority
or by any officer or employes thereof, including the fixing and
collecting of rentals, rates, charges and other revenues.
Section 15. The exercise of the powers granted by this
Act will be in all respects for the benefit of the people of the
Commonwealth, for the increase of their commerce and pros
perity, and for the safeguarding and protection of their health
and living conditions, and as the operation and maintenance of
a project or projects will constitute the performance of essential
governmental functions, the Authority shall not be required to
pay any taxes or assessments upon any project or any property
acquired or used by the Authority under the provisions of this
Act or upon the income therefrom, and the bonds issued under
the provisions of this Act, their transfer and the income there
from (including any profit made on the sale thereof) shall at
all times be free from taxation within the Commonwealth; but

392 Acts of the General Assembly
exemption from taxation shall not extend to real or personal
property in the ownership of private enterprise, or to the income,
operations or securities of private enterprise, except and to the
extent exemption may be provided in any case by the Constitu
tion and other statute laws of the Commonwealth.
Section 16. Bonds issued by the Authority under the pro
visions of this Act are hereby made securities in which all public
officers and public bodies, agencies and instrumentalities of the
Commonwealth (including its Retirement Systems) and its
political subdivisions, all insurance companies, trust companies,
banking associations, investment companies, executors, adminis
trators, trustees and other fiduciaries, and all other persons what
soever who are now or may hereafter be authorized to invest in
bonds or other obligations of a similar nature may properly and
legally invest funds, including capital in their control or belong
ing to them. Such bonds are hereby made securities which may
properly and legally be deposited with and received by any state
or municipal officer or any agency or political subdivision of
the Commonwealth for any purpose for which the deposit of
bonds or other obligations of the Commonwealth is now or may
hereafter be authorized by law.
Section 17. (1) The Authority is hereby authorized and
empowered to acquire by purchase, whenever it shall deem such
purchase expedient, solely from funds provided pursuant to this
Act, such lands, structures, property, rights, rights-of-way, fran
chises, easements and other interests in lands, including lands
lying under water and riparian rights, which are located within
the Commonwealth, as it may deem necessary or convenient for
the construction and operation of any project, upon such terms
and at such prices as may be considered by it to be reasonable
and can be agreed upon between it and the owner thereof, and
to take title thereto in the name of the Authority.
(2) Whenever a reasonable price cannot be agreed upon,
or whenever the owner is legally incapacitated or is absent,
unknown or unable to convey valid title, the Authority is hereby
authorized and enpowered to acquire by condemnation or by
the exercise of the power of eminent domain any lands, prop
erty, rights, rights-of-way, franchise, easements and other prop

CHAfTEB 100 393
erty, including public lands, parks, playgrounds, reservations,
highways or parkways, or parts thereof or rights therein, of any
person, copartnership, association, railroad, public service, public
utility or other corporation, or municipality or political subdi
vision, deemed necessary or convenient for the construction or the
efficient operation of any project or necessary in the restoration
of public or private property damaged or destroyed. Any such
proceedings shall be conducted, and the compensation to be
paid shall be ascertained and paid, in the manner provided by
the Constitution and laws of the Commonwealth then applicable
which relate to condemnation or to the exercise of the power of
eminent domain by the Department of Highways. Title to any
property acquired by the Authority shall be taken in the name
of the Authority. In any condemnation proceedings the court
having jurisdiction of the suit, action or proceedings may make
such orders as may be just to the Authority and to the owners
of the property to be condemned and may require an under
taking or other security to secure such owners against any loss
or damage by reason of the failure of the Authority to accept
and pay for the property, but neither such undertaking or se
curity nor any act or obligation of the Authority shall impose any
liability upon the Commonwealth except as may be paid from
the funds provided under this Act.
(3) If the owner, lessee or occupier of any property to be
condemned shall refuse to remove his personal property there
from or give up his possession thereof, the Authority may pro
ceed to obtain possession in any manner now or hereafter provided
by law.
Section 18. The Authority is hereby authorized to provide
for the issuance of its revenue refunding bonds for the purpose
of refunding any bonds then outstanding which shall have been
issued under the provisions of this Act, including the payment of
any redemption premium thereon and any interest accrued or
to accrue to the date of redemption of such bonds, and, if the
Authority shall so determine, for the additional purpose of con
struction improvements, extensions, or enlargements of the
project or projects in connection with which the bonds to be
refunded shall have been issued. The Authority is further author
ized to provide for the issuance of its revenue bonds for ti

394 Acts of the General Assembly
combined purpose of (a) refunding any bonds then outstanding
which shall have been issued under the provisions of this Act,
including the payment of any redemption premium thereon and
any interest accrued or to accrue to the date of redemption of
such bonds, and (b) paying all or any part of the cost of any
additional project or projects. The issuance of such bonds,
the maturities and other details thereof, the rights of the holders
thereof, and the rights, duties and obligations of the Authority
in respect of the same, shall be governed by the provisions of
this Act in so far as the same may be applicable.
Section 19. It is recognized by the General Assembly that
any site used as a radioactive waste material site will represent a
continuing and perpetual responsibility in the interests of the
public health, safety and general welfare, and that the same
must ultimately be reposed in a sovereign government without
regard for the existence or non-existence of any particular agency,
instrumentality, department, division or officer thereof.
In the event the Authority shall acquire and dedicate one
or more radioactive waste material sites, the Authority may con
vey such sites to the Commonwealth for no other consideration
than an undertaking of the Commonwealth to maintain, safe
guard, and operate the same in the interests of the public health,
safety and welfare. Any such conveyance may contain an express
provision that the Commonwealth thereby assumes such continu
ing and perpetual responsibility, and the Commissioner of Health
( or other officer succeeding to his functions ) is hereby authorized
to sign the same and bind the Commonwealth in that regard;
and any such conveyance may be made subject to any existing
contractual rights of licensees using source, by-product and spe
cial nuclear materials to make use of the site or sites in the dis
position of radioactive waste materials.
Section 20. The General Assembly hereby takes notice of,
and finds as a legislative determination of fact, that until such
time as the General Assembly may take notice of and find as a
legislative determination of fact to the contrary, the development,
promotion and implementation of activities and projects in the
field of atomic energy require special expertise which is not com
mon or currently available to general construction contractors or

Chapter 100 395
operating private enterprise; and that by reason thereof it is in
the public interest and in pursuance of sound public policy of
the Commonwealth that the Authority not be restricted to award
ing contracts for the construction, installation or operation of
such projects as are authorized by this Act by public award on
the basis of competition after such public advertisement or
solicitation as may otherwise be required by general laws. Ac
cordingly, the Authority is specifically empowered in the public
interest (a) without advertising or other public solicitation to
negotiate and enter into contracts, agreements, or leases with
federal or state governmental agencies or private enterprise duly
licensed according to law and prevailing regulations for the in
itiation and operation of any project, and (b) without advertising
or other public solicitation to negotiate and enter into contracts
for the construction and installation of any structures, buildings,
installations and appurtenances necessary or incidental to the
same or any part thereof.
Section 21. For the purpose of carrying out the provisions
of this Act, there is hereby appropriated out of the General Ex
penditure Fund, from available funds otherwise unappropriated,
to The Kentucky Atomic Energy Authority, the following sums
for the fiscal years indicated:
1962-1963 $30,000
1963-1964 $30,000
Section 22. Inasmuch as the Commonwealth, pursuant to
statutory authority, has entered into certain agreements with the
government of the United States, whereby the Commonwealth
has already entered into the field of atomic energy, and inas
much as the Commonwealth now has an opportunity to locate
within its boundaries certain projects which will be beneficial
to its economy if this Act becomes immediately effective, an
emergency is hereby declared to exist and this Act shall become
effective immediately upon its passage by the General Assembly
and approval by the Governor of the Commonwealth.
Approved March 14, 196/

396 Acts of the General Assembly
CHAPTER 101
(S. B. 156)
AN ACT relating to secondary and rural roads and bridges.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 177.360 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is amended
to read as follows:
(1) The Department of Highways shall allocate the funds
set apart for construction, reconstruction and maintenance of
secondary and rural highways by the provisions of KRS 138.220
as follows:
(a) One-fifth shall be apportioned equally among the one
hundred twenty counties.
(b) One-fifth shall be apportioned among the one hundred
twenty counties on the basis of the ratio which the rural popula
tion of each county bears to the total rural population of the state.
Rural population as used here means the population in a county
outside cities, towns, and urban areas having a population of
twenty-five hundred or more as shown by the most recent decen
nial census of the United States Bureau of the Census and county
population shall be determined by the most recent decennial
census of the United States Bureau of the Census.
(c) One-fifth shall be apportioned among the one hundred
twenty counties on the basis of the ratio that the public road
mileage outside of cities, towns and urban areas having a popula
tion of twenty-five hundred or more bears to the total mileage
of such roads for the entire state.
(d) Two-fifths shall be apportioned among the one hundred
twenty counties on the basis of the ratio which the square mile
rural area of the county, bears to the total square mile rural area
of the state. Rural area as used here means that area of the
county outside of cities, towns and urban areas having a popula
tion of twenty-five hundred or more and shown by the most
recent decennial census of United States Bureau of the Census.
(2) A pro rata share of the allocation to each county shall
be deducted at the beginning of each fiscal year and adjusted

Chapter 102 397
quarterly to cover the maintenance, administrative, engineering
and other costs of the program.
(3) Of the total amount apportioned by the provisions of
this Act, a sum not exceeding six percent may be deducted and
placed by the Department of Highways in a special emergency
account to be expended at the direction of the Commissioner of
Highways to meet unforeseen emergencies on rural and secondary
roads and bridges.
(4) Apportionments as required by the provisions of this
Act shall be made on the basis of revenue estimates supplied by
the Department of Revenue and adjusted quarterly in accordance
with the most recent revision of the estimates by the Department
of Revenue.
[The Department of Highways shall continuously study the
needs of the various counties of Kentucky for secondary and
rural roads and shall, on the basis of experience, studies, research
and surveys, prepare a scientific formula for the allocation of the
funds provided for in KRS 138.220 between the various counties
of the Commonwealth of Kentucky for the construction, recon
struction and maintenance of secondary and rural roads.]
Approved March 15, 1962
CHAPTER 102
(S. B. 25)
AN ACT relating to facsimile signatures.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
On any bond, note or debenture issued by a corporation,
charitable or religious institution which is countersigned or
otherwise authenticated by the signature of the officers of
the corporation, or the trustee of a charitable or religious
institution, acting in connection with the issuance, the signa
ture of the officers of the corporation and its seal, or the

398 Acts of the General Assembly
signature of the trustee of a charitable or religious institution
may be facsimiles. In case any officer or trustee who has
signed or whose facsimile signature has been placed upon a
bond, note or debenture shall have ceased to be such officer
or trustee before such bond, note or debenture is issued, it
may be issued by the corporation and charitable or religious
institution with the same effect as if he were such officer or
trustee at the date of its issue.
Approved March 19, 1962
CHAPTER 103
(S. B. 47)
AN ACT relating to sanitation districts.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 220.020 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is amended
to read as follows:
The director of the Division of Sanitary Engineering of the
Department of Health shall, in addition to his other duties, act
as commissioner of sanitation districts, and is vested with juris
diction, power and authority, when the conditions set forth in
KRS 220.010 to 220.520 and certified to by the county board of
health are found to exist, to establish sanitation districts within
any county [or counties containing a city of the first, second,
or third class, in accordance with the provisions of this chapter]
of the Commonwealth.
Approved March 19, 1962
CHAPTER 104
(S. B. 71)
AN ACT relating to title to contraband alcoholic beverages, and declar
ing an emergency.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:

Chatter 104 399
Section 1. Section 244.195 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
(1) Title to contraband included in subsections (3), (4)
and (5) of KRS 244.180 seized in a dry territory shall be vested
in the Alcoholic Beverage Control Board, irrespective of whether
such contraband was seized by peace officers of the city or
county or state administrators or field representatives of the
department, notwithstanding the provisions of KRS 242.380.
(2) The Alcoholic Beverage Control Board shall cause a
sale of such contraband as is included in subsection (1)
of this Act to be made and shall, after deducting the expense of
transporting and selling such contraband, return one-half the
proceeds of such sale to the proper authorities of the city or
county in which such seizure was accomplished solely by the
peace officers of the city or county where such seizure occurred.
The remaining one-half shall be paid to the State Treasury and
shall be credited to the General Fund. Where the evidence for
the seizure of such contraband is obtained by the state adminis
trators or field representatives of the department, the Alcoholic
Beverage Control Board shall cause a sale of such contraband
to be made and shall pay all the receipts to the State Treasury
and be credited to the General Fund.
Section 2. Whereas, under present statutes state and local
governments often realize no gain from the sale of alcoholic
beverage contraband seized in dry territory;
Whereas, the sale of such contraband could provide a source
of much needed additional revenue; and
Whereas, sizeable stocks of contraband alcoholic beverages
are now being held by state and local governments, an emergency
is declared to exist and this Act shall become effective upon its
passage and approval by the Governor.
Approved March 19, 1962

400 Acts of the General Assembly
CHAPTER 105
(S. B. 145)
AN ACT relating to strip mining and reclamation.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 350.010 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
As used in this chapter unless the context otherwise requires :
(1 ) "Strip Mining" means all or any part of the process fol
lowed in the production of commercial coal, or clay from a natural
coal, or clay deposit by the open pit or open cut method or auger
method, but shall not include the casual removal of coal, or clay
in building roads or other developments of the owner's property
for purposes other than the production of coal, or olay by the
open pit or open cut method or auger method;
(2) "Overburden" means all the earth and other materials
which are removed to gain access to the commercial coal, or clay
in the process of strip mining;
(3) "Area of land affected" means the area of land from
which overburden is to be or has been removed and upon which
the overburden is to be or has been deposited;
(4) "Operation" means all of the premises, facilities and
equipment used in the process of producing coal, or clay from a
designated strip mine area;
(5) "Operator" means any person, partnership or corpora
tion engaged in strip mining or prospecting to strip mine who
disturbs more than one-fourth acre or who removes or intends to
remove more than one hundred [two hundred and fifty] tons of
coal, or clay from the earth by strip mining within twelve suc
cessive calendar months;
(6) "Person" means persons, partnership or corporation;
(7) "Division" means the Division of Strip Mining and
Reclamation;
(8) "Director" means the Director of the Division of Strip
Mining and Reclamation of the Department of Conservation;

Chapter 105 401
(9) "Commission" means the Strip Mining and Reclamation
Commission;
(10) "Reclamation" means the reconditioning of the area
of land affected by a strip mining operation under a plan ap
proved by the division [commission].
Section 2. A new section of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is created to read as follows:
The Strip Mining and Reclamation Commission shall have
and exercise the following authority and powers:
( 1 ) To adopt, after giving notice to all operations actively
engaged in strip mining and affording them an opportunity to
appear and offer evidence at a public hearing, general rules and
regulations pertaining to strip mining to accomplish the purposes
of this chapter;
(2) To adopt, without hearings, rules and regulations with
respect to procedural aspects of commission hearings;
(3) To conduct hearings under regulations adopted by the
commission and for the purpose of any investigation or hearing
under this chapter, the commission or any member thereof may
administer oaths or affirmations, subpoena witnesses, compel their
attendance, take evidence and require production of any books,
papers, correspondence, memoranda, agreements, or other docu
ments or records which the Commission deems relevant or ma
terial to the inquiry;
(4) In case of refusal to obey a subpoena issued to any
person, the Franklin Circuit Court, upon application by the Com
mission, may issue to that person an order requiring him to appear
before the Commission, there to produce documentary evidence
if so ordered or to give evidence touching the matter under in
vestigation or in question; and any failure to obey the order of
the court may be punished by the court as a contempt of court;
(5) To issue after hearing, orders requiring an operator to
adopt such remedial measures as are necessary to comply with
this chapter;
(6) Any person aggrieved by a final order of the commis-^*^
sion may obtain a review of the order by filing in the Franklir

402 Acts of the General Assembly
Circuit Court, within sixty days after the entry of the order, a
written petition praying that the order be modified or set aside
in whole or in part. A copy of the petition shall be forthwith
served upon the Commission, and thereupon the Commission
shall certify and file in court a copy of the record before the Com
mission, including therein all pleadings, orders, documentary
exhibits, and the stenographic transcript of the testimony taken
before the Commission. When these have been filed, the court
shall have exclusive jurisdiction to affirm, modify, enforce, or
set aside the order, in whole or in part. No objection to the order
may be considered by the court unless it was urged before the
Commission or there were reasonable grounds for failure to do
so. The findings of the Commission as to the facts, if supported
by substantial evidence, are conclusive. If either party applies
to the court for leave to adduce additional evidence, and shows
to the satisfaction of the court that the additional evidence is
material and that there were reasonable grounds for failure to ad
duce the evidence in the hearings before the Commission, the
court may order that additional evidence be taken before the
Commission in such manner and upon such condition as the court
may consider proper. The Commission may modify its findings
as to the facts, by reason of the additional evidence so taken; and
it shall file any modified or new findings with the court, which if
supported by substantial evidence shall be conclusive, and any
recommendation for the modification or setting aside of the
original order. The commencement of the proceedings under
this section does not, unless specifically ordered by the court,
operate as a stay of the Commission's order. An appeal may be
taken from the judgment of the Franklin Circuit Court to the
Court of Appeals on the same terms and conditions as an appeal
is taken in any civil action.
Section 3. Section 350.050 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
The Division of Strip Mining and Reclamation of the De
partment of Conservation, under the supervision of the Com
missioner of Conservation, shall have and exercise the following
authority and powers:
( 1 ) To exercise general supervision and administration and

Chapter 105 403
enforcement of this chapter and all rules and regulations and
orders promulgated thereunder;
(2) To encourage and conduct investigations, research, ex
periments and demonstrations, and to collect and disseminate in
formation relating to strip mining and the reclamation of lands
and waters affected by strip mining;
[ ( 3 ) To adopt, after giving notice to all operators actively
engaged in strip mining and affording them an opportunity to
appear and offer evidence at a public hearing, general rules and
regulations pertaining to strip mining to accomplish the pur
poses of this chapter;]
(3) [4] To adopt, without hearing, rules and regulations
with respect to [procedural aspects of hearings,] the filing of
reports, [and orders,] the issuance of permits and other matters
of procedure and administration;
[(5) To issue, after hearing, orders requiring the adoption
by an operator of such remedial measures as are found to be
necessary for the reclamation of the area of land affected by his
operation;]
(4) [6] To examine and pass upon all plans and specifi
cations submitted by the operator for the reclamation of the area
of land affected by his operation;
(5) [7] To make investigations or inspections which mav
be deemed necessary to insure compliance with any provision of
this chapter; [and to examine or compel the production of any
records or memoranda relating to a strip mining operation;]
[(8) To conduct hearings under regulations of the Division
of Strip Mining and Reclamation that have been approved by
the Commissioner of Conservation, and to compel the attendance
of witnesses and examine them under oath at such hearings.]
(6) To order, through personnel of the Division, the stop
ping of any operation that is started without first having secured
a permit as required by this chapter.
Section 4. Section 350.060 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:

404 Acts of the General Assembly
( 1 ) No operator shall engage in strip mining without having
first obtained from the division a permit designating the area of
land affected by the operation. The permit shall authorize the
operator to engage in strip mining upon the area of land described
in his application for a period of one year from the date of its
issuance.
(2) An operator desiring a permit shall file an application
which shall state:
(a) The location and area of land to be affected by the
operation, with a description of access to the area from the near
est public highways;
( b ) The owner or owners of the surface of the area of land
to be affected by the permit;
(c) The owner or owners of the coal, or clay to be mined;
(d) The source of the applicant's legal right to enter and
mine the coal, or clay on the land affected by the permit;
(e) The permanent and temporary post office address of
the applicant;
(f) Whether the applicant or any person, partnership or
corporation associated with the applicant, holds or has held any
other permits under this chapter, and an identification of such
permits.
(3) The application for a permit shall be accompanied by
two copies of an accurate map of the area involved. The map
shall:
( a ) Be made by an engineer;
(b) Identify the area to correspond with the application;
(c) Not be part of a map made for some other purpose,
but shall show adjacent deep mining and adjacent surface owners;
(d) Be made to a scale of not less than one hundred feet
to the inch and not to exceed six hundred and sixty feet to the
inch;
( e ) Show the name of all creeks, roads, building, oil and gas

Chapter 105 405
wells and lines on the area to be strip mined and within two hun
dred feet of such area;
(f) Show the total area involved in the operation, the area
of the coal, or clay to be strip mined, and the area of land af
fected.
(4) A basic fee of fifty dollars plus fifteen [ten] dollars
for each acre or fraction thereof of the area of land to be affected
by the operation shall be paid before the permit required herein
shall be issued. The operator shall file with the division a bond
payable to the Commonwealth of Kentucky with surety satis
factory to the division in the penal sum to be determined by the
commission of not less than one hundred dollars nor more than
two hundred fifty dollars for each acre or fraction thereof of the
area of land affected, with a minimum bond of one thousand
dollars, conditioned upon the faithful performance of the require
ments set forth in this chapter and of the rules and regulations of
the commission. In determining the amount of the bond within
the above limits, the commission [division] shall take into con
sideration the purpose for which the bond is required, the char
acter and future suitable use of the land involved, and the cost
of reclamation to be required. In a particular instance where
the circumstances are such as to warrant an exception, the com
mission, in its discretion, may reduce the amount of the bond for
a particular operator to less than the required minimum.
Section 5. Section 350.070 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
The division [commission] may increase or reduce the area
of land affected by an operation under a permit on application
by an operator, but an increase shall not extend the period for
which an original permit was issued. An operator may, at any
time within one year from the date of issuance of the permit, apply
to the division [commission] for an amendment of the permit so
as to increase or reduce the acreage affected by it. The operator
shall file an application and map in the same form and with the
same content as required for an original application under KRS
350.060 and shall pay a basic fee of ten dollars, plus a fee of
fifteen [ten] dollars for each acre or fraction of acre for the in

406 Acts of the General Assembly
crease requested, and shall file with the division [commission] a
supplemental bond in the amount to be determined under the
provision of KRS 350.060 for each acre or fraction of an acre of
the increase approved. If the division [commission] approves a
reduction in the acreage covered by the original permit, it shall
release the bond for each acre reduced, but in no case shall the
bond be reduced below one thousand dollars, except as provided
in KRS 350.060 (4).
Section 6. Section 350.100 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
(1) It shall be the duty of an operator to commence the
reclamation of the area of land affected by his operation as soon
as possible after the beginning of strip mining of that area, and
to complete such reclamation as soon as may be feasible. Before
an operator commences to reclaim an area of land affected, he
shall file with the division [commission] a plan of reclamation.
The division [commission] may require modification of the plan
to meet the requirements of this chapter or the regulations of the
commission [of its regulations]. The division [commission] shall
allow the operator a reasonable period of time to complete the
reclamation of the area of land affected by his operation.
(2) If an investigation indicates that planting so as to pro
vide vegetative cover of an area of land affected by strip mining
may not be successful, the division [commission] may authorize
the operator to defer such planting until the soil has become
suitable for such purposes, or it may authorize the operator to do
planting to provide vegetative cover for a different area of land
in lieu of that covered by his permit. Such different area of land
must be land affected by strip mining in the past which has soil
that has become suitable for planting, of not less acreage than the
land covered by the permit, and be of a character similar to the
land covered by the permit. An application by the operator for
authority to plant a different area of land shall identify the dif
ferent area and shall be accompanied by a map showing its loca
tion, area and boundaries. The application shall be accompanied
by the written consent of the owner of the different area of land,
and by the written consent of the owner of the land covered by
the permit to release the operator from his obligation to provide

Chapter 105 407
a vegetative cover for the land covered by the permit. If the
division [commission] grants the application for the planting of a
different area of land and the planting is carried out in accordance
with its orders, the operator shall be relieved of his obligation to
provide a vegetative cover for the area of land affected by his
operation for which a different area of land has been substituted.
Section 7. A new section of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is created to read as follows:
Where one operator succeeds another at any uncompleted
operation, either by sale, assignment, lease, or otherwise, the divi
sion may release the first operator from all liability under this act
as to that particular operation: Provided, however, that both oper
ators have been issued a permit and have otherwise complied
with the requirements of this act, and the successor operator as
sumes as part of his obligation under this act all liability for the
reclamation of the area of land affected by the former operator.
Section 8. Section 350.110 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
If the division [commission] authorizes an operator to defer
planting to provide vegetative cover for an area of land, it shall
set the time within which such planting shall be carried out. If
the operator has carried out on that area of land the reclamation
plan approved by the division [commission] other than the plant
ing, the division [commission] shall issue to the operator and to
his surety a release of his surety bond or other securities held on
deposit for each acre of land for which such reclamation has
been carried out, less the sum of fifty dollars for each acre, but
in no case shall the bond be reduced below one thousand dollars
except as provided in KRS 350.060. The remaining amount of the
bond of fifty dollars for each acre of land affected shall remain
in the possession of the division [commission] until a satisfactory
planting plan has been carried out, or until it has been forfeited
by the operator. If the remainder is forfeited, it shall be expended
by the division [commission] in a planting program for the area
of land for which it has been posted.
Section 9. Section 350.120 of the Kentucky Revised Statute;
is amended to read as follows:

408 Acts of the General Assembly
Within sixty days after the date of expiration of a permit,
the operator shall file with the division [commission] a report
stating the exact number of acres of land affected by the operation,
the extent of reclamation already accomplished by him, and such
other information as may be required by the rules and regulations
of the division and the commission. The report shall be accom
panied by a copy of the map filed with the original application
which shall show any revisions made necessary by results of the
operation. If the report shows that the operator has complied
with the requirements of this chapter and with the rules and
regulations of the commission and the division and has carried out
a plan of reclamation approved by the division [commission] , the
division [commission] shall approve the report and the bond
filed by him as surety shall be released by the division [commis
sion] . If the division [commission] does not approve the report,
the bond shall not be released until the operator complies with
the orders of the commission and the division to meet require
ments of this chapter and of its regulations.
Section 10. Section 350.130 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
( 1 ) If the requirements of this chapter or the orders of the
division and the commission have not been complied with within
the time limits set by the division or the commission or by this
chapter, the division [commission] shall cause a notice of non
compliance to be served upon the operator by registered mail ad
dressed to the permanent address shown on the application for
a permit. The notice shall specify in what respects the operator
has failed to comply with this chapter or the regulations or orders
of the division and the commission. If the operator has not
reached an agreement with the division [commission] or has not
complied with the requirements set forth by it within forty days
after mailing of the notice, the permit may be revoked by the
division [commission] and the performance bond shall then be
forfeited to the division [commission] .
(2) When a bond is forfeited pursuant to the provisions of
this chapter, the director shall give notice to the Attorney Gen
eral, who shall collect the forfeiture.

Chapter 105 409
Section 11. Section 350.140 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
(1) All sums received through the payment of fees or
the forfeiture of bonds shall be placed in the State Treasury and
credited to a special agency account to be designated as the Strip
Mining and Reclamation Fund.
(2) This fund shall be available to the division [commis
sion] and may be expended for the administration and enforce
ment of this chapter and for the reclamation of land affected
by strip mining operations. Any unencumbered and any unex
pended balance of this fund remaining at the end of any fiscal
year shall not lapse but shall be carried forward for the purposes
of this chapter until expended or until appropriated by subse
quent legislative action.
( 3 ) All such forfeitures hereafter collected, as provided in
this chapter, shall be expended to reclaim and rehabilitate land
affected in accordance with the provisions of this chapter. Inso-
fat as is reasonably practicable, the monies shall be expended upon
the lands for which the permit was issued and for which the bond
was given.
Section 12. Section 350.150 of the Kentucky Revised Stat-
utes is amended to read as follows:
( 1 ) In the reclamation of land affected by strip mining for
which it has funds available, the division [commission] may avail
itself of any services which may be provided by other state
agencies or by agencies of the Federal government, and may com
pensate them for such services. The division [commission] may
cause the reclamation work to be done by its own employees or
by employees of other governmental agencies or through con
tracts with qualified persons. Such contracts shall be awarded
to the lowest or best bidder upon competitive bids after reasonable
advertisement. The division [commission] and any other agency
and any contractor under a contract with the division [commis
sion] shall have the right of access to the land affected to carry
out such reclamation.
(2) A person or organization having qualifications accept
able to the commission, may post bond and assume the liability

410 Acts of the General Assembly
for carrying out the reclamation plan approved by the division
in areas where the mining operation and any necessary grading
have been completed. The division shall then release the bond
posted by the operator for such area.
Section 13. A new section of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is created to read as follows:
The Strip Mining and Reclamation Commission may appoint
such advisory committees as would be of assistance to the com
mission in the development of programs and policies.
Section 14. Section 350.990 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
(1) Any person or operator who violates any of the pro
visions of this chapter or who fails to perform the duties imposed
by these sections, or fails or refuses to obtain a permit as provided
herein, or who violates any determination or order promulgated
pursuant to the provisions of this chapter, shall be liable to a civil
penalty of not more than two hundred fifty dollars for said viola
tion, and an additional civil penalty of not more than two hun
dred fifty dollars for each day during which such violation con
tinues, and in addition, may be enjoined from continuing such
violations as hereinafter provided in this section. Such penalties
shall be recoverable in an action brought in the name of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky by the Attorney General in the
Franklin Circuit Court or in the circuit court having jurisdiction
of the defendant, and all sums recovered shall be placed in the
State Treasury and credited to the Strip Mining and Reclamation
Fund.
(2) It shall be the duty of the Attorney General, upon the
request of the director to bring an action for the recovery of
the penalties herein provided for and to bring an action for a
restraining order, temporary or permanent injunction, against
any operator or other person violating or threatening to violate
any of the provisions of this chapter or violating or threatening to
violate any order or determination promulgated pursuant to the
provisions of this chapter.
(3) Any person who shall wilfully violate any of the pro
visions of this chapter, or any determination or order promul

Chapter 106 411
gated pursuant to the sections of this chapter which have be
come final shall be guilty of a misdemeanor and upon conviction
thereof shall be punished by a fine of not less than five hundred
dollars and not more than one thousand five hundred dollars.
Each day upon which such violation occurs shall constitute a sep
arate offense.
Section 15. The provisions of Chapter 350 of the Kentucky
Revised Statutes shall not be applicable to the mining of ball clay.
Section 16. Section 350.160, 350.180 and 350.190 of the
Kentucky Revised Statutes are repealed.
Approved March 19, 1962
CHAPTER 106
(S. B. 153)
AN ACT relating to the executive branch of state government.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
ARTICLE I. General Administrative Organization
Section 1. Section 12.010 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
(1) In this chapter, unless the context requires otherwise:
(a) "Department" means that basic unit of administrative
organization of state government, by whatever name called,
designated by statute or by appropriate executive action as a
"department";
(b) "Division" means a major branch of a department estab
lished by statute, or by administrative action;
(c) "Administrative body" includes authority, board, bureau,
commission, committee, conference, council, office, and any
other form of organization in the executive branch of government,^'
but does not include "department," "agency," or "division.''

412 Acts of the General Assembly
(2) As used in KRS 12.010 to 12.070, "agency" means a
group of departments, or departments and commissions, or other
administrative bodies, designated by statute or appropriate execu
tive action as an "agency."
[(1) "Department" means any executive or administrative
agency of the state named in KRS 12.020 as an administrative
department; but does not mean any legislative or judicial agency.
(2) "Agency" means any state officer, department, board,
commission, institution, division, or other person or functional
group exercising any executive or administrative function of the
state.
(3) "Division" means a major branch of an administrative
department established by statute or by administrative action.
(4) "State service" means the organization of the executive
and administrative departments of the state government, and the
Legislative Council.
(5) "Position" means any office or employment, or com
bination of them, in the state service, involving an aggregation
of duties to be performed and responsibilities to be exercised by
one person.
(6) "Employe" means the lawful incumbent of a position.
(7) "Independent agency" means any agency of the state
government which is not a part of an administrative department.]
Section 2. A new section of KRS 12.010 to 12.070 is created
to read as follows:
Unless specifically provided otherwise, each administrative
body established by statute or the appropriate executive action
shall be included for administrative purposes in a department or
an agency. When an administrative body is established and the
law establishing it does not specify the department or agency
within which the body is to be included, the Governor shall assign
the body to an existing department or agency.
