Section 4. A new section of KRS 12.010 to 12.070 is created
to read as follows:
(1) The Health and Welfare Agency is established. It
consists of the Department of Child Welfare, the Commission for
Handicapped Children, the Department of Mental Health, the
Department of Economic Security, the Tuberculosis Hospital
Commission, the Department of Health, the Kentucky Commis
sion on Aging, and the Commission on Children and Youth.
(2) For the purpose of consulting or dealing with the
federal government or its representatives in relation to any pro
gram or matter connected with the activity of any department,
commission, or administrative body in the Health and Welfare
Agency, the administrator of the Health and Welfare Agency is
the Commonwealth's single agent. He may delegate this author
ity in specific instances.
Section 5. Section 12.025 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
(1) Recognizing the necessity for grouping certain related
functions of departments and administrative bodies in order to
promote greater economy, efficiency and improved administration
in establishing more effective organizational patterns and also
recognizing the fact that such groupings and revisions in the gen
eral organizational structure need to be made as rapidly as pos
sible when administrative functions change and the needs of
government dictate, the Governor may:
[Recognizing the problems resulting from a rapid expansion
of programs in many departments and in order to promote greater
economy, efficiency and improved administration in the statutory
departments of state government by grouping related functions
and establishing more effective organizational patterns, both of

Chapter 106 417
which require extensive studies extending beyond the regular
legislative session, the Governor may:]
(a) [(1)] Establish, abolish, or alter the organization of
any agency or statutory administrative department, including
changing the name of a department to explain more clearly the
functions performed by it. Also included in this authority shall
be permission to transfer functions, personnel, funds, equipment,
facilities, and records from one department to another. Re
organization made under this section shall be set forth in an
Executive Order, signed by the Governor and filed in the office
of the Secretary of State, which shall explain the changes made
and designate the functions, personnel, funds, equipment, facili
ties, and records, as applicable, to be transferred. The Governor
shall recommend legislation to the next following session of the
[1962] General Assembly to confirm reorganizations effected
under the provisions of this section.
(b) [(2)] Appoint advisory or study committees to assist
and recommend to him on problems concerning the various func
tions and departments of state government. Members of such
committees shall serve without pay, but shall be reimbursed for
necessary and actual expenses.
(2) Recognizing that the purpose of the establishment of
an agency is to increase the economy and efficiency of adminis
tration, the Governor may supplement or limit, or both, the gen
eral powers and duties of agency administrators as they apply
to the administrator of a particular agency. Matters relating
to the powers and duties of a particular agency administrator
shall be set forth in an executive order. In supplementing or
limiting the general powers of an agency administrator as they
apply to the administrator of a particular agency, the Governor
shall have regard for the general nature of the activities embraced
by the agency.
Section 6. Section 12.030 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
The work of each agency, or department where the depart
ment is not a part of an agency, shall be distributed among the
boards, commissions, divisions, [and] institutions, and administra
tive bodies constituting the unit. [provided for by law] An

418 Acts of the General Assembly
Agency Administrator, or a department head in those cases where
the department is not a part of an agency, may submit a recom
mendation that the Governor by executive order, establish, abolish,
or change the name of subordinate units, or transfer the functions,
staff, and funds from one unit to another within the agency or
department. Such recommendations or proposals shall be sub
mitted to the Commissioner of Finance who shall transmit them
along with his comments and recommendations to the Governor.
[On recommendation of the head of a department concurred in
by the Commissioner of Finance, the Governor in writing may
authorize the head of any statutory department to establish
additional divisions, divide or combine existing divisions, change
the name of divisions, or transfer functions and staff from one
division to another within his department. The Commissioner
of Finance, with prior written approval of the Governor, may
authorize any department head to create, combine or divide sub
ordinate units within a division or institution, or otherwise alter
the internal organization of a division or institution.]
Section 7. A new section of KRS 12.010 to 12.070 is created
to read as follows:
When the Governor effects a reorganization under KRS
12.025 or 12.030, by executive order, he shall clearly designate
the order as one relating to reorganization. The Secretary of
State shall keep a separate journal of all executive orders relating
to organization, and he shall keep a current index to this separate
journal.
Section 8. Section 12.040 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
( 1 ) The heads of departments [enumerated in KRS 12.020]
shall have direction and control of their respective departments,
and through their departments shall exercise the powers and
perform the duties vested in the departments under their direction
and control, [and in any agencies thereof] Except as otherwise
expressly provided by law, the heads of [statutory administrative]
departments shall be appointed by the Governor for terms not
exceeding four years on the basis of their merit and fitness to
perform the duties of their respective offices.

Chapter 106 419
(2) Each department head shall maintain a pattern of organi
zation capable of receiving the attachment of administrative bodies
that have functions related to the general function of his depart
ment.
(3) Within the resources of his department, each department
head shall provide administrative bodies in his department with
such facilities and services as will enable those bodies to carry
out the functions with which they are charged.
(4) [(2)] The heads of all departments shall exercise
supervision over the personnel and financial records of their
respective departments.
(5) [(3)] In case of a vacancy or in the absence or dis
ability of the head of a [statutory administrative] department, the
Governor may authorize the head of a division or other adminis
trative officer to act as head of the department.
Section 9. A new section of KRS 12.010 to 12.070 is created
to read as follows:
(1) For each agency established, the Governor shall ap
point an administrator, who shall be a person with experience
or training in administration, who shall serve at the Governor's
pleasure. The position of agency administrator is not within the
state classified service.
( 2 ) The administrator shall coordinate programs conducted
by the departments and commissions in his agency and shall
coordinate the agency programs with related programs conducted
by all other departments. commissions and agencies.
(3) The administrator shall continuously study the pro
grams of public service or regulation administered by the depart
ments and commissions in his agency. When various aspects
of a single broad program are being administered by two or more
departments or commissions in his agency, or when a clearly
needed program has not been assumed by any department or
commission, the administrator shall consult with all department
heads and commissions concerned and determine the most
economical and efficient course of action.
(4) The administrator shall continuously study the internal
administration of his agency and of each department and com

420 Acts of the General Assembly
mission in it. When the economy or efficiency, or both, of the
operations of departments and commissions in his agency can
be increased by the consolidation of facilities or services, the
administrator may recommend to the Governor that the necessary
consolidation be effected under provisions of KRS 12.030. He
may recommend consolidation of these facilities or services in a
separate administrative unit, or he may recommend that facilities
or services existing in one department or commission be expanded
to serve the entire agency.
(5) The administrator is granted all powers reasonably
necessary to enable him to discharge the duties placed on him.
(6) In case of a vacancy or in the absence or disability of
the administrator of an agency, the Governor may authorize the
head of a department or other administrative officer to act as
administrator of the agency.
Section 10. Section 12.070 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
(1) Except as otherwise expressly provided by law, the
members of each administrative board or commission shall be
citizens of Kentucky appointed by the Governor from the state
at large on the basis of their interests in public affairs, their good
judgment, and their knowledge and ability in the field of action
of the department for which appointed, with a view to providing
diversity of interests and points of view in the membership and
without regard to their political or religious opinions or affilia
tions. Before entering upon the duties of their office, all mem
bers of administrative boards and commissions shall take the
oath of office prescribed by Section 228 of the Constitution.
Where appointments to administrative boards and commissions
are made from lists submitted to him, the Governor may reject
the list and require that other lists be submitted.
(2) When a board, commission, or similar administrative
body is established and no provision is made for compensation of
members, members shall serve without pay, but may receive reim
bursement for their actual and necessary expenses.
Section 11. Subsection (2) of Section 64.640 of the Ken
tucky Revised Statutes is amended to read as follows:

ARTICLE II. Department of State
Section 1. As used in KRS Chapters 116, 117, 118, 119 and
124, the terms "State Board of Election Commissioners," "Statf

422 Acts of the General Assembly
Board of Registration and Purgation," and, where applicable,
"state board," mean the State Board of Elections.
Section 2. Section 116.010 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
There shall be a State Board of Elections [Election Commis
sioners] consisting of the Secretary of State and two commis
sioners appointed by the Governor, in the month of April each
year, for a term of one year and until their successors are appointed
and qualified. The appointed commissioners shall be at least
twenty-five years of age and shall be qualified electors of this
state. One commissioner shall be appointed from each of the
two political parties that polled the largest vote in the last pre
ceding election for state officials or presidential electors. If
names are designated to the Governor in writing by the state
central committees of the parties, the appointments shall be made
from those names. The commissioners shall qualify by taking,
before the Secretary of State, an oath faithfully to perform their
duties according to law. The Secretary of State shall make a
certificate of the qualification, which shall be noted upon the
record of the proceedings of the board and preserved among
its records. Vacancies shall be filled by appointment by the
Governor in the same manner as provided for original appoint
ments, and the person appointed to fill the vacancy shall be of
the same political party as his predecessor.
ARTICLE III. Department of Public Safety
Section 1. (1) The Public Safety Advisory Committee is
established in the Department of Public Safety. The committee
shall advise the Commissioner of Public Safety on matters relating
to local fire departments, liquefied petroleum gas safety, traffic
safety, and on other matters on which the Commissioner of Public
Safety may require advice. The Commissioner of Public Safety
is chairman of the committee, and he may divide the committee
into subcommittees.
(2) The Governor shall appoint members of the commit
tee, on recommendation of the Commissioner of Public Safety.
The Governor may appoint members from the memberships of
previously existing boards or committees that had functions

Chapter 106 423
similar to those of the committee. He may appoint members
to serve at his pleasure, or he may appoint them for terms, and
if he so elects he may stagger terms.
Section 2. ( 1 ) The Department of Public Safety shall:
(a) Receive and file fingerprints, photographs and
other records pertaining to the investigation of crime and the
apprehension of criminals; and
(b) cooperate with the state, county and city law
enforcing agencies of other states and of the United States in
order to develop and carry on an interstate and national system
of criminal identification.
(2) Persons in charge of any penal or correctional institu
tion in the state, and all state law enforcement and peace officers
operating identification facilities shall cooperate in providing the
department with fingerprints and descriptions of all persons law
fully committed to their custody or detained by them in cases
where fingerprints and descriptions are taken, together with a
report of the disposition of all cases of such persons.
Section 3. Sections 17.220, 17.230, 17.240, 196.090, and
234.170 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes are repealed.

Section 1. Sections 135.140, 135.150, 135.160, 135.170, and

Section 5. As used in Sections 6 and 7 of this Article,
"division", means the Division of Occupations and Professions.
Section 6. The Division of Occupations and Professions is
established in the Department of Finance. The division is headed
by a director, appointed by the Commissioner of Finance with
the approval of the Governor.
Section 7. (1) The function of the division is to provide
administrative services to the following boards and commissions

which maintain their identity and their full authority for making

The State Board of Accountancy, the office of the Boxing

(2) To the extent that the division does provide adminis

Section 8. The activation of the provisions of Sections 6

quire it. If and when the Governor does so find, he shall declare

Section 4. Section 152.060 of the Kentucky Bevised Stat

(1) The department shall prepare and publish as often as

prises within the Commonwealth.

lished in the Department of Commerce.

ARTICLE VII. Department of Public Information
Section 1. A new section of KRS Chapter 153 is created to
read as follows:
As used in this chapter, "Department of Public Relations"
means "Department of Public Information."
ARTICLE VIII. Department of Libraries
Section 1. As used in KRS 171.130 to 171.300, unless the
context requires otherwise: "Department," "Library Extension
Division," "extension division," and "division" mean the Depart
ment of Libraries.

Chapter 106 43i
Section 2. Section 171.130 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
The Department of Libraries is established. The department
shall be headed by a State Librarian who shall be appointed by
and serve at the pleasure of the Governor, and who shall have
had technical training in the field of library science.
[The agency of the state government heretofore known as
the Library Extension Division of the Department of Library and
Archives shall hereafter constitute an independent agency of the
state government, within the meaning of KRS Chapter 12, under
the name of Library Extension Division. The division shall be
headed by a director who shall be appointed by and serve at
the pleasure of the Governor, and who shall receive a salary to
be fixed by the Governor, subject to the provisions of KRS 64.640.
The director shall have had technical training in the field of
library science and shall have had at least two years of library
experience in an administrative capacity.]
Section 3. Section 171.202 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
The State Librarian shall administer the provisions of KRS
171.201 to 171.205. The Commissioner of Finance shall approve
expenditures of funds under these sections.
[The Director of the Library Extension Division, the Com
missioner of Finance and the Attorney General shall constitute a
Public Library Service Commission for the purpose of adminis
tering the provisions of KRS 171.201 to 171.205. The commission
shall elect a chairman and a secretary from among its members.
It shall promulgate rules and regulations for the administration
of KRS 171.201 to 171.205 and for the allocation of funds appro
priated for the purposes of KRS 171.201 to 171.205. In allocating
funds to counties, the commission shall give primary considera
tion to the need for additional library service in each county.]
Section 4. Section 171.203 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
Any funds appropriated for the purposes of KRS 171.201 to
171.205 shall be expended for aiding in the establishment, ex

432 Acts of the General Assembly
tension and development of local public library facilities (ex
clusive of library buildings, equipment and furniture) through
the purchase of books or bookmobiles, through technical assist
ance, educational and promotional programs, and such other
methods and services as will best accomplish the purpose. Funds
in the Public Library Service Fund shall be paid out by the
State Treasurer on warrants drawn by the Commissioner of
Finance on order of the State Librarian. [Public Library Service
Commission.]
Section 5. Section 171.204 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
To the extent of funds available, grants shall be made to
counties applying for state aid, in the order in which they meet
the requirements established by the State Librarian. [Public
Library Service Commission.] Grants-in-aid thus provided shall
not exceed $5,000 to any county. Each county receiving a state
grant shall expend from local funds, for countywide public
library service during the years for which aid is received a sum
equal to at least five percent of the amount received from state
funds; the ratio of matching to be based on local financial ability
as determined by the assessed valuation of the county.
Section 6. (1) There is hereby established a Library
Science Scholarship Fund.
(2) The State Librarian may grant scholarships for study
in library science at an institution within the Commonwealth
which is accredited by the American Library Association or the
Southern Association of Colleges and Secondary Schools.
(3) The State Librarian shall receive and consider all
applications for scholarships for study in library science and may
grant a scholarship to applicants who are residents of this Com
monwealth and who are deemed by the State Librarian to be
qualified. The State Librarian shall make a careful and full
investigation of the ability, character and qualifications of each
applicant and may personally examine each applicant. The State
Librarian shall, whenever possible, grant financial assistance to
the applicants with the greatest financial need, provided such
persons are found to possess such qualities as give reasonable

Chapter 106 433
assurance of their successfully completing the course of study
made possible by the scholarship.
(4) A scholarship shall not exceed two thousand dollars
per annum and no recipient of a scholarship shall receive more
than two thousand dollars for his entire course of study.
Section 7. (1) To be eligible for a scholarship, an ap
plicant shall contract in writing with the State Librarian that
he will, within six months from the date he completes his term
of study, accept employment with a library program approved
by the State Librarian for a period to be computed at the rate
of one year for each one thousand dollars received, or propor
tional time for lesser amounts.
(2) If the recipient of a scholarship fails to fulfill his
obligations under the contract for a scholarship the entire amount
of scholarship benefits received plus six percent interest thereon
shall become due and payable.
(3) Upon recommendation of the State Librarian, the
Attorney General shall institute proceedings for the purpose of
recovering any amount due the Commonwealth under the pro
visions of this section.
Section 8. This Article becomes effective July 1, 1962.
ARTICLE IX. Department of Economic Security
Section 1. As used in Sections 1 to 6 of this Article, unless
the context otherwise requires, "Commission" means the Kentucky
Commission on Employment of the Physically Handicapped.
Section 2. ( 1 ) There is hereby created a Kentucky Com
mission on Employment of the Physically Handicapped within
the Department of Economic Security. The commission shall
consist of twenty-two members and shall include the Commis
sioner of the Department of Economic Security, the Superin
tendent of Public Instruction and twenty members to be ap
pointed by the Governor. A chairman of the commission shall
be elected at the first meeting and annually thereafter from the
twenty appointed members.

434 Acts of the General Assembly
( 2 ) Of the twenty members first appointed by the Governor
five shall serve for terms of one year, five for terms of two years,
five for terms of three years and five for terms of four years.
At the expiration of these terms appointments shall be for terms
of four years. All twenty appointees shall be eligible for re
appointment and shall serve until their successors have been
appointed and qualified. Vacancies occurring prior to the ex
piration of a term shall be filled by the Governor for the remainder
of the unexpired term.
(3) The commission shall meet at least four times annually
on call of the chairman or on the written request of any eleven
members. A majority of the members of the commission shall
constitute a quorum for the transaction of business. If at any
time the Commissioner of the Department of Economic Security
or the Superintendent of Public Instruction is unable to attend
a commission meeting he shall designate another person who
has authority to act in his behalf at the meeting and who shall
be considered a member of the commission for the purpose of
obtaining a quorum and transacting business.
Section 3. (1) The commission with the approval of the
Department of Economic Security may employ such additional
personnel as may be reasonably necessary for the performance
of the duties prescribed by Sections 1 to 6 of this Article and may
create such advisory committees as it deems necessary. The
commission shall designate a commission member as chairman
of each advisory committee.
(2) The advisory committee members and the twenty
appointed members of the commission shall serve without com
pensation but shall be reimbursed by the Department of Economic
Security for necessary and actual travel expenses incurred in
carrying out the duties of the commission as set forth in Section
4 of this Article. Members of the advisory committees or the
commission shall not be reimbursed for their expenses if they
are employed by any state, federal or county agency and are
allowed travel expenses by their employers.
Section 4. The duties of the commission shall be to:
(1) Promote and aid in the establishment of local pro
grams supporting the employment of the physically handicapped;

Chapter 106 435
(2) Conduct continuing programs of public information
and promotion to educate the public as to problems of employment
of the physically handicapped;
(3) Review existing state programs for employment of the
physically handicapped and make recommendations to the Gov
ernor, to the appropriate departments and agencies of the state,
and to the General Assembly for improvements in and additions
to such programs;
(4) Assist and encourage governmental and private
agencies to coordinate their efforts on behalf of employment of
the physically handicapped;
(5) Cooperate with the federal government and with the
governments of other states and cities in programs relating to
the physically handicapped;
( 6 ) Cooperate with all groups interested in job adjustment
and employment of the physically handicapped, including gov
ernment agencies, organizations, private groups and individuals;
(7) Furnish the Department of Economic Security an
annual report together with recommendations.
Section 5. The Department of Economic Security shall
provide the commission with such staff assistance and informa
tion as it may require for proper performance of its duties as
provided for in Sections 1 to 6 of this Article.
Section 6. The commission shall be empowered to accept
gifts and grants at its discretion. Such funds shall go into a trust
and agency fund to be disbursed by the Commission in further
ance of the provisions of Sections 1 to 6 of this Article.
Section 7. Sections 1 to 6 of this Article become effective
July 1, 1962.

ARTICLE XI. Department of Mental Health
Section 1. A new section of KRS Chapter 210 is created
to read as follows:
As used in this chapter, "mentally retarded" and "mentally
defective" have the same meaning. They apply to one or more
"mentally defective persons," as the singular of that term is
defined in KRS 202.010.
Section 2. The Department of Mental Health shall:
(1) Maintain, operate, and assume program responsibility
for all state institutions and facilities for mental retardation;
(2) Provide rehabilitation services for mentally retarded
persons through educational and training programs;
(3) Provide medical and allied services to mentally re
tarded persons and their families;
( 4 ) Encourage and assist communities to develop programs
and facilities in the field of mental retardation;
(5) Sponsor or carry out research, or both, in the field of
mental retardation;
(6) Assist other governmental and private agencies in the
development of programs and services for mentally retarded per
sons and their families and for the prevention of mental retarda
tion, and coordinate programs and services so developed.
Section 3. The Department of Mental Health may:

Chapter 106 441
(1) Promulgate reasonable rules and regulations for the
purposes of carrying out the provisions of Section 2 of this Article,
including regulations establishing the minimum and maximum
ages within which mentally retarded persons are eligible:
(a) to participate in programs operated by the department;
(b) to become patients in institutions operated by the de
partment;
( 2 ) Participate in the education and training of professional
and other persons in the field of mental retardation, and may
encourage and assist private and public agencies and institutions
to participate in similar education and training;
(3) Do all other things reasonably necessary to carry out
the provisions of Section 2 of this Article.
Section 4. A new section of KRS Chapter 210 is created
to read as follows:
The Division of Mental Retardation is established in the
Department of Mental Health. The head of the division shall be
appointed by the Commissioner with the approval of the
Governor.
Section 5. A new section of Chapter 210 of the Kentucky
Revised Statutes is created to read as follows:
(1) There shall be established an Advisory Council on
Mental Retardation attached to the Department of Mental Health.
(2) The Advisory Council on Mental Retardation shall be
composed of five members, appointed by the Governor, for terms
of four years. Members shall serve until their successors are
appointed. The board shall elect from its membership a chair
man. Members of the board shall serve without compensation
but shall be reimbursed for necessary expenses incurred in the
performance of their duties.
(3) The Advisory Council on Mental Retardation shall act
in an advisory capacity to the Commissioner of Mental Health
in regard to the mental retardation program.
Section 6. Section 202.005 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:

442 Acts of the General Assembly
Wherever used in this chapter, the terms "department" and

196.530, 196.540, 196.550, and 203.230 of the Kentucky Revised

Section 9. This Article becomes effective July 1, 1962.
ARTICLE XII. Department of Health.
Section 1. (1) The Department of Health shall study the
problems of alcoholism within the state, formulate and carry out
programs of education designed to curb the excessive use of
alcohol and reduce the number of alcoholics, establish such
facilities and employ such personnel as are necessary and avail
able for the diagnosis and rehabilitation of alcoholics, and accept
for examination, diagnosis, guidance and treatment any person
coming to it of his own volition for advice and guidance.
(2) The department may contract for space and facilities
to be used for out-patient clinics, and may contract with hos
pitals and private doctors for the emergency hospitalization and
care of chronic alcoholics. The department may enter into
cooperative agreements with municipal and other agencies
operating facilities for treating alcoholics, and it may employ
such medical, psychiatric, psychological, secretarial and other
assistance as may be necessary to carry out the purposes of
this section.

Chapter 106 443
(3) The department may accept gifts, bequests, contribu
tions and patient fees, at its discretion, and use such funds to
further the purposes of this section.
(4) The department may establish rules and regulations
for the admission of patients to its facilities and may set fees for
treatment. Fees shall be scaled according to the ability of
the patient to pay.
Section 2. Section 222.020 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
( 1 ) [For the purpose of carrying out the provisions of KRS
222.010 to 222.070] There is hereby created a commission to be
hereafter known as the Kentucky Commission on Alcoholism.
[The commission shall constitute an agency independent of the
administrative department provided for in Chapter 12 of the
Kentucky Revised Statutes.]
( 2 ) The commission shall consist of seven members with an
interest in and knowledge of the alcoholism problem, said mem
bers to be appointed by the Governor. At least two of the
seven members shall be doctors, and one of the doctors shall be
a psychiatrist. Three members shall be appointed for a term of
one year, two members shall be appointed for a term of two
years and two members shall be appointed for a term of three
years. At the expiration of these terms appointments shall be
for terms of four years. Appointments shall be made by the
Governor to fill any existing vacancies.
(3) The duties of the commission shall be to advise the
Department of Health on all matters relating to alcoholism.
[study the problems of alcoholism within the state, to formulate
and carry out programs of education designed to curb the exces
sive use of alcohol and reduce the number of alcoholics, to
establish such facilities and employ such personnel as are neces
sary and available for the diagnosis and rehabilitation of alco
holics, to accept for examination, diagnosis, guidance and treat
ment, insofar as funds permit, any person coming to it of his
own volition for advice and guidance.]
[(4) A chairman and a vice-chairman of the commission
shall be elected at the first meeting and every year thereafter.

444 Acts of the General Assembly
The commission shall keep a complete and accurate record of
all its proceedings.]
Section 3. Sections 222.010, 222.025, 222.030, 222.040,
222.050, 222.060, and 222.070 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
are repealed.
ARTICLE XIII. Commission on Aging
Section 1. As used in this Article, unless the context other
wise requires:
"Commission" means the Kentucky Commission on Aging.
Section 2. ( 1 ) There is hereby created a Kentucky Com
mission on Aging, within the Health and Welfare Agency. The
commission shall consist of sixteen members and shall include
the Commissioner of the Department of Health, the Commissioner
of the Department of Mental Health, the Commissioner of the
Department of Economic Security, the Superintendent of Public
Instruction and twelve members to be appointed by the Governor,
not more than three of whom may be employees of the Common
wealth. A chairman of the commission shall be elected at the
first meeting and annually thereafter.
(2) Of the citizen members first appointed four shall serve
for terms of one year, four for terms of two years and four for
terms of three years. At the expiration of these terms appoint
ments shall be for terms of three years. All citizen members
shall be eligible for reappointment and shall serve until their
successors have been appointed and qualified. Vacancies occur
ring prior to the expiration of a term shall be filled by the Gover
nor for the remainder of the unexpired term.
(3) The commission shall meet at least six times annually
on call of the chairman or on the written request of any eight
members. A majority of the members of the commission shall
constitute a quorum for the transaction of business. If at any
time the Commissioner of the Department of Health, the Com
missioner of the Department of Mental Health, the Commissioner
of the Department of Economic Security, or the Superintendent
of Public Instruction is unable to attend a commission meeting
he shall designate another person who has authority to act in his

Chapter 106 445
behalf at the meeting and who shall be considered a member of
the commission for the purpose of obtaining a quorum and trans
acting business.
Section 3. The administrator of the Health and Welfare
Agency shall provide the commission with staff assistance, and
shall administer the program of the commission.
Section 4. (1) The citizen members of the commission
shall be reimbursed for necessary travel and other expenses in
curred in the performance of their duties.
(2) The commission may create such advisory committees
as it deems necessary and shall designate a commission member
as chairman of each advisory committee.
Section 5. The duties of the commission shall be to:
( 1 ) Promote and aid in the establishment of local programs
and services for the aging;
(2) Conduct programs to educate the public as to prob
lems of the aging;
(3) Review existing state programs of the aging and to
make recommendations to the Governor, to the appropriate de
partments and agencies of the state, and to the legislature for
improvements in and additions to such programs;
(4) Assist and encourage governmental and private
agencies to coordinate their efforts on behalf of the aging;
( 5 ) Conduct and encourage other organizations to conduct
studies concerning the aging;
(6) Establish, in selected areas and communities of the
state, programs of services for the aging to demonstrate the value
of such programs, and to encourage local agencies to continue
the programs and to create new services where needed. Em
phasis shall be given to services designed to foster continued
participation of older people in family and community life and
to lessen the need for institutional care;
(7) Cooperate with the federal government and with tb
governments of other states in programs relating to the aging.

446 Acts of the General Assembly
Section 6. The commission shall be empowered to accept
gifts and grants at its discretion. Such moneys shall go into a
trust and agency fund to be disbursed by the commission in
furtherance of the purposes of this Article.
Section 7. This Artiole becomes effective on July 1, 1962.
ARTICLE XIV. Commission on Children and Youth
Section 1. As used in this Article, unless the context other
wise requires, "Commission" means the Kentucky Commission
on Children and Youth.
Section 2. (1) There is hereby created within the Health
and Welfare Agency, a Kentucky Commission on Children and
Youth. The commission shall consist of the commissioners of
the Departments of Child Welfare, Economic Security, Health,
and Mental Health, the Superintendent of Public Instruction, the
chief executive officer of the Commission for Handicapped Chil
dren, and eighteen members to be appointed by the Governor.
A chairman of the commission shall be elected at the first meet
ing and annually thereafter.
(2) Of the eighteen members first appointed by the Gov
ernor, four shall serve for terms of one year, four for terms of
two years, five for terms of three years, and five for terms of
four years. At the expiration of these terms, appointments shall
be for terms of four years. All appointed members shall be
eligible for reappointment and shall serve until their successors
have been appointed and qualified. Vacancies occurring prior to
the expiration of a term shall be filled by the Governor for the
remainder of the unexpired term.
(3) The commission shall meet at least two times annually
on call of the chairman or on the written request of any thirteen
members. A majority of the members of the commission shall
constitute a quorum for the transaction of business.
Section 3. (1) The administrator of the Health and Wel
fare Agency shall furnish the commission with necessary staff
assistance, through either an administrative services unit of the
agency or the facilities of a department within the agency.

Chapter 106 447
( 2 ) The commission may appoint such advisory committees
as are necessary to advise it on specific matters relating to chil
dren and youth.
(3) Members of the commission shall serve without com
pensation but shall be reimbursed for necessary and actual ex
penses incurred in attending commission meetings.
Section 4. The duties of the commission shall be to:
(1) Promote and develop effective programs of education,
health, recreation, welfare, public safety and correctional serv
ices for children and youth;
(2) Conduct continuing programs of public information to
educate the public as to problems of children and youth;
(3) Assist and encourage governmental and private agen
cies to coordinate their efforts on behalf of children and youth;
(4) Cooperate with the federal government and with the
governments of other states and cities in programs relating to
children and youth;
(5) Conduct programs of research as to the needs of chil
dren and youth in order to facilitate more comprehensive and
better related social planning and action;
(6) Cooperate with the Department of Child Welfare, the
Department of Economic Security, the Department of Health,
the Department of Mental Health, and the Commission for Handi
capped Children in carrying out the duties prescribed by this
section by making the fullest possible use of the experience and
resources of those departments;
(7) Furnish the Governor a biennial report together with
recommendations.
Section 4. The commission shall be empowered to accept
gifts and grants at its discretion. Such funds shall go into a trust
and agency fund to be disbursed in furtherance of the purposes
of this Article.

CHAPTER 107
(S. B. 155)
AN ACT relating to retirement systems for public employees.
Be
itenacted
bythe General Assembly
ofthe Commonwealth
ofKentucky:
Section (1) Upon service retirement,
a member of the
Kentucky Employees' Retirement System may obtain credit for all
or any part of the service otherwise creditable under the County
Employees Retirement System, the Teachers' Retirement System,
or in the service of the United States Government for which
service credit
isnot otherwise given, upon the payment by the
member of such amount or amounts as are actuarially determined
to be equal to the increased liability of the Kentucky Employees'
Retirement System
byvirtue of such additional service credit.

Chapter 108 449
Section (2) Upon service retirement, a member of the
County Employees' Retirement System may obtain credit for all
or any part of the service otherwise creditable under the Ken
tucky Employees' Retirement System, the Teachers' Retirement
System, or in the service of the United States Government for
which service credit is not otherwise given, upon the payment
by the member of such amount or amounts as are actuarially
determined to be equal to the increased liability of the County
Employees' Retirement System by virtue of such additional service
credit.
Section (3) Upon service retirement, a member of the
Teachers' Retirement System may obtain credit for all or any part
of the service otherwise creditable under the Kentucky Em
ployees' Retirement System, the County Employees' Retirement
System, or in the service of the United States Government for
which service credit is not otherwise given, upon the payment
by the member of such amount or amounts as are actuarially
determined to be equal to the increased liability of the Teachers'

Section 4. The amount paid under this Act shall be con
sidered as accumulated contributions of the individual member.
Section 5. No person shall be allowed credit for the same
period of service in more than one of these three retirement
systems.
Approved March 19, 1962
CHAPTER 108
(S. B. 163)
AN ACT relating to motor vehicle tractor and semi-trailer units.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Motor vehicle tractor and semi-trailer units exclusively
engaged in the transportation of motor vehicles, unmanufac

450 Acts of the General Assembly
tured tobacco, or unmanufactured tobacco products, may, on

and which have been designated by the Commissioner of High

CHAPTER 109
(S. B. 216)
AN ACT relating to time credits on sentence, forfeiture of good time and
conditional release.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 197.045 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
(1) Any person convicted and sentenced to a state penal
institution may receive a credit on his sentence of not exceeding
ten days for each month served, to be determined by the Division
of Corrections of the Department of Welfare from the conduct
of the prisoner. The department shall have the authority to
forfeit any good time previously earned by the prisoner, or to
deny the prisoner the right to earn good time in any amount, if
during the term of imprisonment the prisoner commits any of
fense or violates the rules of the institution.
[(2) Any prisoner having served the term for which he
has been sentenced for a crime committed after the effective date
of this section, less good time deductions, shall be conditionally
released under supervision of the Division of Probation and
Parole, subject to the provision of law and department regula
tions relating to conditionally released prisoners.]
Section 2. Section 439.360 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is repealed.

Chapter 109 451
Section 3. Section 439.430 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
(1) Any parole, probation or conditional release officer
having reason to believe that a parolee or conditional releasee
has violated the terms of his release may arrest such person with
out a warrant or may deputize any other peace officer to do so
by giving him a written statement setting forth that the parolee
or conditional releasee, in the judgment of the probation, parole
or conditional release officer, has violated the conditions of his
release. Such written statement delivered with the parolee or
conditional releasee by the arresting officer to the official in
charge of the station house, jail, workhouse or other place of
detention, shall be sufficient warrant for the detention of the
parolee or conditional releasee. The probation, parole or con
ditional release officer who arrests or causes the arrest of the
prisoner shall notify the director at once of the arrest and
detention of the parolee or conditional releasee, and shall submit
in writing a report showing in what manner there has been a viola
tion of the conditions of release. Thereupon, if the director be
lieves the parolee or conditional releasee should be returned to
prison, the director at once shall submit his recommendations to
the board, and, if the board approves, it shall issue a warrant
upon which the releasee shall be returned to prison, otherwise the
prisoner shall be released upon the order of the director.
(2) A written statement, approved by the director, by a
parole or conditional release officer, and filed with the board
setting forth that the parolee or conditional releasee in the
judgment of such officer has violated the condition of his release,
shall be sufficient cause for the board, in its discretion, to issue
a warrant for the arrest of the parolee or conditional releasee, or
for his return to prison.
(3) A prisoner for whose return a warrant has been issued
by the board, shall be deemed a fugitive from justice or to have
fled from justice. If it shall appear he has violated the provisions
of his release, then the time from the issuing of such warrant
to the date of his arrest shall not be counted as any part of the
time to be served [under his sentence] in determining his final
discharge eligibility date from parole if the board in its discretion
so orders.
Approved March 19, 1962

452 Acts of the General Assembly
CHAPTER 110
(S. B. 232)
AN ACT relating to the Constitution Revision Committee and declaring
an emergency.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 7.170 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
(1) A Constitution Revision Committee is hereby created
as an agency of the Legislative Research Commission to carry
on a program of study, review, examination and exposition of the
Constitution of Kentucky [, in connection with the action taken
by the 1959 Extraordinary Session of the 1958 General Assembly
in enacting House Bill No. 1, and the amendatory, provisions
thereof] . The program shall be so designed and carried on as
to conduct any and all reasonable and appropriate studies, surveys,
analyses, and reports pertaining to or having a bearing thereon;
to analyze and appraise such suggestions for amendment or re
vision as mav be made by any person or persons, groups, or or
ganizations pertaining to or in opposition thereto; and to bring to
public attention such proposals for revision or amendment of the
Constitution in accordance herewith. The committee shall pre
pare and propose in detail [to the Legislative Research Com
mission] from time to time such drafts, amendments, or revision
of the Constitution as will promote the interest and welfare of
the Commonwealth and shall make such drafts public.
(2) The committee shall be composed of thirty-eight mem
bers appointed by majority vote of a committee composed of the
Governor, the Lieutenant Governor, the Speaker of the House of
Representatives and the Chief Justice of the Court of Appeals.
One member shall be appointed from each of the senatorial dis
tricts as set forth in KRS 6.010. The terms of the members initially
appointed shall extend through June 30, 1964 and their successors
shall be appointed for two year terms. All members shall be
eligible for reappointment and shall serve until their successors
have been appointed and qualified. Any vacancies shall be filled
for the remainder of the unexpired term from the senatorial dis
trict in which the vacancy occurred. The Committee upon its

Chapter 110 453
appointment shall elect from its membership a chairman and such
other officers as it deems necessary, to serve for such terms and
perform such duties as the committee shall prescribe. The com
mittee's initial meeting shall be upon call of the Legislative Re
search Commission, and subsequent meeting shall be upon call
of the chairman of the committee.
[ ( 2 ) The committee shall be composed of seven members,
to be appointed by the Governor with due consideration to repre
sentation of the varied professional, social and economic interests.
The Attorney General shall serve ex-officio, without vote, as an
additional member. The terms of the members initially appointed
shall extend through the 15th of December, 1960, and their suc
cessors shall be appointed for one year terms. All members shall
be eligible for reappointment and shall serve until their successors
have been appointed and qualified. Vacancies occurring prior to
the expiration of a term shall be filled by the Governor for the re
mainder of the unexpired term. The committee, upon its ap
pointment and annually thereafter, shall elect from its member
ship a chairman and a co-chairman. The committee shall meet
not less frequently than quarterly at times and places fixed by it
and upon call by the chairman or, in his absence, the co-chairman
at such other times as may be necessary to carry out its duties and
shall publish such report or reports of its activities as the com
mittee deems necessary, subject, however, to the provisions of
subsection (1) above.]
( 3 ) The members of the committee shall serve without com
pensation but shall be reimbursed for their necessary and actual
expenses in attending meetings of the committee and otherwise in
the performance of their duties from funds appropriated to the
Legislative Research Commission for the purposes of KRS 7.170
and 7.180 (or otherwise made available for committee use).
(4) The Legislative Research Commission, from funds ap
propriated to it for the purposes of KRS 7.170, shall conduct such
research studies as the committee may request. The committee
with the approval of the Legislative Research Commission mav
employ and may remove [an executive director to be responsible
for directing and coordinating research studies for the com
mittee and for seeking assistance and advice of interested persons
or groups. The executive director shall be a person who has

454 Acts of the General Assembly
demonstrated an interest in and knowledge of Kentucky consti
tutional development. The committee may employ] such
[other] personnel as may be reasonably necessary to carry out
the work of the committee. Expenditures under KRS 7.170 and
7.180 shall be paid upon vouchers approved by the director of
the Legislative Research Commission from appropriations made
to the Legislative Research Commission for the purposes of KRS
7.170 and 7.180.
[(5) The committee shall, not later than August 1, 1960,
and not less frequently than annually thereafter, schedule and
announce publicly in advance open hearings at which any person,
either individually or as a representative of a group, may present
suggestions for constitutional change. A summation of the sug
gestions presented at such hearings shall be included in each
annual report of the committee.]
(5) [6] The committee shall report to the Governor and
the General Assembly biennially, on or before December 31 pre
ceding each regular session of the General Assembly [by Septem
ber 15th of each year, report to the Legislative Research Com
mission] on the result of its studies and deliberations [during the
year]. [This report shall be published by the Legislative Re
search Commission no later than the 1st day of October, and such
reports shall be transmitted to the incumbent members of and
candidates for election to the General Assembly. Reports shall
be published in such quantities as to permit distribution to all
public agencies, educational institutions, and private groups with
demonstrated interest therein. If a convention shall be held to
revise the Constitution pursuant to the Act of the 1959 Extraordi
nary Session of the 1958 General Assembly, the committee shall
advise and assist that body insofar as it is practicable, and shall
report to it when requested.]
[ ( 7 ) The Governor is authorized to appoint such additional
study committees to assist and report to the Constitution Revision
Committee as he deems necessary or appropriate for the purpose
of investigating, study, analyzing, reviewing, reporting and mak
ing recommendations as to changes in the provisions of the Con
stitution of the Commonwealth of Kentucky pertaining to the
twelve subjects set forth in House Bill No. 1 of the 1959 Extraordi
nary Session of the 1958 General Assembly.]

Chapter 111 455
Section 2. Section 7.180 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is repealed.
Section 3. Whereas, it is necessary that members of the
Constitution Revision Committee be appointed as soon as possible
in order that they may begin to plan and execute studies, an
emergency is declared to exist, and this Act shall become effec
tive upon its passage and approval by the Governor.
Approved March 19, 1962
CHAPTER 111
(S. B. 237)
AN ACT relating to the taking of the sense of the people of the state as
to the necessity and expediency of calling a Constitutional Conven
tion for the purpose of revising or amending the Constitution of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky, and such amendments as may have
been made to the same, as provided by Section 258 of the present
Constitution of Kentucky.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. That the sense of the people of the state as to
the necessity and expediency of calling a convention for the
purpose of revising or amending the present Constitution, and
such amendments as may have been made to the same, shall be
taken as herein provided and as provided in Section 258 of the
Constitution of Kentucky.
Section 2. When this act is passed at the present session of
the General Assembly by a majority of all members elected to
each house of the present General Assembly, that is, when a
majority of all the members of each house shall concur by a yea
and nay vote, to be entered upon their respective journals, this
act shall be spread upon the respective journals of each house
as a law.

456 Acts of the General Assembly
Section 3. If the next General Assembly shall, in like
manner, concur in this act after it becomes a law as herein
provided, it shall provide for having a poll opened in each voting
precinct in this state by the officers provided by law for holding
general elections at the next ensuing regular election to be held
for state officers which does not occur within ninety days from
the final passage of such law, at which time and places the votes
of the qualified voters shall be taken for and against calling the
convention, in the same manner provided by law for taking votes
in other state elections. The vote for and against said proposi
tion shall be certified to the Secretary of State by the same
officers and in the same manner as in state elections. If it shall
appear that a majority voting on the proposition was in favor of
calling a convention, and if the total number of votes for the
calling of the convention is equal to one-fourth of the number of
qualified voters who voted in the last preceding general election
in this state, the Secretary of State shall certify the same to the
General Assembly at its next regular session, at which session
a law shall be enacted calling a convention to revise or amend
the present Constitution of this State, and such amendments as
may have been made thereto, only with respect to the subjects
set out in Section 4 of this act, and no others. Nothing in this
act shall be construed to permit any change in the Bill of Rights.
Section 4. The convention provided to be called by this
act shall be limited in its consideration to the following subjects
and shall consider no others:
( 1 ) The judicial department and courts.
(2) Compensation of public officers and employes.
(3) The order of succession of persons entitled to act as
Governor and the circumstances under which the Governor is
disqualified to act.
(4) The Railroad Commission.
(5) The Legislative Department.
(6) The mode of revision or amendment of the Constitu
tion.
(7) Incompatibility of offices.
(8) Promotion of Industrial Development.

Chapter 112 457
Section 5. Before any Constitution agreed upon by a con
vention that may be called pursuant to this act and to Section
258 of the present Constitution shall take effect and become
operative, the same shall be submitted to the qualified voters
of this Commonwealth, after at least ninety days' notice, and
ratified by a majority of those voting.
Approved March 19, 1962

nicipal government and which cares] any privately operated

sociation and the legal successor thereof.

nomic Security.

or the person in charge of a home for the aged or infirm.
Section 2. Section 216.630 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
All homes for the Aged or Infirm shall make provisions for

ment for fire protection and safety, plumbing and sanitation,

[Licensed hospital or Nursing homes entitled to License]

Section 5. Section 216.660 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes

tain or operate a home for the aged or infirm within this state

CHAPTER 113
(S. B. 266)
AN ACT proposing an amendment to the Constitution of Kentucky re
lating to constitutional amendments and constitutional conventions;
amending sections 256 and 258.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 256 of the Constitution of the Common
wealth of Kentucky is amended to read as follows :
Amendments to this Constitution may be proposed in either
House of the General Assembly at a regular session, and if such
amendment or amendments shall be agreed to by three-fifths of
all the members elected to each House, such proposed amend
ment or amendments, with the yeas and nays of the members of

Chapter 113 463
each House taken thereon, shall be entered in full in their re
spective journals. Then such proposed amendment or amend
ments shall be submitted to the voters of the State for their ratifi
cation or rejection at the next general election for members of
the House of Representatives, the vote to be taken thereon in such
manner as the General Assembly may provide, and to be certified
by the officers of election to the Secretary of State in such man
ner as shall be provided by law, which vote shall be compared
and certified by the same board authorized by law to compare
the polls and give certificates of election to officers for the State
at large. If it shall appear that a majority of the votes cast for
and against an amendment at said election was for the amend
ment, then the same shall become a part of the Constitution of
this Commonwealth, and shall be so proclaimed by the Governor,
and published in such manner as the General Assembly may direct.
Said amendments shall not be submitted at an election which
occurs less than ninety days from the final passage of such
proposed amendment or amendments. Not more than five
[two] amendments shall be voted upon at any one time; nor
shall the same amendment be again submitted within five years
after submission. Said amendments shall be so submitted as to
allow a separate vote on each, and no amendment shall relate to
more than one subject. But no amendment shall be proposed by
the first General Assembly which convenes after the adoption of
this Constitution. The approval of the Governor shall not be
necessary to any bill, order, resolution or vote of the General As
sembly, proposing an amendment or amendments to this Consti
tution.
Section 2. Section 258 of the Constitution of the Common
wealth of Kentucky is amended to read as follows:
When a majority of all the members elected to each House
of the General Assembly shall concur, by a yea and nay vote, to
be entered upon their respective journals, in enacting a law to take
the sense of the people of the State as to the necessity and ex
pediency of calling a Convention for the purpose of revising or
amending this Constitution, and such amendments as may have
been made to the same, such law shall be spread upon their re
spective journals. [If the next] The General Assembly [shall,
in like manner, concur in such law,
it]shall provide for ha vim

464 Acts of the General Assembly
a poll opened in each voting precinct in this state by the officers
provided by law for holding general elections at the next ensuing
regular election to be held for State officers or members of the
House of Representatives, which does not occur within ninety
days from the final passage of such law, at which time and places
the votes of the qualified voters shall be taken for and against
calling the Convention, in the same manner provided by law for
taking votes in other State elections. The vote for and against
said proposition shall be certified to the Secretary of State by the
same officers and in the same manner as in State elections. If it
shall appear that a majority voting on the proposition was for
calling a Convention, and if the total number of votes cast for
the calling of the Convention is equal to one-fourth of the number
of qualified voters who voted at the last preceding general elec
tion in this State, the Secretary of State shall certify the same to
the General Assembly at its next regular session, at which session
a law shall be enacted calling a Convention to readopt, revise or
amend this Constitution, and such amendments as may have been
made thereto. Before any Constitution agreed upon by a Conven
tion shall take effect and become operative, the same shall be sub
mitted to the qualified voters of this Commonwealth, after at
least ninety days' notice, and ratified by a majority of those voting.
Section 3. The amendment of these Sections shall be sub
mitted to the voters of the Commonwealth for their ratification or
rejection at the time and in the manner provided for under Sec
tions 256 and 257 of the Constitution and under Sections 118.170
and 118.430 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes.
Approved March 19, 1962
CHAPTER 114
(S. B. 267)
AN ACT relating to the official flag of the Commonwealth of Kentucky.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 2.030 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:

Chapter 114 465
The official state flag of the Commonwealth of Kentucky
shall be of navy blue silk, nylon, wool or cotton bunting, or some
other suitable material, with the Seal of the Commonwealth en
circled by a wreath, the lower half of which shall be goldenrod
in bloom and the upper half the words "Commonwealth of Ken
tucky", embroidered, printed, painted, or stamped on the center
thereof -The dimensions of the flag may vary[.] , but the length
sJiall be one and nine-tenths times the width and the diameter
of the seal and encirclement shall be approximately two-thirds
the width of the flag.
Section 2. The approved official drawings of the state flag
shall be permanently retained in the files of the office of the
Secretary of State. All state flags for official use of the Com
monwealth shall conform as to color and design with these of
ficial drawings.
Section 3. The emblem at the head of a flag staff used to
display the flag of the Commonwealth of Kentucky shall be the
Kentucky Cardinal in an alert but restful pose, cast in bronze,
brass, or other suitable material.
Section 4. The flying of the state flag at all state buildings
and installations including public school buildings, National
Guard Armories, state parks and other such buildings is con
sidered proper and is encouraged.
Approved March 19, 1962

CHAPTER 115
(S. B. 276)
AN ACT relating to Public Higher Education, and making an appro
priation.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
The Council on Public Higher Education shall have authority
to provide for a program of occupational qualification develop
ment in the Commonwealth. The program may be provided for by
contract with any recognized and accredited municipal junior
college located within the Commonwealth. Such contract shall
provide that the college shall admit to its course of instruction,
up to such percentage of its full teaching capacity as may be
fixed by contract, residents of the Commonwealth who make
application to pursue the course of study of such college; pro
vided however that such persons shall be certified to the Coun
cil as to status of residency by the State Superintendent of Public
Instruction and shall be eligible for admission to said junior
college. The Council on Public Higher Education shall pay to
the college not more than $100.00 per school year for each such
student enrolled. Such payments shall be additional to the
annual tuition fees paid by each such student. The Council shall
have authority to provide by contract that it pay the expense of
specific research projects or programs conducted by the college.
The Council shall make an annual report of its occupational
qualification development program after June 30 of each year,
including an accounting of all moneys received and disbursed.
The council shall have no authority to incur any obligation in
excess of the sums that have been appropriated to it.
Section 2. To carry out the purposes of the Act there is
appropriated to the Council on Higher Education out of the
General Expenditure Fund in the state treasury the sum of
$45,000 for the 1962-63 fiscal year and the sum of $45,000 for
the 1963-64 fiscal year.
Approved March 19, 1962

safety and protection of the civilian population.

39.420, 39.421, and 39.422 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes are
repealed.
Approved March 19, 1962

CHAPTER 118
(S. B. 367)
AN ACT proposing an amendment of Section 246 of the Constitution of
Kentucky relative to compensation of public officers and employees.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. It is proposed that Section 246 of the Constitu

The General Assembly shall limit the maximum compensa

Approved March 19, 1962
CHAPTER 119
(H. B. 9)
AN ACT relating to cricuit courts of continuous session in counties hav
ing less than 150,000 population and having more than one judge.
WHEREAS, the General Assembly finds the facts to exist which entitle
the Sixth Circuit Court District to two judges and the Sixteenth and
Twenty-second Circuit Court Districts to three judges each, and
WHEREAS, the General Assembly, giving due regard to territory, busi
ness, population, and the right of all persons to a speedy trial, as
provided by the Constitution of this Commonwealth, finds that an
additional judge is necessary in each of the Sixth, Sixteenth and
Twenty-second Circuit Court Districts,
NOW, THEREFORE,
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. Each judge of a circuit court of continuous
session having more than one judge, and located in a county

474 Acts of the General Assembly
having less than 150,000 population shall have jurisdiction over
both civil and criminal causes. The judges of such a court shall
establish numbered divisions of the court, and shall adopt uniform
rules for transacting the business of the court, all of which shall
be effective only when approved by the Court of Appeals, in
counties having three judges the three judges may agree among
themselves that one of said judges shall have exclusive jurisdiction
of criminal cases.
Section 2. At each election of judges to a court described
in Section 1 of this Act, beginning with the general election in
1963, each office shall be voted for separately by numbered di
vision. The candidate receiving the largest number of votes cast
for a numbered division of the court shall be elected judge of
that division.
Section 3. In a court described in Section 1 of this Act, a
majority of the judges shall appoint a master commissioner of the
court. In the event of a tie vote, the clerk of the court shall
cast the deciding vote.
Section 4. Section (1) of Kentucky Revised Statutes 28.450
is amended to read as follows:
( 1) Reporters for circuit courts in counties containing a city
of the second class and having two or more judges, shall receive
for their services in taking stenographic notes, a salary of not less
than $5,000 per annum, to be fixed by the fiscal court of said
county and paid in monthly installments by the county.
Section 5. The Sixth, Seventeenth and Thirty-fifth Circuit
Court Districts shall have two judges each, and the Sixteenth and
Twenty-second Circuit Court Districts shall have three judges
each.
Section 6. Sections 24.030, 24.045, 24.050, 24.055, 24.056,
24.060, 24.070, 24.080, 24.090, 24.100, 24.110, 24.120, 24.140,
24.150, 24.160, and 27.020 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes are
repealed.
Section 7. ( 1 ) Within ninety days after this Act becomes

Chapter 120 473
operative, the Governor shall appoint a person having the quali
fications of a circuit judge to preside over the additional author
ized Division of the Circuit Court Distict who, when appointed,
shall be commissioned by the Governor as judge of the Circuit
Court District and shall enter upon the duties of the office as soon
as he shall have qualified, and shall continue in office until his
successor is duly elected and qualifies as provided in subsection
(2) of this section.
( 2 ) At the regular election to be held on the first Tuesday
of the first Monday in November, 1963, and every six years there
after, such additional judge shall be elected, as other circuit judges
are required to be elected, and shall enter upon the duties of the
office on the first Monday in January next succeeding his election.
Section 8. This Act shall become effective as it relates to
the Sixteenth and Twenty-Second Circuit Court Districts on Sep
tember 1, 1962.
Section 9. Whereas, the congested docket of the circuit
court in the Sixth Circuit Court District deprives citizens of this
Commonwealth of a prompt trial, as guaranteed by the Consti
tution of this Commonwealth, an emergency is declared to exist,
and this Act shall become effective as it relates to the Sixth Cir
cuit Court District immediately upon its passage and signature
by the Governor.
Approved March 19, 1962

CHAPTER 121
(H. B. 140)
AN ACT relating to motor vehicles.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 431.105 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:

Chapter 122 483

189.221, 189.222 or 189.270, which violation occurred on a state-

CHAPTER 122
(H. B. 161)
AN ACT relating to tuberculosis sanatoriums.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 215.360 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
Whenever any county has heretofore acquired or may here
after acquire any property for the purpose of forming a district
for the erection and maintenance of a tuberculosis sanatorium
and the property has ceased to be used for that purpose, it may,
upon the petition of the fiscal court of the county, be ordered
sold or leased by the circuit court of the county, or, where the
district is composed of more than one county, the circuit court
of any of the counties, and the net proceeds together with any
excess funds in the treasury of the district paid into the general
fund of the proper county treasury or treasuries. The board of
trustees of the district shall be made defendants to the petition

484 Acts of the General Assembly
unless they have resigned or ceased to act, in which case the
proceeding may be by an ex parte petition by the fiscal court.
In either case the court shall be satisfied that the facts stated
in the petition are substantially true and comply with this
section. The court being so satisfied shall decree the sale or lease
of the property upon just and proper terms and conditions and
shall make the orders necessary to vest the purchaser with title
to the property, or the lessee with possession of the property.
Where the district consists of only one county the circuit court
may, as an alternative to the above, authorize the fiscal court of
said county to take possession of and title to the property and
use it for such public purpose or purposes as the circuit court
may deem proper.
Section 2. Whereas, the immediate use of such property
will contribute greatly to the health and welfare of the citizens
of the state, an emergency is declared to exist, and this Act shall
become effective upon its passage and approval by the Governor.
Approved March 19, 1962
CHAPTER 123
(H. B. 208)
AN ACT relating to a Professional Practices Commission to establish
and administer criteria for professional performance and to recom
mend to the State Board of Education or other agencies appropriate
action based upon the findings of this commission.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. The Governor shall appoint a Professional Prac
tices Commission of twelve members nominated by the teaching
profession and submitted by the Superintendent of Public In
struction to the Governor.
Section 2. Each of the following organizational units shall
submit a panel of three names from each of which the Governor

Chapter 123 485
will select one: The Kentucky Association of School Administra
tors, The Kentucky Association of Secondary School Principals,
The Kentucky Association of Elementary School Principals, The
Kentucky Association of Educational Supervisors, The Depart
ment of Higher Education, and the Kentucky Vocational Associa
tion, the Kentucky Association of Pupil Personnel Workers and
the Kentucky Association of Guidance Counselors. The Depart
ment of Classroom Teachers shall submit a panel of twelve mem
bers from which the Governor will select four. The term "Class
room teacher" for the purpose of this act shall mean any person
for whom certification is required as basis of employment in the
public schools of Kentucky with the exception of any personnel
identified with professional groups named in this Act other than
the Department of Classroom Teachers. Terms will be over
lapping and for a period of three years with members drawing by
lot at the first meeting to determine the length of the term for
each member. No member may succeed himself more than once.
This commission would have the authority to select its own chair
man, establish procedures for the development of standards,
adopt the rules and regulations under which it operates, and
secure legal and other services necessary to its function.
Section 3. The commission is given the initial responsibility
of developing, through the teaching profession itself, criteria of
professional practices in areas including, but not limited to:
(1) Contractural obligations, (2) Transfer and assignment of
teaching personnel, (3) Admission to and continuance in profes
sional service, and (4) Ethical performance.
Section 4. This commission, in administering these criteria,
shall have the authority to reprimand or warn members of the
profession. Further, the commission shall have authority to make
recommendations to the State Board of Education in cases in
volving suspension or revocation of certificates and to make
any other recommendations to the State Board and to local boards
of education which would promote improvement of the teaching
profession. This commission shall have the authority to subpoena
witnesses and place them under oath.
Section 5. The commission shall be financed by the mem

486 Acts of the General Assembly
bers of the teaching profession in the amount necessary to carry
out the purposes of this Act.
Approved March 19, 1962

Section 2. Section 141.020 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
(1) With respect to taxable years beginning after Decem
ber 31, 1960, an annual tax shall be paid for each taxable year bv

as defined in this chapter. The tax shall be determined by apply
ing the rates in subsection (2) to net income and subtracting

(2) The following rates shall be applied to net income:
(a) Two percent of the amount of net income not exceeding

Chapter 124 491

deducted from the result obtained under subsection (2) to arrive
at the annual tax:
(a) Twenty dollars for an unmarried individual;
(b) Twenty dollars for a married individual filing a sep
arate return and an additional twenty dollars for the spouse of

spouse, for the calendar year in which the taxable year of the

section shall be made in the manner prescribed in Section 153
of the Internal Revenue Code;
(c) Twenty dollars credit for each dependent whose gross
income for the calendar year in which the taxable year of the
taxpayer begins is less than six hundred dollars, or who is a child,
as defined in Section 151 (e) (3) of the Internal Revenue Code,
of the taxpayer and who 1. has not attained the age of 19 at the
close of the calendar year in which the taxable year of the tax
payer begins, or 2. is a student. No exemption shall be allowed
for any dependent who has made a joint return with his spouse;
( d ) An additional twenty dollars credit if the taxpayer has
attained the age of sixty-five before the close of the taxable year;
(e) An additional twenty dollars credit for taxpayer's
spouse if a separate return is made by the taxpayer and if tb'

492 Acts of the General Assembly
taxpayer's spouse has attained the age of sixty-five before the
close of the taxable year, and, for the calendar year in which the
taxable year of the taxpayer begins, has no gross income and is
not the dependent of another taxpayer;
(f) An additional twenty dollars credit if the taxpayer is
blind at the close of the taxable year;
(g) An additional twenty dollars credit for taxpayer's
spouse if a separate return is made by the taxpayer and if the
taxpayer's spouse is blind, and, for the calendar year in which the
taxable year of the taxpayer begins, has no gross income and is
not the dependent of another taxpayer;
(h) In the case of nonresidents, the tax credits allowable
under this subsection shall be that proportion of those permitted
therein that is represented by the ratio of the taxpayer's net in

sented by the ratio of the taxpayer's adjusted gross income in this
state to his total adjusted gross income;
(i) In the case of a fiduciary, other than an estate, the
allowable tax credit shall be two dollars;
(j) In the case of an estate, the allowable tax credit shall
be twenty dollars.
(4) An annual tax shall be paid for each taxable year as
specified in this section upon the entire net income, except as
herein provided, from all tangible property located in this state,
from all intangible property that has acquired a business situs in
this state, and from business, trade, profession, occupation or other
activities carried on in this state, by natural persons not residents
of this state. A non-resident individual shall be taxable only upon
the amount of income received by him from labor performed,

Section 3. Section 141.120 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
( 1 ) "Taxable net income" of corporations shall be deter
mined as provided in the following subsections of this section.

These amounts shall be allocated within and without Kentucky,

(a) If the property giving rise to such income, or portion

in Kentucky and the property giving rise to such income, or por
