CHAPTER 195
(H. B. 321)
AN ACT relating to the marketing of eggs.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 260.610 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
(1) All eggs bought or sold by or to retailers, consumers,
and institutional users by licensees shall be identified according
to grade and size, using USDA standards and weight classes for
consumer grades, with the following exception:
Those retailers who sell not more than five cases of eggs
per week that are purchased directly from the Kentucky pro
ducers of such eggs, may sell them to consumers as unclassified.

654 Acts of the General Assembly
(2) Eggs to be offered for sale for human consumption
shall be handled in such manner as to maintain and preserve
the quality and grade in which they are to be offered for sale.
(3) No person shall sell, offer or expose for sale, or have
in his possession for sale, for human consumption, eggs that are
inedible, including eggs that are unfit for human food in whole
or in part, addled or moldy, containing black rot, white rot, or
blood ring, adherent yolks, or bloody whites, incubated beyond
the blood ring stage, or consisting to any extent of filthy decom
posed substance.
Section 2. Section 260.600 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
(1) A license is required of each handler of eggs located
in the Commonwealth subject to the exceptions specified in KRS
260.550. The annual license fees are as follows, varying for
dealers, wholesalers and processors, according to the average
weekly volume of the month in which the licensee handled the
most eggs during the preceding year:
(a) Retailers $ 5.00
(b) Dealers and Wholesalers
1-25 cases $ 5.00
26-50 cases $10.00
51-100 cases $15.00
More than 100 cases $20.00
(c) Processors
Less than 250 cases $20.00
250-500 cases $30.00
501-1000 cases $40.00
More than 1000 cases $50.00
(2) Any egg handler engaged in more than one type of
activity for which a license is provided must pay that license
fee which is the highest of the licenses prescribed for each type
of activity in which he is engaged.
(3) All eggs bought or sold by or to any egg handler must
bear a two cent inspection fee stamp for each fifteen dozen lot

Chapter 196 655
or portion thereof; subject to the exemptions specified in KRS
260.550 and 260.61 0.
(4) All licenses shall expire on April 1 of each year.
Handlers having more than one place of business must hold a
license for each place. Each application shall bear the names
of the persons responsible for candling and grading eggs.
Approved March 21, 1962
CHAPTER 196
(H. B. 353)
AN ACT relating to the regulation of child-care centers.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. In enacting legislation relating to the regulation
of day-care centers, it is the intention of the General Assembly
to enable the Department of Child Welfare to qualify to receive
Federal funds under provisions of the Federal Social Security
Act and to provide for effective regulation of day-care centers.
Section 2. As used in this Act, unless the context otherwise
requires:
(1) "Department" means the Department of Child Wel
fare;
(2) "Commissioner" means Commissioner of Child Wel
fare;
(3) "Day-care center" means any child-care facility, other
than those set out in Section 5 of this Act, which provides full-
or part-time care, day or night, to at least four children not
related to the operator of the child-care facility by blood, mar
riage, or adoption. Day-care center shall not include any child-
care facility operated by a religious organization while religious
services are being conducted.

656 Acts of the General Assembly
Section 3. (1) No person, association, or organization
shall conduct, operate, or maintain any day-care center without
obtaining a license as provided in this Act.
(2) The Commissioner may adopt regulations relating to
license fees and may establish standards of care and service
for the day-care center.
(3) If the day-care center does not meet the standards
prescribed for licensing by the Commissioner, a provisional li
cense may be issued and remain in effect for a period prescribed
by the Commissioner.
(4) Each initial application for a license shall be made
to the department and shall be accompanied by a fee of not
more than thirty-five dollars and shall, excepting provisional
licenses, be renewable upon expiration and reapplication when
accompanied by a fee of not more than ten dollars. Regular
licenses, and renewals, thereof shall expire one year from their
effective date.
(5) No day-care center shall be refused a license or have
its license revoked for failure to meet standards set by the Com
missioner until after the expiration of a period not to exceed
twelve months from the date of the first official notice that the
standards have not been met.
(6) All fees collected under the provisions of this Act
shall be paid into the State Treasury and credited to a special
fund for the purpose of administering this Act, including the
payment of expenses of and to the participants in child-care
workshops. Such funds collected are hereby appropriated for
the use of the department.
Section 4. (1) Any person who violates any of the pro
visions of Section 3 of this Act shall be fined not less than twenty
dollars nor more than two hundred dollars, or imprisoned for not
more than thirty days, or both.
Section 5. Section 158.300 of the Kentucky Revised
Statutes is amended to read as follows:
(1) "Center" means any child-care center, ("private day
nursery,] nursery school, [boarding school,] or kindergarten

Chapter 197 657
[or other like establishment which cares] organized for the pur
pose of providing educational experiences for not less than five
children between the ages of 3 [2] and 6 years, in return for
tuition, fees, or other forms of compensation; provided that
"center" shall not include any school or college now operating
a center authorized by law, any children's home, orphan asylum,
children's aid society, public agency operated by a city or county,
school district operating a center, or any other organization in
corporated and regulated under the laws of the State;
(2) "Superintendent" means Superintendent of Public In
struction;
(3) "Age" means the age attained at a child's last birthday;
( 4 ) "Day" means any twenty-four hour period from twelve
o'clock midnight through eleven fifty-nine p. m.;
(5) "Permit" includes both a "regular" and "provisional"
permit. "Regular" permits are those issued by the superintendent
upon satisfactory proof of compliance with the provisions of
KRS 158.300 to 158.350. "Provisional" permits are those issued
by the superintendent, within the discretion of the superin
tendent, for the purpose of enabling centers to comply with
the provisions of KRS 158.300 to 158.350; no such permits shall
be issued for a period exceeding three years' time from the
date of issuance, and shall be subject to such rules and regula
tions providing for inspection, compliance, suspension, or can
cellation thereof as the superintendent may prescribe as a con
dition to the issuance thereof.
Approved March 21, 1962
CHAPTER 197
(H. B. 361)
AN ACT relating to business development corporations and declaring an
emergency.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:

658 Acts of the General Assembly
Section 1. Section 155.080 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
(1) Any financial institution may request membership in
the corporation by making application to the board of directors
on such form and in such manner as said board of directors may
require, and membership shall become effective upon acceptance
of such application by said board.
(2) Each member of the corporation shall make loans to
the corporation as and when called upon by it to do so on such
terms and other conditions as shall be approved from time to time
by the board of directors, subject to the following conditions:
(a) All loan limits shall be established at the thousand-
dollar amount nearest to the amount computed in accordance
with the provisions of this section;
( b ) No loan to the corporation shall be made if immediately
thereafter the total amount of the obligations of the corporation
would exceed ten times the amount then paid in on the out
standing capital stock of the corporation;
(c) The total amount outstanding on the loans to the
corporation made by any member at any one time, when added
to the amount of the investment in the capital stock of the
corporation then held by such member, shall not exceed:
1. Twenty percent of the total amount then outstanding
on loans to the corporation by all members, including in said total
amount outstanding, amounts validly called for loan but not yet
loaned;
2. The following limit, to be determined as of the time such
member becomes a member on the basis of the audited balance
sheet of such member at the close of its fiscal year immediately
preceding its application for membership, or, in the case of an
insurance company, its last annual statement to the Commissioner
of Insurance; two percent of the capital and surplus of commercial
banks and trust companies; two percent of all reserves, surplus,
and undivided profits of a building and loan association or one-
fourth of one percent of the total outstanding loans made by such
association, whichever is the lesser; [one percent of the total
outstanding loans made by a building and loan association: Pro

Chapter 197 659
vided, however, that any business development corporation
created pursuant to this chapter may in its articles of incorpora
tion, or by appropriate amendment thereto, provided that the loan
limit of a building and loan association member shall be only
one-half of one percent of ten total outstanding loans made by
such building and loan association member.] one percent of the
capital and unassigned surplus of stock insurance companies,
except fire insurance writing companies; one percent of the un
assigned surplus of mutual insurance companies, except fire in
surance writing companies; one-tenth of one percent of the assets
of fire insurance writing companies; and such limits as may be
approved by the board of directors of the corporation for other
financial institutions.
Section 2. Section 155.130 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
(1) The business and affairs of the corporation shall be
managed and conducted by a board of directors, a president and
treasurer, and such other officers and such agents as the corpo
ration by its bylaws shall authorize. The board of directors shall
consist of such number, not less than fifteen nor more than
thirty, [twenty-one] as shall be determined in the first instance
by the incorporators and thereafter annually by the members
and the stockholders of the corporation.
(2) The board of directors may exercise all the powers of
the corporation except such as are conferred by law or by the
bylaws of the corporation upon the stockholders or members and
shall choose and appoint all the agents and officers of the
corporation and fill all vacancies, except vacancies in the office
of director, which shall be filled as hereinafter provided.
(3) The board of directors shall be elected in the first
instance by the incorporators and thereafter at each annual
meeting of the corporation, or, if no annual meeting is held in
any year at the time fixed by the bylaws, at a special meeting
held in lieu of the annual meeting. At each annual meeting,
or at each special meeting held in lieu of the annual meeting,
the members of the corporation shall elect two-thirds of the
board of directors and the stockholders shall elect the remaining
directors. The directors shall hold office until the next annual

660 Acts of the General Assembly
meeting of the corporation or special meeting held in lieu of
the annual meeting after their election and until their successors
are elected and qualified unless sooner removed in accordance
with the provisions of the bylaws. Anv vacancy in the office of a
director elected by the members shall be filled by the directors
elected by the members, and any vacancy in the office of a
director elected by the stockholders shall be filled by the direc
tors elected by the stockholders.
( 4 ) Directors and officers shall not be responsible for losses
unless the same shall have been occasioned by the wilful mis
conduct of such directors and officers.
Section 3. Whereas it is necessary that Sections 1 and 2
of this Act become effective immediately, an emergency is de
clared to exist, and this Act shall become effective upon its passage
and approval by the Governor.
Approved March 21, 1962
CHAPTER 198
(H. B. 415)
AN ACT relating to license fees.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 137.115 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
The fiscal court of each county is hereby given the authority
to impose with respect:
(1) To each restaurant serving meals, a license fee not to
exceed ten dollars per annum;
(2) To each retail outlet of soft drinks or ice cream, a
license fee not to exceed five dollars per annum. In cases where
ice cream and soft drinks are sold by the same retail outlet, one
license tax not to exceed ten dollars per annum;

Chapteb 198 661
(3) To each billiard or pool table or bowling alley, irre
spective of size, where a fee is charged and collected, directly
or indirectly, a license fee not to exceed thirty dollars per annum
for the first table or alley and not to exceed five dollars per an
num for each additional table or alley;
( 4 ) To each place where tobacco products are sold at retail,
a license fee not to exceed ten dollars per annum;
(5) All license fees shall be payable to tlxe county clerk and
be credited to the General Fund of the County to be used for
county purposes only;
(6) The fiscal court of any county may allow the county
clerk a commission not to exceed five percent on the license fees
collected and accounted for by him under this act in addition
to the one dollar and a half fee provided in KRS 64.010.
( 1 ) Any person who violates any of the provisions of KRS
137.020 or 137.030 shall be fined not less than one hundred dol
lars nor more than five hundred dollars, or imprisoned for not less
than thirty days nor more than six months, or both.
(2) (a) Any person who engages in any business or sells
or offers to sell or has on hand for the purpose of sale any article
or exercises any privilege for which a license is required or im
posed by KRS 137.040 to [137.110] 137.115 before procuring
the license and paying the tax, shall be fined not less than twenty-
five dollars nor more than two hundred dollars for each offense,
unless otherwise specifically provided.
(b) Any county clerk who violates any of the provisions
of KRS 137.040 to [137.110] 137.115, or any reasonable rule or
regulation of the Department of Revenue thereunder, shall be
fined not less than fifty dollars nor more than one thousand
dollars for each offense.
(c) Any person who makes under oath a false statement in
securing a license under KRS 137.040 to [137.110] 137.115 shall
be deemed guilty of a misdemeanor.
(3) (a) Any person required to register, make reports, or
collect and pay taxes under the provisions of KRS 137.120 to
137.160 who fails to pay taxes, penalty and interest after becom
ing delinquent, or fails for thirty days after the required date to

662 Acts of the General Assembly
make a report or register, shall pay a penalty of ten percent on the
amount of tax due, and shall pay an additional penalty of five
percent on the first day of each month so long as the tax remains
due and unpaid, except the total penalty paid shall not exceed
twenty-five percent of tax due.
(b) Any person who violates any of the provisions of
KRS 137.120 to 137.160 may be fined not less than one hundred
dollars nor more than five hundred dollars or imprisoned for not
less than thirty days nor more than six months or both.
(4) Any person who violates any of the provisions of KRS
137.170 or 137.180 shall be fined not more than one thousand
dollars or imprisoned in the county jail not more than thirty days,
or both so fined and imprisoned. If the offender is a corporation,
the principal officer or the officer or employe directly responsible
for the violation, or both, shall be punished as provided in this
subsection.
[(7)] (5) Whoever violates any of the provisions of KRS
137.340 to 137.400 shall, upon conviction thereof, be fined not
less than twenty-five dollars nor more than five hundred dollars.
Whoever attaches or causes to be attached to a coin machine any
fictitious evidence of tax payment purported to be issued by the
department, or a counterfeit thereof, shall upon conviction thereof
be fined not less than one thousand dollars, and each separate
fictitious counterfeit license stamp or receipt so attached or used
on a coin machine shall constitute a separate and distinct offense.
Approved March 21, 1962
CHAPTER 199
(H. B. 437)
AN ACT relating to the Legislative Research Commission.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 7.100 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is amended
to read as follows:

Chapter 199 663
It shall be the duty of the Legislative Research Commis
sion to:
(1) Maintain in the State Capitol for the use and infor
mation of the General Assembly, a legislative reference room and
working library, collect, summarize and index information of a
legislative and governmental nature relating to legislation and
legislative policies and index all bills introduced in the General
Assembly. Permanent office space for the Legislative Research
Commission and for the director thereof and employes or sub
committees shall be provided on the third floor of the State
Capitol. When the General Assembly is not in session, the
commission shall have jurisdiction over the use of the House
and Senate Chambers and [, following completion of the reno
vation work on the Capitol begun in 1953,] over the allocation
of all rooms used by the General Assembly while in session. The
commission may provide for such other office space as it may
deem necessary.
(2) Make or cause to be made such investigations into
statute law, legislation, governmental agencies and institutions
and matters of public policy as will aid the General Assembly
in performing its duties in the most efficient and economical
manner. In making such studies and investigations between the
regular sessions of the General Assembly, the commission shall
give priority to those subjects requested by the General Assembly.
(3) Afford to any member of the General Assembly or any
committee of either house of the General Assemblv such infor-
mation and assistance as may be practicable in the preparation
of bills, memorials, resolutions, amendments, alterations and
changes thereto and revisions and substitutes thereof, proposed
to be introduced into the General Assembly.
(4) The commission, at the close of each legislative ses
sion, shall assume custody of all furniture, equipment, records,
materials and supplies, and printed copies of bills in the posses
sion of the clerks of the Senate and House. Immediately prior to
the convening of the next session, the commission shall transfer
all furniture, equipment, materials and supplies to the clerks of
the respective houses. The commission may, when the General
Assembly is not in session, authorize the expenditure of funds

664 Acts of the General Assembly
appropriated to the General Assembly for the purchase, repair
or maintenance of furniture, equipment, materials and supplies
and contract for services needed by the General Assembly.
(5) The commission shall prepare and publish or contract
for the preparation and publishing of a daily index and digest
of the content of and legislative action on all measures introduced
in the General Assembly.
(6) After each election the commission shall conduct a
presession orientation conference for members of the General
Assembly.
Approved March 21, 1962
CHAPTER 200
(H. B. 479)
AN ACT relating to judicial administration.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
A new section of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is created
to read as follows:
It shall be the duty of the Court of Appeals to assist and
supervise the circuit courts of this Commonwealth in all matters
pertaining to the efficient handling of the dockets in the various
circuit courts. It shall be the duty of the Chief Justice of the
Court of Appeals to periodically ascertain the status of the docket
in each of the circuit courts, and he may appoint a special judge
to dispose of cases in any circuit court when, in his discretion, he
finds there are too many undisposed of cases on the docket.
Approved March 21, 1962

Chapter 202 665
CHAPTER 201
(H. B. 480)
AN ACT relating to judicial administration.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. A new section of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is created to read as follows:
It shall be the duty of the Court of Appeals to establish the
terms and the dates for holding court in each of the circuit courts
of this Commonwealth. Before fixing such terms and dates of
any circuit court, the Court of Appeals shall consult the circuit
judge of the particular court under consideration.
Section 2. Section 23.050 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is repealed.
Approved March 21, 1962
CHAPTER 202
(H. B. 495)
AN ACT relating to roads.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. As used in this Act unless the context otherwise
requires:
(1) "Roads" includes highways, bridges, and bridge ap
proaches.
(2) "Encroachment" means any improvement to land con
structed, created or implemented or any change from the original
contour thereof, including but not limited to buildings, fences,
ditches, embankments, driveways or signs, which hinders or
prevents use or maintenance of roads or rights-of-way.
(3) "Person" means any individual, firm, co-partnership,
joint venture, association, social club, fraternal organization, cor

Acts of the General Assembly
poration, estate, trust, business trust, receiver, syndicate, coopera
tive or any other group or combination acting as a unit.
Section 2. Before any person shall proceed to cause or con
tinue or allow to remain in existence any encroachment under,
on or over any part of the right of way of a state highway he
shall first obtain from the Department of Highways a permit
so to do. Any encroachment heretofore or hereafter placed or
allowed to continue or remain under, on or over any road which
is found by the Department of Highways to be interferring in any
way with the safe, convenient and continuous use and mainte
nance of such road shall upon thirty days notice to the person
or to his chief agent by the Department of Highways be removed
or relocated by such person at his own expense. Whenever an
order of the Department of Highways requires such removal
or change in location of any encroachment from the right of way
of a road and the person causing such encroachment or allowing
same to continue or remain fails to remove or change location of
same at his own expense to conform to the order within the time
stated in the notice, the Department of Highways shall proceed
to cause the encroachment to be removed; the expense thereby
incurred shall be paid out of any money available therefor, and
shall be charged against the person and levied and collected
and paid into the State Treasury as provided by law.
Approved March 21, 1962
CHAPTER 203
(H. B. 524)
AN ACT relating to revenue and taxation.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 138.210 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
As used in KRS 138.220 to 138.340, unless the context re
quires otherwise;

Chapter 203 667
(1) "Accountable loss" of gasoline means loss or destruc
tion of "received" gasoline, through wrecking of transportation
conveyance, explosion, fire, flood, or other casualty loss, or "re
ceived" gasoline contaminated and returned to storage; provided
that any such loss shall have been reported within ten days after
discovery of such loss to the department in a manner and form
prescribed by the department, supported by proper evidence
which in the sole judgment of the department substantiates the
alleged loss or contamination and which is confirmed in writing
to the reporting dealer by the department. The department may
make any investigation deemed necessary to establish the bona
fide claim of the loss.
[ ( 1 ) "Accountable loss" of gasoline means loss or destruc
tion of "received" gasoline, through wrecking of transportation
conveyance, explosion, or fire, that has been reported to the De
partment of Revenue in a manner and form prescribed by the
department, supported by positive and conclusive evidence, in
the judgment of the department, of the alleged loss, and confirmed
in writing to the reporting dealer by the department, after any
investigation deemed necessary by the department to establish
the bona fide claim of the loss.]
(2) "Dealer" means any person who:
(a) Imports into this state and stores, or causes to be im
ported into and stored in this state, gasoline for use, distribution,
or sale and delivery in this state after it has been stored in this
state; provided that the term "dealer", as used herein, shall not
include any person who imports gasoline acquired from a re
finer or other supplier who, at the time of such importation, is a
duly licensed dealer in this state, except as may otherwise be pro
vided under regulations of the department.
(b) Receives or accepts delivery of gasoline within this state
into bulk storage facilities owned or operated for the storage of
gasoline for trans-shipment within or without this state.
(c) Refines, produces, distills, manufactures, blends or com
pounds gasoline in this state.
(d) Receives or accepts delivery within this state of gaso
line for resale within this state in amounts of not less than an
average of 100,000 gallons per month during any prior consecu

668 Acts of the General Assembly
tive twelve months' period, when in the opinion of the depart
ment, such person has sufficient financial rating and reputation
to justify the conclusion that he will pay all taxes and comply
with all other obligations imposed upon a dealer.
[ ( 2 ) "Dealer" means any person who imports or causes to
be imported into this state gasoline for storage, use, distribution,
or sale and delivery in this state and after it has reached this

by tank car, barge or pipeline delivery from any point within

138.220 has not been paid; also any person importing or causing
to be imported into this state gasoline in the fuel tank or any
auxiliary tank of his motor vehicle in a quantity in excess of thirty

(3) "Department" means the Department of Revenue.
(4) "Gasoline" means all liquid fuels, including liquids
ordinarily, practically and commercially usable in internal com
bustion engines for the generation of power, and all distillates
of and condensates from petroleum, natural gas, coal, coal tar,
vegetable ferments and all other products so usable except pro
pane, butane or other liquefied petroleum gases, kerosene, cleaner
solvent, fuel oil, diesel fuel and crude oil.
(5) "Received" or "received gasoline" shall have the follow
ing meanings:
(a) Gasoline produced, manufactured or compounded at
any refinery in this state or gasoline acquired by any dealer and
delivered into or stored in refinery, marine or pipeline terminal
storage facilities in this state shall be deemed to be received when
it has been loaded for bulk delivery into tank cars or tank trucks
consigned to destinations within this state.
For the purpose of the proper administration of this chapter
and to prevent the evasion of the tax and to enforce the duty of
the dealer to collect the tax, it shall be presumed that all gasoline
loaded by any licensed dealer within this state into tank cars or
tank trucks is consigned to destinations within this state unless

Chapter 203 669
the contrary is established by the dealer pursuant to rules and

(b) Gasoline acquired by any dealer in this state, and not

cilities, shall be deemed to be received when it has been placed

drawal for use, delivery, sale or other distribution.
Dealers may sell gasoline to licensed bonded dealers in this

storage tanks, without paying the tax on such gasoline. In such

shall be deemed to have received such gasoline at the time of

sible to the state for the payment of the tax thereon and shall

[(5) "Received", as applied to gasoline, means when it has
been imported into this state by any person for use, distribution,
or delivery and sale and when it has been placed in tanks or other

or sale and delivery. Gasoline produced, refined, manufactured
or compounded at any refinery in this state by any person shall be

into tank cars for delivery within this state or placed in any tank
from which sales or deliveries are made at the refinery where it
is produced, refined, manufactured or compounded. Gasoline
imported by any person into this state and received through de

payment of the tax thereon and shall report and pay the tax on

(6) "Refinery" means any place where gasoline is refined,

( 7 ) "Storage" means all gasoline produced, refined, distilled,

( 8 ) "Transporter" means any person who transports gasoline

Section 2. Section 138.290 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes

(1) If any dealer neglects or refuses to make the return

ized by KRS 134.400 and 135.060.
(2) If any taxpayer subject to the penalty provided in sub
section ( 1 ) of this section submits in writing to the department

it may modify the penalty exacted therein to five percent of the

ment may reduce or waive the penalty when in the sole discretion
of the department the exaction of the penalties provided above
would be inequitable or unjust, [provided the five percent penalty

Chapter 203 671
(3) Any dealer or transporter who fails to make any report

mum amount of five thousand dollars until such time as an esti

nancial raring and reputation of the company, to insure payment

The dealer or transporter shall be the principal obligor and the

ment by the dealer to the State Treasurer of all gasoline excise

(2) If liability upon the bond is discharged or reduced,

quire the licensee to file a new bond with satisfactory sureties

672 Acts of the General Assembly
cancel the license of such licensee. If a new bond is furnished

proved by the department, in any amount determined by the

(4) Any surety on a bond furnished as required by this

quired by those sections, or fails to meet the qualifications of a

partment of Revenue to protect the Commonwealth from loss of

(1) "Department" means the Department of Revenue;

ciation, state or political subdivision, or corporation. Whenever

(2) Two-sevenths of all amounts received by reason of the

ment of Highways for the construction, reconstruction, and main

Section 7. Section 138.595 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes

With respect to KRS 138.560 to 138.640, it is unlawful for

viding delivery is made into separate storage facilities identified

partment;]

Any gasoline dealer or special fuels dealer having a license

terest in any such license either disclosed or undisclosed whether

Section 10. Section 138.662 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
(1) City and suburban bus companies operating under a
certificate of convenience and necessity issued pursuant to KRS
Chapter 281 shall be entitled to a refund of five-sevenths [two-
sevenths] of the amount of KRS Chapter 138 taxes paid on motor
fuels used in their regularly scheduled operations in Kentucky.
(2) No person shall be entitled to a refund hereunder un
less he shall have first filed with the Department of Revenue
a bond issued by a surety company authorized to do business in
Kentucky [with approved surety] in an amount of not less than
one thousand dollars nor more than five thousand dollars to be

faithful compliance with this section and upon the payment to
the Commonwealth of any refunds to which he was not entitled.
(3) Applications for refund shall on or before the last day
of each month following the quarter, September 30, December
31, March 31, and June 30, be made to the Department of Reve
nue on forms and in the manner prescribed by it for the refund
of tax paid on motor fuel used by buses during the quarter.
Each application for a refund shall show the number of gallons
of motor fuel purchased during the quarter for use in buses; the
date and quantity of each purchase; the vendor from which such
fuel was purchased; the number of gallons on which refund is
claimed; and such other information as the Department of Reve
nue may require. The application shall be made under oath.

678 Acts of the General Assembly
(4) The Department of Revenue shall audit the applica
tion and make such other investigation as it deems necessary to
determine whether it constitutes a proper claim. When the De
partment of Revenue is satisfied that a refund is proper it shall
authorize five-sevenths [two-sevenths] of the amount of the tax
paid to be refunded as other refunds are made and the amount
refunded shall be deducted from current motor fuel tax receipts.
(5) When the Department of Revenue finds that an ap
plication for a refund contains a false or fraudulent statement
or that a refund has been fraudulently obtained, the Department
of Revenue shall refuse to grant any refunds to the person making
such false or fraudulent statement or fraudulently obtaining a
refund for a period of two years from the date of such findings.
(6) The Department of Revenue may prescribe, adopt and
enforce regulations relating to the administration and enforce
ment thereof.
( 7 ) The refund provided herein shall be effective on motor
fuel purchased July 1, 1962 [1960] and thereafter.
Section 11. The provisions of this Act shall become effec
tive July 1, 1962.
Approved March 21, 1962
CHAPTER 204
(H. R. 79)
A CONCURRENT RESOLUTION relating to regulations promulgated by
the Department of Revenue pursuant to the provisions of House Bill
No. 75 enacted by the General Assembly of this Commonwealth at
its 1960 regular session.
WHEREAS, at its 1960 regular session, the General Assembly enacted
House Bill No. 75 being a "Veterans' Bonus Sales and Use Tax Law";
and
WHEREAS, the administration of the sales and use tax enacted thereby
was delegated to the Department of Revenue by Article 1, Section 68
thereof; and
WHEREAS, the Department of Revenue has adopted various regulations
to facilitate the administration of the duties imposed upon it by
such Act; and

Chapter 205 679
WHEREAS, the General Assembly has examined and made a study
of such regulations,
NOW, THEREFORE,
Be it resolved by the House of the Commonwealth of Kentucky,
the Senate concurring therein:
That such regulations correctly reflect the legislative intent
in the enactment of House Bill No. 75.
Approved March 21, 1962
CHAPTER 205
(S. B. 31)
AN ACT to provide for the creation and regulation of horizontal property
regimes. To be known as the Scott Miller, Jr. Bill.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. This act shall be known as "Horizontal Property
Act."
Section 2. As used in this Act unless the context otherwise
requires:
(a) "Apartment" means an enclosed space consisting of
one or more rooms occupying all or part of a floor in a building
of one or more floors or stories regardless of whether it be
designed for residence, for office, for the operation of any industry
or business, or for any other type of independent use, provided it
has a direct exit to a thoroughfare or to a given common space
leading to a thoroughfare;
(b) "Condominium" means the ownership of single units
in a multiple unit structure with common elements;
(c) "Condominium Project" means a real estate condomin
ium project; a plan or project whereby four or more apartments,
rooms, office spaces, or other units in existing or proposed build
ing (s) or structure(s) are offered or proposed to be offered
for sale;

Chapter 205 681
(k) "Person" means an individual, firm, corporation, part
nership, association, trust or other legal entity or any combination
thereto;
(1) "Property" means and includes the land whether lease
hold or in fee simple and the building, all improvements and
structures thereon and all easements, rights and appurtenances
belonging thereto;
(m) "To record" means to record in accordance with KRS
Chapter 382, or other recording statutes;
(n) All pronouns used herein include the male, female and
neuter genders and include the singular or plural numbers, as
the case may be.
Section 3. Whenever a developer, the sole owner, or the
co-owners of a building expressly declare, through the recordation
of a master deed or lease, which shall set forth the particulars
enumerated by Section 7, their desire to submit their property
to the regime established by this act, there shall be thereby
established a horizontal property regime.
Section 4. Once the property is submitted to the horizontal
property regime, an apartment in the building may be individu
ally conveyed and encumbered and may be the subject of owner
ship, possession or sale and of all types of juridic acts intervivos
or mortis causa, as if it were sole and entirely independent of the
other apartments in the building of which they form a part, and
the corresponding individual titles and interest shall be recordable.
Section 5. Any apartment may be jointly or commonly
owned by more than one person.
Section 6. An apartment owner shall have an exclusive
ownership to his apartment and shall have a common right to a
share, with other co-owners, in the common elements of the
property.
Each co-owner may use the elements held in common in
accordance with the purpose for which they are intended, without
hindering or encroaching upon the lawful rights of the other co-
owners.

682 Acts of the General Assembly
Section 7. The County Clerk shall immediately set up the
mechanics and methods by which recordation of a master deed
or lease and of the individual apartments may be made.
Provisions shall be made for the recordation of the individual
apartments on subsequent re-sales, mortgages and other encum
brances, as is done with all other real estate recordation. The
master deed or lease to which Section 3 refers shall express the
following particulars:
(a) The description of the land, whether leased or in fee
simple, and the building, expressing their respective areas;
(b) The general description and the number of each apart
ment, expressing its area, location and any other data necessary
for its identification;
(c) The description of the general common elements of
the building; and
(d) The common elements, both general and limited, shall
remain undivided and shall not be the object of an action for
partition or division of the co-ownership. Any covenant to the
contrary shall be void.
Section 8. The deed of each individual apartment shall
express the particulars prescribed under letters (a) and (b) of
Section 7 relative to the apartments concerned and shall also
express all encumbrances thereon.
Section 9. Each apartment in a building shall be designated,
on the plans referred to in Section 7 of this Act, by letter or
number or other appropriate designation and any conveyance, or
other instrument affecting title to said apartment, which described
the apartment by using said letter or number followed by the
words "in Horizontal Property Regime"
shall be deemed to contain a good and sufficient description for
all purposes. Any conveyance of an individual apartment shall
be deemed to also convey the undivided interest of the owner in
the common elements, both general and limited, appertaining to
said apartment without specifically or particularly referring to
same.

Chapter 205 683
Section 10. All of the co-owners or the sole owner of a
building constituted into a horizontal property regime may waive
this regime and request the County Clerk to re-group or merge
the records of the filial estates with the principal property, pro
vided, that the filial estates are unencumbered, or if encumbered,
that the creditors in whose behalf the encumbrances are recorded
agree to accept as security the undivided portions of the property
owned by the debtors.
Section 11. The merger provided for in the preceding
section shall in no way bar the subsequent constitution of the
property into another horizontal property regime whenever so
desired and upon observance of the provisions of this Act.
Section 12. The administration of every building consti
tuted into a horizontal property regime shall be governed by
by-laws approved and adopted by the council of co-owners.
The by-laws may be amended from time to time by the council.
Section 13. The administrator, or board of administration,
or the person appointed by the by-laws of the regime, shall keep
a book with a detailed account of the receipts and expenditures
affecting the building and its administration and specifying the
maintenance and repair expenses of the common elements and
any other expenses incurred by or in behalf of the regime. Both
the book and vouchers accrediting the entries made thereon shall
be available for examination by all the co-owners at convenient
hours on working days that shall be set and announced for
general knowledge. All books and records shall be kept in
accordance with good accounting procedures and be audited at
least once a year by an auditor outside of the organization.
Section 14. All co-owners are bound to contribute pro-rata
toward the expenses of administration and of maintenance and
repairs of the general common elements, and, in the proper case,
of the limited common elements of the building, and toward any
other expenses lawfully agreed upon by the council of co-owners.
No owner shall be exempt from contributing toward such
expenses by waiver of the use or enjoyment of the common ele
ments, both general and limited, or by abandonment of the apart
ment belonging to him.

684 Acts of the General Assembly
Section 15. The laws relating to exemptions as set out in
Chapter 391 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes shall be applicable
to the individual apartments which shall have the benefit of said
exemption in those cases the same as in ownership of any other
property.
Section 16. Upon the sale or conveyance of an apartment,
all unpaid assessments against a co-owner for his pro-rata share
in the expenses to which Section 13 refers shall first be paid
out of the sale price or by the purchaser in preference over any
other assessments or charges of whatever nature except the
following:
(a) Assessments, liens, and charges in favor of the state for
taxes past due and unpaid on the apartment; and
(b) Payments due under mortgages duly recorded.
Section 17. The co-owners may, upon resolution of a ma
jority, insure the building against risks, without prejudice to the
right of each co-owner to insure his apartment on his own account
and for his own benefit.
Section 18. In case of fire or any other disaster the insur
ance indemnity shall, except as provided in the next succeeding
paragraph of this section, be applied to reconstruct the building.
Reconstruction shall not be compulsory where it comprises
the whole or more than two-thirds of the building. In such case,
and unless otherwise unanimously agreed upon by the co-owners,
the indemnity shall be delivered pro-rata to the co-owners en
titled to it in accordance with provision made in the by-laws or
in accordance with a decision of three-fourths of the co-owners
if there is no by-law provision.
Should it be proper to proceed with the reconstruction, the
provisions for such eventuality made in the by-laws shall be
observed, or in lieu thereof, the decision of the council of co-
owners shall prevail.
Section 19. Where the building is not insured or where the
insurance indemnity is insufficient to cover the cost of recon
struction, the new building costs shall be paid by all the co

Chapter 205 685
owners directly affected by the damage, in proportion to the value
of their respective apartments, or as may be provided by said
by-laws; and if any one or more of those composing the minority
shall refuse to make such payments the majority may proceed
with the reconstruction at the expense of all the co-owners
benefitted thereby, upon proper resolution setting forth the cir
cumstances of the case and the cost of the works, with the in
tervention of the council of co-owners.
The provisions of this section may be changed by unanimous
resolution of the parties concerned, adopted subsequent to the
date on which the fire or other disaster occurred.
Section 20. Taxes, assessments and other charges of this
State, or of any political subdivision, or of any special improve
ment district, or any other taxing or assessing authority shall be
assessed against and collected on each individual apartment,
each of which shall be carried on the tax books as a separate and
distinct entity for that purpose, and not on the building or prop
erty as a whole. The valuation of the general and limited com
mon elements shall be assessed proportionately among the co-
owners thereof. No forfeiture or sale of the building or prop
erty as a whole for delinquent taxes, assessments or charges shall
ever divest or in anywise affect the title to an individual apart
ment so long as taxes, assessments and charges to said individ
ual apartment are currently paid.
Section 21. The provisions of this chapter shall be in addi
tion to and supplemental to all other provisions of the Kentucky
Revised Statutes, provided that wherever the application of
the provisions of this chapter conflict with the application of such
other provisions, this chapter shall prevail.
Section 22. Whenever they deem it proper, the planning
and zoning commission of any county or municipality may adopt
supplemental rules and regulations governing a horizontal prop
erty regime established under this chapter in order to imple
ment this program.
Section 23. If any provision of this chapter, or any section,
sentence, clause, phrase or word, or the application thereof

636 Acts of the General Assembly
in any circumstance is held invalid, the validity of the remainder
of the chapter and of the application of any such provision,
section, sentence, clause, phrase or word in other circumstances
shall not be affected thereby.
Approved March 22, 1962
CHAPTER 206
(S. B. 68)
AN ACT relating to the adoption of codes and also revision of ordinances
by reference.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. There is hereby added to Chapter 82 of the
Kentucky Revised Statutes the following section:
82.080. Adoption of codes and revisions of ordinances by
references.
( 1 ) The legislative bodies of cities of all classes shall have
power and authority to adopt traffic, building, plumbing, elec
trical and other codes by reference and also revisions and
codifications of ordinances without the necessity of newspaper or
other publication of same, provided that at least five days' notice
of the meeting at which same are adopted has been given in a
newspaper published, or of general circulation, in said city, and
further that said code has been approved at such meeting of said
city legislative body and has been made a part of the public
records of the city by resolution or ordinance.
Approved March 22, 1962

Chapter 207 687
CHAPTER 207
(S. B. 70)
AN ACT relating to unemployment insurance, and declaring an emer
gency.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 341.050 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
Covered employment. (1) As used in this chapter, unless
the context clearly requires otherwise and subject to the other
provisions of this section, "covered employment" means service,
including service in interstate commerce, performed for wages
or under any contract of hire, written or oral, express or implied
in which the relationship of the individual performing such serv
ice and the employing unit for which such services are rendered
is, as to those services, the legal relationship of employer and
employe.
(2) "Covered employment" includes a worker's entire
service performed within or both within and without this state if:
(a) The service is localized in this state; or
(b) The service is not localized in any state, but some of
the service is performed in this state, and:
1. The base of operations, or, if there is no base of opera
tions, then the place from which such service is directed or con
trolled, is in this state; or
2. The base of operations or place from which such serv
ice is directed or controlled is not in any state in which some
part of the services is performed, but the worker's residence is
in this state.
(3) Services not covered under subsections (1) or (2)
of this section, and performed entirely without this state, with
respect to no part of which contributions are required and paid
under an unemployment compensation law of any other state or of
the Federal Government, are covered employment if the worker
performing them is a resident of this state and if the Commis
sioner approves the election of the employing unit for which they

688 Acts of the General Assembly
are performed that the entire service of such worker shall be
deemed to be covered employment.
( 4 ) Service shall be deemed to be localized within a state if:
(a) The service is performed entirely within such state; or
(b) The service is performed both within and without
such state, but the service performed without such state is inci
dental to the worker's service within the state, for example, is
temporary or transitory in nature or consists of isolated trans
actions.
(5) (a) After December 31, 1961, the term "covered em
ployment" shall also include:
1. Service which is not localized in any state but is per
formed by an employe on or in connection with an American
aircraft, if either the contract of service is not entered into within
this state or within any other state and during the performance
of the contract of service and while the employe is employed on
the aircraft, it touches at an airfield in this state: Provided that
the employe is employed on and in connection with such air
craft when outside the United States;
2. Service performed by an officer or member of the crew
of an American vessel on or in connection with such vessel, if
the operating office, from which the operations of such vessel
operating on navigable waters within or within and without the
United States are ordinarily and regularly supervised, managed,
directed and controlled, is within this state.
(b) As used in this section the term "American vessel'
means any vessel documented or numbered under the laws of
the United States; and includes any vessel which is neither
documented or numbered under the laws of the United States
nor documented under the laws of any foreign country, if its
crew is employed solely by one or more citizens or residents of
the United States or corporations organized under the laws of
the United States or of any state; and the term "American air
craft" means an aircraft registered under the laws of the United
States.
(6) Unless the employing unit thereof has elected that

Chapter 207
such services become covered employment under the pro
visions of subsection (3) of KRS 341.250, "covered employment"
does not include:
(a) Agricultural labor;
(b) Domestic service in a private home;
(c) Service performed in the employ of this state, or any
political subdivision thereof or of any of its municipalities;
(d) Service performed in the employ of any other state or
of any political subdivision thereof, or of the United States Gov
ernment or an instrumentality of the United States exempt by
federal law from the contributions imposed by this chapter,
except that to the extent that the Congress of the United States
shall permit states to require instrumentalities of the United
States to make payments into an unemployment fund under a
state unemployment insurance law, all the provisions of this
chapter shall be applicable to such instrumentalities, and to
services performed for such instrumentalities, in the same man
ner, to the same extent and on the same terms as to all other
employing units, individuals, and services; provided that if this
state shall not be certified for any year by the Secretary of Labor
of the United States under section 3304 of the Federal Internal
Revenue Code, the payments required of such instrumentalities
with respect to such year shall be refunded from the fund in the
same manner and within the same period as is provided in
KRS 341.330 with respect to the contributions erroneously col
lected; [and services performed in the employ of a corporation
or agency created under an Act of Congress and engaged in the
performance of a governmental function and the employes of
which are compensated out of funds appropriated by Congress,]
(e) Service with respect to which unemployment com
pensation is payable under an unemployment compensation sys
tem established by an Act of Congress. The Commissioner may
enter into agreements with the proper agencies under such Act
of Congress to provide reciprocal treatment to workers who have,
after acquiring potential rights to benefits under this chapter,
acquired rights to unemployment compensation under such Act
of Congress, or who have, after acquiring potential rights to

690 Acts of the General Assembly
unemployment compensation under such Act of Congress, ac
quired rights to benefits under this chapter;
(f) Service performed by a worker in the employ of his
son, daughter, or spouse and service performed by a child under
the age of twenty-one in the employ of his father or mother;
(g) Service performed in the employ of a corporation,
community chest, fund or foundation, organized and operated
exclusively for religious, charitable, scientific, literary, or educa
tional purposes, or for the prevention of cruelty to children or
animals, no part of the net earnings of which inures to the bene
fit of any private shareholder or individual and no substantial
part of the activities of which is carrying on propaganda, or
otherwise attempting to influence legislation; provided that serv
ice performed in the employ of an organizaion operated for the
primary purpose of carrying on a trade or business for profit
shall not be exempt on the grounds that all of the profits are
payable to one or more organizations exempt under this sub
section; and further provided that services exempt under this
subsection shall not include services performed for an employing
unit with respect to which such employing unit is liable for any
federal tax against which credit may be taken for contributions
paid into a state unemployment insurance fund;
(h) Service performed as a student nurse in the employ
of a hospital or a nurses' training school by a worker who is
enrolled and is regularly attending classes in a nurses' training
school chartered or approved pursuant to the laws of this state;
and service performed as an interne in the employ of a hospital
by a worker who has completed a four years' course in a medical
school chartered or approved pursuant to the laws of this state;
(i) Service performed by a worker for an employing unit
as an insurance agent or as an insurance solicitor, if all such serv
ice performed by such worker for such employing unit is per
formed for remuneration solely by way of commission;
(j) Service performed by a worker under the age of
eighteen in the delivery or distribution of newspapers or shopping
news, not including delivery or distribution to any point for sub
sequent delivery or distribution; or

Chapter 207 691
(k) Service performed by a full-time student who is reg
ularly attending classes at a school, college or university;
( 1 ) Service not in the course of the employing unit's trade
or business performed in any calendar quarter by a worker,
unless the cash remuneration paid for such service is fifty dollars
or more and such service is performed by an individual who is
regularly employed by such employing unit to perform such
service. For the purpose of this paragraph, an individual shall be
deemed to be regularly employed by an employing unit during
a calendar quarter only if—
1. On each of some twenty-four days during such quarter
such individual performs for such employing unit for some por
tion of the day service not in the course of the employing unit's
trade or business, or
2. Such individual was regularly employed ( as determined
under 1. above) by such employing unit in the performance
of such service during the preceding calendar quarter.
(m) Service performed in any calendar quarter in the em
ploy of any organization exempt from income tax under section
501(a) of the Federal Internal Revenue Code (other than an or
ganization described in section 401(a) ), or under section 521 of
the Federal Internal Revenue Code, if the remuneration for such
service is less than $50.
(7) For the purpose of this chapter, if the services per
formed during one-half or more of any pay period by a worker
for the employing unit employing him constitute covered employ
ment, all the services of such worker for such period shall be
deemed to be covered employment; but if the services performed
during more than one-half of any such pay period by a worker
for the employing unit employing him do not constitute covered
employment, then none of the services of such worker for such
period shall be deemed to be covered employment. As used in
this subsection, the term "pay period" means a period (of not
more than thirty-one consecutive days) for which a payment of
remuneration is ordinarily made to a worker by the employing
unit employing him. This subsection shall not be applicable with
respect to services performed in a pay period by a worker for the

692 Acts of the General Assembly
employing unit employing him, where any of such service is
excepted by subsection (6) (e) of this section.
Section 2. Section 341.145 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
Reciprocal arrangements. ( 1 ) The Commissioner may enter
into [administrative] arrangements with the appropriate agencies
of other states or of the Federal Government, or both, [similarly
charged with the administration of unemployment compensation
laws] for the purpose of assisting the Commissioner and such
agencies in the payment of benefits and the furnishing of services
to unemployed or underemployed workers [under the several
laws]. Such arrangements may provide that the respective
agencies shall, for and on behalf of each other, act as agents in
effecting registrations for work, notices of unemployment, and
any other certifications or statements relating to a worker's claim
for benefits; in making investigation, taking depositions, holding
hearings, or otherwise securing information relating to benefit
eligibility and payments; and in such other matters as the Com
missioner considers suitable in effectuating the purpose of these
administrative arrangements.
(2) The Commissioner may enter into arrangements with
the appropriate agencies of other states or the Federal Govern
ment whereby workers performing services in this and other states
for a single employing unit under circumstances not specifically
provided in KRS 341.050, or under similar provisions in the unem
ployment compensation laws of such other states, shall be deemed
to be engaged in employment performed entirely within this state
or within one of such other states.
(3) (a) Any provisions of this chapter to the contrary
notwithstanding, the Commissioner is hereby authorized to enter
into reciprocal arrangements with duly authorized agencies of
other states or of the Federal Government, or both, whereby
workers with potential rights to benefits in this and another state
or other states or the Federal Government may have such rights
determined upon the basis of his combined potential rights in
such jurisdictions. Benefits, based upon such combined potential
rights, may be paid either under provisions of this chapter or
under the provisions of the law of another state or the Federal

Chapter 207 693
Government, or under such combination of provisions of both
laws as may be agreed upon as being fair and reasonable to all
affected interests. No such an arrangement shall be entered into
unless each agency entering into such arrangement shall agree
to reimburse the fund of the other agency to the extent of the
liability incurred under terms of the arrangement. Reimburse
ments to another state or the Federal Government, paid from the
fund pursuant to this subsection, shall be deemed to be benefits
for the purposes of this chapter and charged to employers' re
serve accounts in accordance with the provisions of subsections
(2) and (3) of KRS 341.530 to the extent of calculations made
on wages paid during the base period established by KRS 341.090
and wages paid after such base period; provided, however, bene
fits based on a period previous to the base-wage period estab
lished by KRS 341.090 shall be charged to the pooled account.
(b) In order that such reciprocal arrangements, when en
tered into, may be effectuated, wages for insured work under
an employment security law of another state or of the Federal
Government shall be deemed to be wages earned in covered
employment from a subject employer for the purpose of deter
mining his benefits under this chapter.
Section 3. Section 341.290 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
Contributions from employers not subject to Federal Unem
ployment Tax Act. Any subject employer not required to pay
the tax imposed by section 1600 of the Federal Unemployment
Tax Act (53 Stat. Part I, sections 1600-1610) shall pay an equaliz
ing contribution to be allocated to administration purposes
amounting to three-tenths percent of the wages paid by it prior
to January 1, 1962, with respect to covered employment. The
contributions required under this section shall be credited to the
Unemployment Compensation Administration Fund. The State
Treasurer shall maintain a separate record of all money received
for the Unemployment Compensation Administration Fund under
this section. This money shall not be expended or available for
expenditure in any manner that would permit its substitution for,
or a corresponding reduction in, Federal funds that would be
available in its absence to finance expenditures for the administra

694 Acts of the General Assembly
tion of this chapter. But nothing in this chapter shall prevent
this money from being used as a revolving fund to cover neces
sary and proper expenditures for which Federal funds have been
duly requested but not received, subject to the charging of such
expenditures against such funds when received. The Commis
sioner may also authorize to be charged against such money any
expenditures he deems proper and desirable for the administration
of this chapter, if no other funds are available or can properly be
used to finance such expenditures.
Section 4. A new section of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is created to read as follows:
Cancellation and reissue of checks. None of the funds, ac
counts or monies administered, established, or maintained under
this chapter shall be credited or otherwise transferred to the Gen
eral Expenditure Fund or any other fund or account by reason
of the provisions of KRS 41.370 or subject to escheat under any
other section of the Kentucky Revised Statutes. Any unpaid
check issued pursuant to the provisions of this chapter may be
cleared from the records of the treasurer within the time and
in the manner specified in KRS 41.370, except that the amount
of such check shall remain to the credit of the fund or account
against which it was drawn. Such checks if thereafter presented
for payment may be paid within the time and in the manner set
forth in such section, except that the amount thereof shall be
charged to the fund or account against which it was originally
drawn.
Section 5. The provisions of this Act shall be applicable
on and after January 1, 1962. With respect to calendar years be
ginning before January 1, 1962, the provisions of KRS Chapter
341, as they existed immediately prior to the enactment of this
Act, shall remain applicable, the same as though they had not
been amended by this Act
Section 6. Whereas the major provisions of this Act are
enacted to conform with changes in the Federal Unemployment
Tax Act as amended, which became effective as of January 1,
1962, and it being desirable that the records and accounts of the
Division of Unemployment Insurance reflect such changes as near

Chapter 208 695
the beginning of the tax year as possible, an emergency is de
clared to exist, and this Act shall become effective upon its pas
sage and approval by the Governor.
Approved March 22, 1962
CHAPTER 208
(S. B. 85)
AN ACT relating to insurance.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth

Section 136.330 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is amended

(1) Every foreign life insurance company doing business

panies, shall, by March 1 [January 31] of each year, return to

Acts of the General Assembly

CHAPTER 209
(S. B. 90)
AN ACT relating to marriage, annulment, and divorce statistics.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 213.330 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
Each county court clerk shall, on or before the tenth day of
each month, furnish to the Division of Vital Statistics from the
marriage licenses issued and the marriage certificates returned to
the clerk during the previous month such information as may be
required by the State Department of Health upon forms pre
scribed and furnished by the department. The county court clerk
shall collect from the applicants for a marriage license at the time
the license is issued fifty cents which shall constitute his fee for
forwarding the required information to the Division of Vital Sta
tistics of the State Department of Health.
Section 2. Section 213.340 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
( 1 ) In all actions for divorce, absolute or limited or annul
ment of marriage, the plaintiff or his attorney or legal representa
tive shall file concurrently with the complaint, such information
as is requested on forms prescribed and furnished by the Division
of Vital Statistics.
(2) Each circuit court clerk shall, within forty-five days
after entry of a final judgment of divorce, absolute or limited or
annulment of marriage, [or adoption] complete the form pre

Chapter 210 697
scribed and furnished by [furnish to] the Division of Vital Sta
tistics [such information as may be available from the record to
the State Department of Health upon forms prescribed and fur
nished by the department.] and forward it to the Division of Vital
Statistics.
(3) The circuit court clerk shall be paid a fee of fifty cents
for preparing and forwarding the required information to the Di
vision of Vital Statistics of the State Department of Health. The
fee for each such report shall be added to the court costs allowed
in such cases.
Approved March 22, 1962
CHAPTER 210
(S. B. 104)
AN ACT relating to the correction of the Kentucky Revised Statutes.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 14.050 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
( 1 ) The Secretary of State shall have custody of the books,

Section 15. Section 66.300 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
(1) There shall be a County Debt Commission, consisting
of the Governor, the State Treasurer, the Auditor of Public
Accounts, the Secretary of State, the Attorney-General, the Com
missioner of Highways, the Commissioner of Finance, and the
Commissioner of Revenue. The Governor shall be chairman.

Chapter 210 707
Governor shall be chairman, and when both the Governor and

missioner of Finance shall be chairman. The State Local Finance

ber shall serve without additional compensation, and each mem
ber except the Governor may, by advising the Governor in writing,
authorize an executive officer of the department of which he is
head to act for him as a member of the commission.
(2) The chairman may, at his discretion, call meetings of
the commission and designate the time and place of the meeting.
The chairman and four other members of the commission shall
constitute a quorum. The commission, by a majority vote of the
quorum present, may adopt appropriate rules and regulations
for the enforcement of KRS 66.280 to 66.390, hear and dispose
of appeals from rulings of the State Local Finance Officer, and
perform the other duties imposed upon it.
(3) The commission may hold public hearings on any
question before
it,or the chairman may appoint
acommittee of
the commission or
areferee to hold the hearing with instructions
to report any findings or recommendations to the commission.
The officials or taxpayers of
a county or the creditors thereof
having
amaterial interest in any hearing before the commission
shall, upon their demand, be heard.
(4) The commission shall study the problems of county
finance in Kentucky and other states for the purpose of making
recommendations to the Legislative Research Commission [Legis
lative Council] and the General Assembly. The commission
may require the State Local Finance Officer to prepare studies
on designated subjects, and to publish such studies as
itdirects,
and to supply any available information and make such special
inquiries or investigations as
itdeems necessary.
(5) Any county aggrieved by
a ruling of the State Local
Finance Officer may within fifteen days thereafter appeal to the
commission. The decision of the commission shall be final except
as otherwise provided in KRS 66.280 to 66.390.

ment of Revenue shall cause to be listed for taxation all property

erty assessed shall file in the office of the county clerk of the

mons shall be executed by the sheriff by delivering a copy thereof

sists of tangible personal property the summons shall be placed
in a conspicuous place where the property is located. In the case
of tangible and intangible personal property, where the owner
and his place of residence are unknown and no one has posses
sion of the property, an action for assessment shall be instituted
by filing the petition above mentioned and procuring construc
tive service against the owner under the provisions of CR 4.05,
4.06, 4.07 and 4.08. [ § § 58, 59 and 60 of the Civil Code.] In all
of the above cases an attachment of the property omitted from
assessment may be procured against the owner, at the time of
the institution of the action or thereafter, and without the exe
cution of a bond by the Commonwealth or its relator, by the
representative of the Department of Revenue making an affi
davit that the property described in the petition is subject to
state, county, school or other taxing district tax, and is unassessed
for any taxable year.

ingly permits any motor vehicle owned by such person to be

Section 27. Section 199.480 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
( 1) The following persons shall be made parties defendant
in an action for leave to adopt a child:
(a) The child to be adopted;
(b) The natural living parents of a child under twenty-
one, if the child is legitimate, and its mother if the child is born
out of wedlock, provided that no natural parent need be made
a party defendant in the event the parental rights of such
parent have been terminated under KRS 199.600 to 199.630;
(c) The child's guardian, if it has [have] one;
(d) If the care, custody and control of the child has been
transferred to the department, any other individual, institution or
agency, then the department, such other individual, institution or
agency shall be named a party defendant.
( 2 ) Each party defendant shall be brought before the court
in the same manner as provided in other civil cases except that
if the child to be adopted is under fourteen years of age and
the department, individual, institution or agency has custody of
such child, the service of process upon such child shall be had
by serving a copy of the summons in such action upon the de
partment, individual, institution or agency, any provision of
CR 4.04(3) [Section 52 of the Civil Code of Practice] to the
contrary notwithstanding.
(3) If the child's natural living parents, if the child is
legitimate, or its mother if the child is born out of wedlock, are
parties defendant, no guardian ad litem need be appointed to
represent the child to be adopted.

Section 29. Section 229.020 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:

[department,] as provided in this chapter, no person shall:
( 1 ) Engage in a prize-fight, or a fight for a bet or stakes;
(2) Act as a second in a fight, bear a challenge or the
oral or written acceptance of a challenge for the fight, make up
or aid in making up the stakes for the fight or assist in any
way in the bringing on or conducting of the fight;
(3) Train or prepare, or assist another in training or pre
paring for such a fight in this or any other state;
(4) Be present at any such fight as a voluntary spectator;

trolled or occupied by him for such a fight; or
(6) Engage in, or be voluntarily present at, a "pitched
battle."
Section 30. Section 229.040 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
(1) The Division of Business and Professional Regulation
[Department of Business Regulation] shall have sole control and
jurisdiction over all boxing and wrestling matches and exhibitions
held by any person. No boxing or wrestling match or exhibition
shall be held except by the license of the division [department]
and in accordance with this chapter.
(2) The division [department] may in its discretion issue,
and at its pleasure revoke, a license to hold a boxing or wrestling
match or exhibition.
(3) The division [department] may issue subpoenas for
the attendance of witnesses. Any person who disobeys a sub
poena of the division [department] shall be subject to the same
penalty as if the disobedience occurred in an action in the circuit
court.

utes is amended to read as follows:

720 Acts of the General Assembly
(1) Every license shall be subject to such regulations as
the division [department] prescribes.

be addressed to the division [department] and shall be verified
by the applicant, if an individual, or by some officer, if a cor
poration or association, on whose behalf the application is made.
It shall contain a recital of such facts as show the applicant
entitled to receive a license and such other facts and recitals as
the division [department] requires to be shown.

utes is amended to read as follows:

within fifteen miles of a citv or cities having an aggregate popula
tion of more than seventy-five thousand and less than two
hundred thousand, and one hundred dollars elsewhere. The fee
shall be payable to the division, [department.]

utes is amended to read as follows:

out the payment of any taxes or license, to any accredited college,
university, school, Young Men's Christian Association or Young
Men's Hebrew Association to hold boxing or wrestling matches
or exhibitions upon a sufficient showing that the matches or
exhibitions are to be held by and between bona fide students or
members of such accredited colleges, universities, schools, Young
Men's Christian Associations or Young Men's Hebrew Associa
tions. The division [department] shall also issue a permit, without
payment of taxes or license, to any organization to hold a boxing
or wrestling match or exhibition under the sanction of the Ken
tucky Association of the Amateur Athletic Union of the United
States. No boxer or wrestler who has received any fee, purse
or other compensation shall be eligible to appear in any match
or exhibition sponsored by such a college, university, school,
Young Men's Christian Association, Young Men's Hebrew Asso
ciation, or organization.

Section 34. Section 229.090 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:

ment of the tax imposed by KRS 229.100. Upon the approval

a certificate of filing and approval, which shall be filed by the
applicant with the division [department,] with the application for
the license. No license shall be issued until such certificate has

utes is amended to read as follows:

than those holding a permit under subsection ( 1 ) of KRS 229.080,

ten report, verified by the person, if an individual, or by some
officer, if a corporation or association, showing the number of
tickets sold for the match or exhibition, the amount of the gross
receipts and such other matters as the division [department] pre
scribes. He shall also, within the same period, pay to the division
[department] a tax of five percent of the gross receipts from the
sale of all tickets to the match or exhibition.

Section 43. Section 271.145 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
(1) No corporation shall engage in business other than that
expressly authorized by its articles of incorporation, or the law
under which it was organized, nor shall it hold any real estate,
except such as may be proper and necessary for carrying on its
legitimate business, for a longer period than five years, under
penalty of escheat.
(2) Any action to escheat real estate held contrary to this
section shall be brought in the name of the state in the circuit
court of the county in which any part of it is situated. Before
any such action may be instituted, the Attorney-General or the
Department of Revenue shall give written notice to the corpora
tion that, unless the property is disposed of within two years
after receipt of the notice, an action will be commenced to forfeit
the real estate. No such action shall be brought before that time.
The notice shall contain a brief description of the real estate, and
shall be served as provided by CR 5.02. [Section 628 of the Civil
Code.] The serving of the notice shall not invalidate or impair
the corporation's title to the real estate, nor abridge its right to
convey it before the action, nor affect the title of the person to
whom conveyed.

utes is amended to read as follows:

laws provided such bylaws are not inconsistent with the pro

visions of KRS 355 Art. 8 [Chapter 274 of the Kentucky Revised

utes is amended to read as follows:
