may, pursuant to an agreement in writing, transfer their shares
to any person, or to a corporation having authority to act as
trustee, for the purpose of vesting in such person or corporation,
as trustee, all voting or other rights pertaining to such shares for
a period not exceeding ten years, and upon the terms and con
ditions stated in the agreement.
(2) A duplicate original of such agreement shall be filed
in the registered office of the corporation and a conformed copy
thereof shall be open daily during business hours to the inspec
tion of any shareholder or any depositor under said agreement,
or the attorney of any shareholder or depositor.
(3) Any other shareholder may transfer his shares to the
same trustee upon the terms and conditions stated in said agree
ment, and thereupon shall be bound by all of the provisions
of said agreement.
(4) The certificates of shares so transferred shall be sur
rendered and cancelled, and new certificates therefor issued to
the trustee. The new certificates shall recite that they are issued
pursuant to said agreement. In the entry of transfer on the books
of the corporation it shall also be noted that the transfer is made
pursuant to said agreement.
(5) Unless the agreement otherwise provides, the trustee
shall execute and deliver to the transferors voting trust certifi
cates. Such voting trust certificates shall be transferable in the
same manner and with the same effect as certificates of stock
under the provisions of KRS 355 Art. 8. [Chapter 274 of the
Kentucky Revised Statutes.]
(6) The trustee shall possess all voting and other rights
pertaining to the shares so transferred and registered in his name
subject to the terms and conditions of, and for the period speci
fied in said agreement.

Chapter 210 727
(7) Unless otherwise provided in said agreement:
(a) The trustee may vote in person or by proxy;
( b ) If there are two or more trustees the manner of voting
shall be determined as provided in subsection (4) of KRS 271.315;
(c) Vacancies among the trustees shall be filled by the
remaining trustees;
(d) A trustee shall incur no responsibility as trustee except
for his own individual neglect or malfeasance.

utes is amended to read as follows:

section shall be brought in the name of the Commonwealth of
Kentucky in the circuit court of the county in which any part of
it is situated. Before any such action may be instituted, the
Attorney-General or the Department of Revenue shall give writ
ten notice to the religious society taking or holding the title to

17.03 [Sections 625 to 628 of the Civil Code] .

utes is amended to read as follows:
(1) A lien on a parity with state, county, and municipal
ad valorem tax liens, and superior to the lien of any mortgage
or other encumbrance heretofore or hereafter created is hereby
created in favor of the division upon all property of any subject
employer from whom contributions, interest or penalties are or

728 Acts of the General Assembly
may hereafter become due. This lien shall be for a sum equal
to the amount at any time due from each subject employer to
the division on account of contributions, interest and penalties
thereon. The lien shall commence from such time as the contri
butions, interest or penalties become due.
(2) The commissioner may file notice of this lien with the
county court clerk of any county where the subject employer
has property, giving the name of the subject employer, his ad
dress, and the amount of the lien. The clerk shall file same in
the same manner as lis pendens are filed. The files shall be
designated "Miscellaneous State Tax Liens." The notice, when
so filed, shall be conclusive notice to all persons of the lien on
the property having legal situs in that county, except that noth
ing in this chapter shall be construed to alter or change in any
way the law relative to the rights and duties of a holder in due
course as provided in KRS 355 Art. 3, [KRS Chapter 356,] or
affect the rights of any person taking the property or a hen
thereon for value without actual or constructive notice. The
clerk shall be entitled to a fee of $1.25 for filing the lien, and
said fee shall become a part of the lien as an added cost of the
delinquent subject employer to be paid by him as a part of the
amount necessarv to release the lien, and in no event shall the
Commonwealth, the department or the division be required to
pay same.
(3) In addition and as an alternative to any other remedy,
the commissioner may enforce the lien by petition in the name
of this state to the circuit court of Franklin County, if the min
isterial acts necessary to enforce the lien by the sale of the
liened property or any part of it are performed by the appropriate
officers of the circuit court of the county in which the property
is situated under the direction of and reporting to the Franklin
Circuit Court. The manner of enforcement shall be the same
as that provided for the enforcement of other tax hens.
(4) (a) The commissioner may issue a certificate of re
lease of lien upon the furnishing of a corporate surety bond satis
factory to the commissioner by such employing unit in the amount
of one hundred and twenty-five percent of the sum of such con
tributions, interest and penalty, for which hen is claimed, con
ditioned upon the prompt payment of such contribution, interest

Chapter 210 729
and penalty, for which lien is claimed, conditioned upon the
prompt payment of such contribution, together with interest and
and penalty thereon, by such employing unit to the division in
accordance with the provisions set forth in such bond.
(b) The commissioner may issue a certificate of partial re
lease of any part of the property subject to the lien if he finds that
the fair market value of that part of such property remaining sub
ject to the lien is at least equal to the amount of all other liens
upon such property plus double the amount of the liability for
contributions, interest and penalties thereon remaining unsatisfied.
(c) The commissioner may issue a certificate of partial re
lease of any part of the property or individual piece of property
subject to the hen if he finds that the interest of the Common
wealth in the property to be so released has no value.
Section 48. Section 393.190 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
On any action filed by a county attorney under the provisions
of this chapter, the assistant Attorney-General provided for in
KRS 15.105 [15.140] shall offer assistance and suggestions to the
county attorney in the preparation of the petition or any pleadings,
and revise and correct them as he considers necessary, subject to
the ultimate approval of the commissioner, when he is required
to sign them.
Section 49. Section 403.020 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
( 1 ) A divorce may be granted to either party for the fol
lowing causes:
(a) Such impotency or malformation as prevents sexual
intercourse; or
(b) Living apart without any cohabitation for five con
secutive years next before application.
(2) A divorce may be granted to the party not in fault for
the following causes:
(a) Abandonment for one year;
(b) Living in adultery;

730 Acts of the General Assembly
(c) Condemnation for felony in or out of this state;
(d) Concealment from the other party of any loathsome
disease existing at the time of marriage, or contracting such a
disease afterward;
(e) Force, duress or fraud in obtaining the marriage; or
(f) Uniting with any religious society whose creed and rules
require a renunciation of the marriage covenant, or forbid hus
band and wife from cohabiting.
( 3 ) A divorce may be granted to the wife for the following
causes:
(a) When not in like fault, habitual drunkenness on the
part of the husband of not less than one year's duration, accom
panied by a wasting of the husband's estate and without anv
suitable provision for the maintenance of the wife or children;
(b) Habitually behaving toward her, for not less than six
months in such cruel and inhuman manner as to indicate a settled
aversion to her or to destroy permanently her peace or happiness;
or
(c) Such cruel beating or injury, or attempt at injurv, of
the wife as indicates an outrageous temper in the husband, or
probable danger to her life, or of great bodily injury, from her
remaining with him.
(4) A divorce may be granted to the husband for the fol
lowing causes:
( a) Where the wife is pregnant by another man without the
husband's knowledge at the time of marriage;
(b) When not in like fault, habitual drunkenness on the
part of the wife of not less than one year's duration;
( c) Adultery by the wife, or such lewd, lascivious behavior
on her part as proves her to be unchaste, without actual proof
of an act of adultery;
(d) Habitually behaving toward him, for not less than six
months in such cruel and inhuman manner as to indicate a settled
aversion to him or to destroy permanently his peace or happiness;
or

Chapter 210 731
(e) Such cruel beating or injury, or attempt at injury, of
the husband as indicates an outrageous temper in the wife, or
probable danger to his life, or great bodily injury, from his remain
ing with her.
(5) (a) A divorce may be granted to the same spouse when
either the husband or wife has become permanently and incurably
insane; provided, that no divorce shall be granted under this sub
section unless such permanently incurably insane person shall
have been confined in an insane asylum, hospital or other similar
institution for a period of not less than five successive years prior
to the filing of the petition for divorce, whether such period of
time shall have been prior to or subsequent to June 19, 1946,
or partly both, nor unless the court shall find from the testimony
of two or more physicians competent in psychiatry that such in
sanity, in their opinion, is permanently incurable. Except that
when the insane spouse has been committed upon the testimony
of two doctors to an insane asylum, hospital or other similar in
stitution operated within this state by the Veterans Administra
tion, Public Health Service or other Federal agency and has been
by said agency transferred to its asylum, hospital or other facility
in another state and has been kept at said place or places of trans
fer for more than five years and not restored to competency, said
facts shall constitute sufficient proof of permanent insanity to
authorize the granting of a divorce to the sane spouse.
(b) The petition shall be filed under oath, in the court
having jurisdiction over divorce matters in either the countv where
the plaintiff resides or in the county where the institution is
located in which the insane person is confined.
( c ) Service of process under this subsection shall be made
as provided in CR 4.04(3) [Section 53, Kentucky Civil Code of
Practice] , relating to service of process upon persons of unsound
mind.
(d) In cases arising under this subsection the court shall
possess all powers which courts of equity now have, or which
may hereafter be conferred upon them, relative to alimony, and
custody and control and support of infant children of the parties.
Where the plaintiff is the husband the court may, when necessary
and circumstances warrant, require the plaintiff to pay alimony,

732 Acts of the General Assembly
support or maintenance for the benefit of said defendant and
make provisions for the reasonable expense for the burial of
said defendant, or it may require the plaintiff to give bond to
the state of Kentucky, conditioned upon the payment by the
plaintiff for the care and maintenance of said defendant during
the remainder of her life and necessary funeral expenses. The
court may, upon notice, modify or revoke any orders or decrees
relating to the custody of infant children and their support and
the support and maintenance of the defendant.
Section 50. Sections 64.490, 95.990, 141.022, 164.286, and
187.630 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes are repealed.
Approved March 22, 1962
CHAPTER 211
(S. B. 118)
AN ACT relating to the Department of Child Welfare and to Child Wel
fare and Adoptions.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 199.011 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
As used in this chapter, unless the context otherwise requires
( 1 ) "Commissioner" means Commissioner of Child Walfare
(2) "Department" means the Department of Child Welfare
(3) "Child" means any person who has not reached his
eighteenth birthday;
(4) "Neglected or dependent child" means any child who
is under such improper parental care and control or guardianship
as to injure or endanger the morals, health or welfare of himself
or others;
(5) "Abandoned child" means any child who by reason of
the desertion of his parents or guardian is in destitute circum
stances;

Chapter 211 733
(6) "Child-caring institution or agency" means any agency
or institution other than a state institution or one certified by an
appropriate agency as operated primarily for educational or med
ical purposes, in which four or more children, not related by
blood, adoption or marriage to the person maintaining the insti
tution, are cared for and maintained [in residence] for compen
sation or otherwise;
( 7 ) "Child-placing institution or agency" means any agency
or institution other than a state agency which arranges for the
placement of children in child-caring homes or child-caring insti
tutions, or which arranges for the placement of children for adop
tion;
(8) "Child welfare worker" means an employe of the de
partment designated as such by the Commissioner or social worker
employed by a county or city who has been approved by the
department to handle, under its supervision, services to children;
(9) "Boarding home or foster parent" as used in subsection
(4) of KRS 199.600 means persons who board children held in
custody by the department.
Section 2. Section 199.420 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
( 1 ) The commissioner shall have the power and authority
to adopt, amend or rescind such rules and regulations as he deems
necessary or suitable for the proper administration of the func
tions of the department, [division] including qualification for the
receipt of federal funds and for cooperation with other state
and federal agencies. Such rules and regulations shall become
effective in the manner and at the time prescribed by the com
missioner.
(2) In the administration of KRS 199.410 to 199.670 the
commissioner shall cooperate to the fullest extent possible with
any agency of this state or any other state of the United States.
(3) The commissioner is authorized, subject to the pro
visions of KRS Chapters 12, 18, 42, 45, and 64, to appoint, fix
the compensation and prescribe the duties and powers of such
officers and employes as may be necessary in the performance of

734 Acts of the General Assembly
his duties under KRS 199.410 to 199.670. All positions shall be
filled by persons selected and appointed on a nonpartisan merit
basis, in accordance with merit standards established by law.
The commissioner shall not employ or pay any person who is
an officer or committee member of any political party organiza
tion. The commissioner may delegate to any such person so ap
pointed such power and authority as he deems reasonable and
proper for the effective administration of KRS 199.410 to 199.670.
(4) The commissioner shall have the power and authority
to elect coverage for the workers in the department, [division]
under the provisions of KRS Chapter 341, and may elect coverage
for such workers under the Workmen's Compensation Law of
this state. In the event such coverage is elected the payment of
contributions under KRS Chapter 341 and premiums under the
Workmen's Compensation Law shall be deemed a proper cost
of administration.
( 5 ) The salaries and expenses of the Commissioner and his
staff, shall be considered a proper cost of administration [of KRS
199.410 to 199.670, to be charged to the Child Welfare and Benefit
Fund in that proportion which the cost of such services rendered
in the administration of KRS 199.410 to 199.670 bears to the
overall cost of the services rendered in the administration of the
department] and charged to the funds allocated to the Depart
ment of Child Welfare.
Section 3. Section 199.470 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
(1) Any adult person who is a resident of this state or
who has resided in this state for twelve months next before filing,
may file a petition for leave to adopt a child in the circuit court
of the county in which the petitioner resides.
(2) If petitioner is married, the husband or wife must join
in a petition for leave to adopt a child unless the petitioner is
married to a natural parent of the child to be adopted, except
that if the court finds that the requirement of a joint petition
would serve to deny the child a suitable home, such requirement
may be waived.
(3) No petition for the adoption of any child under the age

Chapter 211 735
of sixteen shall be filed until after the child has lived continuously
in the home of the petitioner for at least three months immediately
prior to the filing of the petition.
(4) No petition for adoption shall be filed unless prior to the
filing of the petition the child sought to be adopted has been
placed for adoption by a licensed child-placing institution or
agency or by the department, or the child has been placed with
written approval of the Commissioner; but no such approval
shall be necessary in the case of:
(a) A child sought to be adopted by a stepparent, grand
parent, sister, brother, aunt or uncle;
(b) A child received by the proposed adopting parent or
parents from an agency without this state with the written consent
of the Commissioner.
(5) Subsection (4) of KRS 199.470 shall not apply to those
children placed for adoption prior to its effective date.
(6) All persons other than a licensed child-placing agency
or institution and the department who wish to place or receive
a child shall make written application to the commissioner for
permission to place or receive a child. The commissioner shall
grant or refuse permission for the applicant to place or receive
a child as early as practicable, but, in any case, the decision shall
be made within sixty days after the receipt of the application.
In any case where the department refuses to approve the
placement of a child for adoption when requested by the parent
or parents of the child, or refuses the request of any person or
persons that a child be placed with him or them for adoption,
the decision of the department in so refusing shall be final un
less within ten days after notice of refusal, the natural or pro
posed adopting parent or parents shall appeal to the circuit court
of the county in which the adoption is proposed, provided, how
ever that no placement shall be disapproved on the basis of the
religious, ethnic, or inter-faith background of the adoptive ap
plicant, if such placement is made with the consent of the parent.
The department shall be made a party defendant to the appeal.
(7) No person, association or organization, other than the
department or a licensed child-placing institution or agency shall

736 Acts of the General Assembly
place a child or act as intermediary in the placement of a child
for adoption or otherwise; except in the home of a stepparent,
grandparent, sister, brother, aunt or uncle, or upon written ap
proval of the Commissioner. Nothing in this subsection shall be
construed to limit the Department of Economic Security in carry
ing out its aid to dependent children program in accordance with
Chapter 205 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes.
Section 4. Section 199.480 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
( 1) The following persons shall be made parties defendant
in an action for leave to adopt a child:
( a ) The child to be adopted;
( b ) The natural living parents of a child under twenty-one,
if the child is legitimate, and its mother if the child is born out
of wedlock, provided that no natural parent need be made a party
defendant in the event the parental rights of such parent have
been terminated under KRS 199.600 to 199.630, or under a com
parable statute of another jurisdiction;
(c) The child's guardian, if it have one;
(d) If the care, custody and control of the child has been
transferred to the department, any other individual, institution
or agency, then the department, such other individual, institution
or agency shall be named a party defendant.
(2) Each party defendant shall be brought before the court
in the same manner as provided in other civil cases except that
if the child to be adopted is under fourteen years of age and the
department, individual, institution or agency has custody of such
child, the service of process upon such child shall be had by serv
ing a copy of the summons in such action upon the department,
individual, institution or agency, any provision of Section 52 of
the Civil Code of Practice to the contrary notwithstanding.
(3) If the child's natural living parents, if the child is legiti
mate, or its mother if the child is born out of wedlock, are parties
defendant, no guardian ad litem need be appointed to represent
the child to be adopted.

Chapter 211 737
Section 5. Section 199.600 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
( 1 ) In a proceeding involving a neglected or abandoned
child, as defined in KRS 199.011, the circuit court may terminate
all parental rights of the parents of such child, and declare the
child to be a ward of the state, and may vest the care, custody
and control of the child in the department or in any licensed
child-caring or child-placing agency or institution, if facilities
are available to receive the child, if it is pleaded and proved in
a private hearing that the parents have abandoned or deserted
the child, or have substantially and continuously or repeatedly
refused, or being able have neglected, to give the child parental
care and protection, or that the child is neglected and that the
parents have been adjudged mentally incompetent [to retain
their parental rights] and such judgment has been in effect for
not less than one year.
(2) If the circumstances described in subsection (1) of
this section exist with respect to only one of the two legitimate
parents, the parental rights of that parent may be terminated,
in which case the child shall not be declared to be a ward of the
state, unless with the voluntary consent of the other parent.
(3) In an action to terminate parental rights under this
section the parents, if the child is legitimate, or the mother if
the child is born out of wedlock, must be personally summoned;
or where personal service cannot be made, must be constructively
summoned as provided in the Civil Code of Practice.
(4) In the event the infant is made party defendant to the
action, the boarding home or foster parent shall not be served
with summons notwithstanding provisions of Rule 4.04 subsection
(4), Kentucky Rules of Civil Procedure.
(5) [4] The court may be convened at any time wherever
the judge thereof may be found within his district, irrespective
of the regular term, for the purpose of terminating parental rights
under this section, and shall complete the same as soon as possible.
(6) [5] Proceedings under this section may be instituted
upon petition by the department, any probation officer, any state
or local law enforcement officer, any county or commonwealth's
attorney or a natural parent.

738 Acts of the General Assembly
Section 6. Section 199.990 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
(1) [2] (a) Except as provided in paragraph (b) of this
subsection, any person who violates KRS 199.320 shall be fined
not more than five hundred dollars or imprisoned in the countv
jail or workhouse not more than twelve months, or both;
(b) Instead of the penalty imposed by paragraph (a) of
this subsection, the court may suspend the sentence and release
the defendant on probation for one year upon his recognizance,
with or without sureties in such sum as the court directs. The
recognizance shall provide that the defendant shall make his
personal appearance in court whenever ordered to do so, and
shall provide and care for the child as directed by the court,
and shall further comply with the orders of the court. If the
court is satisfied by information or proof, under oath, that the
defendant has violated the terms of the order, it may revoke
the order and sentence him under the original conviction. Unless
so sentenced, the defendant shall be discharged at the end of the
year, and the conviction shall become void;
(c) County courts shall have exclusive jurisdiction of all
prosecutions under KRS 199.320.
(2) [3] Any person who violates subsection (1) of KRS
199.330, shall be fined not more than five hundred dollars or im
prisoned in the county jail or workhouse not more than one year,
or both.
(3) [4] Any person who interferes with any officer or
agency acting under authority of subsection (2) of KRS 199.330,
shall be fined not more than twenty dollars, or imprisoned in the
county jail or workhouse not more than ninety days, or both.
(4) [5] Any person who violates KRS 199.340 shall, for the
first offense, be fined not more than twenty dollars, or imprisoned
in the county jail or workhouse not more than ninety days, or
both; and for any subsequent offense he shall be fined not more
than one hundred dollars or imprisoned in the penitentiary for not
more than one year, or both.
(5) [6] All fines, penalties and forfeitures collected for any
violation of KRS 199.330, 199.340 or of any law relating to or

Chapter 211 739
affecting children, shall be paid on demand to the incorporated
society for the prevention of cruelty to children or to the charit
able organization which institutes or conducts the prosecution.
One-half of all fines collected for violations of KRS 199.330 or
199.340 shall be paid to any regularly incorporated society for
the prevention of cruelty to animals which institutes or conducts
the prosecution.
(6) [7] Any person who violates subsection (1) of KRS
199.350 shall be imprisoned in the county jail not less than ten
nor more than sixty days, or fined not less than twenty-five nor
more than two hundred dollars, or both.
(7) [8] Any person who upon thirty days' notice fails to
remove a child from the state when required to do so under a
bond as provided in paragraphs (c) or (d) of subsection (2) of
KRS 199.350 shall forfeit one thousand dollars, to be recovered
upon the bond by a suit in the name of the county in which the
bond is filed.
(8) [9] Any person violating any of the provisions of KRS
199.380 to 199.400 shall be guilty of an offense, and upon con
viction thereof, shall be fined not more than five hundred dollars
or imprisoned for not more than twelve months, or be both fined
and imprisoned, in the discretion of the court.
(9) [10] Any person who violates any of the provisions of
KRS 199.430, 199.470, 199.570 and 199.590, or 199.640 to 199.670
or any rule or regulation under such sections the violation of
which is made unlawful shall be fined not less than $500 nor more
than $2,000 or imprisoned for not more than six months, or both.
Each day such violation continues shall constitute a separate
offense.
(10) [11] Any person who willfully violates any other of
the provisions of KRS 199.420 to 199.670 or any rule or regula
tion thereunder, the violation of which is made unlawful under
the terms of those sections, and for which no other penalty is
prescribed in those sections or in subsection (1) of this section,
or in any other applicable statute, shall be fined not less than $20
nor more than $200 or imprisoned for not more than thirtv days,
or both.
Approved March 22, 1962

740 Acts of the General Assembly
CHAPTER 212
(S. B. 119)
AN ACT relating to juvenile courts.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 208.010 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
As used in KRS 208.010 to 208.590 and 208.990, unless the
context otherwise requires:
( 1 ) "Department" means the Department of Child Welfare;
(2) "Child" means any person who has not reached his
twenty-first [eighteenth] birthday;
(3) "Court" means the juvenile session of the county court;
(4) "Child Welfare Worker" means any employee of the
Department of Child Welfare, designated as such by the Com
missioner of the Department of Child Welfare, or a social worker
employed by a county or city, who has been approved by the
Department of Child Welfare to handle, under its supervision,
services to children;
( 5 ) "Neglected or Dependent Child" has the same meaning
as in KRS 199.011 (4);
(6) "Abandoned Child" has the same meaning as in KRS
199.011 (5);
(7) "Needy Child" means one who is in want and deprived
of any of the following: the necessities for living; the education
required by law; or the supervisory, medical, or other care neces
sary for his well-being.
Section 2. Section 208.020 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
( 1 ) The juvenile session of the county court of each county
shall have exclusive jurisdiction in proceedings concerning any
child, living, or found, within [in] the county and has not reached
his eighteenth birthday:

Chapter 212 741
(a) Who has committed a public offense, except a moving
motor vehicle offense, involving a child sixteen years of age or
older. The [juvenile session of the county] court shall not have
jurisdiction in any prosecution of a moving motor vehicle offense
involving a child sixteen years of age or older. Juvenile offenders
sixteen years of age or older accused of moving motor vehicle
offenses shall be treated as any adult offender; provided, however,
that the term "moving motor vehicle offense" shall not be deemed
to include the offense of stealing or converting a motor vehicle
nor operating the same without the owner's consent, nor any of
fense which constitutes a felony; or
(b) Who does not subject himself to the reasonable control
of his parents, teacher, guardian or custodian by reason of being
wayward or habitually disobedient; or
( c ) Who is an habitual truant from home or from school; or
(d) Who is found by the court to be dependent, neglected,
needy or abandoned.
(2) The court of each county wherein a public offense, as
defined in KRS 208.020 (1) (a), is committed by a child who is a
resident of another county of this state, shall have concurrent
jurisdiction over such child with the court of the county wherein
the child resides, or the court of the county where the child is
found. Whichever court first acquires jurisdiction of such child
may proceed to final disposition of his case, or, in its discretion
may make an order transferring the case to the court of the county
of his residence, or the county wherein the offense was committed,
as the case may be.
(3) The court of each county shall have exclusive jurisdic
tion of persons who, and no person shall:
(a) Knowingly encourage, aid, cause or in any manner
contribute to the conditions which cause, or to cause a child
who has not reached his eighteenth birthday to become delin
quent, neglected, dependent, needy or charged with a crime; or
(b) Willfully fail to do any act that will directly tend to
prevent a child who has not reached his eighteenth birthday be
coming delinquent, neglected, dependent, needy, abandoned or
charged with a crime.

742 Acts of the General Assembly
(4) Nothing in Chapter 208, of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes, [this chapter] shall deprive other courts of the jurisdiction
[right] to determine the custody or guardianship of children upon
writs of habeas corpus, or to determine the custody or guardian
ship of children when such custody or guardianship is in
cidental to the determination of other causes pending in
such other courts; nor shall anything in Chapter 208, [this
Chapter] of the Kentucky Revised Statutes, affect the jurisdiction
of circuit courts over adoptions and [over] proceedings for ter
mination of parental rights. The juvenile court shall have no
jurisdiction to make permanent awards of custody of a child, but
if the court finds an emergency to exist affecting the welfare of a
child, it may make temporary orders for its custody, pending the
outcome of proceedings in circuit court to determine its per
manent custody. Such orders shall be entirely without prejudice
to the proceedings for permanent custody of the child, and shall
remain in effect until modified or set aside by the court. Upon
the entry of a temporary or final judgment in the circuit court,
awarding custody of such child, all prior orders of the juvenile
court in conflict therewith shall be deemed cancelled.
Section 3. Section 208.160 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
When the health or physical condition of any child before
the juvenile court requires
it,the court may order the child to be
placed in
apublic hospital or institution for treatment or special
care, or in
aprivate hospital or institution which will provide treat
ment and care. In order to ascertain the physical condition
ofa
child, [whether the child
isin normal health] the court may cause
the child to be examined by
ahealth officer or children's clinic or
any reputable physician who will conduct the examination.
Section
4. Section 208.170 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
isamended to read as follows:
(
1
) If, during the course of any proceeding in the juvenile
court,
itappears to the court that there
isreasonable cause to be
lieve that
achild before the court has committed
afelonv, and,
at the time of commission of the offense, the child was sixteen
years of age or older, or was less than sixteen years of age, but the

Chapter 212 743
offense was murder or rape, including being an accessory to either
of said offenses before the fact, and the court is of the opinion
that the best interests of the child and of the public require that
the child be tried and disposed of under the regular law governing
crimes, the court in its discretion may make an order transferring
the case to the circuit court of the county in which the offense
was committed. No child shall be considered a felon for any
purpose until transferred to, tried and convicted of a felony by
a circuit court.
(2) When juvenile court so transfers a case to the circuit
court:
( a ) If a grand jury considers the case and is satisfied there
is sufficient evidence to indict the child, it may either return an
indictment or may return a written report to the circuit court
recommending that the child be committed to the department.
If the court believes that such commitment would be proper, it
may order the child committed to the department.
(b) If, during any stage of the trial in the circuit court,
the child, or his parent or guardian so requests, the judge in his
discretion may stop the trial and commit the child to the depart
ment.
( c) If neither of the procedures specified in paragraphs ( a )
and (b) of this subsection are employed, the child shall be tried
as any other defendant.

CHAPTER 213
(S. B. 174)
AN ACT relating to newspaper publication of election ballots.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 424.290 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is amended
to read as follows:
(1) Not less than three days before any primary or regular
election the county court clerk shall cause to be published in a
newspaper a copy [facsimile] of the ballot or ballots, or when
voting machines are to be used, of the face of the voting machines
[showing the ballot labels in place] . Where the ballots or
voting machine faces differ for various precincts within the county,
a copy [facsimile] of each different ballot or voting machine
face shall be published, with appropriate identification. The
cost of publication shall be paid by the county, except that the
cost of publishing any ballot or voting machine face that is

Chapteb 214 751
limited to a city election or a district election other than a school
district election, shall be paid by the city or the district as the
case may be.
(2) "Copy", as used in (1), means a summary of a ballot or
voting machine face showing all the pertinent information that
will appear on a particular ballot or voting machine face.
Approved March 22, 1962
CHAPTER 214
(S. B. 235)
AN ACT relating to the compensation of county officers and employees.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 64.530 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
(1) Except as provided in subsections (2) and (3) of this
section, the fiscal court of each county shall fix the compensation
of every county officer and employe. For the purposes of this
section, master commissioners and receivers in counties other
than those containing a population of 75,000 or more, and justices
of the peace and constables in all counties, shall be deemed to
be county officers, and deputies or assistants of county officers
shall be deemed to be county employes, but employes of county
boards or commissions which are now authorized by law to fix
the compensation of their employes shall not be deemed to be
county employes for the purposes of this section. In the case of
officers compensated from fees, or partly from fees and partly
by salary, the fiscal court shall fix the maximum compensation
that the officer may receive, from both sources, and also shall
have authority to fix the number of deputies and assistants, and
the compensation thereof, and the maximum amount that the
officer may expend each year for expenses of his office. In the
case of county officers elected by popular vote, the compensation
of any such officer, and of his deputies and assistants, during the

752 Acts of the General Assembly
term of those in office on June 30, 1950, shall not be fixed at
less than that fixed by the law in force and effect on November
1, 1949. In the case of county officers elected by popular vote,
for terms commencing after June 30, 1950, the annual compensa
tion of the officer and of his deputies and assistants shall be
fixed by the fiscal court not later than the first Monday in May
in the year in which such officers are elected, and shall not be
changed during the term.
(2) Nothing in this section shall apply to county tax com
missioners or their deputies, assistants and expenses, in any
count)', or to the circuit court clerk, county court clerk, sheriff,
jailer, master commissioner or receiver, and their deputies,
assistants and expenses, in counties having a population of 75,000
or more.
(3) Justices of the peace serving on a fiscal court in any
county, and county commissioners serving on a fiscal court in
any county other than one containing a city of the first, second,
third or fourth class, shall be paid for their services, out of the
county treasury, not to exceed fifteen dollars per day for each
day of actual attendance of the meetings of the fiscal court. The
fiscal court shall fix the amount to be received within the above
limit, but with the exception of the terms that are being served
on June 30, 1950, no change of compensation shall be effective
as to any member of a fiscal court during his term of office. The
compensation of county commissioners serving on fiscal courts
in counties containing a city of the first, second, third or fourth
class shall continue to be limited as provided in subsections ( 1 ) ,
(2), (3) and (4) of KRS 67.110, and nothing in KRS 64.480 to
64.760 is intended to authorize any change in such compensation
during the term of any member. County commissioners or
justices of the peace shall not receive any compensation for their
services on the fiscal court, other than as provided in this
section or subsections (1), (2), (3) and (4) of KRS 67.110, nor
shall they receive any compensation for serving on committees
of the fiscal court.
(4) Circuit court clerks in counties other than those
containing a population of 75,000 or more shall receive as com
pensation per year the maximum compensation allowed by law
for circuit court clerks of this Commonwealth, provided fees

Chapter 215 753
or funds for this purpose are sufficient. The first deputy shall
be allowed to receive a salary not to exceed $6600 [$5400] per
year; the second deputy shall be allowed to receive a salary not
to exceed $5400 [$4800] per year and other deputies shall be
allowed to receive a salary not to exceed $4800 [$3600] per year.
Each circuit clerk shall be allowed to fix the amount of the salary
of each of his deputy clerks.
Section 2. The effective date of this Act shaLl be July 1,
1962.
Approved March 22, 1962
CHAPTER 215
(S. B. 236)
AN ACT relating to alcoholism.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 222.180 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is amended
to read as follows:
(1) Any resident of the Commonwealth of Kentucky may
apply for treatment for alcoholism by filing a written application
with the clerk of the circuit court or with the clerk of the county
court of the county in which he resides. Any such application
shall be supported by a medical statement, made by a licensed
physician, stating that in the opinion of the physician the ap
plicant is an alcoholic and that the applicant is mentally com
petent to make such application.
(2) Upon receipt of such application by the clerk he shall
refer the application to the proper court, and the court shall have
the authority to order and direct treatment of the applicant as
in KRS 222.150.
(3) Any applicant, committed under this section, having
an estate which can be subject to debt, shall pay the cost of his
own transportation and that of any attendant ordered by the
court to accompany him to the institution to which he is ordered.
(4) The clerk shall receive from the petitioner a fee of
$10.00 for filing and processing the application for commitment.
Approved March 22, 1962

754 Acts of the General Assembly
CHAPTER 216
(S. B. 274)
AN ACT relating to interlocal contracts.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. This Act may be cited as the Interlocal Co
operation Act.
Section 2. It is the purpose of this Act to permit local
governmental units to make the most efficient use of their powers
by enabling them to cooperate with other localities on a basis
of mutual advantage and thereby to provide services and facili
ties in a manner and pursuant to forms of governmental organiza
tion that will accord best with geographic, economic, population
and other factors influencing the needs and development of local
communities.
Section 3. As used in this Act, unless the context otherwise
requires:
"Public agency" means any political subdivision of this state;
any agency of the state government or of the United States.
Section 4. ( 1 ) Any power or powers, privileges or author
ity exercised or capable of exercise by a public agency of this
state may be exercised and enjoyed jointly with any other public
agency of this state, or of the United States to the extent that
the laws of the United States permit such joint exercise or enjoy
ment. Any agency of the State government when acting jointly
with any public agency may exercise and enjoy all of the powers,
privileges and authority conferred by this Act upon a public
agency.
( 2 ) Any two or more public agencies may enter into agree
ments with one another for joint or cooperative action pursuant
to the provisions of this Act. Appropriate action by ordinance,
resolution or otherwise pursuant to law, of the governing bodies
of the participating public agencies shall be necessary before any
such agreement may enter into force.

Chapter 216 755
(3) Any such agreement shall specify the following:
(a) The duration of the agreement;
(b) The precise organization, composition and nature of
any separate legal or administrative entity created thereby to
gether with a statement of the powers delegated thereto; pro
vided such legal entity may be legally created;
( c ) The purpose or purposes of such legal or administrative
entity;
(d) The manner of financing the joint or cooperative
undertaking and of establishing and maintaining a budget there
for;
( e ) The permissable method or methods to be employed in
accomplishing the partial or complete termination of the agree
ment and for disposing of property upon such partial or complete
termination;
(f) Any other necessary and proper matters.
(4) In the event that the agreement does not establish a
separate legal or administrative entity to conduct the joint or
cooperative undertaking, the agreement shall, in addition to items
(a), (c), (d), (e) and (f) enumerated in subsection (3) of this
section, contain the following:
(a) Provision for an administrator or joint board responsi
ble for administering the joint or cooperative undertaking. In
the event that a joint board is established, the public agencies
party to the agreement shall be represented thereon;
(b) The manner of acquiring, holding and disposing of real
and personal property used in the joint or cooperative under
taking.
(5) No agreement made pursuant to this Act shall relieve
any public agency of any obligation or responsibility imposed
upon it by law except that to the extent of actual and timely
performance thereof by a joint board or other legal or administra
tive entity created by an agreement made pursuant to this Act,
said performance may be offered in satisfaction of the obligation
or responsibility.
( 6 ) Every agreement made pursuant to this Act shall, prior
to and as a condition precedent to its entry into force, be sub
mitted to the Attorney General who shall determine whether the

756 Acts of the General Assembly
agreement is in proper form and compatible with the laws of
this state. The Attorney General shall approve any agreement
submitted to him under this subsection unless he finds that it does
not meet the conditions set forth in this Act. In the event that
the agreement does not meet such conditions, the Attorney Gen
eral shall detail in writing, addressed to the governing bodies
of the public agencies concerned, the specific respects in which
the proposed agreement fails to meet the requirements of law.
Section 5. (1) Whenever any two or more public agen
cies, as defined in this Act, enter into an agreement for joint or
cooperative action pursuant to the provisions of this Act, any such
public agency acting separately or jointly with one or more of
any such agencies, may acquire, construct, maintain, add to and
improve the necessary property, real and personal, which is re
quired in order to perform the functions under the agreement,
and for the purpose of defraying the costs incident to the per
formance of such agreement, may borrow money and issue ne
gotiable revenue bonds.
(2) Any public agency or agencies may borrow money and
issue bonds under this section pursuant to an order, resolution
or ordinance of its or their legislative or administrative body or
bodies, which order, resolution or ordinance shall set forth the
terms of the agreement in full, the amount of the revenue bonds
to be issued and the maximum rate of interest. In every instance
the order, resolution or ordinance shall provide that the joint or
cooperative action is being undertaken pursuant to the provisions
of this Act.
(3) The bonds may be issued to bear interest at a rate not
to exceed six percent per annum, payable either annually or semi
annually, and shall be executed in such manner and be payable
at such times not exceeding thirty years from the date of issuance
and at such place or places as the public agency or agencies
determines.
(4) The bonds may provide that they or any of them may
be called for redemption prior to maturity, on interest payment
dates not earlier than one year from the date of issuance of the
bonds.

Chapter 216 757
(5) Any public agency is hereby empowered to accept
donations or gifts to the joint or cooperative action from any
source and to accept appropriations and grants to the joint or
cooperative action from the Federal Government or any agency
thereof and appropriations from the state or any county, city or
other political subdivision and, at the option of the public agency
or agencies, to pledge such donations, gifts or appropriations to
the payment of revenue bonds issued to finance the cost of such
joint or cooperative action.
(6) Bonds issued pursuant to this section shall be nego
tiable and shall not be subject to taxation. If any officer whose
signature or countersignature appears on the bonds or coupons
ceases to be such officer before delivery of the bonds, his signa
ture or countersignature shall nevertheless be valid and sufficient
for all purposes the same as if he had remained in office until
delivery. The bonds shall be sold in such manner and upon such
terms as the public agency or agencies deem best. In no event
shall any bonds be negotiated on a basis to yield more than six
percent, except as provided in subsection (4) of this section.
The bonds shall be payable solely from the revenue derived from
the joint or cooperative action and shall not constitute an in
debtedness of the state, county, city or political subdivision. It
shall be plainly stated on the face of each bond that it has been
issued under the provisions of this Act.
(7) All money received from the bonds shall be applied
solely for the acquisition, construction, maintenance, improve
ment or operation of the joint or cooperative action, and the
necessary expense of preparing, printing and selling said bonds,
or to advance the payment of interest on the bonds during the
first three years following the date of the issuance of the bonds.
(8) Before the issuance of the bonds the public agencies
party to the agreement shall, by orders, resolutions or ordinances
of their respective legislative bodies, set aside and pledge the
income and revenue of the joint or cooperative action including
rents, royalties, fees and proceeds of sales of property and from
rates and charges for services derived or rendered by the joint
or cooperative action into a separate and special fund to be used
and applied in payment of the cost of the maintenance, operation
and depreciation incident to the joint or cooperative action. The

758 Acts of the General Assembly
orders, resolutions or ordinances shall determine and fix the
amount of revenue necessary to be set apart and applied to the
payment of principal and interest of the bonds, and the proportion
of the balance of the income and revenue to be set aside as a
proper and adequate depreciation account. The remaining pro
portion of such balance shall be set aside for the reasonable and
proper operation and maintenance of the joint or cooperative
action.
(9) The rents, royalties, fees, rates and charges for the
service or sale of the joint or cooperative action shall be fixed and
revised from time to time so as to be sufficient to provide for the
payment of interest upon all bonds and to create a sinking fund
to pay the principal of such bonds when due, and to provide for
the operation and maintenance of the joint or cooperative action
and an adequate depreciation account.
Section 6. In the event that a public agency or agencies
determine to transfer any of its employes to the joint or coopera
tive action, which employers are subject to any civil service laws
or regulations, such employes shall not lose any rights or benefits
which have accrued prior to such transfer.
Section 7. In the event that the joint or cooperative action
is such that its employes would be afforded civil service pension
rights or benefits if they were employes of a county or city, such
employes may be afforded the protection of civil service laws or
regulations; provided, however, that such protection is available
under the laws of this state.
Section 8. Before any agreement made pursuant to this Act
shall become operative or have force and effect, a certified copy
thereof shall be filed with: the county clerk of the county which
is party to the agreement; the city clerk of the city which is party
to the agreement; with the county clerk of the county wherein
any other political subdivision of the state is located which is
party to such agreement; and with the Secretary of State.
Section 9. In the event that an agreement made pursuant
to this Act shall deal in whole or in part with the provision of
services or facilities with regard to which an officer or agency

Chapter 217 759
of the state government has constitutional or statutory powers of
control, the agreement shall, as a condition precedent to its entry
into force, be submitted to the state officer or agency having such
power of control and shall be approved or disapproved by such
officer or agency as to all matters within the jurisdiction of such
officer or agency in the same manner and subject to the require
ments governing the action of the Attorney General pursuant
to Section 4 (6) of this Act. The requirement of this section
shall be in addition to and not in substitution for the requirement
of submission to and approval by the Attorney General under
Section 4 (6) of this Act.
Approved March 22, 1962
CHAPTER 217
(S. B. 283)
AN ACT relating to crimes and punishments.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 437.110 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is amended
to read as follows:
(1) No two or more persons shall confederate or band
themselves together and go forth for the purpose of intimi
dating, alarming, disturbing or injuring any person, or of taking
any person charged with a public offense from lawful custody
with the view of inflicting punishment on him or of preventing
his prosecution, or of doing any felonious act.
(2) No two or more persons shall confederate or band
themselves together and go forth for the purpose of molesting,
damaging or destroying any property of another person, whether
the property is molested, damaged or destroyed or not.
(3) Any person who violates this section shall be pun
ished by a fine not exceeding five thousand dollars or imprison
ment not exceeding one year or both, in the discretion of the jury.
Approved March 22, 1962

760 Acts of the General Assembly
CHAPTER 218
(S. B. 285)
AN ACT relating to water districts, amending KRS 74.020 and KRS
74.120, and declaring an emergency.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. KRS 74.020 is amended to read as follows:
(1) A water district shall be administered by a Board of
Commissioners, which shall control and manage the affairs of
the district. The term of each Commissioner shall be four years,
except as provided herein.
(a) If a district lies wholly within a single county, the
Board of Commissioners shall consist of three residents of the
district who shall be appointed by the county judge. Initial
appointments shall be for terms of two, three and four years, as
designated by the court. Commissioners now serving under ap
pointments shall continue to serve until the end of their respective
terms.
(b) If a district formed in a single county extends its area
to include territory in an adjacent county, as provided by KRS
74.115, the Board of Commissioners shall consist of five residents
of the district, three of whom shall be appointed by the county
judge of the county having the greater population within the
boundaries of the district, and two of whom shall be appointed
by the county judge of the remaining county. Initially upon the
establishment of the extension of the district into the second
county, the county judge of that county shall appoint only two
commissioners, regardless of population requirements, but if
the second county shall be determined to have the larger popu
lation within the district of the two counties, he shall have the
authority to fill the next vacancy existing in the representation
of the original county. Orders establishing the extension shall
provide for the staggering of initial terms in an equitable manner.
(2) Vacancies shall be filled by [the countv court] the
same appointing authority which is empowered to make the
original appointment. Vacancies resulting from cause other than
expiration of the term shall be filled for the unexpired term only.

Chapter 218 761
(3) The commission shall elect a chairman, secretary and
treasurer. Each commissioner shall execute a bond, approved
by the county court, conditioned on the faithful performance of
the duties of his position.
(4) Each commissioner shall receive an annual salary of
not more than twenty-four hundred dollars, [to be fixed by the
county court and] which shall be paid out of the water district
fund. In the case of single county districts the salary shall be
fixed by the county court; in two county districts it shall be fixed
by agreement between the two county courts, except that in
districts governed by a Board of Commissioners appointed under
the provisions of Section 1 (l)(c) of this Act, each Commis
sioner shall receive compensation in the amount of ten dollars
per diem for each meeting of the Board which he attends, but
for no more than three meetings in any one month, and he
shall be reimbursed for his reasonable and necessary expenses
in performing authorized duties for the district.
In fixing the salary of the commissioners, the court shall
take into consideration the financial condition of the district and
its ability to meet its obligations as they mature.
Section 2. KRS 74.120 is amended to read as follows:
( 1 ) All or any part of an incorporated city may be included
in the boundaries of any existing water district or water district
being newly organized, provided the governing body of such
city by resolution or ordinance gives, or has given, its consent.
(2) The commission may contract with any city which is
not included in the boundaries of the district for the purpose
of furnishing water or gas to the residents of such city and may
contract with any city for the purpose of obtaining water or gas
for the use of the district. The assignment to the commission or
to the water district of any valid outstanding franchise for the
distribution of water or natural, artificial, or mixed gas to the
residents of any city shall constitute a contract with the city
within the meaning of this section.
(3) When the commission shall contract with any city of
the first five classes in the manner prescribed in this section,
such city shall be deemed a part of the district during the life

762 Acts of the General Assembly
of the contract, but only for the purpose of carrying out the pro
visions of the contract and for the purpose of permitting repre
sentation on the Board of Commissioners. Nothing herein shall
impair the ownership by the contracting city of its own system,
or empower the district to take any action not authorized by
the contract.
Whereas there are areas in the State in immediate need of
benefits in this legislation, an emergency is declared to exist and
this Act shall become effective upon its passage and approval by
the Governor.
Approved March 22, 1962

Chapter 221 763
CHAPTER 220
(S. B. 305)
AN ACT relating to the rights of an arrested person.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
A new section of the Code of Practice in Criminal Cases is
created to read as follows:
(1) The officer or other person in charge of a police sta
tion, jail, or other place of confinement to which an arrested
person is taken shall immediately permit the arrested person to
communicate by telephone or by the most expeditious manner,
if no telephone is available, with a relative, friend, or an attorney.
(2) If the arrested person is unable, because of drunken
ness, illness, or any other reason, to communicate with any one,
the officer or other person in charge of the police station, jail,
or other place of confinement shall immediately attempt to learn
the identity of the arrested person; and shall, from this informa
tion, immediately notify, if possible, the arrested person's rela
tives or friends of his arrest and detention.
( 3 ) Any attorney-at-law shall, at the request of the arrested
person or of some one acting in his behalf, be permitted to visit
the arrested person.
( 4 ) Violation of any provision of this section is punishable,
upon conviction, by a fine of not more than $500.
Approved March 22, 1962

Chapter 222 765
CHAPTER 222
(S. B. 342)
AN ACT relating to revenue and taxation.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 157.380 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is amended
to read as follows:
(1) On or before July 1 of each year the Department of
Revenue shall determine the aggregate assessed value of all
property subject to assessment for school tax purposes, and
shall determine the percentage the equalized value of property
in each district is of the total equalized value of all property in
the Commonwealth subject to taxation for school purposes and
shall certify such information to the Superintendent of Public
Instruction for use in determining the required local tax effort for
each district. In school districts where the required local tax effort
includes assessments on which tax payments may be legally
deferred, the local district board of education may request the
State Board of Education to adjust the assessment of such prop
erty in order to reduce the required local tax effort during any
year in which the estimated taxes to be received are at least
fifteen percent less than the taxes that would be received if
tax payments were on a current basis. Upon receipt of such
request, the State Board of Education shall ask the Revenue
Department to verify the fact that the request is reasonable and
that on the basis of the best information available, the taxes to be
received will be at least fifteen percent less than the taxes that
would be received if payments were on a current basis. If the
request is approved by the State Board of Education and the
Revenue Department, the following procedure shall be followed
by the Revenue Department in adjusting assessments on such
property for purposes of KRS 157.380:
(a) Determine the revenue the district can reasonably ex
pect to receive from tax payments on property on which deferred
tax payments are permitted by law.
(b) Calculate the adjusted assessed valuation of property
to be used for purposes of KRS 153.380 by dividing the revenue

766 Acts of the General Assembly
the district expects to receive by the local tax rate in dollars and
multiply the quotient by 100.
(c) Differences between estimated and actual receipts in
any fiscal year shall be carried forward to the next fiscal year
and reflected in the receipts for that year in calculating ad
justed assessments for property subject to deferred tax payments.
(d) An accurate record of adjusted assessments shall be
kept from year to year in order that the local school district
may be put on an actual assessment basis as soon as possible
for purposes of KRS 157.380.
(2) If such assessment has not become final by July 1 of
any year, the Department of Revenue shall, on the basis of
other information as may be available, make an estimate of the
assessment of all property subject to local school tax in each
school district and the estimate shall be used in lieu of the ag
gregate assessed value.
(3) [(2)] Except as provided in subsection (1) of this
Act, in determining the required local tax effort for districts
which participate in the public school foundation program.'
fund, the Superintendent of Public Instruction shall divide
the aggregate assessed value of all property in the Commonwealth
subject to taxation for school purposes by one hundred and
multiply the quotient thereof by one dollar and ten cents. The
product thereby obtained shall be multiplied by each district's
percentage of the total equalized value of all property in the
Commonwealth subject to taxation for school purposes.
(4) [(3)] Any district's levy shall be at such rate as is
necessary to provide the required local tax effort.
Approved March 22, 1962
CHAPTER 223
(S. B. 343)
AN ACT relating to unemployment insurance.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:

Chapter 223 767
Section 1. Section 341.270 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
Determination of employers contribution rate. (1) Each
subject employer's contribution rate shall be two and seven-tenths
percent except as otherwise provided in this section.
(2) No subject employer's contribution rate shall be less
than two and seven-tenths percent unless:
(a) Benefits were chargeable to his account with respect
to the thirty-six consecutive months immediately preceding the
computation date; and
(b) His reserve account balance equals or exceeds five
times the largest amount of benefits charged against it within
any one of the three years last preceding the computation date.
(3) Except as otherwise provided in this section, for the

of "Table A" shall be in effect.
(b) Equals or exceeds three and one-half but is less than

and the contribution rate for any subject employer whose indi

(5) As used in this section and elsewhere in this chapter,
unless the context clearly requires otherwise:
(a) "Trust fund balance" means the amount of money
in the Unemployment Insurance Fund, exclusive of monies trans
ferred to the fund under the provisions of the Federal Employ
ment Security Administrative Financing Act of 1954, which
have been appropriated or are subject to appropriation by the
legislature. In determining the amount in the fund as of a given
date all money received by the Division as of such date shall
be considered as being in the fund on such date.
(b) "Taxable wages" means the payroll or payrolls by
which contributions were measured for a specified period.
(c) "State-wide reserve ratio" means the percentage ratio
obtained by dividing the taxable wages for the twelve months
ending on the 30th day of September into the "trust fund bal
ance" as of the immediately following 31st day of October.
(d) "Benefit cost ratio" means the percentage ratio ob-

Chapter 223 769
tained by dividing the taxable wages for the sixty months ending
on the 30th day of September, which have been reported to
the Division on or before the immediately following 31st day of
October, into the total amount of benefits paid from the fund
during that same sixty months period.
(e) An employer's "reserve ratio" means the percentage
ratio of his reserve account balance as of the computation date
to his taxable payrolls for the thirty-six months last preceding
such computation date.
(f) An employer's "reserve account balance" means the
amount by which contributions credited to his reserve account
exceed benefits charged to such account. If benefits charged
to an account exceed contributions credited to such account as
of the computation date, such account shall be considered as
having a debit balance and a reserve ratio of "less than zero."
(g) "Computation date" means the date, occurring at least
once in each calendar year and within twenty-seven weeks prior
to the effective date of new rates of contribution, as of which
individual employer contribution rates are computed. The Com
mission shall by regulation prescribe the computation date.
Section 2. Section 341.370 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
Conditions of benefit disqualification. (1) According to
the circumstances in each case, a worker shall be disqualified
from receiving benefits or serving a waiting period:
(a) For not less than one nor more than sixteen weeks
immediately following the commission of the disqualifying act,
if,subsequent to his most recent work, he has failed without
good cause either to apply for available, suitable work when so
directed by the employment office or the Commissioner or to
accept suitable work when offered him, or to return to his cus
tomary self-employment when so directed by the Commis
sioner; or
[(b) For not less than six nor more than sixteen weeks
immediately following the commission of the disqualifying act,
ifhe has left his most recent suitable work voluntarily without
good cause; or]

770 Acts of the General Assembly
(b) [(c)] For not less than six nor more than sixteen
weeks immediately following the commission of the disqualifying
act, if he has been discharged for misconduct connected with his
most recent work, but legitimate activity in connection with
labor organizations or failure to join a company union shall not
be construed as misconduct.
(2) A worker shall be disqualified from receiving bene
fits or serving a waiting period for the duration of any period
of unemployment with respect to which:
(a) He was discharged for dishonesty in connection with
his most recent work;
(b) He voluntarily quit his most recent work to marry or
for the purpose of attending school, or to become self-employed;
or [.]
(c) He has left his most recent suitable tvork voluntarily

(3) A worker shall be disqualified from receiving benefits
for any week with respect to which he knowingly made a false

and, within the succeeding twenty-four months, for such addi
tional weeks immediately following the date of such determi
nation, not to exceed a total of fifty-two, as may be determined
by the Commissioner.
(4) No worker shall be disqualified under paragraph (b)
[or (c)] of subsection (1) or subsection (2) of this section un
less the employer, within a reasonable time as prescribed by
commission regulation, notifies the Division and the worker in

the alleged voluntary quitting or discharge for misconduct if

known to the Commissioner prior to the time benefits are paid
to such worker; provided further, that the exercise of such right
by the Commissioner, in the absence of timely notice from the
employer, shall not relieve the employer's reserve account of
benefit charges under the provisions of KRS 341.530 (3).
