Chapter 224 773
CHAPTER 224
(S. B. 345)
AN ACT relating to county buildings and the financing thereof.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
( 1 ) Upon the fiscal court adopting an order reciting the
necessity of financing the acquisition, construction remodeling or
improvement of a county building or buildings of any type which
a county has the legal right to acquire, construct, remodel or
improve and requesting the county judge to appoint a County
Buildings Commission, the county judge of such county shall
thereupon appoint a County Buildings Commission composed of
three members. That one of such members shall have his term
of office designated as three years, one of such members shall
have his term of office designated as two years and the remain
ing member shall have his term of office designated as one year.
Upon the expiration of the term of office of each of these com
missioners, the county judge shall appoint a commissioner to
succeed him to serve for a term of three years. Each commis
sioner shall execute a bond to be approved by the county judge
and shall be sworn to faithfully perform the duties of his posi
tion. Vacancies shall be filled by the county judge.
The commissioners shall elect a chairman, secretary and
treasurer.
(2) The treasurer of the Commission shall pay out the
funds of the Commission only upon presentation of warrants
signed by the chairman and countersigned by the secretary of
the Commission. The treasurer shall execute bond to the Com
mission in an amount fixed by the Commission.
(3) Such Commission shall be a public corporation and
commission and shall be considered as a public agency and
instrumentality of the county whose county judge appoints
same, in order to finance projects under the provisions hereof.
(4) For the purpose of providing county buildings for
county purposes or to remodel or improve same, the county of
which the members of such Commission are residents may con
vey to the County Buildings Commission a fee simple title, with

774 Acts of the Genebal Assembly
covenant of general warranty, to a site now held or hereafter
acquired by such county.
(5) Every County Buildings Commission to which a site
for a building has been conveyed, on which a building is to be
constructed, remodeled or improved, as provided in section (4)
hereof, shall enter into a contract or contracts with some person
for the erection, remodeling or improvements on the site of a
building with the necessary appurtenances according to the plans
and specifications adopted by the County Buildings Commission
and approved by the county.
(6) Immediately upon the approval of the plans and
specifications, as provided in section (5) hereof, the county
shall offer to lease the building for a term of one year from
the time the building is completed and ready for occupancy. The
lease, by its terms, shall give the lessee the option to extend
the lease from year to year for periods of one year until the
original term of the lease has been extended for the total
number of years acceptable to the County Buildings Commis
sion, not exceeding thirty years, at a rental which, if paid for
the original term and for each of the full number of years for
which the term is extended, will amortize the total cost of the
erection, remodeling or improvement of the building and ap
purtenances, provide an adequate maintenance fund and, in
addition thereto, a sum sufficient to pay the cost of insuring
the building against loss or damage by fire and windstorm or
other calamity in such sum as may be agreed upon by the
parties thereto.
(7) Any County Buildings Commission may establish and
erect, remodel, or improve county buildings and necessary ap
purtenances within the county, under the provisions hereof, for
the purpose of supplying the county with adequate buildings
necessary to carry out its rights, duties and powers.
(8) (A) When any County Buildings Commission desires
to construct, remodel or improve a building under the provisions
of section (7) hereof, the County Buildings Commission shall,
by resolution, cause plans and specifications for the building
to be duly made and filed in the office of the county clerk. The
plans and specifications shall give a full description of the build

Chapter 224 775
ing to be constructed, remodeled or improved, the details thereof
and the manner of construction. The plans and specifications shall
be prepared by an architect selected by the County Buildings
Commission and approved by the county. (B) If the plans and
specifications are approved and if the county offers to lease the
building under a lease of the kind provided in section (6) hereof,
the County Buildings Commission shall cause its secretary to
advertise for bids, and thereafter the County Buildings Com
mission, through its chairman, may contract for the construction,
remodeling or improvement of the building.
(9) For the purpose of defraying the cost of acquiring,
constructing, remodeling or improvement of any buildings and
appurtenances thereto under the provisions of Section ( 7 ) hereof,
any County Buildings Commission may borrow money and is
sue negotiable bonds. No such bonds shall be issued until the con
ditions hereof including the provisions of section (6) hereof as
to a lease, have been complied with and until authorized by
a resolution specifying the proposed undertaking, the amount of
bonds to be issued and the maximum rate of interest the bonds
are to bear, not exceeding 6% per annum. The resolution shall
further provide that the building or buildings and appurtenances
are to be constructed, remodeled, improved or acquired under
the provisions hereof.
( 10) All bonds issued under the provisions hereof may bear
interest at a rate not exceeding 6% per annum, payable semi
annually, and shall be executed in such manner and be payable
at such times, not exceeding thirty years from the date thereof,
and at such place as the County Buildings Commission deter
mines. The bonds shall be sold in such manner and upon such
terms as the County Buildings Commission deems for its best
interests.
(11) All bonds issued under the provisions hereof shall
have all of the qualities of negotiable instruments and shall not
be subject to taxation. If any of the officers whose signatures
appear on the bonds or coupons cease to be such officers be
fore delivery of the bonds, the signatures shall nevertheless be
valid for all purposes, the same as if the officers had remained
in office until delivery. The bonds shall be payable solely from
revenue derived from the building, as provided in section (15)

776 Acts of the General Assembly
hereof, and shall not constitute an indebtedness of the Countv
Buildings Commission within the meaning of the constitutional
provisions or limitations. It shall be plainly stated on the face
of each bond that it was issued under the provisions hereof
and does not constitute an indebtedness of the County Build
ings Commission.
( 12 ) All money received from any bonds issued pursuant
to the provisions hereof shall be used solely for the establishment,
remodeling, erection, or improvement of the building and nec
essary appurtenances, except that the money may be used also
to advance the payment of the interest on bonds during the
first three years following the date of the bonds. There shall
be a statutory mortgage lien upon the buildings and appurtenances
in favor of the holder of the bonds and coupons.
( 13 ) The building and appurtenances shall remain subject
to the statutory mortgage lien provided by section (12) hereof,
until the payment in full of the principal and interest on the
bonds. Any holder of the bonds or of any of the coupons may,
either at law or in equity, protect and enforce the lien and may
by action enforce and compel performance of all duties required
herein, including the making and collecting of sufficient rents,
the segregation of income and revenue and the application
thereof.
( 14 ) If there is any default in the payment of principal or
interest on any of the bonds, any court having jurisdiction of the
action may appoint a receiver to administer the building on
behalf of the County Buildings Commission, with power to charge
and collect rentals sufficient to provide for the payment of any
bonds or obligations outstanding against the building and for the
payment of operating expenses and to apply the income and rev
enues in conformity with the provisions hereof and of the reso
lution authorizing such bonds.
(15) At or before the issuance of the bonds, the County
Buildings Commission, by resolution, shall set aside and pledge
the income of the building into a special fund to be used and
applied in payment of the cost and maintenance of the building.
The resolution shall definitely fix the amount of revenues nec
essary to be set aside and applied for the payment of principal

Chapter 224 777
and interest on the bonds. The balance of the income shall be set
aside for the reasonable and proper maintenance of the building,
including a sufficient sum to pay the cost of insurance. The
County Buildings Commission may provide by resolution any
provision and stipulation it deems necessary for the administra
tion of the income for the security of the bond holders.
(16) The sinking fund shall be deposited in a depositary
selected by the County Buildings Commission. The deposit,
where practicable, shall be continuously secured by a pledge to
the County Buildings Commission of direct obligations of the
United States, exclusive of accrued interest, at all times at least
equal to the balance on deposit in the account or in some other
manner acceptable to the purchasers or holders of the bonds.
The securities shall be deposited with the County Buildings
Commission or held by a trustee or agent satisfactory to such
Commission. The sinking fund may be invested in direct obli
gations of the United States.
(17) If a surplus is accumulated in the maintenance fund
equal to the cost of maintaining the building during the re
mainder of the calendar or fiscal year, as may be provided by
the resolution required by section (15) hereof, and the cost of
maintaining and operating the building for the succeeding like
calendar or fiscal year, the excess over such amount shall be
transferred to the sinking fund.
( 18 ) The County Buildings Commission may issue refund
ing bonds for the purpose of providing funds for the payment
of any outstanding bonds, in accordance with the proceedings
prescribed for the issuance of the original bonds. The refunding
bonds shall be secured to the same extent and shall have the
same source of payment as the bonds which are refunded.
(19) If the County Buildings Commission finds that the
bonds authorized will be insufficient to accomplish the purpose
desired, additional bonds may be authorized and issued sub
ject to the same procedure.
(20) When the county has paid rent sufficient to amortize
the cost of erecting, acquiring, remodeling or improvement of
the building and appurtenances and to maintain the building and
pay the cost of insurance, the County Buildings Commission

778 Acts of the General Assembly
shall thereupon convey the premises to the county and shall
transfer any balance remaining in the funds provided for in
sections (15) through (17) hereof to the account of the county.
(21) Notwithstanding any of the other provisions hereof,
the County Buildings Commission may, by declaring its desire
to do so, provide, in addition to all of the other provisions
authorized herein, in connection with the issuance of its bonds,
that such bonds shall be its general obligations and may, in con
nection with the issuance of its bonds, execute a mortgage deed
of trust in favor of a trustee, providing that in the event of de
fault by the Commission in the payment of interest or principal
of its bonds or in the event of default of any other covenant
contained in such mortgage deed of trust the trustee, upon be
half of the bondholders, may institute and carry through fore
closure proceedings, with the property secured by the mort
gage deed of trust put up for sale on foreclosure proceedings,
the proceeds of such sale to be used for the benefit of the bond
holders. In connection with such plan of financing, such pro
visions may be inserted in the bonds themselves and in the
mortgage deed of trust as may be necessary to protect the bond
holders and in order to make such bonds salable with the
lowest net interest cost to the Commission. If the County Build
ings Commission declares its intent by resolution to follow the
provisions of this section, and in order to do so it is necessarily
inconsistent that the provisions of any other section of this
law be complied with, such other provisions of this law which
are necessarily inconsistent with the provisions of this section
shall not be complied with to the extent but only to the extent
of such necessary inconsistency. If general obligations of the
County Buildings Commission are issued pursuant to the pro
visions of this section, such obligations shall be general obliga
tions only of the County Buildings Commission as a public
corporation and shall not be general obligations of the individual
members of such County Buildings Commission to any extent. It
is the intent of this section to permit to the County Buildings
Commission, if it desires to do so, to issue obligations under this
section which may also be secured in the same way that obliga
tions issued under the provisions of other sections of this law are
secured, to the full extent that this may be done consistent with
the provisions of this section.

Chapter 225 779
(22) The provisions hereof are additional and alternate
methods for the acquisition, construction, remodeling and im
provement of buildings by counties and do not include, alter,
amend or repeal any other statute. No proceedings shall be re
quired for the issuance of any bonds under the provisions of this
law, except such as are prescribed herein.
(23) The provisions of this law are severable, and if any
section, paragraph, clause or provision should ever be held to
be invalid, it shall not affect the validity of the remaining pro
visions of this law, it being hereby declared to be the intent
that the provisions which are valid shall be upheld and in full
force and effect.
Approved March 22, 1962
CHAPTER 225
(S. B. 360)
AN ACT relating to group life insurance for state employes.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
A new section of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is created to
read as follows:
Section 1. The Commissioner of Finance, upon the recom
mendation of the Commissioner of Personnel, may procure from
one or more life insurance companies, authorized to do business
in this State, a policy or policies of group life insurance insuring
the lives of all or any class or classes of state employes. The
policy or policies shall be approved by the Commissioner of In
surance and may contain such provisions as he approves whether
or not otherwise permitted by the insurance laws. The term
"State employee", for purposes of this Act, shall mean a person
who is regularly employed by any department, board, agency, or
branch of state government, and who is also a contributing mem
ber of any one of the retirement systems administered by the state.

780 Acts of the General Assembly
Section 2. The premiums may be paid by the policyholder
(a) wholly from funds contributed by the insured employee, by
payroll deduction or otherwise, ( b ) wholly from funds contributed
by the state or department or agency, or (c) partly from each.
No payment of premium by the state or department, board or
agency shall constitute compensation to an insured employee for
the purposes of any statute fixing or limiting the compensation of
such an employee; any premium or other expense incurred by the
state or department, board or agency shall be considered a proper
cost of administration.
Section 3. The policy or policies may also provide acci
dental death and dismemberment insurance and may contain such
provisions with respect to the class or classes of employes covered,
amounts of insurance for designated classes or groups of employes,
terms of eligibility, continuation of insurance after retirement, and
such other provisions as the Commissioner of Insurance may
approve.
Section 4. The Commissioner of Finance is authorized to
perform all acts necessary or advisable for the purpose of contract
ing for and maintaining insurance under the provisions hereof.
Approved March 22, 1962

CHAPTER 228
(S. R. 48)
A JOINT RESOLUTION requesting the Legislative Research Commission
to make a study and investigation of the laws of the Commonwealth
pertaining to elections.
WHEREAS, it is essential for the preservation of the democratic proc
esses in this Commonwealth that our election laws remain fair and
impartial at all times; and

Chapter 228 783
WHEREAS, there have been from time to time protests from the elec
torate concerning abuses of this Commonwealth's election laws;
NOW, THEREFORE,
Be it resolved by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. That the Legislative Research Commission is
authorized and directed to make an investigation and study of
the laws of the Commonwealth pertaining to elections.
Section 2. The study authorized and directed by this reso
lution shall include, but shall not necessarily be limited to:
( 1 ) A field observation of election practices throughout the
Commonwealth;
(2) A field observation of the performance of Registration
and Purgation Boards throughout the Commonwealth;
(3) A field observation of Absentee Voting procedures and
an examination of Court records of cases charging misuse of the
Absentee Ballot; and
(4) An examination of preparations and training of elec
tion officials.
Section 3. That all such state agencies as the Legislative
Research Commission may require, are directed to cooperate to
the fullest extent in the preparation of the study.
Section 4. That the Legislative Research Commission, after
it has conducted the study, be authorized and is directed to
formulate recommendations for changes, consistent with the facts
ascertained by reason of this study, in the election laws of the
Commonwealth, particularly in regard to the conduct of elec
tions and in enforcement procedures.
Section 5. That the Legislative Research Commission shall
include its findings and recommendations in a report to the
General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, such report
to be submitted to the 1964 Regular Session.
Approved March 22, 1962

CHAPTER 231
(H. B. 8)
AN ACT relating to the organization and government of cities of the sixth
class.

to read as follows:
(1) The board of trustees shall meet on the first Monday
in January next succeeding the election of its members, and shall
elect one of its number chairman. The board shall hold regular
meetings once a month, at a time fixed by ordinance. Special
meetings may be called at any time by the chairman or by three
members, by written notice delivered at least three hours prior
to the time specified for the proposed meeting. All meetings shall
be held [within the city limits] at a place designated by ordinance,
and shall be public. Such meeting place shall be within the city
limits, except that, if there is no suitable meeting place available
within the city limits the city legislative body may by ordinance
designate as its meeting place a building outside the city limits
which is both suitable and available for use as a meeting place and
is closest to the city limits. "Available" means available at such
time and on such days as the city legislative body may designate
for its regular meetings, at no rental or at a rental which shall be
reasanable and prudent in terms of iixe cities' current budget.
(2) A determination by the city legislative body that a
particular building, whether within or without the city limits, is
either unsuitable or unavailable as a meeting place, or both, shall
constitute prima facie evidence as to its correctness. Such deter
mination may be attacked in any manner otherwise proper; but if
any court should hold such determination to be incorrect, such
holding shall apply only to acts of the legislative body that become
final after the holding becomes final, and no act of the city legis
lative body shall be held to be invalid, void, or otherwise ineffec
tive solely on the ground that such determination was incorrect.
( 3 ) [ ( 2 ) ] At any meeting of the board of trustees a ma
jority of the members shall constitute a quorum, but a less number

Chapter 233 787
may adjourn from time to time and compel the attendance of
absent members under penalties prescribed by ordinance. The
chairman shall preside at all meetings of the board, and may vote
as other members. If the clerk is absent the chairman shall ap
point one of the members of the board clerk pro tem.
Approved March 22, 1962

CHAPTER 234
(H. B. 74)
AN ACT relating to procedure in criminal cases.
PREAMBLE
The Criminal Code Committee, established by the General Assembly for
the purpose of improving criminal procedure in the courts of the
Commonwealth, having reported its recommendations to the Legis
lative Research Commission, the Commission having approved the
recommendations and formulated plans for their execution, and the
Court of Appeals being in accord with these plans, the General
Assembly establishes this revised procedure in criminal cases, doing
so on the following principle, finding and declaration of policy and
purpose:
The Constitution of the Commonwealth of Kentucky vests the
judicial power of the Commonwealth, except inpeachment, in the
Court of Appeals and the constitutional courts, and in prohibiting
the establishment of other courts, manifest an intention to vest this
power in the Judicial Department more exclusively than was in

Chapter 234 789
dicated by the previous constitution; and it is recognized that the
vesting of judicial power carries with it the authority to regulate
judicial proceedings where the Constitution does not specifically
authorize the exercise of some part of that power by another
department;
The General Assembly finds that, although rules developed under
the third constitution by the legislature have governed criminal pro
ceedings as a matter of comity on the part of the courts since that
time, the administration of civil justice under rules of procedure
promulgated by the Judicial Department subsequent to revision of
civil procedure in 1952 has demonstrated the merit of having pro
cedural rules promulgated by the department responsible for their
proper functioning;
It is therefore declared to be the policy of the General Assembly,
insofar as the Legislative Department is empowered to express
policy on matters of judicial procedure, that prescription of rules
governing details of procedure will be left to the discretion of the
Judicial Department after the effective date of this Act; and
It is further declared that the purpose of this Act is to abolish the
Code of Practice in Criminal Cases, and to put in statute form pro
visions of that code relating to subjects not properly within the
scope of judicial rule-making, and to revise those and related stat
utes so as to reflect current legislative policy in matters governed
by them; to prescribe a better expression of current legislative policy
for those matters in which both legislative and judicial discretion
are involved; and to complement this purpose by prescribing a set
of revised procedural rules consistent with that purpose for the
governance of criminal proceedings, but subject to the rule-making
authority of the Court of Appeals.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 0. The rules in this section are prescribed in ac
cordance with the principle, finding and declaration contained
in the preamble to this Act, and with modifications thereof shall
be known as the Rules of Criminal Procedure, and may be cited
by the abbreviation, "RCr" or by the full title.
RULES OF CRIMINAL PROCEDURE
General Provisions
Rule 1.02. Scope and Application. The Rules of Criminal
Procedure govern the procedure in the courts of the Common
wealth of Kentucky in all criminal proceedings.

790 Acts of the General Assembly
Rule 1.04. Purpose and Construction. The Rules of Crimi
nal Procedure are intended to provide for a just determination
of every criminal proceeding. They shall be construed to secure
simplicity in procedure, fairness in administration and the elimi
nation of unjustifiable expense and delay.
Rule 1.06. Definitions. As used in these rules, unless the
context requires otherwise:
(a) "Magistrate" includes any judicial officer performing
judicial functions authorized by law;
(b) "Attorney for the Commonwealth" includes the At
torney General, the Commonwealth's attorney, the court at
torney, a city attorney or city prosecuting attorney, exercising
functions assigned to them by law, and an authorized assistant
of any of them.
Rule 1.08. Papers; Filing and Service. Where the Rules of
Criminal Procedure do not specifically prescribe the manner of
filing and service of papers, the Rules of Civil Procedure gov
erning such matters shall apply.
Rule 1.10. Time. The provisions of the Rules of Civil
Procedure as to Time shall apply to the procedures in the Rules
of Criminal Procedure where the latter rules do not specifically
provide otherwise. The time shall not be extended for a motion
for a new trial, a motion in arrest of judgment, the period for
filing the record on appeal, except as provided, for the period
for taking an appeal.
Arrest
Rule 2.02. Complaint. The complaint is a written state
ment of the essential facts constituting the offense charged.
It shall be made upon oath and signed by the complaining party
before a magistrate.
Rule 2.04. Warrant or Summons; Issuance. (1) If from
an examination of the complaint it appears to the magistrate
that there is probable cause to believe that an offense has been
committed and that the defendant committed it, a warrant shall
issue for the arrest of the defendant.

Chapter 234 791
(2) A summons may issue instead of a warrant if the
magistrate has reasonable grounds to believe that the defendant
will appear in response thereto, or if the defendant is a cor
poration.
(3) If a defendant who has been duly summoned fails to
appear, or if there is probable cause to believe that he will fail
to appear, a warrant of arrest shall issue.
(4) More than one warrant or summons may issue on the
same complaint.
Rule 2.06. Warrant or Summons; Requisites. ( 1 ) A war
rant of arrest shall be in writing and in the name of the Com
monwealth, shall be signed by the magistrate with the title
of his office and shall state the date when issued and the munici
pality or county where issued. It shall name or describe the of
fense charged to have been committed, specify the name of the
defendant, or, if his name is unknown, any name or description
by which he can be identified with reasonable certainty and
the name of the complaining party or parties. The warrant shall
be directed to all peace officers in the Commonwealth and shall
direct that the defendant be arrested and brought before the
magistrate issuing it or, if he is absent or unable to act, before
the nearest available magistrate.
(2) A summons shall be in the same form as a warrant
except that it shall summon the defendant to appear before the
magistrate issuing it at a stated time and place.
Rule 2.08. Defective Warrant, Summons or Citation. (1)
No person arrested under a warrant or appearing in response
to a summons or citation shall be discharged from custody or
dismissed because of any defect of form in the warrant or sum
mons, but the warrant or summons may be amended so as to
remedy any such defect.
(2) If during the preliminary hearing or trial of any per
son arrested under a warrant, or appearing in response to a
summons or citation it appears that the warrant, summons or
citation does not properly name or describe the defendant, or
the offense with which he is charged, or that although not guilty

792 Acts of the General Assembly
of the offense specified in the warrant, summons or citation,
there is probable cause to believe that he is guilty of some
other offense, the magistrate shall not discharge or dismiss the
defendant but shall forthwith cause a new complaint to be filed
and shall thereupon issue a new warrant or summons.
Rule 2.10. Warrant and Summons; Execution and Service.
(1) A warrant of arrest may be executed by any peace officer.
The officer need not have the warrant in his possession at the
time of arrest, but in that event he shall inform the defendant
of the offense charged and the fact that a warrant has been
issued, and upon request show the warrant, or a copy of it, to
the defendant as soon as possible.
(2) A summons may be served by any peace officer. It
shall be served upon a defendant by delivering a copy to him
personally, or by leaving it at his dwelling house or usual place
of abode with some person of suitable age and discretion then
residing therein.
(3) A summons to a corporation shall be served by deliv
ering a copy to an officer or managing or general agent or to
any other agent authorized by appointment or by law to receive
process.
Rule 2.12. Warrant or Summons; Return. (1) The officer
executing a warrant shall make return thereof to the magistrate
before whom the defendant is brought.
(2) The officer serving a summons shall make return
thereof to the magistrate before whom the summons is return
able on or before the day named therein for the appearance of
defendant. If the summons is served in a county other than that
in which it was issued, the return may be made by mail.
Rule 2.14. Right to Contact Attorney. (1) A person ar
rested and in jail shall have the right to make immediate com
munications for the purpose of securing the services of an at
torney.
(2) Any attorney-at-law entitled to practice in the courts
of this Commonwealth shall, at the request of the person arrested,
or of some one acting in his behalf, be permitted to visit the person
arrested.

Chapteb 234 793
Proceedings Before the Magistrate
Rule 3.02. Appearance before the Magistrate. (1) An
officer making an arrest under a warrant issued upon a complaint
shall take the arrested person without unnecessary delay before
a magistrate as commanded in the warrant. If the arrest is made
in a county other than that in which the warrant was issued and
the arrested person is not taken as commanded in the warrant,
he shall be taken before a magistrate of the county in which the
arrest is made, who shall admit him to bail for his appearance
before the proper magistrate to whom the bail bond and other
papers may be transmitted by mail. If the offense is non-bailable,
or if the person arrested is unable to give bail, the magistrate shall
commit him to jail and he shall be taken as commanded in the
warrant within a reasonable time by an officer of the county in
which it was issued.
(2) Any person making an arrest without a warrant shall
take the arrested person without unnecessary delay before a
magistrate. If the magistrate before whom the arrested person
is taken is in a county other than the county in which the offense
was committed, the magistrate shall proceed as directed in sub
section ( 1 ) as on an arrest under warrant in a county other than
that in which the warrant was issued.
Rule 3.04. Trial or Preliminary Hearing. When a person
is brought or appears before a magistrate having jurisdiction of
the offense charged he shall proceed with the trial. If the
magistrate does not have jurisdiction of the offense charged but
does have jurisdiction to hold a preliminary hearing, he shall pro
ceed to hold the preliminary hearing. If the magistrate does not
have jurisdiction to either try the case or hold the preliminary
hearing, he shall order the defendant to be taken before a court
of competent jurisdiction for further proceedings and may admit
him to bail for his appearance before that court, if the offense is
bailable, or commit the defendant to jail to await the trial or
preliminary hearing.
Rule 3.06. Bail for Good Behavior. (1) If there are rea
sonable grounds to believe that the release of a defendant would
endanger persons or property, the magistrate shall require bail

794 Acts of the General Assembly
in any sum deemed reasonable, to keep the peace and for his
good behavior for the period of one year.
(2) The applicable provisions governing bail shall apply to
bail for good behavior.
Rule 3.08. Statement of Magistrate. The magistrate shall
inform the defendant of the charge against him, of his right to
have a preliminary hearing or a trial. The defendant shall also be
informed that he is not required to make a statement and that any
statement made by him may be used against him. The magistrate
shall notify the attorney for the Commonwealth, allow the de
fendant reasonable time and opportunity to consult counsel, and
admit the defendant to bail if the offense is bailable.
Rule 8.10. Preliminary Hearing; Waiver. (1) The de
fendant may waive a preliminary hearing.
(2) Notwithstanding waiver of the preliminary hearing,
the magistrate shall on demand of the attorney for the Common
wealth permit examination of the witnesses for the Common
wealth. The defendant may cross examine the witnesses offered
by the Commonwealth.
Rule 3.12. Transfer of Defendant to Another County. If it
appears from either the complaint or evidence introduced at the
hearing that the offense was committed in a county other than
that in which the hearing or trial is being held, the magistrate
may admit the defendant to bail for his appearance before the
proper magistrate of the county in which the offense was com
mitted on a day named in the bond, if the offense is bailable, or
shall commit the defendant to jail to await his transfer within a
reasonable time to the county in which the offense was committed
by a peace officer of that county. The bond and other papers mav
be transmitted by mail to the magistrate before whom the de
fendant is bound to appear.
Rule 3.14. Decision after Hearing. If the defendant waives
the preliminary hearing or if from the evidence it appears to the
magistrate that there is probable cause to believe that an offense
has been committed and that the defendant committed it, the
magistrate shall hold him to answer in the circuit court and

Chapter 234 795
commit him to jail or admit him to bail, if the offense is bailable;
otherwise, he shall be discharged.
Rule 3.16. Minutes and Transcript of Testimony. The mag
istrate shall keep minutes of all proceedings of the preliminary
hearing, which shall include the name, address and substance of
the testimony of each witness. When the Commonwealth de
mands an examination of its witnesses full stenographic notes shall
be made of the testimony. A copy of the minutes or a transcript of
the testimony of witnesses for the Commonwealth shall be fur
nished to either party upon request and payment therefor.
Rule 3.18. Order of Commitment, Bail. If the defendant
is committed to jail, the magistrate shall make out a written order
of commitment, signed by him, which shall be delivered to the
jailer by the peace officer who executes the order of commitment.
If the offense is bailable, the magistrate must fix the sum for which
bail is to be given and enter it upon the order of commitment.
Thereafter, the bail shall be taken by the clerk of the court in
which the defendant is held to appear.
Rule 3.20. Recognizance of Witnesses. When the defen
dant has been held to answer the charge the magistrate shall
cause each of the material witnesses on behalf of the Common
wealth and, at the defendant's request, each of such witnesses for
him as he may suggest, to enter into a recognizance before him,
to the effect that he will attend and testify in the court to which
the defendant has been held to answer, or forfeit a sum not less
than one hundred dollars to the Commonwealth of Kentucky.
If witnesses for the defendants are recognized, it shall be stated
in the recognizance that they are such.
Rule 3.22. Transmission of papers. If at the conclusion of
the preliminary hearing the defendant is held to answer, the
magistrate shall transmit all papers in the proceedings and any
bail taken by him to the clerk of the court to which the defendant
has been held.
Bail
Rule 4.02. Application for Bail. Unless made in open
court, the application for bail shall be in writing, signed by the

796 Acts of the General Assembly
defendant or his counsel, stating the name of the defendant, the
offense for which he is committed and the name, occupation^ and
residence of the proposed surety or sureties, and, if a bonding
company, the name and address of the local agent.
Rule 4.04. Burden of Proof in Capital Cases. On the hear
ing of an application for admission to bail made before or after
indictment for a capital offense, the burden of showing that the
proof is evident or the presumption great that the defendant is
guilty of the offense is on the Commonwealth.
Rule 4.06. Amount of Bail. If the defendant is admitted
to bail, the amount thereof shall be such as in the judgment of the
court will insure compliance with the terms of the bond, having
regard to the ability of the defendant to give bail, the nature and
circumstances of the offense charged, the weight of the evidence
against him, the character and reputation of the defendant and
the probability of the defendant's complying with the terms of
the bond.
Rule 4.08. Qualification and Justification of Sureties. Each
surety, except a corporate surety which is approved as provided
by law, shall be a resident or owner of real estate within the Com
monwealth and shall justify his sufficiency by affidavit in which
he shall describe the property by which he proposes to justify his
sufficiency, the encumbrances thereon and the number and
amount of other bonds and undertakings for bail entered into
by him and remaining undischarged. No bond shall be approved
unless the surety thereon appears to be qualified.
Rule 4.10. Persons Not Qualified as Surety. No attorney-
at-law, sheriff, deputy sheriff, judge, magistrate, clerk, deputy
clerk or master commissioner shall be taken as surety on any bail
bond.
Rule 4.12. Sufficiency of Sureties. If there is only one
surety he shall be worth the amount specified in the bond exclu
sive of the amount of any other undertaking on which he may
be principal or surety, and exclusive of property exempt from
execution and over and above liabilities. If there are several
sureties they shall in the aggregate be worth that amount exclusive

Chapter 234 797
of the amount of other undertakings, and of the exemptions and
liabilities mentioned above.
Rule 4.14. Continuation of Bail. Bail taken at any stage
of the proceedings shall continue in effect to insure the appearance
of the defendant for any and all purposes at all stages of the
proceedings unless increased, decreased or otherwise modified by
the court in which the charge is pending.
Rule 4.16. Defects in Bond or Recognizance. (1) Neither
a bail bond nor a recognizance shall be invalid because of any
defects of form, omission, recital, or condition, or because of any
other irregularity, provided the official before whom it was en
tered into was legally authorized to take
it,the amount of bail
isstated, and
it can be ascertained therefrom before which
magistrate or court the principal
isbound to appear.
(2)
Ifno day
isfixed for the appearance of the defendant,
or an impossible day, or
aday in vacation, the bond or recogni
zance,
iffor
apreliminary hearing, shall bind the defendant to
appear within ten days from the time
it
isgiven;
iffor
atrial,
shall bind the defendant to appear on the first day of the term of
court which commences more than ten days from the time
it
is
given.
(3) In every instance in which the presence
isrequired
of an individual for whom an appearance bond has been posted,
the clerk of the court in which the individual
isto appear shall
notify in writing the surety on said appearance bond. The
notice shall state the time, date, and place the principal of the
bond
isrequired to appear and shall be delivered to said surety
not less than forty-eight hours prior to the time set for such
appearance. None of the foregoing provisions shall apply where
the appearance bond specifies
adefinite return date.
Rule 4.18. Deposit of Money or Bonds as Bail. Whenever
the defendant
isadmitted to bail in
a specified sum, he, or an
other in his behalf, may deposit said sum of money or non-
registered bonds of the United States or this Commonwealth
equal in market value to the specified sum, together with his
personal undertaking and the undertaking of such other person,
ifthe money or bonds are deposited
byanother, with the court

798 Acts of the General Assembly
in which he is held to appear, and take a certificate of such de
posit. After bail has been taken a deposit may, in like manner, be
made, which shall exonerate the surety or sureties.
Rule 4.20. Custody of Deposits. Money deposited as bail
shall be held in accordance with KRS 41.300. Bonds deposited
as bail shall be kept in the custody of the clerk of the court.
Money and bonds so deposited shall be paid or delivered accord
ing to the orders of the court in which the prosecution is pending.
Rule 4.22. Application of Deposit to Fine or Costs. Upon a
final judgement's being rendered against the defendant for a
fine and costs, or either, in the prosecution of a cause in which
money has been deposited as bail by the defendant himself, if the
money still remains on deposit and unforfeited, and such fine
and costs, or either, have not been paid, such money, or so much
thereof as may be necessary, shall be applied to the payment of
such fine and costs, or either.
Rule 4.24. Surrender of Principal; Exoneration. (1) At
any time before the forfeiture of the bond before the forfeiture
of the bail any surety may procure a certified copy of the bail
bond which shall authorize him or any peace officer to whom it
is presented to arrest the principal in any county within the Com
monwealth and deliver him and the certified copy of the bail
bond to the jailer in the county in which the prosecution is pend
ing. The jailer shall acknowledge the surrender in writing.
(2) Upon presentation of the writing executed by the
jailer, the court before which the defendant has been held to
answer shall, after five days' notice to the county attorney,
order that the surety or sureties be exonerated from liability on
the bond or recognizance and that any money or bonds that
have been deposited as bail be returned to the person making
the deposit.
(3) Surrender of the accused to the jailer in the county in
which the prosecution is pending at any time by the surety shall
operate as a discharge or release of the surety from his responsi
bility and when such formal surrender is made, the responsibility
of the surety shall cease, whether or not the accused has been
brought to final trial and conviction or acquittal. However,

Chapter 234 799
unless formal surrender of the accused has been made as pro
vided in this section, the appearance and answer of the accused,
upon call made as provided for in Rule 4.26, shall not operate
as a discharge or release of any surety from his responsibility
until the final trial and conviction or acquittal of such accused.
Rule 4.26. Forfeiture of Bail. ( 1 ) If, at the time fixed for
appearance, before any circuit court, an accused shall fail to
appear and answer when called, the court shall, on motion of the
Commonwealth's attorney, forthwith enter a judgment decreeing
the forfeiture of the bond and against such person and his surety
or sureties for the full amount thereof. The forfeiture shall be
noted on the record and endorsed on the bond, which endorse
ment shall be sufficient evidence of the forfeiture of the bail.
(2) If the declaration of forfeiture is made by an inferior
court and the amount of bail is beyond its jurisdiction, or a lien
on real estate is involved, the bond shall be filed with the clerk
of the circuit court of the county.
(3) The court may direct that a forfeiture be set aside,
upon such conditions as the court may impose, if it appears that
justice does not require the enforcement of the forfeiture.
(4) No judgment decreeing the forfeiture of the appear
ance bond shall be rendered in case it shall be made to appear
to the satisfaction of the court by affidavit of a reputable physi
cian that the principal of such bond is prevented from attending
by some physical disability.
(5) No judgment decreeing the forfeiture of the appear
ance bond shall be rendered in case it shall be made to appear
to the satisfaction of the court that the principal of such bond is
prevented from attending because of detention in a jail or peni
tentiary in another jurisdiction. A sworn affidavit of the jailer,
warden, or other responsible officer of said jail or penitentiary
shall be considered adequate proof of the principal's detention.
Rule 4.28. Judgment Against Surety. (1) If within twenty-
four hours after the rendition of any judgment forfeiting an
appearance bond, the amount of said bond shall not have been
paid, the Commonwealth's attorney shall cause said judgment to
be recorded in the office of the circuit clerk in which said bond

800 Acts of the General Assembly
shall have been forfeited, and may, at any time, cause said judg
ment to be recorded in every county in which he shall think
such recordation proper, and every recordation of said judgment
shall be without cost and shall operate as a judicial mortgage
against both the principal and his surety. If said judgment shall
not have been paid, it shall be the duty of the Commonwealth's
attorney, at the expiration of the time allowed by law for the
taking of an appeal, or within the time allowed by law for the
surety to return the fugitive to the jurisdiction of the court in
which said charge was made, to cause to issue a fieri facies
against both the principal and his surety.
(2) After entry of judgment, the court for sufficient cause
may remit the sum speoified in the bail bond in whole or in part.
(3) Any judgment forfeiting an appearance bond rendered
under the provisions of this rule shall, at any time within 180
days after rendition, be set aside upon the appearance and trial
and conviction or acquittal or upon a continuance granted upon
motion of the Commonwealth's attorney after such appearance.
Rule 4.30. Order of Arrest of Principal. The court shall
enter an order directing the arrest of the principal:
(1) When there has been a breach of a condition of the
bond;
( 2 ) Upon a forfeiture;
(3) When the court is satisfied that there is an insufficiency
of sureties; and
(4) Upon the principal's being indicted for an offense
which is not bailable.
Grand Jury
Rule 5.02. Charge to Grand Jury. The court shall swear
the grand jurors and oharge them to inquire into every offense
for which any person has been held to answer and for which
an indictment or information has not been filed, or other offenses
which come to their attention or of which any of them has
knowledge. The court shall further instruct the grand jurors
concerning inspections and reports which are required of them
by law.

Chapter 234 801
Rule 5.04. Foreman; Oath to Witnesses. The court shall
appoint one of the grand jurors to be foreman who shall admin
ister an oath or affirmation to each witness who testifies before
the grand jury.
Rule 5.06. Attendance of Witnesses. The circuit clerk,
upon request of the foreman of the grand jury, or of the attorney
for the Commonwealth, made during a term of court or in vaca
tion, shall issue subpoenas for witnesses. The attendance of
witnesses may be coerced as in other judicial proceedings.
Rule 5.08. Evidence for Defendant. The grand jurors are
under no duty to hear evidence for the defendant, but may do so.
Rule 5.10. Evidence Supporting Indictment. The grand
jurors shall find an indictment where they have received what
they believe to be sufficient competent evidence to support
it,
but no indictment shall be quashed or judgment of conviction
reversed on the ground that there was not sufficient competent
evidence to support the indictment.
Rule 5.12. Compelling Testimony. When
awitness before
the grand jury refuses to testify or to answer
aquestion put to
him, the foreman shall state the refusal to the court in the pres
ence of the witness. After hearing the witness,
if the court
decides that he
isbound to testify or answer and he persists in
his refusal, the court shall proceed against the witness as in cases
of similar refusal in open court.
Rule 5.14. Duties of Prosecuting Attorney. (1) The at
torney for the Commonwealth, or his assistant, designated by
him, shall attend the grand jurors when requested by them, and
he may do so on his own motion, for the purpose of examining
witnesses in their presence, or of giving the grand jurors legal
advice regarding any matter cognizable
bythem. He shall also,
when requested
bythem, draft indictments.
(2) The grand jurors may at any time question witnesses
in
aclosed session.
Rule 5.16. Transcript of Testimony. (1) The attorney for
the Commonwealth may appoint
astenographer to take in short

802 Acts of the General Assembly
hand the testimony of witnesses or he may cause the testimony
to be taken by a recording device, but the record so made shall
include the testimony of all witnesses on that particular investiga
tion. The shorthand notes or the recordings and transcript of
the same, if any, shall be delivered to and retained by the attorney
for the Commonwealth.
(2) The stenographer and any typist who transcribes the
stenographer's notes or recordings shall be swom by the foreman
not to disclose any testimony or the names of any witnesses except
to the attorney for the Commonwealth or when testifying in
court, and except that any person indicted by the grand jury shall
have a right to procure a transcript of any stenographic notes or
recordings or any part thereof by paying the prescribed fee
therefor.
Rule 5.18. Presence of Other Persons with Grand Jury. No
person except the attorney for the Commonwealth, or his as
sistant, designated by him, a stenographer appointed by him,
the witness under examination, and an interpreter, if needed,
shall be present during sessions of the grand jury. No person
shall be present while the grand jurors are deliberating or
voting. Any person violating either of these prohibitions may be
held in contempt of court.
Rule 5.20. Return of Indictment. The indictment shall be
returned by the foreman in the presence of the grand jury to
the judge in open court, filed with the clerk of the court and
remain in his office as a public record.
Rule 5.22. Procedure upon Failure to Indict. (1) If the
defendant has been held to answer and the votes of the grand
jurors are insufficient in number to find an indictment, the
foreman shall so report to the court in writing forthwith. The
court shall order a discharge of the defendant, exoneration of
the bail or a refund of any money or bonds deposited in lieu of
bail as the case may be.
(2) The failure of the grand jury to return an indictment
does not prevent the charge from being submitted to another
grand jury.

Chapter 234 803
Rule 5.24. Secrecy of Proceedings; Disclosure. Each grand
juror shall keep secret the proceedings of the grand jury and
the testimony given before
it,except that he may be required
by the court to disclose the testimony of
a witness examined
for the purpose of ascertaining its consistency with the testi
mony given
bythe witness on the trial, or for the purpose of
proceeding against the witness for perjury or false swearing;
and
itshall be the duty of the foreman of the grand jury to
communicate to the attorney for the Commonwealth, when re
quested, the substance of the testimony before them.
Indictment and Information
Rule 6.02. Use of Indictment and Information. All offenses,
except those required
bylaw to be prosecuted by indictment,
may be prosecuted by information.
Rule 6.04. Information. The attorney for the Common
wealth may file an information charging the commission of an
offense either upon his own knowledge, information, and belief,
or in his discretion upon the basis of
averified complaint sub
mitted to him.
Rule 6.06. Signatures. All indictments shall be signed
by
the foreman of the grand jury. All informations shall be signed
by an attorney for the Commonwealth. No objection to an in
dictment or information on the ground that
itwas not signed
as herein required may be made after
aplea to the merits has
been filed or entered.
Rule 6.08. Names of Witnesses. The names of all the
witnesses who were examined shall be endorsed on the indict
ment. The names of all persons upon whose statements the in
formation
isbased shall be endorsed thereon. Failure to endorse
the names shall not affect the validity or sufficiency of the in
dictment or information, but the court, on motion of the defend
ant, shall direct the names to be endorsed, and may grant such
continuance of the case as
itdeems necessary in the interest
of justice.
Rule 6.10. Requisites of Indictment or Information. (1)

804 Acts of the General Assembly
The indictment or information shall contain a caption setting
forth the name of the court and the names of the parties.
(2) The indictment or information shall contain, and shall
be sufficient if it contains a plain, concise and definite statement
of the essential facts constituting the specific offense with which
the defendant is charged. It need not contain any other matter
not necessary to such statement, nor need it negative any ex
ception, excuse or proviso contained in any statute creating or
defining the offense charged.
(3) Allegations made in one count may be incorporated
by a reference in another count. It may be alleged in any count
that the means by which the defendant committed the offense
are unknown or that he committed it by one or more specified
means. The indictment or information shall state for each count
the official or customary citation or any applicable statute, rule,
regulation or other provision of law which the defendant is al
leged therein to have violated; but error in the citation or Its
omission shall not be ground for dismissal of the indictment or
information or for reversal of a conviction if the error or omission
did not mislead the defendant to his prejudice.
(4) The indictment or information shall contain a formal
conclusion.
Rule 6.12. Effects of Defects. An indictment or informa
tion shall not be deemed invalid, nor shall the trial, judgment
or other proceedings thereon be stayed, arrested or in any man
ner affected for any defect or imperfection which does not tend
to prejudice the substantial rights of the defendant upon the
merits.
Rule 6.14. Surplusage. Unnecessary allegations may be
disregarded as surplusage, and on motion of the defendant may
be stricken from the indictment or information by the court.
Rule 6.16. Amendment. The court may permit an indict
ment or information to be amended any time before verdict or
finding if no additional or different offense is charged and if
substantial rights of the defendant are not prejudiced.
Rule 6.18. Joinder of Offenses. Two or mora offenses may

Chapter 234 805
be charged in the same information or two or more offenses
whether felonies or misdemeanors, or both, may be charged in
the same indictment, in a separate count for each offense, if the
offenses are of the same or similar character or are based on the
same acts or transactions connected together or constituting
parts of a common scheme or plan.
Rule 6.20. Joinder of Defendants. Two or more defendants
may be charged in the same indictment or information if they
are alleged to have participated in the same act or transaction
or in the same series of acts or transactions constituting an of
fense or offenses. Such defendants may be charged in one or
more counts together or separately and all of the defendants
need not be charged in each count.
Rule 6.22. Bill of Particulars. The court for cause shall
direct the filing of a bill of particulars. A motion for such bill
may be made at any time prior to arraignment, or thereafter in
the discretion of the court. A bill of particulars may be amended
at any time subject to such conditions as justice requires.
Rule 6.52. Warrant or Summons upon Indictment or Infor
mation; Issuance. Upon the request of the attorney for the
Commonwealth the court shall direct the clerk to issue a war
rant for each defendant named in the indictment or information.
The clerk shall issue a summons instead of a warrant upon the
request of the attorney for the Commonwealth or on direction
of the court. Upon request of the attorney for the Commonwealth,
the clerk shall issue more than one warrant or summons for
the same defendant. He shall deliver the warrant or summons
to a peace officer for execution or service. If a defendant fails
to appear in response to the summons, a warrant shall issue.
Rule 6.54. Warrant or Summons; Requisites. (1) The
requisites of a warrant on an indictment or information shall
be the same as for a warrant issued on a complaint except that
it shall be signed by the clerk, it shall name or describe the
offense charged in the indictment or information, and it shall
command that the defendant be arrested and brought before
the court. If the offense is bailable and the warrant is issued

806 Acts of the General Assembly
at the discretion of a circuit judge, the amount of bail shall be
fixed by the court and endorsed thereon, and the bail so en
dorsed may be taken by the clerk.
(2) The summons shall be in the same form as the war
rant except that it shall summon the defendant to appear before
the court at a stated time and place.
Rule 6.56. Warrant or Summons; Execution or Service and
Return. (1) The warrant shall be executed or the summons
served as provided for the execution of warrants or the service
of summons issued upon complaints.
(2) The return of the warrant or summons shall be made
to the court issuing it.
Production of Evidence
Rule 7.02. Subpoenas. Upon the request of the attorney
for the Commonwealth or the defendant or his attorney, the clerk
of the court shall issue subpoenas.
Rule 7.04. Attendance of Witnesses. The provisions of the
Rules of Civil Procedure shall apply to and govern the summon
ing and coercing the attendance of witnesses, and compelling
them to testify in all criminal proceedings, except that the attend
ance of witnesses residing in any part of the Commonwealth may
be coerced, and it shall not be necessary to tender to the witnesses
any compensation for expenses, or otherwise, before process of
contempt shall issue.
Rule 7.06. Bail for Material Witness. If it appears by affi
davit that the testimony of a person is material in any criminal
proceeding and that it may become impracticable to secure his
attendance by subpoena, the court may require him to give bail
for his appearance as a witness in an amount fixed by the
court. If he fails to give bail, the court may commit him to cus
tody pending final disposition of the proceeding in which the
testimony is needed, may order his release if he has been detained
for an unreasonable length of time and may modify at any time
the requirement as to 'bail.

Chapter 234 807
Rule 7.08. Documentary Evidence. A subpoena may com
mand the person to whom it is directed to produce the books,
papers, documents, or tangible things designated therein; but the
court, upon motion made promptly and in any event at or before
the time specified in the subpoena for compliance therewith,
may quash or modify the subpoena if it is unreasonable or oppres
sive. The Rules of Civil Procedure shall govern coercion of pro
duction of documentary evidence.
Rule 7.10. Grounds for Taking Depositions. (1) If it
appears that a prospective witness may be unable to attend or
prevented from attending a trial or hearing or is or may become
a non-resident of the Commonwealth, that his testimony is
material and that it is necessary to take his deposition in order to
prevent a failure of justice, the court at any time after the pre
liminary hearing or the filing of the indictment or the information
may upon motion and notice to the parties order that his testi
mony be taken by deposition and that any designated books,
papers, documents, or tangible objects, not privileged, be pro
duced at the same time and place.
(2) If a witness is committed for failure to give bail to
appear to testify at a trial or hearing, the court on written motion
of the witness and upon notice to the parties may direct that his
deposition be taken at the expense of the Commonwealth. After
the deposition has been subscribed the court may discharge the
witness.
Rule 7.22. Discovery and Inspection. Upon motion of a
defendant, the court may order the attorney for the Common
wealth to permit the defendant or his attorney to inspect and
copy or photograph designated books, papers, documents or
tangible objects obtained from or belonging to the defendant or
obtained from others by seizure or by process, upon a showing
that the items sought may be material to the preparation of his
defense and that the request is reasonable. The order shall specify
the time, place and manner of making the inspection and of taking
the copies or photographs and may prescribe such terms and con
ditions as are just.

808 Acts of the General Assembly
Arraignment and Pleadings
Rule 8.02. Arraignment. Arraignment shall be conducted
in open court and shall consist of reading the indictment or in
formation to the defendant or stating to him the substance of the
charge and calling upon him to plead thereto. He shall be given
a copy of the indictment or information before he is called upon
to plead. Defendants who are jointly charged may be arraigned
separately or together in the discretion of the court.
Rule 8.04. Assignment of Counsel. If on arraignment or
thereafter, in felony cases, the defendant appears in court with
out counsel, the court shall advise him of his right to counsel,
and shall assign counsel to represent him at every stage of the
proceedings unless he elects to proceed without counsel or is
able to obtain counsel.
Rule 8.06. Insanity. If upon arraignment or during the
proceedings there are reasonable grounds to believe that the de
fendant is insane, the proceedings shall be postponed and the
issue of sanity determined as provided by law. If the defendant is
found to be insane, the court shall direct that he be confined1 in a
mental institution until his mind is restored at which time he shall
be returned to the court for further proceedings.
Rule 8.08. Pleas. A defendant may plead not guilty or
guilty. The court may refuse to accept a plea of guilty, and shall
not accept the plea without first determining that the plea is
made voluntarily with understanding of the nature of the charge.
If a defendant refuses to plead or if the court refuses to accept
a plea of guilty or if a defendant corporation fails to appear,
the court shall enter a plea of not guilty.
Rule 8.10. Withdrawal of plea. At any time before judg
ment the court may permit the plea of guilty to be withdrawn and
a plea of not guilty substituted.
Rule 8.12. Pleadings. Pleadings in criminal proceedings
shall be the indictment or the information, and the plea of
guilty or not guilty. No other plea, demurrer, or motion to quash
shall be used, and defenses and objections which before the effec

Chapter 234 809
tive date of these rules could have been raised by one or more
of them shall be raised only by motion to dismiss or to grant
appropriate relief.
Rule 8.14. Motions. An application to the court for an
order shall be by motion which shall be in writing unless made
during a hearing or trial, shall state with particularity the grounds
therefor, and shall set forth the relief or order sought.
Rule 8.16. Defenses Which May Be Raised by Motion.
Any defense or objection which is capable of determination with
out the trial of the general issue may be raised' before trial by
motion.
Rule 8.18. Defenses Which Must Be Raised by Motion.
Defenses and objections based on defects in the institution of the
prosecution or in the indictment or information other than that it
fails to show jurisdiction in the court or to charge an offense may
be raised only by motion before trial. The motion shall include
all such defenses and objections then available to the defendant.
Failure to present any such defense or objection as herein provided
constitutes a waiver thereof, but the court for cause shown may
grant relief from the waiver. Lack of jurisdiction or the failure
of the indictment or information to charge an offense shall be
noticed by the court at any time during the proceedings.
Rule 8.20. Time of Making Motion. The motion raising
defenses or objections shall be made before the plea is entered, but
the court may permit it to be made within a reasonable time there
after without withdrawal of the plea.
Rule 8.22. Hearing on Motion. The motion before trial
raising defenses or objections shall be determined before trial un
less the court orders that it be deferred for determination at the
trial of the general issue. An issue of fact shall be tried by a
jury if a jury trial is required by law. All other issues of fact
shall be determined by the court with or without a jury or on
affidavits or in such other manner as the court may direct.
Rule 8.24. Effect of Determination. (1) If a motion is
determined adversely to the defendant he shall be permitted to

810 Acts of the General Assembly
plead if he had not previously pleaded. A plea previously en
tered shall not be affected by the adverse determination of a
motion.
(2) If the court grants a motion based on a defect in the
institution of the prosecution or in the indictment or information,
it may also order that the defendant be held in custody or that
his bail be continued for a period of time not to exceed the date
of discharge of the grand jury assembled at the next term of
court pending the filing of a new indictment or information.
Rule 8.26. Improper Venue; Transfer. If it appears from
either the indictment, information or evidence that the offense
was committed in a county other than that in which the trial is
being held, the defendant may move for a transfer of the prosecu
tion to the proper venue. If the motion is sustained, the court
shall order a transfer of the original papers including the bail
bond, if any, or certified copies thereof, to the clerk of the court
of the county to which the transfer of the prosecution is made.
The court also may order that the bail be continued for his
appearance or that the defendant be held in custody to await
his transfer to the county to which the prosecution is transferred
by a peace officer of that county. The defendant shall be deemed
to be held to answer in the court of the county in which the
offense was committed from the date of the sustaining of the
motion.
Rule 8.28. Presence of Defendant. (1) The defendant
shall be present at the arraignment, at every stage of the trial
including the impaneling of the jury and the return of the verdict,
and at the imposition of the sentence. The defendant's voluntary
absence after the trial has been commenced in his presence shall
not prevent proceeding with the trial up to and including the
verdict. The defendant may be permitted to remain on bail
during the trial.
(2) A corporation may appear by counsel for all purposes.
(3) In prosecutions for misdemeanors, the court may per
mit arraignment, plea, trial and imposition of sentence in the
defendant's absence.
i*.

Chapter 234 811
TRIAL
General
Rule 9.02. Time of Hearing or Trial. All prosecutions shall
proceed when the defendant appears or is brought before the
court unless postponed for cause.
Rule 9.04. Postponement of Hearing or Trial; Motion and
Affidavit. The court, upon motion and sufficient cause shown
by either party, may grant a postponement of the hearing or trial.
A motion by the defendant for a postponement on account of
the absence of evidence may be made only upon affidavit show
ing the materiality of the evidence expected to be obtained, and
that due diligence has been used to obtain it. If the motion is
based on the absence of a witness, the affidavit must show what
facts the affiant believes the witness will prove, and not merely
the effect of such facts in evidence, and that the affiant believes
them to be true. If the attorney for the Commonwealth consents
to the reading of the affidavit on the hearing or trial as the
deposition of the absent witness, the hearing or trial shall not
be postponed on account of his absence.
Rule 9.12. Consolidation of Offenses for Trial. The court
may order two or more indictments or informations or both to
be tried together if the offenses, and the defendants, if more than
one, could have been joined in a single indictment or information.
The procedure shall be the same as if the prosecution were under
such single indictment or information. However, any defendant
in any criminal action shall be entitled to a separate trial, if
demand is made therefor, prior to the time a jury is impaneled.
If there is no jury, then such demand shall be made before any
evidence is offered.
Rule 9.14. Misjoinder of Offenses. If two or more offenses
are charged in the same indictment or information and they can
not be properly joined, the Commonwealth may be required to
elect which offense it will prosecute.
Rule 9.16. Separate Trials. If it appears that a defendant
or the Commonwealth is prejudiced by a joinder of offenses or

812 Acts of the General Assembly
of defendants in an indictment or information or by joinder for
trial, the court may order separate trials of counts, grant separate
trials of defendants or provide whatever other relief justice
requires.
Rule 9.22. Objections; Exceptions Unnecessary. An objec
tion is made by a party making known to the court, at the time
the ruling or order of the court is sought, the action which he
desires the court to take or his objection to the action of the
court, and on request of the court, his grounds therefor; but if a
party has no opportunity to object to a ruling or order, the
absence of an objection does not thereafter prejudice him. Ex
ceptions to the rulings or orders of the court are unnecessary.
Rule 9.24. Harmless Error. Any error, defect, irregularity
or variance which does not affect substantial rights shall be
disregarded.
Rule 9.26. Substantial Error. A palpable error which af
fects substantial rights may be considered by the court on motion
for a new trial or by the Court of Appeals on appeal, even though
insufficiently raised or preserved for review, and appropriate
relief may be granted upon a determination that manifest injustice
has resulted from the error.
Jury
Rule 9.30. Selection of Jury. (1) (a) In a jury trial in
circuit court the clerk, in open court, shall draw from the jury
box sufficient names of the persons selected and summoned for
jury service to compose a jury as required by law. If one or
more of them is challenged, the clerk shall draw from the box
as many more as are necessary to complete the jury.
(b) If there is an irregularity in drawing from the jury
box, the names of the jurors so drawn shall be returned to the
box.
(c) When it appears that the names in the jury box are
about to become exhausted, the judge may obtain additional
jurors by drawing from the drum, or, with the consent of the

Chapter 234 813
parties, by ordering the sheriff or a bailiff appointed by the
court to summon any number of qualified persons.
(2) In inferior courts the magistrate shall order the sheriff
or the attending officer to summon as many qualified persons as
are necessary to compose the jury, unless otherwise provided
by law.
Rule 9.32. Additional Jurors. The court may direct the
clerk to draw one or two names from the jury box in addition to
the number required by law to compose the jury. All jurors so
drawn shall be empaneled and hear the case. Should it become
necessary to excuse a juror for any reason, the trial shall continue
unless the number of jurors is reduced to less than the number
required by law. If the membership of the jury consists of more
than the number required by law after it has been instructed by
the court, the clerk, in open court, shall place the name of each
juror on a separate piece of paper, fold it so as to conceal the
name and put it in a box provided for that purpose. The clerk,
after mixing the names, shall draw from the box sufficient names
to reduce the jury to the number required by law.
Rule 9.33. Jurors From Adjoining Counties. If the judge
of the court be satisfied after having made a fair effort, in good
faith, for that purpose, that, from any cause, it will be imprac
ticable to obtain a jury free of bias in the county wherein the
prosecution is pending, he shall be authorized to order the sheriff
to summon a sufficient number of qualified jurors from some
adjoining county in which the judge shall believe there is the
greatest probability of obtaining impartial jurors. When the
sheriff is so ordered he shall go to the circuit judge of such
adjoining county, if he is in the county at the time, and request
the judge to draw from the jury drum a list of names sufficient
in number to equal the number ordered summoned; and to record
the names upon paper, certify and sign it. Whereupon the said
sheriff shall summon the number ordered to be summoned from
such list. If the circuit judge is not in the county at the time,
then the sheriff shall select such jurors from the panels which
served, and whose names appeared of record, as jurors at the
previous regular term of court in such adjoining county. If the
jurors who served at the preceding term are not sufficient in

814 Acts of the General Assembly
number to meet the number ordered summoned then the sheriff
shall select the remainder from the list which served at the next
to the last preceding term. From the persons so summoned the
jury may be formed.
Rule 9.34. Challenge to Panel. A motion raising an irreg
ularity in the selection or summons of the jurors or formation of
the jury must precede the examination of the jurors.
Rule 9.36. Challenge to Individual Jurors. (1) The chal
lenges for cause shall be made first by the Commonwealth and
then by the defense. Each party shall exhaust his challenges to
each juror before the other begins. When there is reasonable
ground to believe that a juror cannot render a fair and impartial
verdict on the evidence, he shall be excused as disqualified to
serve.
(2) When more than one defendant is being tried, the order
in which challenges to a juror may be made shall be prescribed
by order of court.
(3) Challenges for peremptory or for cause must be made
before the juror is accepted except that the court for good cause
may permit such challenges until the jury is sworn.
Rule 9.38. Examination of Jurors. The court may permit
the attorney for the Commonwealth and the defendant or his
attorney to conduct the examination of prospective jurors or may
itself conduct the examination. In the latter event the court
shall permit the attorney for the Commonwealth and the defend
ant or his attorney to supplement the examination by such further
inquiry as it deems proper. The court may itself submit to the
prospective jurors such additional questions submitted by the
parties or their attorneys as it deems proper.
Rule 9.40. Peremptory Challenges. (1) If the offense
charged is a felony, the Commonwealth is entitled to five per
emptory challenges and the defendant or defendants jointly to
fifteen peremptory challenges. If the offense charged is a mis
demeanor, the Commonwealth is entitled to three peremptory
challenges and the defendant or defendants jointly to three per
emptory challenges.

Chapter 234 815
(2) If one or two additional jurors are called, each side
is entitled to one additional peremptory challenge.
(3) If more than one defendant is being tried, the court
may at its discretion allow additional peremptory challenges to
each defendant.
Proceedings
Rule 9.42. Order of Proceeding. The jury shall be sworn
to try the issue after which the trial shall proceed in the follow
ing order, unless the court for special reasons otherwise directs:
( 1 ) The attorney for the Commonwealth shall state to the
jury the nature of the charge and the evidence upon which he
relies to support it;
(2) The defendant or his attorney may state his defense
and the evidence upon which he relies to support it or he may
reserve his opening statement until the conclusion of the evidence
for the Commonwealth;
(3) The attorney for the Commonwealth must offer the
evidence in support of the charge;
(4) The defendant or his attorney may make his opening
statement, if reserved, and offer his evidence in support of his
defense;
(5) The parties respectively may offer rebutting evidence,
unless the court, for good reason in furtherance of justice, per
mits them to offer evidence-in-chief;
(6) The parties may submit or argue the case to the
jury. In the argument, the attorney for the Commonwealth shall
have the conclusion and the defendant or his attorney the open
ing. If more than one counsel is to take part in the closing argu
ment on either side, or if several defendants have separate
defenses and appear by different counsel, the court shall arrange
the order of argument, always giving the attorney for the Com
monwealth the closing argument.
Rule 9.44. Proof of Official Record. An official record or
any entry therein or the lack of such a record or entry may be
proved in the manner provided by the Civil Rules.

816 Acts of the General Assembly
Rule 9.46. Expert Witnesses. The court may order the
parties to show cause why expert witnesses should not be ap
pointed, and may request the parties to submit nominations.
The court may appoint any expert witnesses agreed upon by
the parties, and may appoint witnesses of its own selections.
An expert witness shall not be appointed by the court unless
the witness consents to act. A witness so appointed shall be
informed of his duties by the court at a conference in which
the parties shall have opportunity to participate. A witness so
appointed shall advise the parties of his findings, if any, and
may thereafter be called to testify by the court or by any
party. He shall be subject to cross-examination by each
party. The court may determine the reasonable compensation
of such witness and direct its payment out of such funds as may be
provided by law. The parties also may call expert witnesses of
their own selection at their own expense.
Rule 9.48. Separation of Witnesses. If either a defendant
or the Commonwealth requests
it,the judge may exclude from
the hearing or trial any witness of the adverse party not at
the time under examination, so that he may not hear the testi
mony of the other witnesses. This provision shall not apply to
the parties to the proceeding.
Rule 9.50. Exclusion of Infants from Courtroom. In any
trial for rape, attempted rape, seduction, unlawfully detaining
awoman and similar offenses the judge may exclude from the
courtroom, and from the hearing of the testimony and argu
ments, all infants under the age of sixteen years, except those
who are witnesses in the case or kin to one of the parties.
Rule 9.52. Avowals.
Ifan objection to
a question pro
pounded to
awitness
issustained
bythe court, upon the request
of the examining attorney an avowal may be made in the manner
provided in the Civil Rules.
Itshall be the duty of the court to instruct the jury on all
