CHAPTER 238
(H. B. 126)
AN ACT relating to the Speaker of the House of Representatives.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. Between sessions of the General Assembly an
office shall be maintained in the State Capitol Building for the
use and benefit of the Speaker of the House of Representatives.
Except when the General Assembly is in session the Speaker of
the House of Representatives shall receive twenty-five dollars
per day for each day he is engaged in the duties of his office and
shall be reimbursed for necessary traveling expenses.
Section 2. To carry out the purposes of this Act, there is
allocated to the Legislative Research Commission out of funds
appropriated under House Bill 40 enacted at the 1962 Regular
Session the sum of ten thousand nine hundred dollars for the
1962-63 fiscal year, and the sum of nine thousand nine hundred
and seventy-five dollars for the 1963-64 fiscal year.
Approved March 22, 1962
CHAPTER 239
(H. B. 128)
AN ACT relating to fire department aid.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:

Chapter 240 857
Section 17.250 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is amended
to read as follows :
(1) The commissioner shall allot on an annual basis the
funds appropriated for fire department aid in the amounts not
to exceed two thousand dollars [$1,000] to the fire departments
in those cities of the third through sixth classes, fire prevention
districts organized pursuant to KRS Chapter 75, county districts
established under authority of KRS 67.320, and all other organ
ized fire departments recognized by the commissioner as fire
departments operated and maintained on a non-profit basis in
the interest of the health, safety, prosperity and security of the
inhabitants of the Commonwealth. With the advice of the Fire
Department Committee, he shall prescribe regulations and stand
ards for determining the amounts to be granted. Such standards
shall be based on the number of firemen, both paid and volunteer,
the amount of equipment, housing facilities available and such
other matters or standards as will best effect the purposes of
the fire department aid law. No allotment shall exceed the total
value of the funds, equipment, lands and buildings made avail
able to the local fire units from any source whatever for the year
in which the allotment is made.
(2) To carry out the purposes of the fire department aid
law, there is appropriated to the Department of Public Safety
out of the General Expenditure Fund in the State Treasury the
sum of $100,000.00 for the 1962-1963 fiscal year, and the sum of
$100,000.00 for the 1963-64 fiscal year.
Approved March 22, 1962
CHAPTER 240
(H. B. 153)
AN ACT relating to revenue and taxation.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. A new section of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is created to read as follows:

858 Acts of the General Assembly
The Department of Revenue may by regulation refund or
waive the cigarette tax imposed by the provisions of Chapter 138
of the Kentucky Revised Statutes on any cigarettes donated to
hospitals or other eleemosynary institutions for the benefit of,
or for the use of, patients or inmates of such institutions. The
Department shall also prescribe the method by which cigarettes
donated shall be transferred to any such institutions.
Approved March 22, 1962
CHAPTER 241
(H. B. 156)
AN ACT relating to local fairs. This shall be known as the Emerson
Beauchamp Bill.
WHEREAS, it is a recognized fact that the agricultural fair is one of
the most colorful, honored, and revered institutions in the American
way of life, and
WHEREAS, it is a well-proven fact that said fairs are the most important
and successful mediums for promoting improvement in livestock
and farm products generally for bringing about continued improve
ment in the rural way of life and in fostering closer association of
urban and rural folk resulting in a better understanding between
them, and
WHEREAS, in recognition of the vital role they portray in community
life, it is the established policy of many states to grant substantial
financial aid to such fairs in order to guarantee and foster their
continued existence, and
WHEREAS, in Kentucky numerous fairs need added emphasis and re
sources to improve livestock shows and agricultural exhibits,
NOW, THEREFORE,
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 247.220 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
( 1 ) The Commissioner of Agriculture shall make grants of
state funds to qualified local fairs to be used by them to pay pre
mium awards for exhibits and displays of domestic livestock or
other agricultural products. The grant of state funds made to any

Chapter 241 859
fair shall in no event exceed one-half of the amount of premiums
paid out for livestock shows and agricultural exhibits and in no
instance shall the allotment exceed fifteen hundred dollars. The
premiums actually awarded shall conform to those appearing on
the premium list issued by the fair. [The State Board of Agricul
ture may cooperate with county and community fairs by supple
menting premiums offered at such fairs for exhibits of livestock
and poultry; for breeding classes for horses, including those two
years old or under and for breeding stock of any age; and for any
and all classes of fruits, vegetables, seeds or other crops for which
exhibits are held at such fairs, to the extent of from five hundred
dollars to one thousand dollars annually, if the fair association
receiving such support offers at least an equal amount in these
classes. If the fair association offers a lesser amount the financial
support given by the state under this section shall be reduced in
proportion.]
(2) There shall be a Fair Council in the Department of
Agriculture, Labor and Statistics. The Council shall act in an ad
visory capacity to the Commissioner in all matters pertaining to
the adminstration of the Department's fair program. It shall be
called into session at such times as there are matters for its con
sideration. It shall meet at least twice each calendar year at
Frankfort or at such other place that may be determined. [Any
fair association offering special prize money for 4-H Club or Smith-
Hughes agricultural student projects may receive from the board
an additional sum ranging from two hundred and fifty dollars to
five hundred dollars, if each fair association appropriates an equal
amount in these classes.]
(3) The Council shall be composed of the Commissioner as
Chairman ex officio and the presidents or their designated repre
sentatives of the following state groups: Kentucky Livestock Im
provement Association, Kentucky Farm Bureau Federation, Ken
tucky Association of Fairs and Horse Shows, Inc., the Director of
Vocational Agriculture Education, and the Dean of the College
of Agriculture of the University of Kentucky.
(4) To qualify for a grant of state funds, a fair shall meet
standards set by the Commissioner and his advisory council whose
approval may be given only if the fair:

860 Acts of the General Assembly
(a) Provides in its by-laws for holding an annual fair running
for at least three days;
(b) Presents, through the medium of youth organizations
such as 4-H Clubs, Future Farmers of America, and other similar
organizations, an educational program concerning the production
and marketing of livestock and other related subjects;
(c) Complies with all other rules and regulations which the
Department of Agriculture is hereby authorized to promulgate;
(d) Appoints one or more members to its fair board from
County Livestock Improvement Associations and County Farm
Bureaus and selects one or more county extension agents and vo
cational agriculture teachers for counties served by the fair as
members of the board. Wherever County Livestock Improvement
Associations and Farm Bureaus are in existence, appointees are to
be nominated to the fair board by these organizations. Where fairs
serve an area, appointments may be made from all counties within
the particular area. It sfiall be the responsibility of the appointees
to aid in establishing livestock premium lists and planning agricul
tural exhibits.
(5) Any fair receiving a grant of state funds shall file with
the Director of Livestock Shows and Fairs in the Department of
Agriculture, by December first of the year in which the grant is
received, satisfactory proof that all state premium awards have
been paid and a financial report by a certified public accountant.
Section 2. There is hereby appropriated out of the General
Expenditure Fund of the State Treasury to the Kentucky Depart
ment of Agriculture for the fiscal year 1963-64 a sum of fifty
thousand dollars to carry out the provisions of KRS 247.220 and
ten thousand dollars for each of the fiscal years 1962-63 and
1963-64 to provide personnel to help plan, promote, and service
these events and administer the Department's program.
Section 3. Section 247.230 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is repealed.
Approved March 22, 1962

Chapter 242 861
CHAPTER 242
(H. B. 183)
AN ACT relating to purebred cattle and making an appropriation.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 246.250 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is amended
to read as follows:
(1) As used in this section, unless the context otherwise
requires:
(a) "Department" means the Department of Agricul
ture, Labor and Statistics.
( b ) "Commissioner" means the Commissioner of Agri
culture, Labor and Statistics.
(c) "Purebred" means registered by the appropriate
association, organization or authority.
(d) Neither "show" or "exhibition" or "show, exhibi
tion and sale" shall include those held as a part of or in connection
with any county or other local fair.
(2) It shall be the duty of the Department of Agriculture,
Labor and Statistics to aid, encourage, foster and promote the
development and the improvement of the purebred cattle industry
in Kentucky. To that end, it shall be the duty of the department
to promote and to aid and assist in the promotion of annual shows
and sales of purebred cattle in Kentucky with the object and pur
pose of establishing Kentucky as one of the greatest centers in the
United States for the breeding, producing, raising, exhibition,
showing and sale of purebred cattle and for said purposes there
hereby is appropriated out of the General Expenditure Fund to
the department the sum of thirty [thirty-one] thousand dollars
for each of the fiscal years 1962-1963 and 1963-1964 [1956-1957
and 1957-1958] .
(3) [(a)] From the appropriation made in subsection (2)
of this section, six thousand dollars shall be used and applied in
each of said fiscal years in each of the following events: [as fol
lows:] Not less than four thousand dollars nor more than five
thousand dollars shall be used and applied to the payment of

862 Acts of the General Assembly
premiums for the largest purebred Aberdeen-Angus, purebred
Hereford, purebred Shorthorn, purebred Polled Hereford, pure
bred Jersey, purebred Holstein-Freisian, purebred Guernsey, pure
bred Ayrshire, and purebred Brown Swiss show and sale which
may be held in Kentucky in each of the fiscal years 1962-1963
and 1963-1964 [1956-1957 and 1957-1958] which is open on equal
terms and conditions to all [Aberdeen-Angus] breeders in the
United States who are or may be eligible to enter any of the above
shows and sales. The remainder shall be used and applied by the
commissioner for appropriate advertising of such shows and sales
in such livestock journals and other advertising media as the com
missioner in his discretion may determine.
[(b) From the appropriation made in subsection (2) of
this section, six thousand dollars shall be used and applied in each
of said fiscal years as follows : Not less than four thousand dollars
nor more than five thousand dollars shall be used and applied to
the payment of premiums for the largest purebred Hereford show
and sale which may be held in Kentucky in each of the fiscal years
1956-1957 and 1957-1958 which is open on equal terms and con
ditions to all Hereford breeders in the United States. The re
mainder shall be used and applied by the commissioner for
appropriate advertising of such show and sale in such livestock
journals and other advertising media as the commissioner in his
discretion may determine.]
[(c) From the appropriations made in subsection (2) of
this section. six thousand dollars shall be used and applied in each
of said fiscal years as follows : Not less than four thousand dollars
nor more than five thousand dollars shall be used and applied to
the payment of premiums for the largest purebred Shorthorn show
and sale which may be held in Kentucky in each of the fiscal
years 1956-1957 and 1957-1958 which is open on equal terms and
conditions to all Shorthorn breeders in the United States. The
remainder shall be used and applied by the commissioner for ap
propriate advertising of such show and sale in such livestock
journals and other advertising media as the commissioner in his
discretion may determine.]
[(d) From the appropriation made in subsection (2) of
this section, six thousand dollars shall be used and applied in each
of said fiscal years as follows: Not less than four thousand dollars

Chapter 242 863
nor more than five thousand dollars shall be used and applied
to the payment of premiums for the largest purebred Polled Here
ford show and sale which may be held in Kentucky in each of
the fiscal years 1956-1957 and 1957-1958 which is open on equal
terms and conditions to all Polled Hereford breeders in the United
States. The remainder shall be used and applied by the com
missioner for appropriate advertising of such show and sale in
such livestock journals and other advertising media as the com
missioner in his discretion may determine.]
(4) Any show, exhibition and sale of purebred cattle to
be eligible to receive the aforesaid appropriations must provide
that the entries in all classes must be open upon equal terms to
the breeders of such cattle in all of the states of the United States,
and the Dominion of Canada may be included. The commissioner
shall determine with respect to each of the aforesaid breeds of
cattle which show, exhibition and sale is the largest to be held in
Kentucky in each year and, in making such determination, he may
consider such shows, exhibitions and sales as may have been held
in Kentucky in any previous year and in any previous two-year
period.
(5) The remainder of the appropriations made in subsec
tion (2) of this section for each of said fiscal years shall be used
and applied by the department as follows, to-wit: At least five
thousand dollars of the remainder of said appropriation shall be
used to add premium money otherwise available for shows and
exhibitions of purebred dairy cattle to be held in Kentucky at
such places [and in such regions or districts] as the commissioner
may determine; and the remaining balance thereof shall be used
and applied by the department in advertising and in promoting
such purebred dairy cattle shows and exhibitions in such live
stock journals and other advertising media as the commissioner in
his discretion may determine.
( 6 ) No show or exhibition and no show, exhibition and sale
of any purebred cattle shall qualify for or be eligible to receive
any part of the appropriation made in this section unless and until
there is provided and made available from other sources an
amount for premiums equal to or in excess of the respective ap
propriations made available by the provisions of this section for
such show or exhibition or show, exhibition and sale, or unless

Acts of the Genehal Assembly
there is provided and made available from some other source as
amount for premiums equal to or in excess of the amount which
the commissioner mav allocate to or for such show or exhibition
or show, exhibition and sale under the authoritv vested in him bv
this section.
Approved March 22. 1962
CHAPTER 243
(H. B. 194)
AN ACT relating to the office of Assistant County Attorney.
Be it enacted by the General Aisembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 69.275 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
( 1 ) The office of assistant county attorney in counties hav
ing a population of less than 150,000 and not containing a city
of the second class, is hereby created.
( 2 ) The assistant county attorney shall be appointed by the
county attorney for the duration or unexpired part of his term,
but shall be subject to removal at any time by the county attorney.
(3) The assistant county attorney shall possess the qualifica
tions of a county attorney, and during the absence from the
county of the county attorney or his inability to attend shall act
as county attorney and have the same powers and perform all
the duties now or hereafter imposed on county attorneys by law,
and while so acting shall be paid and receive the fees and
commissions that the county attorney would have received if
present and acting, the county attorney to continue to receive
the salary fixed by the fiscal court. [, but in no event shall the
total amount of salaries, fees and commissions paid the county
attorney and his assistant county attorney acting under the
provisions hereof exceed the sum of five thousand dollars
in any one year.]

Chapter 244 865
Section 2. Section 69.276 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
( 1 ) The county attorney in counties containing a city of the
second, third or fourth class may appoint an assistant county at
torney for the duration of his term, subject to removal at any
time by the county attorney.
(2) The assistant county attorney shall receive an annual
salary to be fixed by the fiscal court [at not less than $1200 nor
more than $1800] and paid out of the excess fees received by
the county attorney or out of the general county levy, and such
payment shall not diminish the total salary, fees or commissions
paid to the county attorney. He shall possess the qualifications
required of a county attorney and shall have the same powers
and perform the same duties as the county attorney, subject to
the direction and control of the county attorney. In the absence
of the county attorney, the assistant shall act as county attorney,
without additional compensation.
Approved March 22, 1962
CHAPTER 244
(H. B. 207)
AN ACT relating to education.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Article I. PROCEDURE IN SCHOOL BOARD CAUSES
Section 1. (1) The Superintendent of Public Instruction
or the State Board of Education, shall recommend, by written
charges, to the proper school authorities having immediate juris
diction the removal of any superintendent of schools, principal,
teacher or other public school officer who in his or their opinion
is guilty of immorality, misconduct in office, incompetency or
willful neglect of duty.
(2) The Superintendent of Public Instruction shall recom
mend by written charges the suspension by the State Board of

866 Acts of the General Assembly
Education of any district board member or superintendent of
schools who in his opinion is guilty of immorality, misconduct in
office, incompetency or willful neglect of duty. The State Board
of Education shall, after a summary hearing as provided by regu
lations, suspend any district board of education member or Super
intendent of Schools, who in the opinion of the majority of the
State Board of Education is guilty of gross immorality, misconduct
in office, incompetence or willful neglect of duty. Such action
by the State Board of Education may be taken upon a recom
mendation of the Superintendent of Public Instruction, or such
action may be taken by a majority vote of the State Board of
Education without recommendation from the Superintendent of
Public Instruction.
(3) The State Board of Education may suspend a district
superintendent of schools under subsection (2) of this section
only if after 30 days of receipt of the written charges specified
in subsection (1) of this section, the district board of education
has refused to act, has acted in bad faith, arbitrarily or
capriciously, or if a recommendation to the district board would
have been futile.
Section 2. ( 1 ) Any officer suspended by the State Board
of Education under section 1. subsection (2) of this Article shall
be furnished with a written statement, specifying ki detail the
reasons for suspension and naming a date and place at which
the officer may appear before the State Board of Education to
answer the charges. The date shall be not less than twenty
nor more than thirty days after the service of such charges upon
the officer. Within ten days after receipt of the charges, the
officer may notify the State Board of Education of his intention
to appear and answer such charges. If the officer fails to notify
the Board of his intention to appear and answer such charges,
the State Board of Education may remove the officer by a
majority vote and such dismissal shall be final.
(2) Upon receipt of the officer's notice of intention to
appear and answer such charges the State Board of Education
shall isue such subpoenas as shall be necessary for the determina
tion of the issues involved. The issues shall be heard at the
time and place set and the hearing shall be public or private

Chapteb 244 867
at the discretion of the State Board of Education. Both parties
may be represented by counsel and may require the presence of
witnesses upon subpoena. Each witness shall be required to take
oath before an officer of the board. The State Board of Educa
tion shall provide for a stenographic report of the proceedings
and furnish the officer with a copy.
( 3 ) Upon completion of both sides of the case, but within
ninety days from the date the officer was suspended, the State
Board of Education may by a majority vote remove the officer.
If within ninety days from the date of suspension, the State Board
has not removed the officer, or has dismissed the charges, the
suspended officer shall be reinstated and shall be paid his full
salary for the period of such suspension.
(4) The officer shall have a right to appeal both as to law
and to fact to the circuit court for trial without a jury. If said
appeal is not made within thirty days after removal, then the
decision of the State Board of Education shall be final. Said
appeal shall be an original action in said court and shall be
commenced by the filing of a petition against the State Board
of Education, in which petition the facts shall be alleged upon
which the officer relies for a reversal or modification of the order
of removal. Upon service or waiver of summons in said appeal,
the State Board of Education shall forthwith transmit to the clerk
of said court for filing a transcript of the original notice of charges
and a transcript of all evidence adduced at the hearing, where
upon the cause shall be at issue without further pleading and
shall be advanced and heard without delay. The court shall
examine the transcript and record of the hearing before the State
Board of Education and shall hold such additional hearings as
it may deem advisable, at which it may consider other evidence
in addition to such transcript and record. Upon final hearing,
the court shall grant or deny the relief prayed for in the petition.
Either the officer or the State Board of Education may appeal
from the action of the court to the Court of Appeals. If the
decision of the court is against removal, the officer shall be paid
his full salary from the date of suspension. Such payment shall
be made from funds appropriated to the State Department of
Education.
Section 3. The State Board of Education, upon suspension

868 Acts of the General Assembly
of any officer or district board of education member under
Section 1. (2) and (3) of this Article, shall name a person to
fill the vacancy caused by such suspension. Persons appointed
by the State Board of Education to fill vacancies under this
Article shall hold office only during the time an officer is
suspended, not to exceed ninety days from the date of suspension.
At the expiration of such period, vacancies shall be filled in
the manner provided by law for the office. Persons appointed by
the State Board of Education to fill vacancies caused by suspension
shall be paid from funds of the district board of education.
Any person employed to fill the position of a superintendent
who has been removed by the State Board of Education under
Section 1 of this Article shall be employed by the district board
of education for a period not to exceed one year if the super
intendent has appealed to the courts and if the courts have not
taken final action.
Section 4. Section 156.110 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is repealed.
Section 5. The Attorney General, upon the written recom
mendation of either the Governor, the Auditor of Public Accounts,
the Superintendent of Public Instruction, or the State Board of
Education, shall institute the necessarv actions to recover school
funds, from any source, which he believes have been erroneously
or improperly allowed or paid to any person.
Section 6. The jurisdiction of all actions brought under the
provisions of this Article shall be vested in the circuit court of
the county in which the school district is located.
Section 7. Upon motion, without cause, of any party to
any action referred to in Section 6 of this Article, the judge shall
vacate the bench and the parties by agreement may elect one
of the attorneys of the court to preside on the trial or hear the
application or hold the court for the occasion. Any special judge
so selected shall have all the powers and be subject to all the
responsibilities of the regular judge of the court.
Section 8. If the parties cannot agree upon an attorney
as provided in Section 7 of this Article or if the regular judge

Chapter 244 869
determines that sufficient time is not available in which to
ascertain if the parties can agree upon a member of the bar to
act as judge, as provided in Section 7 of this Article, or if he
determines that it is impracticable to attempt to obtain agree
ment because of the large number of parties to the action to be
tried, or for any reason, the clerk shall at once certify the facts
to the Chief Justice of the Court of Appeals, who shall immedi
ately designate a regular circuit judge, if one be available, and,
if not, an attorney having the qualifications of a judge, to hold
the court or try the action.
Article II. FISCAL MANAGEMENT
Section 1. As used in Section 1 to Section 5 of this Article:
(a) "Committee" means the State Committee for School
District Audits;
(b) "State board" means the State Board of Education;
(c) "Board" means the board of education of a school
district;
(d) "Accountant" means a certified public accountant or
a public accountant registered with the State Board of Account
ancy.
Section 2. (1) There shall be a State Committee for
School District Audits comprised of the Governor, or a person
designated by him, the Attorney General and the Superintendent
of Public Instruction. The Governor, or the person designated
by him, shall be the chairman of the committee.
(2) The committee shall have the accounts of each board
audited not less than once every two fiscal years. The committee
also may, at any time, cause to be made a comprehensive and
complete audit of any board. Upon the written request of the
State Board, the Superintendent of Public Instruction, the Attor
ney General, the Auditor of Public Accounts or the Governor the
committee may cause the accounts of a board to be audited.
Each audit shall cover such period of time, and shall include
such auditing procedures and standards as the committee mav
designate.

870 Acts of the General Assembly
(3) Audits authorized under this section are in addition
to any audits contemplated under KRS 11.090, KRS 156.200,
or KRS Chapter 43.
(4) The actual expense of any audit authorized under this
section shall be borne equally by the district board of education
and by the committee from funds allocated to it.
Section 3. (1) The committee shall select, to make the
audit authorized under Section 2 of this Article, accountants who
have no personal interest in the financial affairs of the board
or of any of its officers or employees.
(2) Immediately upon completion of each audit, the
accountant shall prepare a report of his findings and recom
mendations in such form and in such detail as the committee
may prescribe. The report shall be to the committee and in such
number of copies as specified by the committee. The committee
shall furnish one copy to the State Board of Education, one
copy to the district board of education to which the report
pertains and one copy to the Superintendent of Public Instruction,
and one copy to the Auditor of Public Accounts. The district
board of education shall keep a copy of the report on file in
the office of the superintendent of schools of the district and the
report shall be open to inspection by any interested person
subject to reasonable rules as to time and place of inspection.
Section 4. (1) The accountant shall have access to and
may examine all books, accounts, reports, vouchers, correspond
ence files, records, money and property of any board. Every
officer or employee of any such board having such records or
property in his possession or under his control shall permit access
to and examination of them upon the request of the accountant.
(2) The committee may require information on oath from
any person touching any matters relative to any account that
the accountant is required to audit. The committee may admin
ister the oath, or have it done by any officer authorized to
administer an oath.
(3) The committee may issue process and compel the
attendance of witnesses before it, and administer oaths and com

Chapter 244 871
pel witnesses to testify in any of the investigations the accountant
is authorized to make.
Section 5. ( 1 ) Any officer or employee of a board or any
other person who prevents, attempts to prevent, or obstructs an
examination by the accountant made under Section 2 and 3 of
this Article is guilty of a high misdemeanor, and shall, upon
indictment and conviction in the circuit court of competent
jurisdiction, be fined Five Hundred Dollars.
(2) Any person who fails or refuses to permit the exami
nation provided for in Section 4 of this Article or who interferes
with such examination shall be fined not less than One Hundred
Dollars or imprisoned in the county jail for not less than one
month nor more than twelve months, or both. Each refusal
shall constitute a separate offense.
(3) Any person who has custody of any books, accounts,
reports, vouchers, correspondence, files, records, money and
property that the accountant is authorized to examine under
Section 2 and 4 of this Article who fails or refuses when called
upon by the committee for that purpose to permit the
accountant to inspect any of such materials shall upon con
viction in the circuit court of competent jurisdiction, be fined
not more than Five Hundred Dollars and be subject to removal
as provided by law.
(4) Any person who refuses to be sworn when required
by the Committee to be sworn for the purpose mentioned in
Section 4 (2) of this Article shall be fined not more than Five
Hundred Dollars.
(5) Any witness called by the Committee under Section
4 (3) of this Article who fails, without legal excuse, to attend
or testify shall be fined not more than Five Hundred Dollars.
Section 6. The State Board of Education, upon requisition
to the Division of Purchases in the Department of Finance, is
authorized to secure price contract agreements for the purchase
of supplies and equipment by district boards of education. The
board, by regulation, shall specify the particular types of sup
plies and equipment for which contracts shall be secured and
may revise such lists from time to time. The board, in consulta

872 Acts of the General Assembly
tion with the Division of Purchases, shall fix standards of quality
and quantity and shall develop standard specifications for such
supplies and equipment. The Division of Purchases shall enter
into price contract agreements under the provisions of KRS
42.060 and 42.070. Such contracts shall establish sources of
supply, maximum prices to be paid, and shall set forth the
length of time for which such contracts shall be valid, not to
exceed one year.
Section 7. The Superintendent of Public Instruction shall
furnish full information on established price contracts to each
district board of education. Any board of education may pur
chase supplies and equipment from the vendor to whom the
contract has been awarded, under the terms of the contract.
Any board of education may advertise for its own bids on supplies
and equipment which meet the specifications of the contracts
awarded bv the Division of Purchases. Any board of education,
after advertising for bids, may award contracts if the Superin
tendent of Public Instruction certifies that such a bid offers
supplies and equipment which meet the standards and specifica
tions fixed by the State Board of Education and that the bid
price is lower than that established by the price contract
agreement.
Article III. ORGANIZATION OF THE STATE BOARD
Section 1. Section 156.030 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
There shall be a State Board of Education composed of [The
Superintendent of Public Instruction and] seven lay members.
The board shall elect a chairman from among its members at
its first regular meeting in each fiscal year. [The Superintendent
of Public Instruction shall be chairman of the board. ] Beginning
with 1940 the Governor shall appoint two [lay] members every
year for a term of four years to take the place of the members
appointed under the provisions of Chapter 65 of the Acts of 1934
whose terms expire that year, except that every fourth year the
Governor shall appoint one [lay] member for a term of four
years to take the place of the [lay] member whose term expires
that year. The term of each [lay] member of the board shall

Chapter 244 873
begin on July 1 in the year in which the member is appointed.
In the case of a vacancy the Governor shall fill the vacancy for
the unexpired term. Members appointed to take the place of
members whose terms expire after the effective date of this
Article shall be appointed from the districts established by KRS
21.120 in the numerical order of the districts. The board shall
be a corporation by the name of the Kentucky State Board of
Education, with the usual corporate powers.
Section 2. Section 156.060 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
A majority of the board shall constitute a quorum for the
transaction of business. The board shall meet not less than
every three months and at such other times as it may be called
as provided in this section. Special meetings of the board may
be called by the chairman; and upon written request of two
members the chairman shall call a special meeting of the board
to be held not later than twenty days following receipt of the
written request. The chairman shall give notice through the
secretary by registered mail to each member of the board at least
ten days prior to the time of any meeting, unless all members
of the board waive notice in writing. The offices of the board
shall be at the seat of government in the Department of Educa
tion and shall be provided by the state. Members of the board
may receive a per diem of twenty-five [ten] dollars for each
regular or special meeting attended and actual expenses for at
tending meetings, and may be reimbursed for other actual and
necessary expenditures incurred [in the state] in the perform
ance of their duties authorized by the board. The per diem
and expenses shall be paid out of the appropriation for the State
Board of Education. [However no member of said board shall
receive more than one hundred dollars per annum for per diem
under the provisions of this section.]
Section 3. Section 156.040 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
The members of the State Board of Education shall have
the same qualifications as school board members, except that
members of the State Board of Education shall be at least thirty

874 Acts of the General Assembly
years of age. Appointments shall be made without reference
to [place of residence in the state,] occupation, political affilia
tion, or similar considerations. No member at the time of his
appointment or during the term of his service shall be engaged
as a professional educator.
Section 4. This Article III shall become effective on the
first Monday in January, 1964, except that Section 2 of Article
III shall become effective July 1, 1962. This section is not
intended to prevent the provisions of other articles of this Act
that contains no effective date clause from becoming effective
at an earlier time.
Article IV. DISCRIMINATION AGAINST TEACHERS AND
OTHER SCHOOL EMPLOYEES
Section 1. No teacher or employee of any district board
of education shall be appointed or promoted to, or demoted or
dismissed from, any position or in any way favored or discrimin
ated against with respect to employment because of his political
or religious opinions or affiliations or ethnic origin.
Section 2. No person shall use or promise to use, directly
or indirectly, any official authority or influence, whether possessed
or anticipated, to secure or attempt to secure for any person an
appointment or advantage in appointment to a position as teacher
or employee of any district board of education, or an increase in
pay or other advantage in employment in any such position, for
the purpose of influencing the vote or political action of any
person.
Section 3. (1) Any person who willfully violates any pro
vision of Section 1 or 2 of this Article shall be confined in jail for
not less than thirty days nor more than six months.
(2) Any teacher or employee of a district board of educa
tion who willfully violates any provision of Section 1 or 2 of this
Article shall be ineligible for employment in the common schools
for a period of five years.

Chapter 244 875
Article V. TEACHERS RANK
Section 157.390 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is amended
to read as follows:
(1) (a) The Superintendent of Public Instruction, under
regulations of the State Board of Education, shall classify teachers
in rank as follows:
Rank I. Those holding regular certificates and who have
a master's degree and who have earned [twenty-four] thirty
semester hours of additional approved graduate work, and those
teachers who, as of September 1, 1962, were included in Rank I,
having earned twenty-four semester hours of additional approved
graduate work.
Rank II. Those holding regular certificates and who have
a master's degree or its equivalent.
Rank III. Those holding regular certificates and who have
an approved four-year college degree or the equivalent.
Rank IV. Those holding certificates and who have ninety-
six to one hundred and twenty-eight semester hours of approved
college training or the equivalent; provided, however, that per
sons holding emergency certificates shall not be classified higher
than this rank.
Rank V. Those holding certificates and who have sixty-
four to ninety-five semester hours of approved college training
or the equivalent.
Rank VI. Those holding certificates and who have thirty-
two to sixty-three semester hours of approved college training
or the equivalent; provided, however, that effective July 1, 1965,
no teacher in Rank VI shall be included in calculating the amount
to be included in the foundation program of a district for teach
ers' salaries, unless the teacher holds a life certificate.
Rank VII. Those holding certificates and who have fewer
than thirty-two semester hours of approved college training or
the equivalent; provided, however, that effective July 1, 1964,
no teacher in Rank VII shall be included in calculating the
amount to be included in the foundation program of a district
for teachers' salaries.

876 Acts of the General Assembly
(b) In determining ranks, the Superintendent of Public
Instruction, under regulations of the State Board of Education,
shall classify teachers who hold valid certificates in the respective
ranks according to approved college semester hours of credit.
The Superintendent of Public Instruction, in defining preparation
for certain types of vocational teachers as equivalent to college
training shall give consideration to apprenticeship training and
industrial experience.
(2) [The amount to be included in the 1960-61 school
year in the foundation program of a district for teachers' salaries
shall be determined by multiplying the number of teachers in
each rank, not to exceed the number of classroom units allowed,
by the amount set forth in the following schedule for each rank:
Rank I $4400
Rank II 4100
Rank III 3800
Rank IV 2800
Rank V 2500
Rank VI 2100
Rank VII 1800]
Beginning with the school year 1961-62, the amount to be
included in the foundation program of a district for teachers'
salaries shall be determined by multiplying the number of teach
ers in each rank, not to exceed the number of classroom units
allowed, by the amount set forth in the following allotment
schedule for each rank:
Rank I $4600
Rank II 4300
Rank III 4000
Rank IV 2900
Rank V 2600
Rank VI 2200
Rank VII 1900
Provided, the amount to be included for vocational units, super
visory units, and units for administrators, directors of pupil per
sonnel and special instructional services shall be increased pro
portionately if the personnel for such units are employed for
longer than the regular school term and such employment is ap

Chapteb 244 877
proved by the Superintendent of Public Instruction under regula
tions of the State Board of Education. Where the actual number
of teachers employed by the district varies from the number
allowed under the provisions of KRS 157.310 to 157.440, the num
ber to be included in each rank shall be in the same ratio that
the number of teachers actually in each rank is to the total num
ber of teachers employed.
(3) The amount to be included in the foundation program
for other current expenses shall be determined by multiplying the
number of classroom units by nine hundred dollars.
(4) The amount to be included in the foundation program
for capital outlay shall be determined by multiplying the number
of classroom units by six hundred dollars.
(5) The amount to be included in the foundation program
of each district for transportation shall be determined by mul
tiplying the aggregate attendance of transported children by the
allowable cost per pupil per day for that district determined in
accordance with the provisions of KRS 157.370.
(6) The total cost of the foundation program for each dis
trict shall be the sum of the allotments in subsections (2), (3),
(4), and (5) of this section.
Article VI. SCHOLARSHIPS
Section 1. There is hereby established a Teacher Education
Scholarship Fund. Upon the recommendation of the Superin
tendent of Public Instruction the State Board of Education shall
appoint a Teacher Education Scholarship Board, to consist of five
persons who are professionally engaged in Teacher education.
Members shall be appointed as soon as practicable after the
effective date of this Article and shall serve until July 1, 1964.
The Scholarship Board shall elect its own chairman and shall
meet at such times and places as it deems proper. Members
shall serve without pay, but may be reimbursed for actual and
necessary expenses incurred in the performance of their duties.
Section 2. The Teacher Education Scholarship Board shall
receive and consider applications for scholarships from public

878 Acts of the General Assembly
school teachers who are teaching science, mathematics, or a
foreign language, or who are school librarians, and who do not
have a minor in the subject they are teaching. A minor shall
consist of eighteen semester hours of work planned by the college
and approved by the State Department of Education, unless
otherwise specified by regulation of the State Board of Education.
Each applicant shall furnish a statement from the superintendent
of the district in which he is teaching that the superintendent
plans to assign the applicant to teach the same subject the next
school year. The Scholarship Board shall make a careful and
full investigation of the ability, character and qualifications of
each applicant. It may personally examine each applicant and
shall require each applicant to take a written examination to be
administered by the Scholarship Board.
Section 3. (1) The Teacher Education Scholarship Board
shall award scholarships not to exceed two hundred thousand
dollars for the 1962-64 biennum to applicants whom the Board
deems qualified. Scholarships shall be awarded solely for sum
mer work at any accredited college or institution of the applicant's
choice within the Commonwealth. Each scholarship shall con
sist of two hundred dollars, payable at the end of the summer
term. The scholarship shall be paid only if the applicant suc
cessfully completes at least six semester hours of academic courses
in the subject for which the scholarship was granted. Each per
son to whom a scholarship is granted shall take at least six semes
ter hours of courses approved by the college in the subject for
which the scholarship was granted and may take three additional
semester hours in that subject or another subject which he is
teaching and in which he does not have a minor.
( 2 ) In awarding scholarships for the second year, the Board
shall give priority to persons who were not awarded a scholarship
the first year, provided, however, that an applicant may be
awarded a scholarship the second year if he successfully com
pleted six semester hours the first year and is otherwise qualified
under the terms of this Article.
Section 4. ( 1 ) To be eligible for a scholarship, an applicant
shall contract in writing with the State Board of Education that
he will accept employment during the next school year with the

Chapter 244 879
district board of education :by which he is employed at the time
of application. If the recipient of a scholarship fails to fulfill
his obligations under the contract, the entire amount of scholar
ship benefits received shall become due and payable.
( 2 ) Upon recommendation of the State Board of Education,
the Attorney General shall institute proceedings for the purpose
of recovering any amount due the Commonwealth.
Section 5. This Article shall become effective at any time
to the extent that funds become available from any unexpended
portions of the Foundation Program Fund.
Article VII. SCHOOL PROGRAMS
Section 1. Section 158.060 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
Twenty school days, or days in which teachers are actually
employed in the schoolroom, shall constitute a school month in
the common schools. The legal holidays designated by the State
Board of Education to be observed shall be counted as school
days. No teacher shall teach on Saturdays except in cases of
emergency and then only upon authorization of the State Board
of Education. Six hours of actual school work shall constitute
a school day. The daily session, including recesses and inter
mission, shall not exceed nine hours. A pupil who is absent,
excused or dismissed from any regularly scheduled class to attend
not more than four field trips in a school term may be considered
in attendance on those days, if the field trip is part of a regularly
scheduled class; provided, however, that upon application of any
district superintendent, the Superintendent of Public Instruction
or the State Board of Education may, if sufficient cause be shown,
grant authority to use an additional day or days for special field
trips or exceptional events.
Section 2. Section 158.070 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
(1) The minimum school term shall be 185 days, [nine
months.] The Superintendent of Public Instruction, upon the
approval of the State Board of Education may extend the mini

880 Acts of the General Assembly
mum term to ten months when the resources of the school fund
or contributions by local taxation justify the extension, but no
extension shall be made if it reduces the salaries of the teachers
below the minimum fixed by law. A board of education may
extend its terms beyond the minimum term set by the Superin
tendent of Public Instruction.
(2) The board of education of any school district may use
no more than four days of the minimum school term for holidays,
three days for in-service training and professional work, and three
days for attendance at state and district meetings of professional
educational associations. The State Board of Education, upon
recommendation of the Superintendent of Public Instruction, shall
adopt regulations governing the use of school days.
[(2) If extending the term to the minimum provided in
subsection ( 1 ) of this section will reduce the salaries of teachers
in districts because of extending the terms of grades which were
operated for less than nine months during the school year ending
June 30, 1952, boards of education may operate such grades for
less than nine months for such a term as will be approved by the
Superintendent of Public Instruction as meeting the requirements
of this section.]
Approved March 22, 1962

CHAPTER 248
(H. B. 219)
AN ACT relating to livestock disease control.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 257.010 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
As used in this chapter, unless the context requires otherwise:
(1) "Board" means the Board of Agriculture;
(2) "Commissioner" means the Commissioner of Agricul
ture, Labor and Statistics;
(3) "Communicable disease" includes hog cholera, brucel
losis, leptospirosis, anthrax, black leg, catarrhal influenza of cattle,
contagious pleuro-pneumonia, foot and mouth disease or aphthous
fever, glanders, hemmonhagic septicaemia, [hog cholera,] mal-
adie du coit or dourine, mange of cattle, necrobacillosis and foot
rot in sheep, hydrophobia, rinderpest, scabies in cattle, Texas tick

884 Acts of the General Assembly
or southern cattle fever, tuberculosis, or any other disease pro
claimed by the board to be of a transmissible character;
(4) "Experiment station" means the Agricultural Experi
ment Station;
(5) "Owner" means any person owning or leasing from
another, or having in charge any domestic animal; and
( 6 ) "Premises" means any portion of land, or any structure
erected on land, and any vehicle or vessel used in the transporta
tion of passengers, goods or animals.
Section 2. Section 246.210 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
(1) The department or its agents shall have free access
within reasonable hours to any farm, orchard, garden, elevator,
warehouse, building, cellar, freight or express office or car, freight
yard, vehicle, vessel, boat, container or any other place which,
for probable cause, it may be necessary or desirable for the agents
to enter in order to enforce a quarantine against the European
corn borer ( Pyrausta nubilaslis ) .
( 2 ) The board or any of its members or representatives may
after reasonable notice enter any premises for the purpose of in
specting or testing livestock to determine the existence of, or to
combat in any way, communicable diseases, [making the necessary
inspections or in any way combating communicable diseases
among livestock.] The owner of the livestock to be tested or in
spected shall, after reasonable notice confine and present such
livestock to the representatives of the board. Whenever the board
or any of its representatives determine through inspection or test
ing that any livestock is infected with a communicable disease
they may enter any premises after reasonable notice and remove
therefrom the diseased livestock after an appraisal has been made
as provided in KRS 257.110, and have such livestock destroyed or
slaughtered and the owner indemnified as provided in KRS
257.120 to KRS 257.150. When necessary the board or any of its
members or representatives may call upon peace officers for as
sistance. Such officers shall render assistance when ordered to
do so by the board or any of its members, representatives, agents
or employees.

Chapter 248 885
Section 3. Section 246.220 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
(1) No person shall in any way hinder, refuse or defeat
entrance or inspection by an agent of the department who is
performing the duties of his office for the purpose of combating
the European corn borer under (1) of KRS 246.210.
(2) No person shall interfere in any wav with the board
or any of its members or representatives in the inspection of

on any premises when the object of the inspection or testing is to
determine the existence of or to combat in any way, communicable
disease in livestock, [the combating of livestock diseases.] No
person shall interfere in any way with the board or any of its

of livestock found to be infected with a communicable disease.
(3) No person shall hinder the efforts of the State Ento
mologist or his agents in combating Japanese beetles under KRS
246.210 and 249.200.
(4) No person shall hinder the Entomologist in the perform
ance of any of his duties as State Inspector of Apiaries.
(5) No person shall interfere in any way with inspectors
or assistants while they are discharging their duties under KRS
250.020 to 250.170.
(6) No person shall interfere with or attempt to hinder any

under KRS 248.250.
(7) No person shall hinder, prevent or attempt to prevent

dollars for each of the fiscal years 1962-63 and 1963-64.
Approved March 22, 1962
