CHAPTER 289
(H. B. 514)
AN ACT relating to podiatry.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 311.380 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
As used in KRS 311.390 to 311.520, unless the context other
wise requires:
(1) "Board" means the State Board of Podiatry [Chirop
ody].
(2) "Podiatry" ["Chiropody"] means the diagnosis, and
the [local,] medical, mechanical and surgical treatment, of ail
ments of the human foot, and massage and the use of drugs in
connection therewith, except amputation of the foot or toes, or
the use of anesthetics other than local, or the use of drugs or medi
cine other than local or the use of narcotic drugs other than those
which are designated by regulation of the State Board of Health
for use by podiatrists in their practice. [; provided, however, that
in connection with such treatment analgesics, antipyretics, and
codeine may be used orally and antibiotics or sulfanilamide agents
may be used through local application.]
(3) "Podiatrist" ["Chiropodist"] means one who practices
podiatry [chiropody].

Chapter 289 1009
Section 2. Section 311.400 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
No person shall profess to be a podiatrist [chiropodist], or
practice or assume the duties incident to podiatry [chiropody] in
this state, or use the title "podiatrist" ["chiropodist"] or any words
or letters which designate or tend to designate to the public that
the person is a podiatrist [chiropodist] , unless he holds a valid,
current license from the board, registered in the county in which
the holder is engaged in such practice. Any person who violates
this section shall forfeit any right to compensation for services
rendered.
Section 3. Section 311.410 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
(1) There is hereby established in the Department of
Health a State Board of Podiatry [Chiropody] which shall con
sist of five practicing podiatrists [chiropodists], each appointed
by the Governor. All appointments, including appointments to
fill vacancies, shall be made from lists of three names for each
position submitted by the Kentucky Podiatry Association [of
Chiropodists]. Any vacancy shall be filled for the unexpired
term by the Governor, as provided in the original appointment.
(2) A person to be eligible for appointment as a member
of the [said] board shall be at least twenty-one years of age, of
good moral character, a resident of this state, and a licensed and
practicing podiatrist [chiropodist] in this state for at least five
consecutive years next preceding the date of his appointment.
No member of the board shall be a stockholder, officer or mem
ber of the faculty or board of trustees of any school, college or
institution of podiatry or chiropody.
[ ( 3 ) The State Board of Chiropody, established by Chapter
197 of the Acts of 1952, is hereby abolished, and all rights, func
tions, powers and duties of said board and of the State Board of
Health under said Act are hereby transferred to and vested in
the State Board of Chiropody established by this section, upon
June 17, 1954. All records, files and moneys shall be turned over
to the State Board of Chiropody established by this section upon
June 17, 1954.]

1010 Acts of the General Assembly
(3) [(4)] The terms of office of the five members first ap
pointed shall be one, two, three, four and five years, respectively,
and the respective terms of the first members shall be designated
by the Governor at the time of their appointment. Upon the
expiration of the respective terms of the members first appointed,
the term of each successor shall be five years, or until his suc
cessor shall be appointed and qualified.
(4) [ (5) ] The board shall elect one of its members as pres
ident. The Commissioner of Health shall be secretary of the
board. He may appoint an assistant secretary and delegate to him
any or all of his powers and duties under KRS 311.390 to
311.520. The secretary may, subject to approval by the board,
fix the compensation of the assistant secretary, and employ and
fix the compensation of all personnel required for the administra
tion of KRS 311.390 to 311.520. The board may [, and one of its
members as secretary. The board is authorized to fix the com
pensation of the secretary and to employ and fix the compensation
of all personnel required by it for the administration of KRS
311.390 to 311.520. The board shall have power to] make all
rules and regulations, not inconsistent with KRS 311.390 to KRS
311.520, as may be necessary to implement and carry out the pro
visions and purposes of KRS 311.390 to 311.520.
(5) [(6)] The board shall hold meetings at least twice a
year and as frequently as it deems necessary at such times and
places as the board may designate. A majority of the members
shall constitute a quorum.
(6) [(7)] The board may sue and be sued in its own
name.
Section 4. The State Board of Chiropody, established by
chapter 219 of the Acts of 1954 is abolished, and all rights, func
tions, powers and duties of such board under such Act are trans
ferred to and vested in the State Board of Podiatry established by
KRS 311.410, as amended by section 3 of this Act. All records,
files and moneys of the State Board of Chiropody shall be turned
over to the State Board of Podiatry on the effective date of this
Act.
Section 5. Section 311.420 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:

Chapter 289 1011
( 1 ) The board shall conduct examinations at least twice a
year at such times and places as the board deems convenient for
applicants for licenses to practice podiatry [chiropody] in this
state, and shall admit to examination any applicant who:
(a) Is at least twenty-one years of age;
(b) Is a citizen of the United States and a resident of the
State of Kentucky;
(c) Is of good moral character and temperate habits; and
(d) Has completed a course in and graduated from a
school or college of podiatry or chiropody approved by the board.
( 2 ) The examination shall embrace the subjects of anatomy,
physiology, chemistry, bacteriology, histology, pathology, der
matology, pedic-surgery, physio-therapy, diagnosis and treatment,
materia medica and therapeutics, and other subjects as these
subjects relate to antiseptics and anaesthetics and clinical podiatry
or chiropody, but the examination shall be so limited in its scope
as to cover only the minimum requirements for podiatry or chir
opody educations.
(3) Applicants for examination shall present their creden
tials to the board by mail upon forms furnished by the board, and
shall receive due notice of the place and date of the examination.
Each application shall contain proof of the qualifications re
quired of the applicant, shall be verified by the applicant under
oath, and shall be accompanied by an examination fee of fifty
dollars.
( 4 ) The board shall issue a license to any person who com
plies with the provisions of KRS 311.380 to 311.520 and who
satisfactorily passes the examination.
(5) The members of the board shall receive no compensa
tion for their services, but shall be paid their traveling and other
necessary expenses while employed upon the business of the
board.
Section 6. Section 311.440 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
Every person licensed to practice podiatry [chiropody] shall,
within thirty days after issuance, file his license with the county

1012 Acts of the General Assembly
clerk of each county in which he desires to engage in the practice
of podiatry [chiropody] , and shall pay the county clerk a record
ing fee of fifty cents. The clerk shall keep a record of podiatrists'
[chiropodists'] licenses in a book in his office.
Section 7. Section 311.450 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
(1) Every license issued for the practice of podiatry [chir
opody] shall expire on June 30 following the date of issuance
unless sooner revoked and cancelled.
(2) On or before June 1 of each year, the board shall send
notices to all licensed podiatrists [chiropodists] in this state, at
their last known addresses, advising them that the annual license
renewal fee is due on July 1 of each year. Every registered
podiatrist [chiropodist] shall renew his license on or before July
1 of each year by the payment to the board of an annual license
renewal fee of ten dollars. If such renewal fee is not paid on or
before July 1, the board shall notify the delinquent licensee by
mail at his last known address that such fee is due and unpaid
and that a delinquent penalty of twenty-five dollars is assessed,
in addition to the renewal fee of ten dollars, and must be paid on
or before January 1. If such fees and penalties are not paid by
January 1, it shall be the duty of the board to suspend or revoke
the [said] license for non-payment of the annual renewal and de
linquent fees for the current year.
(3) All fees collected under the provisions of KRS 311.380
to 311.520, or the rules and regulations adopted pursuant thereto,
shall be paid into the State Treasury, and credited to a trust and
agency fund to be used in defraying the costs and expenses in
the administration of KRS 311.380 to 311.520 including, but not
limited to, salaries and necessary travel expenses. No part of
this fund shall revert to the general funds of this Commonwealth.
Section 8. Section 311.460 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
(1) No person shall offer to practice podiatry [chiropody]
under the name of any company. Any person practicing or offer
ing to practice podiatry [chiropody] shall practice under his
own name only, as his name appears in his license.

Chapter 289 1013
(2) No person practicing or offering to practice podiatry
[chiropody] shall use any title or abbreviation thereof except
the designation "Podiatrist." ["Chiropodist."] The terms "Foot
Doctor" and Chiropodist, or either of them, [Specialist"] may be
used in conjunction with the title "Podiatrist," ["Chiropodist,"]
but not as a substitute therefor and not alone.
Section 9. Section 311.470 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
(1) Any person who practices podiatry [chiropody] shall
display his license, together with the annual license renewal
certificates, in a conspicuous place in the office in which he
practices.
(2) No person practicing or offering to practice podiatry
[chiropody] shall in any manner advertise the prices charged
for his services; nor shall he advertise in newspapers, in periodi
cals, by handbills or circulars, or in bold-face type in any printed
matter, or by the use of any form of display sign; except that he
may, upon opening an office or upon change of address, mail or
publish announcements to that effect provided such announce
ments shall not appear more than three times in public print, and
the form of such announcement shall be determined by the board.
Section 10. Section 311.480 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
The board may refuse to license or renew, or may suspend
or revoke the license of any podiatrist [chiropodist] , upon proof
that he:
( 1 ) Has been convicted of a felony;
( 2 ) Has been convicted of a misdemeanor involving [guilty
of] moral turpitude or conduct likely to deceive or defraud the
public;
(3) Has employed, hired, procured or induced a person
not licensed to practice podiatry [chiropody] in this state [to]
so to practice;
(4) Has aided or abetted in the practice of podiatry
[chiropody] a person not licensed to practice podiatry [chir
opody] in this state;

1014 Acts of the General Assembly
(5) Has been granted a license upon a mistake of a ma
terial fact; or
(6) Has violated any provision of KRS 311.380 to 311.520.
Section 11. Chapter 5, Acts of 1952, and Sections 311.415
and 311.430 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes are repealed.
Approved March 22, 1962
CHAPTER 290
(H. B. 555)
AN ACT relating to revenue and taxation.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. A new section of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is created to read as follows:
In the case of any corporation transacting business both
within and without this state, the Department of Revenue may
authorize or require such corporation to determine its net income
within this state by means of separate accounting, provided (a)
that the net business income allocable to Kentucky under the
formula prescribed by KRS 141.120 is substantially greater or
lesser than the actual amount of net business income attributable
to this state and (b) that its accounting records clearly reflect
such net income on a separate accounting basis. Any taxpayer
aggrieved by the decision of the department may appeal to the
Kentucky Tax Commission and the Franklin Circuit Court in
the manner and subject to the conditions provided in KRS 131.110
and 131.120. This section shall apply to all taxable years com
mencing on or after January 1, 1962.
Approved March 22, 1962

CHAPTER 293
(H. R. 68)
A CONCURRENT RESOLUTION relating to Zollicoffer Park, the site
of the Battle of Mill Springs.
WHEREAS, the Battle of Mill Springs was fought one hundred years
ago as of January 19, 1962, and was the first decisive and significant
battle of the War Between the States and was the turning point of
that war; and

Chapter 293 1017
WHEREAS, on that spot General Felix K. Zollicoffer, soldier of the
Confederate Army, fell while engaged in mortal combat; and was
the first general officer to fall in the Civil War; and
WHEREAS, Lieutenants Bailey Peyton, Jr., and H. M. R. Pogg and
more than one hundred and fifty of their Confederate associates in
the Battle of Fishing Creek, fell also with General Zollicoffer for
right as they saw it; and
WHEREAS, all the aforementioned are a part of that great host who
crowned southern manhood with glorious immortality and gave
their lives, the noblest of all offerings, as duty called; and
WHEREAS, history will ever point in reverence and pride to the sacred
place where those heroes so peacefully sleep; and
WHEREAS, they now rest beneath a mound in a sleep that knows no
awakening, one hundred and fifty strong, together in eternal rest as
they were in battle, all valiant soldiers of the Confederacy from
Alabama, Mississippi and Tennessee; and
WHEREAS, mankind knows not who they were, but rather the whole
world knows what they were, an intrepid band of brave men in
the glory and splendor of their youth, who died far from home and
loved ones, but who fill heroes' graves about which the aura of
glory eternally shines; and
WHEREAS, that mound where the flower of southern manhood rests,
is devoid of any marker, identification, crude stone or polished
marble shaft to purport to the world their dedication, their honor,
their valor, their glory, and their supreme sacrifice; and
WHEREAS, for one hundred years those noble men have slept in a
common grave with naught but the angels of mercy to weep for
them; and
WHEREAS, the eternal stars in their silent orbits keep constant vigil,
the angels of heaven look down and weep and Almighty God watches
over these men, yet mortal man has not raised a hand to recognize,
honor, or respect that hallowed spot,
NOW, THEREFORE,
Be it resolved by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. That the Commonwealth of Kentucky, through
its appropriate agencies, confer official recognition upon Zollicof
fer Park.
Section 2. That action be taken to encourage the preser
vation of that historical site.

1018 Acts of the General Assembly
Section 3. That a memorial of some substantial substance
be erected therein in order to perpetuate the dignity and honor
of those who fell in battle at that site.
Section 4. That the Clerk of the House of Representatives
transmit copies of this Resolution to the Governor of the Com
monwealth, the Kentucky Historical Society, and the Department
of Public Information.
Approved March 22, 1962

CHAPTER 296
(S. B. 86)
AN ACT relating to independent business schools.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. As used in this Act, unless the context other
wise requires:
(1) "Independent business school," "business school" or

1020 Acts of the General Assembly
"school" means an educational institution privately owned and
operated by an individual, partnership, association or corporation
offering business courses through residence, extension or corre
spondence for which tuition is charged, in such subjects as: type
writing, shorthand (manuel or machine), filing and indexing,
receptionist's duties, key-punch, teletype, penmanship, bookkeep
ing, accounting, office machines, business arithmetic, English,
business letter writing, salesmanship, personality development,
leadership training, real estate, insurance, traffic management,
business psychology, economics, business management, charm
and modeling, air line training and other related subjects of
similar character.
(2) "Board" means the State Board of Business Schools.
( 3 ) "Agent" means any person employed by a school to act
as agent, solicitor, broker or independent contractor to procure
students for such school by solicitation of enrollment in any form
made at any place other than the main office or principal place of
business of the school.
(4) "Superintendent" means superintendent of public in
struction.
Section 2. This Act shall not apply to any school offering a
four year college course recognized by the State Department of
Education nor shall it apply to any religious and nonprofit in
dependent business school exempt from taxation under the laws of
this state, or which is subject to the provisions of KBS 156.160.
Section 3. (1) No person shall conduct, operate, main
tain or establish an independent business school or use the term
"independent business school," "business college," or any words
which designate or tend to designate to the public that the op
erator of such institution is qualified to conduct, operate and
maintain an independent business school, unless he holds a
valid current license from the board.
(2) No person shall in any way solicit any person or
group of persons in this state to enroll at or attend any inde
pendent business school unless such person holds a valid license
as agent of the school for which he is soliciting.

Chapter 296 1021
Section 4. ( 1 ) There is hereby established a State Board
of Business Schools which shall consist of the Superintendent of
Public Instruction and six persons to be appointed by the Gov
ernor. Four members of the board shall be persons who, for at
least five years next preceding the date of their appointment,
shall have occupied executive or managerial positions with an
independent business school in Kentucky. These four appoint
ments shall be made from a list of two names for each position
submitted by the Kentucky Association of Independent Business
Colleges. Two members of the board shall be persons occupied
in commerce or industry in Kentucky.
(2) Upon expiration of each of the respective terms of the
members first appointed, the term of each successor shall be
four years or until his successor shall be appointed and qualified.
(3) The Superintendent of Public Instruction shall serve
as ex officio president of the board and the members of the
board shall elect one of their number as secretary. The board is
authorized to fix the compensation of the secretary and to employ
and fix the compensation of all personnel required by it for the
administration of the provisions of this Act. The board shall have
the power to make all rules and regulations, consistent with the
provisions of this Act, as may be necessary to carry out the pro
visions and purposes of this Act.
(4) The board shall hold meetings at least twice a year
and as frequently as it deems necessary at such times and places
as the board may designate and the majority of the members
shall constitute a quorum.
( 5 ) The board may sue and be sued in its own name.
(6) The members of the board, except the secretary, shall
receive no compensation for their services but may be paid their
travel and other expenses while employed upon the business of
the board.
(7) The board shall administer the provisions of law per
taining to the conduct, operation, maintenance and establish
ment of independent business schools, and the activities of agents
thereof when acting as such.

1022 Acts of the General Assembly
Section 5. (1) Any person seeking a license to conduct,
operate, maintain or establish an independent business school
shall make verified application to the board on forms prepared
and furnished by the board setting forth the following informa
tion:
(a) The title or name of the school, together with the names
of the owner or owners, and if the owner be a corporation the
names and addresses of the officers thereof;
(b) The specific courses and fields of instruction which
will be offered;
( c ) The place or places where such instruction will be given
and a description of the physical and sanitary facilities thereof;
(d) A specific listing of the equipment available for in
struction in each course; and
(e) The education and teaching qualifications of the in
structors and supervisor.
(2) Each initial application for a license shall be accom
panied by a filing fee of fifty dollars, no part of which shall be
refunded if a license is denied or if the application is withdrawn.
(3) Upon receipt of an application for a license accom
panied by the filing fee, the board, or any of its members, shall
conduct such an investigation as it or they deem reasonable and
necessary and upon completion of such investigation to the satis
faction of the members of the board, the license shall be granted or
denied by the board pursuant to Section 8 of this Act.
(4) Any person seeking a license to act as agent for an
independent business school shall make verified application to
the board on forms prepared and furnished by the board setting
forth the following information:
(a) The name and permanent address of the applicant,
together with his date of birth and the name of the school for
which an agent's license is sought;
(b) A verified statement setting out evidence of the hon
esty, truthfulness and integrity of the applicant;
( c ) The verified recommnedation of two reputable persons
certifying that the applicant is truthful, honest and of good re
pute and that a license as agent should be granted him.

Chapter 296 1023
(5) Each initial application for an agent's license shall be
accompanied by a filing fee of twenty-five dollars, no part of
which shall be refunded if a license is denied or if the application
is withdrawn.
(6) Upon receipt of an application for an agent's license
accompanied by the filing fee, the board, or any of its members,
may conduct such an investigation as it or they deem reasonable
and necessary, and upon completion of such investigation, if any
be made, the license shall be granted or denied by the board
pursuant to Section 8 of this Act.
(7) Every agent shall obtain a separate license for each
school which he represents as agent, provided, however, that
the filing fee for each license applied for after the first applica
tion shall be ten dollars.
(8) Nothing in this section shall be construed to require
an employe or officer of a licensed independent business school
to hold an agent's license merely because such employe or of
ficer accepts the applications and enrollments at the main office
or principal place of business of the independent business school.
Section 6. The board shall maintain a listing of the name
and address of every person licensed under this Act and shall
publish such listing at least once a year by mailing such listing
to each independent business college licensed under this Act.
Changes in the listing may be published in the same manner at
such times as the board in its discretion may determine.
Section 7. (1) Every license issued for the operation of
an independent business school shall expire on September 30th
following the date of issuance unless sooner revoked and can
celled.
( 2 ) Between August 15 and September 1 of each year, the
board shall send notices to all licensed independent business
schools in this state advising them that the annual license renewal
fee is due on October 1 of each year. Every licensed independent
business school shall renew its license between September 1 and
October 1 of each year by the payment to the board of an annual
license renewal fee of twenty-five dollars. If such renewal fee is

1024 Acts of the General Assembly
not paid on or before October 1, the board shall notify the de
linquent licensee by registered or certified mail that such fee
is due and unpaid and that a delinquent penalty of fifty dollars
is assessed in addition to the renewal fee of twenty-five dollars
and must be paid on or before January 1. If such fees and pen
alties are not paid by January 1 the board shall suspend or revoke
said license for non-payment of annual renewal and delinquent
fees for the current year.
(3) Every license issued to an agent shall expire on Sep
tember 30 following the date of issuance unless sooner revoked
or cancelled.
(4) Between August 15 and September 1 of each year, the
board shall send notices to all licensed agents advising them that
the annual license renewal fee is due on October 1 of each year.
Every licensed agent shall renew his license between September 1
and October 1 of each year by the payment to the board of an
annual license renewal fee of five dollars for each license held.
If such renewal fee or fees are not paid on or before October 1,
the board shall notify the delinquent licensee by certified or reg
istered mail that such fee or fees are due and unpaid and that a
delinquent penalty of ten dollars for each license held is assessed
in addition to the renewal fee of five dollars and must be paid
on or before January 1. If such fees and penalties are not paid
by January 1, the board shall revoke said license or licenses for
nonpayment of annual renewal and delinquent fees for the current
year.
(5) All fees collected under the provisions of this Act or the
rules and regulations adopted pursuant to this Act shall be paid
into the State Treasury and credited to a trust and agency fund
to be used in defraying the cost and expenses of the administra
tion of this Act, including, but not limited to, salaries and neces
sary expenses. No part of this fund shall revert to the general funds
of the Commonwealth.
Section 8. ( 1 ) The board shall refuse to license or renew
the license or shall suspend or revoke the license of any inde
pendent business school on proof that the individual applicant
or holder has been convicted of a felony, a misdemeanor involving

Chapter 296 1025
moral turpitude or has been guilty of conduct likely to deceive
or defraud the public, or that the applicant or holder:
(a) has employed, hired, procured, or induced a person
not licensed to act as agent for such independent business school;
or
(b) has been granted a license upon mistake of a material
fact; or
(c) has furnished false, misleading or incomplete informa
tion to the board; or
(d) has presented information to prospective students re
lating to the school or to employment opportunities after enter
ing into or completing courses offered by the school which was
false, misleading or fraudulent; or
(e) has failed to provide or maintain premises or equip
ment in a safe and sanitary condition to such an extent that such
failure constitutes a violation of law; or
(f) has failed to provide and maintain financial resources
adequate for the satisfactory conduct of the courses of instruction
offered; or
(g) has failed to retain a sufficient and qualified staff of
instructors and administrative personnel; or
( h ) has proposed to conduct or has conducted instruction
in a course or field not approved by the board for that particular
school.
(2) The board shall refuse to license or renew the license
or shall suspend or revoke the license of any applicant or agent on
proof that the individual applicant or holder:
( a ) has been convicted of a felony, a misdemeanor involving
moral turpitude or has been guilty of conduct likely to deceive
or defraud the public; or
(b) has employed, hired, procured or induced an un
licensed person to act as agent for such independent business
school; or
(c) has been granted a license upon mistake of a material
fact; or
(d) has furnished false, misleading or incomplete informa
tion to the board; or

1026 Acts of the General Assembly
(e) has presented information to prospective students re
lating to the school or to employment opportunities after entering
into or completing the course offered by the school which is false,
misleading or fraudulent.
Section 9. (1) The board may suspend or revoke a li
cense or may refuse to renew a license only after a hearing of
which the holder of the license has been given adequate notice
by mailing a copy of the notice by certified or registered mail
at least twenty days prior to the hearing. At the hearing, the board
and the holder of the license shall have the benefit of counsel.
(2) The board may administer oaths, take the depositions
of witnesses as in civil cases and compel them to attend before
it in person as in civil cases by subpoena issued in the name of
the Commonwealth and over the signature of the president of
the board.
(3) Proceedings for the suspension or revocation of a li
cense may be instituted by any individual or organization filing a
verified complaint with the secretary of the board or such pro
ceedings may be instituted by the board acting upon its own
initiative or by the president or secretary of the board acting on
behalf of the board.
(4) The board may institute in its own name proceedings
to restrain and enjoin temporarily or permanently violations of
this Act regardless of whether the defendant has been convicted
for violations of the penal provisions of this Act, and the board
shall not be required to pay any costs or filing fee or furnish
any bond in connection therewith.
(5) Hearings held by the board under subsection (1) of
this section shall be held in the county where the holder of the
license lives or operates an independent business school.
Section 10. (1) County and Commonwealth's Attorneys
and the Attorney General within their respective jurisdictions shall
prosecute all violations of the penal provisions of this Act and
shall, when requested by the board, represent the board in matters
relating to the enforcement of this Act.
( 2 ) A licensee whose license has been revoked or suspended

Chapter 296 1027
by the board or who has been refused a renewal license may,
within thirty days of the entry of the order, appeal to the circuit
court of the county in which the hearing was held. The person
taking the appeal shall serve upon the secretary of the board, by
certified or registered mail, a written notice of appeal and shall file
with the clerk of the circuit court to which such appeal is taken
an appeal bond with good and sufficient surety, to be approved
by the clerk, conditioned for the speedy prosecution of the appeal
and the payment of any costs charged against him upon the ap
peal. The board, within ten days after the service of notice of
appeal and the filing and approval of the bond, shall transmit
to the clerk of the court a certified copy under the seal of the
board of the evidence upon which the ruling of the board was
based. The clerk shall then docket the case and it shall be tried
in all respects like an ordinary civil action. Either party may ap
peal from the judgment of the circuit court to the Court of Ap
peals as in a civil action. The order appealed from shall be stayed
from the date of the approval of the bond until the final deter
mination of the appeal.
Section 11. (1) Any person who violates the provisions of
Section 3 ( 1 ) of this Act shall be fined not less than one hundred
dollars nor more than five thousand dollars and each dav the
violation continues shall constitute a separate offense.
(2) Each first violation of Section 3 (2) of this Act shall be
punishable by fine of not less than fifty dollars nor more than one
thousand dollars or by imprisonment for not less than one month
nor more than twelve months, or both fine and imprisonment.
Each subsequent violation of Section 3 (2) shall be punishable
by a fine of not less than one hundred dollars nor more than five
thousand dollars and imprisonment for not less than three months
nor more than twelve months, or both fine and imprisonment.
Section 12. The terms of office of the four members of the
board first appointed, from executive or managerial personnel of
independent business schools shall be for one, two, three, and four
years respectively and the terms of office of the two members of
the board first appointed, from commerce or industrv shall be for
three and four years respectively.
Became law without Governor's signature, March 29, 1962

1028 Acts of the General Assembly
CHAPTER 297
(S. B. 224)
AN ACT relating to planning and zoning.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 100.330 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
(1) Each commission shall consist of five "city members"
and five "county members."
(2) The city members shall be chosen in the following
manner: The city manager and the city engineer shall be ex
officio members of the commission. If there is no city manager,
the mayor shall be an ex officio member. If there is no city
engineer, the mayor shall appoint some other city administra
tive official to serve as an ex officio member. The mayor shall
appoint the remaining three city members who shall be residents
of the city and shall hold no other city or county office. All ap
pointments by the mayor shall be subject to approval by the city
legislative body.
(3) The county members shall be chosen in the following
manner: the county judge shall designate one member of the
county legislative body and the county engineer to serve as an
ex officio member [s] of the commission. If there is no county
engineer, the county judge shall appoint some other county
administrative official to serve as an ex officio member. The re
maining three county members shall be chosen by the countv
judge and shall [be residents of the county outside the limits
of the city holding] hold no other city or county office. All ap
pointments by the county judge shall be subject to approval by
the fiscal court.
(4) The citizen members initially appointed by the mayor
shall hold office according to designation in their appointment,
one of them for two years, and the others for terms of four
years each, and upon the expiration of their respective terms,
the successor of each shall hold his office for a term of four
years. Vacancies in the offices of members appointed by the

Chapter 297 1029
mayor shall be filled by successors appointed by the mayor for
the unexpired term.
(5) The county citizen members initially chosen by the
county judge shall hold office one of them for two years, the
others for terms of four years each. Upon expiration of their
respective terms, the successor of each shall hold his office for
a term of four years. Vacancies in the office of the appointive
county members shall be filled by a successor appointed by the
county judge with the approval of the fiscal court for the re
mainder of the term of office in which the vacancy has occurred.
(6) Where any ex officio member in the employment of
the city or county vacates his official position he shall ipso facto
vacate his office as a member of the commission. The terms of
the ex officio members shall correspond to their respective official
tenures except that the terms of the city and county appointive
administrative officials shall terminate respectively, with the
terms of the mayor and county judge selecting them. Any in
cumbent otherwise qualified under this section shall hold office
until his successor is chosen and qualified; provided, however,
that appointea members may be removed by the appointing
authority on written charges after a public hearing.
Section 2. Section 100.350 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
(1) The commission may prepare and approve a compre
hensive plan for the physical development of the whole or any
portion of the city of the second class. The comprehensive plan
may include a master plan, as defined in KRS 100.046. The com
mission may adopt the master plan by a majority vote following
a public hearing.
(2) The commission may make and adopt other plans and
maps of the whole or any portion of the city of the second class,
and of any land within the municipal area, and it may make
changes in the plans or maps when it deems it advisable.
(3) In the maps or plans the commission may provide
among other things for changed regulations of all facilities for
traffic, transportation or communication and for new or changed
harbors, streets, street fixtures, bridges, subways, parks, play

1030 Acts of the Genebal Assembly
grounds or any other public grounds or public structures or public
improvements or appurtenances, and for the general location
and extent of public utilities and terminals whether publicly or
privately owned or operated, for water, light, sanitation, trans
portation, communication, power or other purposes, and for the
removal, relocation, widening, narrowing, abandonment or ex
tension of any public ways or works or structures with a view to
the systematic planning of the city and its environs.
(4) The commission may make and adopt plans for the
control, development and preservation of historical landmarks,
for the design and location of statuary and other works of art
which are or may become the property of the city or the county,
and may make recommendations to any public authorities or to
any persons in the city and municipal area concerning the loca
tion of any building, structures or works to be constructed by
them.
(5) The commission may lay off and establish in the city
and municipal area zones or districts of such number, shape and
area as the commission believes are best to carry out the pur
poses of KRS 100.320 to 100.490, and from time to time change
zones or districts as to area or boundary or location. The zones
or districts shall be designated by maps or plans as well as bv
boundaries, and the boundaries may be expressed in general
descriptive terms. The commission may make or adopt maps or
plans showing the zones or districts, or changes, with appropriate
designations and may prescribe and enforce regulations and re
strictions on the construction, alteration, repair or use of build
ings, structures or land, including the regulation and restriction
of the height, number of stories and size of buildings and other
structures, the percentage of lots that may be occupied, the
size of yards, courts and other open spaces, the density of popula
tion and the location and use of buildings, structures and land
for trade, industry, residence or other purposes.
(6) All regulations and restrictions shall be uniform for
each class or kind of buildings throughout each zone or district,
but the regulations or restrictions in one zone or district, may
differ from those in other zones or districts.
(7) The commission may enter upon lands and make ex
aminations and surveys for the purpose of exercising its powers.

Chapter 297 1031
(8) The commission may make, print and publish and dis
tribute generally copies of any plan, map, report or recommenda
tion made by it.
(9) During the time when the commission is preparing and
approving a comprehensive plan for the physical development
of the whole or any portion of the city of the second class or the
county area, or both, the commission may adopt a temporary
interim zoning plan which shall be effective for a period of not
more than one year, or until the comprehensive plan is passed
into law by the legislative body required to approve same, which
ever shall first occur. Said temporary zoning plan shall be made
effective by following the procedure outlined in KRS 100.390
through 100.410.
Section 3. Section 100.351 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
The commission may exercise the same powers in relation
to the county area as it has in relation to the city, [and] mu
nicipal, and planning areas [as provided in KRS 100.350] .
Section 4. Section 100.354 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
(1) Whenever the commission has adopted the master
plan, or any part of the master plan or amendment thereto, of
the city and county area or of one or more major districts thereof,
no street, square, park, or other public ground, or open space
or public building or structure, or public utility, whether pub
licly or privately owned, shall be constructed or authorized in
the city and county area or planned district [which does not
conform to the recommendations of the plan] until the location,
character and extent thereof has been approved by the com
mission.
(2) In case of disapproval the commission shall communi
cate its reasons to the legislative body, which may overrule the
disapproval by the recorded vote of not less than a majority
of its entire membership. If the public way, ground, space, build
ing, structure or utility is one the authorization or financing of
which is not within the jurisdiction of the legislative body, then

1032 Acts of the General Assembly
the submission to the planning commission shall be by the body
having jurisdiction and the commission's disapproval may be over
ruled by such body by a vote of not less than two-thirds of its
membership. If the commission fails to act on a proposal within
sixty days after submission, approval shall be conclusively pre
sumed.
Section 5. Section 100.355 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
(1) The lawful use of a building or premises, [land for
trade, industry or residence, in cities of the second class and in
counties containing such a city] existing at the time of the
adoption of any zoning regulations affecting it may be continued
[or restrictions, or at the time of the adjustment or revision there
of, or amendment thereto,] although such use does not conform
to the provisions of such [new] regulations [or restrictions] ,
except as otherwise provided herein [may be continued] .
(2) [The use of a building or structure existing at the time
of the adoption of any zoning regulation or restriction, or at the
time of any adjustment or revision thereof or amendment thereto,
although such use does not conform to the provisions of such new
regulations or restrictions, may be continued, and a] No non
conforming use [of the building or structure] may be changed
to another nonconforming use [of the same or more restricted
classification] , or enlarged or extended beyond the scope and
area of its operation at the time the regulation is adopted which
makes its operation nonconforming.
Section 6. Section 100.360 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
( 1 ) All plats of the subdivision of lands within the planning
area, and all instruments of dedication of land for public use,
shall be submitted to the commission and approved by it in writ
ing before they are offered for record or accepted by the legis
lative body. The commission may establish special regulations
to which subdivision plats of no planning significance must con
form, and may then authorize its secretary to approve and sign
such plats on its behalf whenever such plats conform to the com
mission's special regulations. The approval of the commission

Chapter 297 1033
shall not be deemed the legislative body's acceptance of the
dedication of any street or other public ground shown on the plat
or set forth in the instrument.
(2) No street or other public ground shown on a subdi
vision plat of land within the planning area shall be accepted by
the city or county unless the plat and location have been approved
by the commission. The legislative body may submit to the com
mission an ordinance proposing to accept the dedication of any
unapproved street or ground, and, if approved by the commis
sion, the city or county may accept the dedication by a majority
vote of its legislative body, or, if disapproved by the commission,
the dedication may be accepted by a vote of not less than two-
thirds of the legislative body.
(3) All plans, maps, regulations, and restrictions adopted
by the commission shall be made in accordance with a compre
hensive design to promote the public health, safety, morals or
general welfare, by lessening congestion in the streets or by secur
ing safety from fire, panic or other dangers, or by providing ade
quate light and air, or by preventing overcrowding of land, or by
avoiding the undue concentration of population, or by facilitat
ing the adequate provision of transportation, water, sewerage,
schools, parks, playgrounds or other public requirements. All
regulations and restrictions shall be made with reasonable con
sideration of the character of each zone or district affected, and
its peculiar suitability for particular uses.
Section 7. Section 100.380 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
( 1 ) The legislative body shall provide for the expenses and
accommodations necessary for the work of the commission, and
may levy a tax for that purpose if necessary. The commission
may also accept and utilize any funds, personnel or other assist
ance made available by the city, county, state or federal gov
ernment, or private sources, and may enter into agreements of
contracts regarding the acceptance or utilization of such funds
or other assistance.
(2) The annual expenditure of [public] funds by the com
mission shall be limited to the amounts appropriated, or other

1034 Acts of the General Assembly
wise received, for each fiscal year. The fiscal year shall conform
to the fiscal year of the city.
Section 8. Section 100.430 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
(1) Each city of the second class shall have a board of
adjustment, consisting of seven [five] members. Five [four]
members of the board of adjustment shall be appointed by the
mayor, each for a term of four years, except that, in case of those
first appointed, two members shall be appointed for a term of
four [two] years, and two members [shall be appointed] for
a term of three [four] years and one member for a term of two
years. The remaining members of the board of adjustment shall
be appointed for a term of four years by the fiscal court. All
members of the board of adjustment shall serve without com
pensation.
(2) Any member of the board may be removed by the
appointing authority for cause, on written charges after public
hearing. Vacancies shall be filled by the appointing authority
for the unexpired term.
Section 9. Section 100.470 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
(1) The board of adustment may:
(a) Adopt rules and regulations for its own government,
not inconsistent with any law, city ordinance, or order or resolu
tion of the fiscal court, with reference to the plans under con
sideration by the board;
(b) Hear and decide appeals and applications for special
exceptions;
(c) Authorize, upon application for variance, such vari
ances from the terms of the ordinance or of any plans, rules, or
regulations made thereunder, as will not be contrary to the pub-
he interest, where a literal enforcement of the provisions of the
ordinance, plans, rules or regulations would result in unnecessary
hardship, and so that the spirit of the ordinance shall be observed
and substantial justice done.

Chapter 298 1035
( 2 ) In exercising its powers, the board may reverse, affirm
or modify an order or decision of an administrative official.
(3) The board of adjustment shall have the same powers
in the county area as it has in the city or municipal area.
Became law without Governor's signature, March 29, 1962
CHAPTER 298
(S. B. 321)
AN ACT imposing a penalty for certain sexual perversions, practices
or misconduct.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 435.105 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is amended
to read as follows:
( 1 ) Any person of the age of seventeen years or over who
carnally abuses the body, or indulges in any indecent or immoral
practices with the body or organs of any child under the age of
fifteen years, or who induces, procures or permits a child under
the age of fifteen years to indulge in immoral, sexual or indecent
practices with himself or any person shall be guilty of a felony,
punishable on conviction thereof by imprisonment in the pen
itentiary for not less than one year nor more than ten years.
(2) Any person of the age of seventeen years or over who
carnally abuses the body, or indulges in any indecent or immoral
practices with the body or organs of any other person of the age
of fifteen years or over or who induces, procures or permits any
person of the age of fifteen years or older to indulge in immoral,
sexual or indecent practices with himself or any other person,
not otherwise denounced in this chapter, shall be guilty of a
felony, punishable on conviction thereof by an imprisonment
in the penitentiary for not less than one nor more than five years.
Became law without Governor's signature, March 29, 1962

1036 Acts of the General Assembly
CHAPTER 299
(H. B. 225)
AN ACT relating to the excise tax on alcoholic beverages.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 243.730 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is amended
to read as follows:
(1) (a) Wholesalers of distilled spirits and wine shall
pay and report the tax levied by KRS 243.720 (1) and (2) on
or before the fifteenth day of the calendar month next succeed
ing the month in which possession or title of the alcoholic bev
erages is acquired, in accordance with the rules and regulations
of the Department of Revenue designed reasonably to protect
the revenues of the Commonwealth.
(b) [After March 31, 1960] Brewers of malt beverages
shall pay and report the tax levied by KRS 243.720 (3) on or be
fore the fifteenth day of the calendar month next succeeding the
month in which the brewer sells, transfers, or passes title of the
malt beverage to a distributor, retailer or consumer within this
state, in accordance with rules and regulations of the Depart
ment of Revenue designed reasonably to protect the revenues of
the Commonwealth.
(2) (a) Every wholesaler of distilled spirits or wine be
fore using, selling or distributing by sale or gift distilled spirits
and wine shall qualify with the Department of Revenue. In order
to so qualify and in lieu of furnishing a bond or other security,
each wholesaler who was licensed in this state on February 29,
1960, shall make an advance payment of tax to the department
in an amount equal to twice the average monthly liability of such
wholesaler for taxes imposed by KRS 243.720 (1) and (2) dur
ing the calendar year 1959 as shown by records of the department.
[If such wholesaler shall have in his possession unused tax
stamps which he has already purchased from the department,
he may apply the unused stamps toward the amount of tax
required by this subsection according to the rules and regulations
of the department.]
( b) Any person who acquired or who shall acquire a license

Chapter 299 1037
as a wholesaler of distilled spirits or wine on or after March 1,
1960 shall immediately prepare and file a report on a form pre
scribed by the department. Such report shall contain an estimate
of the total number of wine gallons of distilled spirits and the
total number of gallons of wine for which the wholesaler expects
to become liable for the payment of tax under KRS 243.720 ( 1 )
and (2) during the first twelve months that he is engaged in
business. Immediately upon the receipt of the report from the
wholesaler, the department shall determine the average monthly
amount of tax imposed by KRS 243.720 (1) and (2) for which
it appears that the wholesaler may become liable and shall bill
the wholesaler for an amount of tax equal to twice the monthly
average. The department shall have the power to adjust the
estimate in order to make it conform as nearly as practicable
with the past experience of other wholesalers who have been
engaged in business for twelve months or more, and in no event
shall the advance payment required of any new wholesaler be
less than one-half of the average amount of advance payments
required under paragraph (a) of this subsection, as determined
by the department. The bill for advance tax shall be due and
payable immediately upon receipt by the wholesaler. He shall
make payment to the department by cashier's or certified check
before using, selling or distributing by sale or gift any distilled
spirits or wine.
(3) (a) Beginning April 1, 1960, every brewer before
selling or distributing by sale or gift malt beverages, or before
importing malt beverages into the state, shall qualify with the
Department of Revenue. In order to so qualify, each brewer who
was licensed in this state on February 29, 1960 or who was
qualified to import malt beverages into this state on Februray
29, 1960, shall make an advance payment of tax to the department
in an amount equal to twice the average monthly liability of
such brewer for taxes imposed by KRS 243.720 (3) during the
calendar year 1959 as shown by records of the department. [If
such brewer shall have in his possession unused barrel stamps
which he has already purchased from the department, he may
apply the unused barrel stamps toward the amount of tax
required by this subsection according to the rules and regulations
of the department.]

1038 Acts of the General Assembly
[(b) Upon application and proof of previous compliance
with the payment and reporting of the tax in accordance with
the applicable regulations, the department may substitute bond,
with or without approved surety, as determined in its discretion
upon consideration of the financial condition of the applicant,
for one-half of the advanced payments made pursuant to sub
section (2) and this subsection, and the amount of such pre
payment for which bond is substituted shall be applied as credits
on the tax due by such brewer. The balance of such prepay
ments shall be refunded, in cash or by credit, only at such time
as the brewer ceases doing business in Kentucky in accordance
with the rules and regulations of the department.]
(b) [c] Any person who acquired or who shall acquire a
license as a brewer, or who shall apply for authority to import
malt beverages into this state on or after March 1, 1960, shall
immediately prepare and file a report on a form prescribed by
the department. Such report shall contain an estimate of the
total quantity of malt beverages for which the brewer expects to
become liable for the payment of tax under KRS 243.720 (3)
during the first twelve months that he is engaged in business.
Immediately upon the receipt of the report from the brewer, the
department shall determine the average monthly amount of tax
imposed by KRS 243.720 (3) for which it appears that the
brewer may become liable and shall bill the brewer for an
amount of tax equal to twice the monthly average. The depart
ment shall have the power to adjust the estimate in order to
make it conform as nearly as practicable with the past experi
ence of other brewers who have been engaged in business for
twelve months or more, and in no event shall the advance pay
ment required of any new brewer be less than one-half of the
average amount of advance payment required under paragraph
(a) of this subsection, as determined by the department. The
bill for advance tax shall be due and payable immediately upon
receipt by the brewer. He shall make payment to the department
by cashier's or certified check before selling or distributing malt
beverages or before importing malt beverages into this state.
[(4) No part of any advance payment made pursuant to
subsections (2) and (3) of this section shall be subject to refund
but shall be restored to the wholesaler or brewer in the form of a

Chapter 300 1039
credit or credits only at such time as the wholesaler or brewer
ceases doing business in Kentucky according to rules and regu
lations of the department.]
(4) (a) Upon application and proof of previous compliance
with the payment and reporting of the tax in accordance with the
applicable regulations, the department may substitute bond, with
or without approved surety, as determined in its discretion upon
consideration of the financial condition of the applicant, for all
or any part of the advanced payments made pursuant to this
section.
(b) Upon substitution of bond as provided herein, advance
payment of taxes made by the wholesaler or brewer pursuant to
this section shall be applied as credits on the tax due by such
wholesaler or brewer pursuant to rules and regulations of the
department. In the event that any wholesaler or brewer shall
cease doing business in this state, any such advance payment of
taxes, not previously credited, shall be refunded pursuant to rules
and regulations of the department.
[(5) This section shall not be construed in any manner
which would require any wholesaler or brewer who has already
made an advance payment of tax prior to March 25, 1960, accord
ing to rules and regulations of the Department of Revenue to
make any additional advance payment in order to qualify
hereunder.]
[ (6) The department is hereby authorized and empowered
to require a corporate surety bond in an amount satisfactory to
the department from all persons who were qualified to import
malt beverages into this state on February 29, 1960, but who did
not in fact import any malt beverages after March 25, I960.]
Became law without Governor's signature, March 29, 1962

1040 Acts of the General Assembly
CHAPTER 301
(H. B. 347)
AN ACT relating to abandoned motor vehicles.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Any person who leaves a motor vehicle on property belong
ing to the Department of Highways for a longer period than
thirty days and under circumstances indicating abandonment
shall be fined not less than twenty-five dollars nor more than
one hundred dollars, or imprisoned for not less than ten nor more
than thirty days.
Any such vehicle so allowed to remain on property belong
ing to the Department of Highways shall be towed away by
officers, agents, or employees of the Department of Highways.
In addition to other penalties herein provided, cost of towing
and any storage charges which may accrue shall be paid by
the owner of the vehicle before such vehicle is released from
the custody of the Department of Highways.
Any such vehicle which is towed away by officers, agents,
or employees of the Department of Highways and which is not
claimed by the owner thereof or his authorized agent within sixty
days, may be sold to pay towing and storage charges, which shall
constitute a hen against such vehicle within the meaning of
this act. The proposed sale shall be advertised for at least three
times in some newspaper of general circulation in the county
wherein the vehicle was left on property belonging to the De
partment of Highways, the last advertisement to be made at least
six days before the sale is held and notice thereof shall be sent
by registered mail to any person known to have any interest in
the vehicle at his last known address at least ten days before the
sale is held.
A transfer of registration shall be issued to the purchaser
by the county clerk of the county wherein the vehicle is sold.
The person in whose name the vehicle was last registered shall
comply with the provisions of KRS 186.190 to accomplish the
purposes of this act.

Chapter 302 1041
If such vehicle is unfit for future use, the Department may
dispose of it for its salvage value in the same manner as provided
for the disposition of other such materials.
Became law without Governor's signature, March 29, 1962
CHAPTER 302
(H. B. 355)
AN ACT relating to tobacco.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. A Tobacco Board of Trade shall be organized
on every market at which tobacco is offered for sale at auction
and each board shall have the objects, purposes, and powers to:
promote a more orderly market for the sale of tobacco at auc
tion; prevent market gluts by arranging for a more orderly flow
of tobacco through the warehouses; encourage ethical practices
in the auction system; support the continuation of the produc
tion control program; adopt bylaws or regulations defining noting
rights of the Tobacco Board of Trade members; collect dues
and initiation fees to cover the operating expenses of the To
bacco Board of Trade; adopt regulations for the conduct of the
tobacco market including the allocation of selling time and selling
space. -- •
Section 2. The membership of each Tobacco Board of
Trade shall be divided into two classes; active members and
associate members. Any person, partnership, corporation, or as
sociation operating a loose leaf auction warehouse or warehouses
shall be eligible for active membership in the Tobacco Board
of Trade, provided, however, that each Board of Trade shall
have the right to establish the basis of voting rights for its
warehouse members according to floor space, ownership, number
of separate warehouse buildings, or otherwise. Any person, part
nership, corporation, or association not eligible for active mem

1042 Acts of the General Assembly
bership but holding a tobacco dealer's license and purchasing
tobacco pursuant to said license upon the market governed by
the Tobacco Board of Trade shall be eligible for election as
an associate member. Each associate member shall have the
option of becoming, upon written notice to the Tobacco Board
of Trade, either a participating or a non-participating member.
A non-participating member shall not have the right to vote
upon any question coming before the Tobacco Board of Trade.
A participating associate member shall not participate in or have
any voice or vote in the management, conduct, activities, allot
ments of sale time and/or hours, affixing of dates for the open
ing or closing of the tobacco markets serviced bv the Tobacco
Board of Trade, or in any other manner or respect relating
strictly to the functions normally performed by the warehouse
members of the Tobacco Board of Trade.
Section 3. Every tobacco warehouse offering tobacco for
sale at auction shall be a member of its local Tobacco Board
of Trade, and membership in good standing shall be deemed to
be a reasonable requirement by such Tobacco Board of Trade as
a condition precedent to participating in the business of operating
a tobacco warehouse or the purchase of tobacco at auction
therein.
Became law without Governor's signature, March 29, 1962
CHAPTER 303
(H. B. 362)
AN ACT relating to transportation.
WHEREAS, restrictions upon the rights of carriers to compete on equal
terms for traffic are not in the interest of the citizens of Kentucky;
and
WHEREAS, restraints upon such competition may injure industries
located in Kentucky by making them less able to compete for
markets; and
WHEREAS, agreements in restraint of trade are not in keeping with
public policy of Kentucky;

Chapter 304 1043
NOW, THEREFORE,
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. As used in this Act, unless the context requires
otherwise, the term "labor organization" means any organization
of any kind, or any agency or employe representation committee
or plan, in which employes participate and which exists for the
purpose, in whole or in part, of dealing with employers concerning
grievances, labor disputes, wages, rates of pay, hours of employ
ment or conditions of work.
Section 2. It shall be unlawful for any carrier or shipper of
property, or any association of such carriers or shippers, to agree
to pay, or to pay, to or for the benefit of a labor organization,
directly or indirectly, any charge by reason of the placing upon,
delivery to, or movement by rail, or by a railroad car, of a motor
vehicle, trailer or container which is also capable of being moved
or propelled upon the highways.
Section 3. It shall be unlawful for any labor organization to
accept or receive or to agree to accept or receive from any carrier
or shipper of property, or any association of such carriers or ship
pers, any payment described in Section 2 of this Act.
Section 4. Any corporation, association, organization, or
person that violates Sections 2 and 3 of this Act shall be fined
not less than one hundred dollars nor more than one thousand
dollars for each offense. Each act of violation, and each day
during which such an agreement remains in effect, shall consti
tute a separate offense.
Became law without Governor's signature, March 29, 1962
CHAPTER 304
(H. B. 436)
AN ACT relating to claims against the Commonwealth of Kentucky.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:

1044 Acts of the Genebal Assembly
Section 1. In enacting this Act, it is the expressed intent of
the General Assembly to formulate an exception to the one year
statute of limitations in which a claim against the Commonwealth
must be presented before the Board of Claims. Facts have been
found by the General Assembly which clearly prove that this
one year statute of limitations works inequities and hardships
against a certain class of citizens of this Commonwealth due to
the following reasons. The Court of Appeals has recently held
that the General Assembly cannot, by act or resolution, give an
individual permission to sue the Commonwealth due to the fact
that a general remedy has been provided by KRS 44.070 to 44.170,
and due to the fact that subsequent to this opinion of the Court,
the amount recoverable under the provisions of KRS 44.070 to
44.170 has been increased. This Act is passed with the intent
to correct the inequities that have resulted.
Section 2. Section 44.110 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
(1) A claim must be presented to the board within one year
from the time it shall have accrued; except that any claim that
accrued between March 1, 1956, and July 1, 1957, may be pre
sented up to and including June 30, 1958.
(2) The provisions of subsection (1) of this section shall
not be applicable to any person who was diligently pursuing his
remedies in the courts of this Commonwealth prior to the holding
of the Court of Appeals in 1958 to the effect that the courts of
this Commonwealth may not render a judgment against the
Commonwealth due to the general remedy provided in KRS
44.070 to 44.170.
(3) Any person who qualifies under the provisions of sub
section (2) of this section may, within a period of one year from
the effective date of this Act, present his claim before the Board
of Claims as provided in KRS 44.070 to 44.170.
Became law without Governor's signature, March 29, 1962

Chapter 305 1045
CHAPTER 305
(H. B. 451)
AN ACT relating to the acquisition of ferries within certain distances
of interstate bridges.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. As used in this Act, "interstate bridge" shall mean
any bridge across a navigable stream one terminus of which is
located in the Commonwealth of Kentucky and the other terminus
of which is located in a neighboring state other than the Com
monwealth of Kentucky.
Section 2. The provisions of this Act shall apply to any
bridge or bridges constructed by any agency, commission or
administrative body of the Commonwealth of Kentucky acting
alone or in conjunction with any agency, commission or adminis
trative body of any other state, or the Federal Government.
Section 3. Prior to the opening for public use of any
interstate bridge, the construction of which is authorized by any
law of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, regardless of the state
agency, commission or administrative body so authorized to
make such construction, the state agency, commission or admin
istrative body so authorized to construct, operate and maintain
any such interstate bridge shall purchase the ferry, equipment,
franchises, rights and privileges used in connection with the
operation of any ferry which has been in continuous operation
for at least fifteen years prior thereto and which is located within
five miles of the site of said bridge, and pay the owner therefor
a fair cash value; provided, that in the event such agency,
commission or administrative body and the owner of such ferry
shall be unable to agree upon a fair cash value, the valuation
of such property shall be determined by three persons; one to be
selected by the agency authorized to make such construction,
one to be selected by the owners of the ferry and the third to
be selected by these two. The ferry shall be valued as a going
concern, but no allowance shall be made for future growth. In
the event the owner of such ferry shall not agree to the establish

1046 Acts of the General Assembly
ment of such valuation according to the foregoing method, thenin such event, the agency, commission or administrative bodyshall not be required to purchase such assets from the ownerof the ferry.
Section 4. Should the Commonwealth of Kentucky, or any
agency, commission or administrative body thereof, join with anyother state or any agency, commission or administrative bodythereof for the purpose of constructing any bridge or bridges as
herein referred to, the parties to such joint venture or under
taking shall agree as to which of them shall proceed to acquire
a ferry or ferries, as referred to in Section 3 hereof.
Section 5. All Acts in conflict herewith are repealed.
Became law without Governor's signature, March 29, 1962
